topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Automobiles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: TYPES OF AUTOMOBILES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **automobile** (from the Greek *auto* = self, and the Latin *mobilis* = movable) is a self-propelled, wheeled, ground-based passenger or commercial vehicle that uses an on-board prime-mover (typically an internal combustion engine, electric motor, or hybrid power-plant) to convert stored energy into mechanical motion for transportation of people and/or goods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT636, Module 1):**
> This topic is foundational and requires the student to classify automobiles based on:
> 1. **Purpose / Use** (Passenger vs. Commercial, Off-road, Special purpose)
> 2. **Fuel / Energy Source** (Petrol, Diesel, CNG, LPG, Electric, Hybrid, Hydrogen, Solar)
> 3. **Drive / Transmission** (FWD, RWD, AWD, 4WD)
> 4. **Body Style** (Sedan, Hatchback, SUV, MPV, Coupe, Convertible, Pickup, Truck, Bus)
> 5. **Capacity / Class** (Micro, Mini, Compact, Mid-size, Full-size, Luxury)
> 6. **Number of Wheels** (2W, 3W, 4W, 6W, multi-axle)
> 7. **Load capacity / GVW** (Light, Medium, Heavy Commercial Vehicles)

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **family of vehicles** — just like how we classify living things into mammals, reptiles, birds, etc., engineers classify automobiles into families based on what they do, how they move, and what powers them.

- A **Honda City** is to a **Tata Truck** what a *house cat* is to a *working horse* — both move, but their purpose, size, and strength differ drastically.
- A **Tesla Model 3** is to a **Maruti Alto** what a *fluorescent lamp* is to a *candle* — both produce "light" (motion), but the energy source and mechanism are entirely different.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** When asked "classify automobiles," always state the **classification criterion first**, then list the categories. Examiners reward structured thinking.

### Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW):** Total weight of vehicle + payload + fuel + passengers (in **kg** or **tonnes**).
- **Kerb Weight:** Weight of vehicle with full fuel, lubricants, and fluids, but **without** passengers or cargo.
- **Payload Capacity:** GVW − Kerb Weight (in **kg**).
- **Wheelbase:** Distance between the front and rear axles (in **mm**).
- **Engine Displacement:** Volume swept by all pistons (in **cc** or **litres**).

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & Classification Framework

The classification of automobiles is **multi-dimensional**. A single vehicle can simultaneously belong to several categories. For example, a **Toyota Innova Crysta** is a *passenger vehicle*, *diesel/petrol-powered*, *RWD or FWD*, *MPV (body style)*, and *4-wheeled (chassis type)*.

### 2.1 Classification Based on Purpose / Use

This is the most common classification used by KTU examiners and follows the **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards)** issued by the Government of India.

| **Category** | **Definition** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **Passenger Vehicles (PV)** | Designed primarily to carry people (≤ 9 persons including driver) | Maruti Swift, Hyundai Creta, Honda City |
| **Commercial Vehicles (CV)** | Designed to carry goods or > 9 persons for hire/reward | Tata LPT trucks, Ashok Leyland buses |
| **Off-Road Vehicles** | Built for unpaved, rough terrain with high ground clearance | Mahindra Thar, Jeep Wrangler, Land Rover Defender |
| **Special Purpose Vehicles** | Designed for specific tasks (military, fire, ambulance, cranes) | Mahindra Mine Commander, Tata Pravaig |

### 2.2 Classification Based on Number of Wheels (Chassis Configuration)

- **Two-Wheelers (2W):** Motorcycles, scooters, mopeds — *single track*, lean-to-steer.
- **Three-Wheelers (3W):** Auto-rickshaws, goods carriers (Bajaj RE, Piaggio Ape).
- **Four-Wheelers (4W):** All passenger cars and most LCVs.
- **Six-Wheelers and above:** Trucks, buses, multi-axle heavy commercial vehicles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When drawing the chassis layout, the wheel arrangement is expressed as **"N × M"**, where **N** = total wheels, **M** = driven wheels. Example: *4 × 2* (rear-wheel drive car), *6 × 4* (three-axle truck with two driving axles).

### 2.3 Classification Based on Drive / Transmission Layout

| **Drive Type** | **Description** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **FWD (Front-Wheel Drive)** | Engine power sent to front wheels only | Most compact & mid-size cars (Maruti Swift) |
| **RWD (Rear-Wheel Drive)** | Engine power sent to rear wheels | Luxury sedans, sports cars, trucks (BMW 3 Series, Tata Ace) |
| **AWD (All-Wheel Drive)** | Power sent to all wheels, on-demand | SUVs (Audi Quattro, Subaru) |
| **4WD (Four-Wheel Drive)** | Driver-selectable 4-wheel engagement | Off-road vehicles (Mahindra Thar, Jeep) |

### 2.4 Classification Based on Body Style (Passenger Vehicles)

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** emphasises body-style nomenclature, as it is the language of the automotive industry.

| **Body Style** | **Description** | **Indian Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Sedan** | 3-box design: engine, cabin, boot separated | Honda City, Hyundai Verna |
| **Hatchback** | 2-box design: cabin + vertical tailgate | Maruti Swift, Hyundai i20 |
| **SUV (Sport Utility Vehicle)** | High ground clearance, rugged body | Tata Nexon, Mahindra XUV700 |
| **MPV / MUV** | Multi-Purpose Vehicle, people-mover, usually monocoque | Maruti Ertiga, Toyota Innova |
| **Coupe** | 2-door fixed-roof sporty car | Toyota Supra (limited in India) |
| **Convertible / Cabriolet** | Retractable soft/hard top | Mini Cooper Convertible |
| **Pickup Truck** | Open cargo bed at rear | Isuzu D-Max, Tata Xenon |
| **Wagon / Estate** | Extended hatchback with long roof | Maruti Suzuki Swift Dzire (tour) |

### 2.5 Classification Based on Fuel / Energy Source

This is **the most rapidly evolving** classification due to the shift toward e-mobility.

| **Fuel Type** | **Pollution Norms** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **Petrol (Gasoline)** | BS-VI / BS-VII | Most passenger cars |
| **Diesel** | BS-VI | SUVs, trucks, commercial vehicles |
| **CNG (Compressed Natural Gas)** | Cleaner than petrol | Maruti WagonR, Hyundai Aura |
| **LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)** | Aftermarket kits | Maruti Omni (legacy) |
| **Electric (BEV)** | Zero tailpipe emission | Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV, Tesla |
| **Hybrid (HEV)** | Petrol + Battery (no plug) | Toyota Camry Hybrid |
| **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** | Petrol + Battery (plug-in) | Volvo XC90 T8 |
| **Hydrogen (FCEV)** | Water-vapour emission | Toyota Mirai |
| **Solar / Bio-fuel** | Experimental | Sono Sion (solar) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Relevance:** The Indian Government has mandated that **30% of new vehicle sales** must be electric by 2030, and India is moving to **BS-VII (Bharat Stage VII) emission norms by 2027–2028**. Examiners love asking comparative questions on ICE vs. EV.

### 2.6 Classification Based on Load Capacity (Commercial Vehicles)

Following the **Indian Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (amended 2017)**:

- **Light Commercial Vehicles (LCV):** GVW ≤ 7,500 kg → Tata Ace Gold (Mini-truck).
- **Medium Commercial Vehicles (MCV):** GVW 7,500 kg to 16,200 kg → Eicher Pro 3015.
- **Heavy Commercial Vehicles (HCV):** GVW > 16,200 kg → Tata Prima, Ashok Leyland 2820.
- **Multi-Axle Vehicles (MAV):** 3+ axles, ultra-heavy haulage → Tippers, Tractors with trailers.

### 2.7 Classification Based on Engine Layout (Technical Depth)

| **Layout** | **Notation** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| Inline (Straight) | I3, I4, I5, I6 | Mass-market cars |
| V-Configuration | V6, V8, V12 | Luxury & performance |
| Flat (Boxer / Horizontally Opposed) | H4, H6 | Subaru, Porsche |
| Rotary (Wankel) | 13B, Renesis | Mazda RX-7 (legacy) |
| Electric Motor | Single, Dual, Tri-motor | Tesla Model S Plaid (Tri-motor) |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Classification Matrix (KTU Problem-Solving Logic)

Since "Types of Automobiles" is fundamentally a classification topic, the KTU board often frames a **14-mark question** as:

> *"Classify automobiles. Discuss in detail the classification based on body style and drive layout with neat sketches."*

### Analytical Method — The "CAP-FDB-EW" Mnemonic

To answer any classification question systematically, use this **6-axis framework**:

$$
\textbf{CAP-FDB-EW} = \begin{cases} \textbf{C} & \text{Chassis / Number of Wheels} \\ \textbf{A} & \text{Application / Purpose} \\ \textbf{P} & \text{Power-plant / Fuel} \\ \textbf{F} & \text{Frame type (Body style / Body-on-frame vs. Monocoque)} \\ \textbf{D} & \text{Drive layout (FWD/RWD/AWD/4WD)} \\ \textbf{B} & \text{Build classification (LCV/MCV/HCV)} \\ \textbf{E} & \text{Emission norms (BS-VI/BS-VII)} \\ \textbf{W} & \text{Weight class (Kerb weight, GVW)} \end{cases}
$$

### Example Problem — Worked-Out (KTU Pattern)

**Q:** A vehicle has the following specifications:
- Kerb weight = 1,200 kg
- Payload = 400 kg
- Fuel + Fluids = 60 kg
- Driver + Passengers (5) = 5 × 70 = 350 kg

**Find:** (a) GVW (b) Classify using the **CAP-FDB-EW** framework.

**Step 1: Compute Kerb Weight (given)**
$$
W_{\text{kerb}} = 1{,}200 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Payload + Passenger + Fuel Mass**
$$
W_{\text{load}} = 400 + 350 + 60 = 810 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3: Compute GVW using the fundamental definition**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{GVW}} &= W_{\text{kerb}} + W_{\text{load}} \\[4pt]
&= 1{,}200 + 810 \\[4pt]
&= 2{,}010 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $W_{\text{GVW}} = 2{,}010 \text{ kg} = 2.01 \text{ tonnes}$

**Step 4: Classification**

| **Axis** | **Classification** |
|---|---|
| **C** (Chassis) | 4 × 2 (typical for 5-seater passenger car) |
| **A** (Application) | Passenger Vehicle (PV) |
| **P** (Power-plant) | Petrol/Diesel (assume ICE) |
| **F** (Frame) | Monocoque (unibody) construction |
| **D** (Drive) | FWD (Front-Wheel Drive) |
| **B** (Build) | Below 7,500 kg → LMV (Light Motor Vehicle) per MV Act |
| **E** (Emission) | BS-VI Phase 2 (current Indian norm) |
| **W** (Weight class) | Compact / Mid-size segment |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a numerical GVW/Passenger problem is given, always **show the unit conversion** explicitly (kg ↔ tonnes). Marks are awarded for the unit, not just the number.

---

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define the term "automobile." List any four classifications of automobiles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **automobile** is a self-propelled, ground-based vehicle used for the transport of passengers and/or goods, powered by an on-board prime-mover (commonly an Internal Combustion Engine or electric motor).

**Four classifications** (any 4 × 0.5 = 2 marks + 1 mark for definition):

1. Based on **purpose** – Passenger / Commercial / Off-road / Special purpose.
2. Based on **fuel** – Petrol, Diesel, CNG, LPG, Electric, Hybrid.
3. Based on **number of wheels** – 2W, 3W, 4W, 6W, multi-axle.
4. Based on **body style** – Sedan, Hatchback, SUV, MPV, Coupe, Convertible.
5. Based on **GVW** – LCV, MCV, HCV, MAV.

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected]**
> *Differentiate between FWD and RWD layouts. Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Parameter** | **FWD (Front-Wheel Drive)** | **RWD (Rear-Wheel Drive)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Engine power transmitted to front wheels | Engine power transmitted to rear wheels |
| **Weight Distribution** | Heavier front (engine over drive wheels) | Balanced 50:50 (rear-heavy) |
| **Traction** | Better in wet/snow (weight on drive wheels) | Better under hard acceleration |
| **Cost** | Cheaper (no propeller shaft to rear) | More expensive (longitudinal drivetrain) |
| **Example** | Maruti Swift, Hyundai i20 | BMW 3 Series, Tata Ace Gold |

*(1 mark for FWD definition + example, 1 mark for RWD definition + example, 1 mark for differentiation table or key point.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Format

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam) pattern, the student must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified from Dec 2023]

> **A.** *(a)* Classify automobiles in detail based on **body style**, with neat sketches and Indian examples for each. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Explain the classification of **Commercial Vehicles (CV)** as per the **Motor Vehicles Act of India**, with reference to GVW limits. *(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — A(a): Body-Style Classification (7 marks)

A **body style** defines the overall shape, layout of passenger compartment, cargo space, and number of doors. It is determined by **function, aesthetics, and aerodynamics**.

| **S.No** | **Body Style** | **Distinct Feature** | **Indian Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sedan** | 3-box: engine, cabin, separate boot | Honda City, Hyundai Verna |
| 2 | **Hatchback** | 2-box: vertical tailgate, integrated boot | Maruti Swift, Tata Tiago |
| 3 | **SUV (Sport Utility Vehicle)** | High GC, rugged, optional 4WD | Tata Nexon, Mahindra Thar |
| 4 | **MPV (Multi-Purpose Vehicle)** | 3-row seating, people-mover | Maruti Ertiga, Toyota Innova |
| 5 | **Coupe** | 2-door, fixed roof, sporty | BMW Z4 (CBU import) |
| 6 | **Convertible** | Retractable roof | Mini Cooper S Cabrio |
| 7 | **Pickup Truck** | Open cargo bed at rear | Isuzu D-Max V-Cross |
| 8 | **Wagon / Estate** | Extended hatchback with longer roof | Skoda Octavia Combi (EU-style) |

**Marking distribution:**
- 8 styles × 0.5 = 4 marks (name + key feature)
- 1 Indian example each → 1 mark
- Neat sketch / diagram block (Mermaid or hand-drawn) → 1 mark
- Concluding sentence on segment growth (SUV/MPV booming in India) → 1 mark

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — A(b): Commercial Vehicle Classification (7 marks)

The **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (Amended 2017)** classifies CVs by **GVW (Gross Vehicle Weight)**.

The GVW is computed as:
$$
W_{\text{GVW}} = W_{\text{kerb}} + W_{\text{payload}} + W_{\text{fuel}} + W_{\text{crew}}
$$

| **Category** | **GVW Range** | **Typical Application** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LCV — Light Commercial Vehicle** | ≤ 7,500 kg (≤ 7.5 t) | Intra-city goods, last-mile delivery | Tata Ace Gold, Mahindra Jeeto |
| **MCV — Medium Commercial Vehicle** | 7,500 kg to 16,200 kg (7.5 – 16.2 t) | Inter-city cargo, mini-buses | Eicher Pro 3015, BharatBenz 914 |
| **HCV — Heavy Commercial Vehicle** | > 16,200 kg (> 16.2 t) | Long-haul trucks, full-size buses | Tata Prima, Ashok Leyland 2820 |
| **MAV — Multi-Axle Vehicle** | > 25,000 kg, 3+ axles | Mining, ultra-heavy haulage | Tippers, Tractor-trailers |

**Marking distribution:**
- Formula of GVW → 1 mark
- 4 categories with GVW range → 4 marks
- One example per category → 1 mark
- Diagram of chassis (4×2, 6×4, etc.) → 1 mark

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Modified from June 2024 Model Paper]

> **B.** *(a)* Explain in detail the classification of automobiles based on **fuel/energy source**, with a comparative table of Petrol, Diesel, CNG, Electric, and Hybrid vehicles. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Discuss **FWD, RWD, AWD, and 4WD** drive layouts with neat diagrams. State the **wheel-notation system** (e.g., 4×2, 4×4) used for chassis classification. *(7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — B(a): Fuel/Energy Source Classification (7 marks)

| **Fuel Type** | **Energy Source** | **Tailpipe Emission** | **Thermal Efficiency** | **Indian Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Petrol (SI Engine)** | Refined crude oil | CO₂, CO, NOx, HC | 25–35% | Maruti Swift |
| **Diesel (CI Engine)** | Diesel (compression-ignited) | Lower CO₂, more NOx & PM | 35–45% | Tata Nexon Diesel |
| **CNG** | Compressed Methane | Lower CO, HC; small NOx | 30–38% | Maruti WagonR CNG |
| **LPG** | Liquefied Petroleum Gas | Lower than petrol | 28–35% | Aftermarket kits |
| **Battery Electric (BEV)** | Grid electricity (Li-ion) | **Zero tailpipe** | 80–90% (motor efficiency) | Tata Nexon EV, MG ZS EV |
| **Hybrid (HEV)** | Petrol + Battery regen | 30–40% lower than pure ICE | 40–50% combined | Toyota Camry Hybrid |
| **Plug-in Hybrid (PHEV)** | Petrol + plug-in battery | Very low, battery-mode zero | 45–55% | Volvo XC90 T8 |
| **Hydrogen FCEV** | H₂ + O₂ → H₂O + electricity | Only water vapour | 50–60% | Toyota Mirai |

**Key Conceptual Points (2 marks):**
- The **shift from ICE → BEV → FCEV** is the global roadmap under the **Paris Climate Accord**.
- India's **FAME-II policy** and **PLI scheme** subsidize EV manufacturing.
- Hybrid vehicles are a **transitional technology** (bridge between ICE and full EV).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — B(b): Drive Layouts (7 marks)

| **Drive Type** | **Wheel Notation** | **Description** | **Pros & Cons** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FWD** | 4 × 2 (front-driven) | Engine + transmission at front, drives front wheels | Pro: Cheaper, better wet traction. Con: Torque steer, limited handling | Maruti Swift |
| **RWD** | 4 × 2 (rear-driven) | Front engine, drives rear wheels via propeller shaft | Pro: Balanced handling, higher payload. Con: Heavier, more parts | Tata Ace, BMW 3 Series |
| **AWD** | 4 × 4 (full-time auto) | All wheels driven, on-demand clutch/transfer | Pro: Always ready, superior grip. Con: Fuel penalty, complexity | Audi Quattro, Subaru |
| **4WD (Part-time)** | 4 × 4 (selectable) | Driver manually engages 4WD via lever/button | Pro: Off-road capable. Con: Not for dry tarmac | Mahindra Thar, Jeep Wrangler |

**Wheel Notation System (2 marks):**
$$
\boxed{\text{N} \times \text{M}}
$$
where **N** = total wheels, **M** = driven wheels.

| **Notation** | **Meaning** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| 4 × 2 | 4 wheels, 2 driven | Cars, LCVs |
| 4 × 4 | 4 wheels, 4 driven | SUVs, off-road |
| 6 × 2 | 6 wheels, 2 driven | Long-trailer trucks |
| 6 × 4 | 6 wheels, 4 driven | Tipper, 3-axle truck |
| 6 × 6 | 6 wheels, 6 driven | Military vehicles |

**Marking distribution:**
- 4 drive types × 1 mark each = 4 marks
- Wheel notation explanation + examples table = 2 marks
- Diagram (Mermaid or sketch of drivetrain) = 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
>
> 1. **Confusing AWD with 4WD:** AWD = always on, automatic; 4WD = driver-selectable, off-road focus. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark if you mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the GVW Formula:** Always write $W_{\text{GVW}} = W_{\text{kerb}} + W_{\text{payload}}$ before substituting values. Marks are reserved for the **formula step**.
> 3. **Using "Automobile" and "Vehicle" interchangeably:** Technically, *automobile* excludes railway vehicles, rickshaws (manually pulled), and aircraft. Be precise.
> 4. **Skipping Diagrams:** A 7-mark sub-part without a diagram loses at least **1 mark**. Even a hand-drawn rough sketch earns that mark.
> 5. **Ignoring Indian Examples:** KTU examiners **love Indian context** (Maruti, Tata, Mahindra, Ashok Leyland). Always cite Indian OEMs for full marks.
> 6. **Wheel notation confusion:** Writing "4×4" for a car without specifying which wheels are driven is incomplete. Be specific: "front 2 driven" or "rear 2 driven" or "all 4 driven."

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Revision Points — Print This & Memorise**

- ✅ **Automobile** = *self-propelled + ground + wheeled + on-board prime-mover*. Remember the 4 attributes.
- ✅ **Wheel notation:** $N \times M$ — N = total wheels, M = driven wheels (e.g., 6×4 tipper).
- ✅ **CV Classification by GVW** — LCV (≤7.5 t), MCV (7.5–16.2 t), HCV (>16.2 t), MAV (multi-axle).
- ✅ **GVW Formula:** $W_{\text{GVW}} = W_{\text{kerb}} + W_{\text{payload}} + W_{\text{fuel}} + W_{\text{crew}}$.
- ✅ **Body styles:** Sedan (3-box), Hatchback (2-box), SUV (high GC), MPV (people-mover), Pickup (open bed), Coupe (2-door), Convertible (retractable roof).
- ✅ **Drive layouts:** FWD (front-driven), RWD (rear-driven), AWD (auto all-wheel), 4WD (manual 4-wheel). Do not confuse AWD ↔ 4WD.
- ✅ **Fuel evolution:** Steam → Petrol → Diesel → CNG/LPG → Hybrid → BEV → Hydrogen FCEV. The future is **electric + hydrogen**.
- ✅ **Indian emission norms:** Currently **BS-VI Phase 2 (2023 onwards)**, moving to **BS-VII by 2027–28**.
- ✅ **Frame types:** Monocoque (unibody — most cars) vs. Body-on-Frame (BoF — trucks, SUVs, off-roaders).
- ✅ **Engine layout shorthand:** I4 (inline-4), V6, V8, H4 (boxer/flat), W12, Electric (single/dual/tri-motor).
- ✅ **Government policy (must-know for KTU):** FAME-II, PLI scheme, EV 30% mandate by 2030, Ethanol blending E20 target.
- ✅ **Indian OEMs to quote:** Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors, Mahindra & Mahindra, Ashok Leyland, Eicher, Bajaj, TVS.
- ✅ **Mermaid / Block diagram is mandatory** for any 7-mark body-style or drive-layout question.
- ✅ Always cite the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** when classifying commercial vehicles.

> **🎯 Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"Define → Classify → Compare → Indian Example → Sketch → Conclude."* Follow this 6-step structure for any classification question and you will secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limiting Dimensions as per Central Motor Vehicles Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Limiting Dimensions as per Central Motor Vehicles Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (CMVR Context)

The **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989** is the principal set of subordinate legislation framed under the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** of the Government of India. It prescribes, among other things, the **statutory limiting (maximum/minimum permissible) overall dimensions, weights, and constructional features** that every motor vehicle plying on Indian public roads must conform to. These limits are enforced by the testing agencies (ARAI, iCAT, CIRT) before a vehicle receives a type-approval certificate.

For KTU PESFT636 (Automobile Engineering and Safety), the topic covers the following families of dimensions:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statutory Vehicle Dimensions (as per CMVR 1989 — Rules 93 to 104)**
> 1. Overall Length ($L_{ov}$)
> 2. Overall Width ($W_{ov}$)
> 3. Overall Height ($H_{ov}$)
> 4. Wheelbase ($L_{wb}$)
> 5. Front Overhang ($O_f$) and Rear Overhang ($O_r$)
> 6. Ground Clearance ($h_{gc}$)
> 7. Track Width ($T$)
> 8. Minimum Turning Radius ($R_{turn}$)

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Mailbox" Analogy

Imagine every Indian road as a **long transparent mailbox**. To prevent traffic chaos, no vehicle can be longer, wider, or taller than the slot of the mailbox allows. The CMVR is essentially a **"national envelope specification"** for that mailbox.

- **Why a maximum length?** → To prevent long trailers from blocking intersections and to allow the vehicle to fit inside a standard parking bay or a toll plaza lane.
- **Why a maximum width?** → To leave a safe gap for oncoming traffic and pedestrians on two-lane roads.
- **Why a maximum height?** → To safely clear flyovers, underpasses, and overhead electric traction lines.
- **Why a minimum ground clearance?** → To prevent the chassis from scraping on speed-breakers, potholes, and steep ramps.
- **Why a maximum turning radius?** → To guarantee that the vehicle can negotiate the standard **SH:1 turning template** (a 7.5 m outer radius circle used in Indian intersection design).

> [!NOTE]
> The Indian Road Congress (IRC) designs the **road infrastructure**; the CMVR designs the **vehicle envelope** to fit that infrastructure. Both must agree, which is why every dimension in the CMVR is correlated with an IRC standard.

## 1.3 Categories of Vehicles Governed

The CMVR partitions vehicles into classes, and each class has its own dimensional envelope. The principal classes are:

| Class | Typical Examples |
|---|---|
| **L-category (Two-wheelers)** | Motorcycles, scooters, mopeds |
| **3-W (Three-wheelers)** | Auto-rickshaws, delivery trikes |
| **M-category (Passenger cars)** | Sedans, hatchbacks, SUVs |
| **N-category (Goods carriers)** | Light, medium, and heavy trucks |
| **Bus category** | Mini-bus, standard bus, articulated bus |
| **Tractor-trailers / Articulated** | Multi-axle tractor-trailers |
| **Construction Equipment Vehicles (CEV)** | Dumpers, tippers, cranes (off-road) |

## 1.4 Why These Dimensions Matter in Engineering Design

A vehicle designer cannot arbitrarily pick the dimensions. The CMVR envelope acts as a **hard constraint** during the conceptual design stage:

$$ \text{Design Space} = \big\{(L, W, H, L_{wb}, O_f, O_r, T, R) : L \le L_{max}, W \le W_{max}, \dots\big\} $$

Any design that violates even one constraint is illegal for road use, regardless of how fuel-efficient or comfortable it might be.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Logic Behind Each Dimension

### 2.1.1 Overall Length ($L_{ov}$)
**Geometric Definition:**
$$ L_{ov} = L_{wb} + O_f + O_r $$
where $O_f$ and $O_r$ are the front and rear overhangs measured from the centers of the extreme axles to the foremost and rearmost points of the vehicle, respectively.

**Engineering Logic:**
- Front overhang affects **approach angle** ($\alpha$): $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{h_{gc}}{O_f}$
- Rear overhang affects **departure angle** ($\beta$): $\tan \beta = \dfrac{h_{gc}}{O_r}$
- CMVR caps $L_{ov}$ to prevent vehicles from **overhang-ing past an intersection** during a turn.

### 2.1.2 Overall Width ($W_{ov}$)
- Measured at the widest fixed point (excluding rear-view mirrors and side lamps).
- Standard lane width on Indian roads = **3.5 m** (IRC:64).
- A maximum $W_{ov}$ of **2.5 m** ensures a 0.5 m clearance per side on a single lane.

### 2.1.3 Overall Height ($H_{ov}$)
- Sum of ground clearance + chassis height + body height.
- Indian flyover vertical clearances are typically **5.0 m to 5.5 m** (IRC:SP:84).
- Height also affects **roll-over stability** (lateral acceleration limit):
$$ a_{y,roll} = \frac{T}{2H_{CG}} \cdot g $$

### 2.1.4 Wheelbase and Overhangs
- Longer wheelbase → better high-speed directional stability.
- Shorter overhangs → smaller turning circle but reduced ramp angles.

### 2.1.5 Ground Clearance ($h_{gc}$)
- Minimum values prescribed to negotiate the **IRC standard speed-breaker** (3.7 m arc with 0.115 m height).
- For trucks: $h_{gc} \ge 250$ mm; for buses: $h_{gc} \ge 260$ mm.

### 2.1.6 Turning Radius
- **Minimum Turning Radius (MTR)** is measured to the **outermost point of the front wheel** when the steering is at full lock.
- Corresponds to IRC:SP:73 turning template (Outer = 7.5 m / Inner = 5.5 m).
- For articulated buses/trailers, MTR applies to the **outermost edge of the vehicle body**, not the wheel.

### 2.1.7 Track Width ($T$)
- Distance between the centrelines of the wheels on the same axle.
- Wider track → lower **roll-axis tilt angle**:
$$ \phi_{roll} = \frac{2 H_{CG} \cdot a_y}{T \cdot g} $$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the "must-memorise" portion for any CMVR question in the ESE (End-Semester Examination).**

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Typical CMVR Limit (Heavy Vehicles) | Unit | Key Dependency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overall Length — Rigid 2-axle | $L_{ov}$ | 11.5 | m | $L_{wb} + O_f + O_r$ |
| 2 | Overall Length — Rigid 3-axle | $L_{ov}$ | 12.0 | m | $L_{wb} + O_f + O_r$ |
| 3 | Overall Length — Articulated | $L_{ov}$ | 18.0 (as per 2018 notification) | m | king-pin to rear of trailer |
| 4 | Overall Width | $W_{ov}$ | 2.5 | m | lane width |
| 5 | Overall Height | $H_{ov}$ | 3.8 (4.2 for specific buses) | m | flyover clearance |
| 6 | Front Overhang (max) | $O_f$ | 1.5 | m | approach angle |
| 7 | Rear Overhang (max) | $O_r$ | 1.5 (or 60% $L_{wb}$) | m | departure angle |
| 8 | Wheelbase (min) | $L_{wb}$ | 2.5 (cars) | m | stability |
| 9 | Ground Clearance (min) | $h_{gc}$ | 0.250 | m | speed-breaker |
| 10 | Turning Radius (max outer) | $R_{turn}$ | 12.0 / 13.0 | m | IRC:73 |
| 11 | Track Width (typical HMV) | $T$ | $\ge 1.8$ | m | roll stability |
| 12 | Rear Overhang ratio rule | $O_r / L_{wb}$ | $\le 0.60$ | - | load distribution |

> [!WARNING]
> The **2018 Gazette notification (G.S.R. 272(E))** revised some heavy-vehicle length limits upward. The classic 11.5 m / 12 m / 18 m figures are still the most commonly tested values in KTU board exams; always mention both the old rule and the latest amendment if the question says "as per the latest CMVR."

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Toll-plaza lane design:** The 2.5 m width rule directly drives toll-booth island width.
- **Multi-storey car parking:** The $L_{ov}$ cap determines bay dimensions in metro stations.
- **Bridge load posting:** Although weight is governed by separate axle-load rules, the dimensional envelope decides the *vehicle class* posted on a bridge.
- **Insurance and type approval:** Every production vehicle carries a "Form 22" (Road Worthiness Certificate) signed against the CMVR limits.

## 2.4 Penalty of Non-Compliance

Under Section 190(2) of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988, a vehicle that exceeds the CMVR dimensional envelope can be:
1. Detained at the check post.
2. Fined (first offence: ₹1,000 to ₹2,000; subsequent: up to ₹10,000).
3. Disqualified from plying until rectified.

> [!NOTE]
> This legal backdrop is frequently asked in **2-mark / 3-mark questions**: *"Why are the CMVR dimensions called 'limiting dimensions'?"* — The answer is precisely because crossing them makes the vehicle illegal, not merely suboptimal.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Numerical Pattern

A frequently-tested 14-mark KTU problem couples **two vehicle classes** with one **derived quantity**. The analytical matrix is summarised below.

| Given (CMVR rule) | To Find | Required Derivation Path |
|---|---|---|
| $L_{ov}$, $O_f$, $O_r$ | $L_{wb}$ | $L_{wb} = L_{ov} - O_f - O_r$ |
| $L_{wb}$, $O_f$, $L_{ov}$ | $O_r$ | $O_r = L_{ov} - L_{wb} - O_f$ |
| $h_{gc}$, $O_f$ | Approach angle $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \arctan(h_{gc} / O_f)$ |
| $h_{gc}$, $O_r$ | Departure angle $\beta$ | $\beta = \arctan(h_{gc} / O_r)$ |
| $T$, $H_{CG}$ | Critical roll angle $\phi_{cr}$ | $\phi_{cr} = \arctan(T / (2 H_{CG}))$ |
| Track + Wheelbase | Minimum swept area | $A = \pi (R_{turn}^2 - (R_{turn} - L_{ov})^2)$ |
| $L_{wb}$ and axle load | Rear-axle load transfer | $W_{r,tr} = W \cdot a_x \cdot H_{CG} / L_{wb}$ |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Derivations

### 3.2.1 Deriving the Overall Length from Wheelbase and Overhangs

**Problem (model KTU type):** A 2-axle rigid truck has a wheelbase of **6.0 m** and front and rear overhangs of **1.4 m** and **3.6 m** respectively. Verify whether the vehicle complies with CMVR Rule 93, and compute its overall length.

**Step 1 — State the rule.**
The CMVR Rule 93 prescribes a maximum overall length of **11.5 m** for a 2-axle rigid heavy goods vehicle.

**Step 2 — Write the governing geometric equation.**
$$ L_{ov} = L_{wb} + O_f + O_r $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**
$$ L_{ov} = 6.0 + 1.4 + 3.6 $$

$$ L_{ov} = 11.0 \text{ m} $$

**Step 4 — Compare with the CMVR ceiling.**
$$ L_{ov} = 11.0 \text{ m} \;<\; L_{max} = 11.5 \text{ m} $$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**
The vehicle complies. **Margin of compliance** = $11.5 - 11.0 = 0.5$ m.

> [!WARNING]
> Many KTU students incorrectly **add the wheelbase twice** (once on each side). The wheelbase is the distance *between* the axles, not a sum — so write the equation once, clearly.

### 3.2.2 Deriving the Rear Overhang Limit Ratio

**Problem:** Show that the CMVR condition $O_r \le 0.6 \cdot L_{wb}$ ensures that the rear-axle vertical load $W_r$ remains positive even under emergency braking with deceleration $a_x = 0.6\,g$.

**Step 1 — Free-body diagram and sign convention.**
Let total vehicle mass be $M$ and total weight $W = Mg$. Take moments about the front-axle contact patch during braking.

$$ W_r \cdot L_{wb} = W \cdot (b + a) - M \cdot a_x \cdot H_{CG} $$

where $b$ is the CG distance from the rear axle, $a$ is the CG distance from the front axle, and $a + b = L_{wb}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute the CG location in terms of wheelbase.**

The CG is at a distance $\dfrac{W_r}{W} \cdot L_{wb}$ from the front axle, so $a = \dfrac{W_r}{W} L_{wb}$ and $b = L_{wb} - a$.

**Step 3 — Rearranging for $W_r$ under braking.**

$$ W_r = \frac{W \cdot b}{L_{wb}} - \frac{M \cdot a_x \cdot H_{CG}}{L_{wb}} $$

**Step 4 — Worst-case CG position.**
The CMVR rule effectively fixes the CG ratio through the overhang rule. With $O_r$ (rear of the body measured from the rear axle), the most rearward permitted load position pushes the CG to the rear. The empirical rule of thumb adopted in Indian Standards is:

$$ \frac{b}{L_{wb}} \ge 0.4 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad O_r \le 0.6 \, L_{wb} $$

**Step 5 — Apply the worst-case braking deceleration.**

$$ W_r = W \left[ 0.4 - \frac{a_x \cdot H_{CG}}{g \cdot L_{wb}} \right] $$

For $W_r \ge 0$:
$$ \frac{a_x \cdot H_{CG}}{g \cdot L_{wb}} \le 0.4 $$

For $a_x = 0.6 g$ and typical $H_{CG}/L_{wb} \approx 0.4$ (loaded truck), we get $W_r \approx 0.16\,W$, which is positive — confirming compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU board examiner does **not** expect a full vehicle-dynamics derivation for a CMVR question; the above is the **maximum-depth treatment** for a 14-mark question that asks "Justify the CMVR rear-overhang rule."

### 3.2.3 Deriving the Approach Angle from Ground Clearance and Front Overhang

**Step 1 — Define the angle geometrically.**
Approach angle is the largest ramp angle the vehicle can climb without the front bumper touching the ramp:

$$ \alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{h_{gc}}{O_f}\right) $$

**Step 2 — Substitute CMVR values for a heavy truck.**
$h_{gc} = 0.25$ m, $O_f = 1.4$ m (max allowed):

$$ \alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.25}{1.4}\right) = \arctan(0.1786) $$

$$ \alpha \approx 10.13^{\circ} $$

**Step 3 — Compare with industry norm.**
Most heavy trucks target $\alpha \ge 15^{\circ}$, so the CMVR-prescribed front overhang is the **limiting (i.e. binding) constraint**, not the ground clearance.

### 3.2.4 Deriving the Critical Turning Radius for an Articulated Vehicle

**Step 1 — Acknowledge the geometric difference.**
For a tractor-trailer combination, the swept path of the **rear trailer corner** is the binding constraint, not the front wheel.

**Step 2 — Apply the low-speed off-tracking formula (≈ Ackermann-equivalent for trailers).**

$$ R_{rear} = R_{front} - L_{wb,tractor} \cdot \cos\theta - \tfrac{1}{2}L_{trailer} \cdot \tan\theta $$

where $\theta$ is the articulation angle at the king-pin.

**Step 3 — For a low-speed tight turn, $\theta \to 90^{\circ}$ (jack-knife limit).**
At $\theta = 90^{\circ}$, the term $\tan \theta \to \infty$, so $R_{rear}$ becomes the *minimum* physically achievable value. The CMVR caps this through the **king-pin-to-rear-trailer** dimension rather than wheelbase alone, which is why the 18 m limit for articulated vehicles is measured *rear-of-trailer to front-of-tractor*, not just wheelbase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU board expects the student to **state the rule, write the formula, plug in the numbers, and conclude with a compliance statement**. The derivation logic above is the standard valuation-key pattern.

## 3.3 Analytical Compliance Matrix (Self-Check Tool)

| Vehicle Type | $L_{max}$ (m) | $W_{max}$ (m) | $H_{max}$ (m) | Compliance Check |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2-axle rigid truck | 11.5 | 2.5 | 3.8 | $L \le 11.5$ ✓ |
| 3-axle rigid truck | 12.0 | 2.5 | 3.8 | $L \le 12.0$ ✓ |
| Articulated tractor-trailer | 18.0 | 2.5 | 3.8 | King-pin rule ✓ |
| Mini-bus | 6.0 | 2.2 | 2.7 | Length-rule ✓ |
| Standard bus | 12.0 | 2.5 | 3.8 | Length-rule ✓ |
| Passenger car (M1) | — | 1.9 (typ.) | 2.1 (typ.) | Not CMVR-capped |
| Motor cycle | — | 1.0 (handlebar) | — | Handlebar rule ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vehicle Dimensional Envelope (Side View)

```mermaid

    nodeA["VEHICLE SIDE PROFILE"]:::top
    nodeB["Overall Length L_ov"]:::dim

    nodeI["Departure Angle beta"]:::angle
    nodeJ["CMVR Rule 93 Check"]:::rule

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE

    classDef top fill:#1f2937,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram treats the vehicle as a system of interacting parameters, not as a literal sketch. Every blue "dim" node feeds into the red "rule" node, which then issues a binary compliance output — this mirrors the **type-approval flow** used by ARAI.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern for PESFT636. Marks are split exactly as per the official 14-mark-with-choice module question.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, model]** Define the term *"limiting dimensions"* as used in the Central Motor Vehicles Rules.

**Model Answer:**
*"Limiting dimensions" refers to the maximum (and in some cases minimum) permissible values of overall length, width, height, wheelbase, overhangs, ground clearance, and turning radius of a motor vehicle, as prescribed by the Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989 (under the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988). Any motor vehicle that does not lie within these limits is deemed non-compliant and cannot be offered for road use in India. These dimensions act as a national vehicle-envelope specification.*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, model]** State the CMVR-prescribed maximum overall width of a heavy goods vehicle and briefly explain why this particular value was chosen.

**Model Answer:**
The CMVR prescribes a maximum overall width of **2.5 m** for heavy goods vehicles. This value is chosen because the standard Indian lane width is **3.5 m** (as per IRC:64), leaving a **0.5 m clearance per side** for opposing traffic, road-edge irregularities, and pedestrian buffer. A wider vehicle would compromise safety on single-lane and two-lane roads and would not fit standard toll-booth lanes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern]** *(a)* Explain in detail the various **limiting dimensions** of a heavy commercial vehicle as prescribed by the CMVR 1989, with their typical numerical values. *(b)* A 3-axle rigid truck has a wheelbase of **7.5 m** and front and rear overhangs of **1.3 m** and **3.2 m** respectively. Verify whether the truck complies with the CMVR overall-length rule, and compute its approach and departure angles given a ground clearance of **0.26 m**.

#### Part (a) — Detailed Solution (7 Marks)

The CMVR Rule 93 and its subsequent amendments prescribe the following limiting dimensions for heavy commercial vehicles:

1. **Overall Length ($L_{ov}$):**
   - 2-axle rigid: **11.5 m maximum**
   - 3-axle rigid: **12.0 m maximum**
   - Articulated tractor-trailer: **18.0 m maximum** (king-pin to rear of trailer)

2. **Overall Width ($W_{ov}$):** **2.5 m maximum**, measured at the widest fixed point excluding mirrors and lamps.

3. **Overall Height ($H_{ov}$):** **3.8 m maximum** (4.2 m for certain long-distance coach configurations as per State Transport Authority approvals).

4. **Front Overhang ($O_f$):** Limited to **1.5 m maximum** to preserve approach angle.

5. **Rear Overhang ($O_r$):** Limited to **1.5 m maximum**, or **60% of the wheelbase**, whichever is less.

6. **Wheelbase ($L_{wb}$):** No statutory maximum, but a *practical* minimum of 2.5 m for stability.

7. **Ground Clearance ($h_{gc}$):** Minimum **0.25 m** (unladen) for trucks; 0.26 m for buses.

8. **Track Width ($T$):** Typically **≥ 1.8 m** for the rear axle of HMVs.

9. **Minimum Turning Radius ($R_{turn}$):** Outer radius limited to **12 m to 13 m** depending on vehicle class.

These dimensions collectively ensure the vehicle fits within Indian road infrastructure (lane width, flyover clearance, intersection template) and does not compromise longitudinal or lateral stability.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute overall length:**
$$ L_{ov} = L_{wb} + O_f + O_r $$
$$ L_{ov} = 7.5 + 1.3 + 3.2 = 12.0 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Compare with CMVR limit for 3-axle rigid:**
$$ L_{ov} = 12.0 \text{ m} \;\; \text{vs} \;\; L_{max} = 12.0 \text{ m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Complies (just)}. $$

**Step 3 — Rear-overhang rule check:**
$$ O_r / L_{wb} = 3.2 / 7.5 = 0.427 \le 0.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Complies}. $$

**Step 4 — Approach angle:**
$$ \alpha = \arctan\!\left(\frac{h_{gc}}{O_f}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.26}{1.3}\right) = \arctan(0.2) $$
$$ \alpha \approx 11.31^{\circ} $$

**Step 5 — Departure angle:**
$$ \beta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{h_{gc}}{O_r}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0.26}{3.2}\right) = \arctan(0.0813) $$
$$ \beta \approx 4.65^{\circ} $$

**Conclusion:** The truck complies with the CMVR length rule but with zero margin. The departure angle is small, which is the typical trade-off for high-payload volume — a designer would have to reduce the rear overhang to improve $\beta$.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern]** *(a)* With the help of neat sketches, describe the various **limiting dimensions** of a motor vehicle as per the Central Motor Vehicles Rules. *(b)* A passenger bus has an overall length of **12.0 m**, a wheelbase of **6.2 m**, and a front overhang of **2.4 m**. Compute the rear overhang and verify compliance with both the absolute and proportional CMVR rules.

#### Part (a) — Descriptive Solution (7 Marks)

*(The descriptive content is the same as Part (a) of Question A above; here, emphasis is on the **schematic** description.)*

The principal limiting dimensions of a motor vehicle, as prescribed by the CMVR 1989 (Rules 93 to 104), are:

- **Overall length ($L_{ov}$):** maximum rigid length = 11.5 m (2-axle) or 12 m (3-axle).
- **Overall width ($W_{ov}$):** maximum 2.5 m.
- **Overall height ($H_{ov}$):** maximum 3.8 m.
- **Wheelbase ($L_{wb}$):** distance between the centres of the front and rear axles.
- **Front overhang ($O_f$):** distance from the centre of the front axle to the foremost point of the vehicle.
- **Rear overhang ($O_r$):** distance from the centre of the rear axle to the rearmost point of the vehicle.
- **Ground clearance ($h_{gc}$):** minimum height of the lowest fixed point above the road.
- **Turning radius ($R_{turn}$):** radius of the path traced by the outer front wheel at full lock.
- **Track width ($T$):** distance between the centrelines of the tyres on the same axle.

The geometric relationship is:
$$ L_{ov} = L_{wb} + O_f + O_r $$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the rear overhang:**
$$ O_r = L_{ov} - L_{wb} - O_f $$
$$ O_r = 12.0 - 6.2 - 2.4 = 3.4 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Absolute rule check:**
$$ O_r = 3.4 \text{ m} \;\; \text{vs} \;\; O_{r,max,abs} = 1.5 \text{ m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Non-compliant}. $$

**Step 3 — Proportional rule check:**
$$ O_r / L_{wb} = 3.4 / 6.2 = 0.548 \le 0.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Complies proportionally}. $$

**Step 4 — Apply the "lesser of the two" rule:**
The CMVR mandates that $O_r$ be the **lesser** of the absolute and proportional limits. Here the absolute limit binds:

$$ O_{r,allowed} = \min(1.5,\; 0.6 \times 6.2) = \min(1.5,\; 3.72) = 1.5 \text{ m} $$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
The bus **does not comply** with the absolute rear-overhang rule. To achieve compliance, the designer must either:
- Reduce the overall length to $L_{ov} = L_{wb} + O_f + O_{r,allowed} = 6.2 + 2.4 + 1.5 = 10.1$ m, or
- Increase the wheelbase to at least $L_{wb,min}$ such that $0.6\,L_{wb,min} \ge 3.4$ m, i.e., $L_{wb,min} \ge 5.67$ m (already satisfied) — but the **absolute** rule still binds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common mistake is to apply only the **proportional rule** ($O_r \le 0.6\,L_{wb}$) and ignore the **absolute** rule ($O_r \le 1.5$ m). The two rules are *jointly* enforced — both must be satisfied. In Question B, the proportional rule is satisfied, but the absolute rule fails, so the vehicle is non-compliant. Many students lose 2–3 marks by missing this "and" condition.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CMVR 1989** is the Indian government's subordinate legislation that defines the *envelope* of a road-legal motor vehicle.
- The **limiting dimensions** include overall length, width, height, wheelbase, front and rear overhangs, ground clearance, turning radius, and track width.
- For heavy commercial vehicles, the canonical limits are **L = 11.5 m / 12 m / 18 m** (2-axle / 3-axle / articulated), **W = 2.5 m**, **H = 3.8 m**.
- The geometric identity **$L_{ov} = L_{wb} + O_f + O_r$** is the most-tested single equation in this topic.
- The **approach and departure angles** are derived as $\alpha = \arctan(h_{gc}/O_f)$ and $\beta = \arctan(h_{gc}/O_r)$.
- The **rear-overhang rule** has *two* components — an absolute cap (1.5 m) **and** a proportional cap (0.6 $L_{wb}$) — both must be satisfied.
- The **CMVR envelope is correlated with IRC standards** for road width, flyover height, and intersection templates; the 7.5 m outer / 5.5 m inner turning template is a classic IRC reference.
- A non-compliant vehicle cannot be type-approved by **ARAI / iCAT** and is illegal for road use under Section 190(2) of the Motor Vehicles Act.
- Always state the **rule number** (e.g., "as per CMVR 1989, Rule 93") when answering KTU questions — the examiner allocates marks for the **citation**, not just the number.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision tip:** Memorise the *single line* "$L_{ov} = L_{wb} + O_f + O_r$", the *table* of canonical values, and the *two-part* rear-overhang rule. These three items will cover roughly 80% of marks awarded on this topic in a typical KTU 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Engine? (The Prime Mover of an Automobile)

### Formal Definition
An **engine** is a prime mover that converts a specific form of energy (typically chemical energy stored in a fuel) into **mechanical work** (rotational kinetic energy) usable for propulsion or driving auxiliary systems. In the context of **PESFT636 — Automobile Engineering and Safety**, the term "Engine" predominantly refers to the **Internal Combustion (IC) Engine**, which is the heart of modern road vehicles, two-wheelers, and a large fraction of commercial transport.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> An *Internal Combustion Engine* is a heat engine in which the combustion of a fuel (typically petrol, diesel, or an alternative fuel) takes place **inside the engine cylinder**, with the combustion products acting directly on the moving parts (piston, rotor) to deliver useful work.

### Key Operational Words (Board-Examiner Focus)
- **Combustion inside** the cylinder (contrast with steam engines, where combustion is external).
- **Reciprocating motion** of the piston is converted to **rotational motion** of the crankshaft via a slider-crank mechanism.
- The output is **rotational mechanical work** (torque × angular speed).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Lung of the Automobile"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of an engine as the **lungs of a car**. Just as lungs inhale oxygen, mix it with fuel (glucose), burn it slowly (metabolism), and exhale the products (CO₂ + H₂O) to produce energy for movement — the engine inhales **air**, mixes it with **fuel**, burns the mixture inside a confined space (combustion chamber), and pushes out **exhaust gases**, converting the explosive chemical release into the rotation of wheels.
>
> The four "breathing strokes" of a 4-stroke engine are like one complete human breath cycle: **Intake → Compression → Power (Ignition) → Exhaust**.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical SI Value | Practical Range (Passenger Car) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Specific gas constant of air | $R$ | **287 J/(kg·K)** | — |
| Specific heat ratio (air) | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | 1.33–1.4 |
| Atmospheric pressure (STP) | $p_0$ | **101.325 kPa** | — |
| Indicated Thermal Efficiency (Otto) | $\eta_{th,\,Otto}$ | < **60 %** | 25–35 % |

---

## 1.2 Broad Classification of Engines (High-Yield Taxonomy)

Engines are classified along multiple axes. **This is a 14-mark KTU favourite.**

### Classification Tree (Memorise This Hierarchy)

- **A. Based on Combustion Location**
  - **External Combustion Engine (ECE)** — e.g., Steam engine, Stirling engine
  - **Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)** — e.g., Petrol, Diesel, Gas engines

- **B. Based on Ignition Method (sub-category of ICE)**
  - **Spark Ignition (SI)** — Petrol / Gasoline engine (ignited by spark plug)
  - **Compression Ignition (CI)** — Diesel engine (ignited by heat of compression)

- **C. Based on Cycle of Operation**
  - **Otto Cycle** → SI engines (constant volume heat addition)
  - **Diesel Cycle** → CI engines (constant pressure heat addition)
  - **Dual / Limited Pressure Cycle** → Modern CI (mixed)
  - **Atkinson / Miller** → Over-expanded SI variants
  - **Brayton Cycle** → Gas turbine (jet engine — outside 4-wheel scope but asked)

- **D. Based on Number of Strokes per Cycle**
  - **4-Stroke Engine** — One power stroke every 2 crank revolutions (720°)
  - **2-Stroke Engine** — One power stroke every 1 crank revolution (360°)

- **E. Based on Type of Fuel**
  - Petrol / Gasoline
  - Diesel
  - CNG (Compressed Natural Gas)
  - LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)
  - Bio-diesel, Ethanol, Hydrogen (alternative fuels — covered in Module 4 / 5)

- **F. Based on Valve / Port Arrangement**
  - **Poppet valve** (overhead valve, OHV; overhead camshaft, OHC)
  - **Sleeve valve**
  - **Rotary / Wankel** (rotary engine)
  - **Port** (2-stroke only)

- **G. Based on Cooling**
  - **Air-cooled** (e.g., Bajaj Pulsar 150 single cylinder)
  - **Water-cooled** (e.g., most modern cars)

- **H. Based on Application**
  - **Stationary** (generator sets)
  - **Automotive** (cars, bikes)
  - **Marine** (ships)
  - **Aircraft** (piston or jet)

### Real-World Linkage (Engineering Utility)
> [!NOTE]
> In a Maruti Suzuki Alto K10 (SI, 4-stroke, water-cooled, petrol) vs. a Tata Ace Gold Diesel (CI, 4-stroke, water-cooled) vs. a Royal Enfield Classic 350 (SI, single cylinder, air-cooled) — the **same physics** (Otto / Diesel cycle) operates with **different design choices** to meet cost, fuel economy, and power-density targets.

---

## 1.3 Engine Terminology — The Language of IC Engines

Every KTU exam problem (and the rest of this module) uses these terms. **Master them.**

| Term | Definition | Formula / Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Bore (d)** | Inner diameter of the cylinder | $d$ |
| **Stroke (L)** | Distance travelled by piston between TDC and BDC | $L$ |
| **TDC** | Top Dead Centre — piston at the highest position | — |
| **BDC** | Bottom Dead Centre — piston at the lowest position | — |
| **Clearance Volume (Vc)** | Volume above piston at TDC | — |
| **Swept Volume (Vs)** | Volume displaced by one stroke | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 L$ |
| **Total Volume (V1)** | Cylinder volume at BDC | $V_1 = V_c + V_s$ |
| **Compression Ratio (r)** | Ratio of max to min volume | $r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{V_c + V_s}{V_c}$ |
| **Mean Effective Pressure (Pm)** | Hypothetical constant pressure giving same work | $W = P_m \cdot V_s$ |
| **Engine Speed (N)** | Crank RPM | $N$ in rpm |
| **Indicated Power (IP)** | Power developed in cylinder | — |
| **Brake Power (BP)** | Power at the crankshaft output | — |
| **Friction Power (FP)** | $FP = IP - BP$ | — |
| **Mechanical Efficiency** | $\eta_m = BP / IP$ | — |
| **Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{th} = BP / (\dot{m}_f \times CV)$ | — |
| **Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dot{m}_{actual}/\dot{m}_{theoretical}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently define terms in 2-mark "define in one sentence" questions. Always include the **symbol and the SI unit** to score full marks.

---

## 1.4 Visual Schematic — Engine Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENGINES] --> B[External Combustion]
    A --> C[Internal Combustion ICE]
    B --> B1[Steam Engine]
    B --> B2[Stirling Engine]
    C --> D[Based on Ignition]
    D --> D1[SI - Spark Ignition]
    D --> D2[CI - Compression Ignition]
    D --> D3[GT - Gas Turbine Brayton]
    C --> E[Based on Strokes]
    E --> E1[4 Stroke]
    E --> E2[2 Stroke]
    C --> F[Based on Cooling]
    F --> F1[Air Cooled]
    F --> F2[Water Cooled]
    C --> G[Based on Valve]
    G --> G1[Poppet Valve]
    G --> G2[Sleeve Valve]
    G --> G3[Wankel Rotary]
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 4-Stroke Spark Ignition (SI) Engine — Operation

A 4-stroke SI engine completes one **working cycle in 4 piston strokes (2 crank revolutions = 720°)**. The four strokes are:

### Stroke 1 — **Intake (Suction) Stroke**
- Piston moves **TDC → BDC**.
- **Intake valve OPEN**, exhaust valve CLOSED.
- The descending piston creates a partial vacuum (pressure drops to ~ **0.8 bar**).
- A mixture of **petrol vapour + air** is drawn through the carburettor (or injected via port fuel injection / direct injection) into the cylinder.

### Stroke 2 — **Compression Stroke**
- Piston moves **BDC → TDC**.
- **Both valves CLOSED.**
- The fuel-air mixture is compressed from $V_1$ to $V_c$.
- Compression ratio $r = V_1 / V_c$ is typically **8 to 12** for SI engines.
- Temperature rises to ~ **300–400 °C** (well below auto-ignition temperature of petrol, ~ 250 °C, hence SI).

### Stroke 3 — **Power (Expansion / Working) Stroke**
- Piston at TDC; **both valves CLOSED**.
- Spark plug fires at a precise moment (**ignition timing**, typically 5°–30° BTDC).
- Combustion at approximately **constant volume** → gas pressure rises sharply to **30–60 bar**.
- The high-pressure gas pushes the piston from **TDC → BDC** — this is the **only stroke producing useful work**.
- Expansion ratio $r_e = V_1/V_2$ (in ideal Otto, $r_e = r$).

### Stroke 4 — **Exhaust Stroke**
- Piston moves **BDC → TDC**.
- **Exhaust valve OPEN**, intake valve CLOSED.
- Burnt gases (CO₂, H₂O vapour, CO, NOx, unburnt HC) are pushed out.
- Exhaust valve typically closes *after* TDC (about 5°–10° ATDC) to utilise exhaust gas inertia for scavenging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Hook:** Out of 4 strokes, only **1 stroke produces work**. The other 3 consume work (friction, pumping). This is why **flywheel mass** is critical — it stores energy during the power stroke and releases it across the other three strokes to keep rotation smooth.

### Real-World Analogy
> Think of it as a **cricket bowler**: the run-up (intake) and the jump-load (compression) are *preparation*. The actual delivery (power stroke) is the *useful action*. The follow-through (exhaust) completes the motion. Only the delivery "scores runs."

---

## 2.2 The 4-Stroke Compression Ignition (CI) Engine — Operation

A 4-stroke CI engine also completes the cycle in **720°** but the combustion differs:

### Stroke 1 — **Intake Stroke**
- **Intake valve OPEN**, exhaust valve CLOSED.
- Piston moves **TDC → BDC**.
- **Only air** is inducted (no fuel in the cylinder).

### Stroke 2 — **Compression Stroke**
- Piston moves **BDC → TDC**.
- Both valves CLOSED.
- Air is compressed to a **much higher ratio** $r = $ **14 to 22** (CI engines need higher compression to achieve auto-ignition temperature).
- Temperature of compressed air reaches ~ **500–900 °C**, well above diesel's auto-ignition temperature (**~ 210 °C**).

### Stroke 3 — **Power Stroke**
- Near TDC, **fuel injector** sprays high-pressure diesel (~ 200–2000 bar via common rail) directly into the hot compressed air.
- Diesel auto-ignites on contact with hot air — **no spark plug required**.
- Combustion occurs at approximately **constant pressure** (in ideal Diesel cycle) — modern engines use a *dual cycle* (limited pressure) for a balance of efficiency and noise.
- Piston pushed **TDC → BDC**; gas pressures ~ **60–120 bar**.

### Stroke 4 — **Exhaust Stroke**
- Identical principle to SI — burnt gases expelled via exhaust valve.

### SI vs CI — Why the Difference?

| Parameter | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel | Petrol | Diesel |
| Compression Ratio | 8–12 | 14–22 |
| Inducted Medium | Air + Fuel mixture | Air only |
| Ignition | Spark plug | Auto-ignition |
| Combustion Process | Constant Volume (Otto) | Constant Pressure (Diesel) |
| Max Cylinder Pressure | 30–60 bar | 60–120 bar |
| Thermal Efficiency | Lower (33–40%) | Higher (38–45%) |
| Weight | Lighter | Heavier (stronger construction) |

---

## 2.3 The 2-Stroke Engine — Compact Power

A 2-stroke engine completes one **working cycle in 1 crank revolution (360°)**.

### Cycle Phases
The 2-stroke engine achieves intake, compression, power, and exhaust *without dedicated intake and exhaust strokes*, by using **ports** in the cylinder wall covered/uncovered by the piston itself.

1. **Upward stroke** (BDC → TDC): The piston first **uncovers the exhaust port** (gas escapes, pressure drops), then **uncovers the transfer port** (fresh charge enters from crankcase, displacing exhaust — *scavenging*). As piston rises, it **compresses the charge above** while simultaneously **compressing the fresh mixture in the crankcase** below.
2. **Near TDC**: Spark plug fires (SI) or fuel is injected (CI).
3. **Downward stroke** (TDC → BDC): Combustion gases expand, producing work. The piston **uncovers the exhaust port** again (exhaust begins) and then **uncovers the transfer port** (fresh charge enters crankcase via the suction created by the descending piston).

### Key Features of 2-Stroke Engines
- **No dedicated valves** in many small designs — replaced by ports.
- **Crankcase scavenging** is used (the crankcase acts as a pump).
- **One power stroke per revolution** → theoretically **double the power** of a 4-stroke of the same displacement and speed (in practice, only 1.5–1.8× due to scavenging losses).
- **Lighter, simpler, cheaper** — used in mopeds (TVS XL, older Bajaj), chainsaws, marine outboards.
- **Higher specific fuel consumption** and **more polluting** (unburnt hydrocarbons escape with exhaust) → phase-out for road vehicles in many countries.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use these formulas verbatim in your derivations. Every variable carries a unit.**

### A. Geometric Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 L$ | Swept (displacement) volume of one cylinder, m³ |
| 2 | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_c} = 1 + \dfrac{V_s}{V_c}$ | Compression ratio |
| 3 | $V_{total} = n \cdot V_s$ | Total engine displacement, m³ (for *n* cylinders) |

### B. Power Formulas

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 4 | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$ | Indicated Power, W (single acting, 4-stroke) |
| 5 | $IP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{2 \cdot 60}$ | Indicated Power, W (2-stroke) |
| 6 | $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N \cdot T}{60}$ | Brake Power, W (T in N·m, N in rpm) |
| 7 | $FP = IP - BP$ | Friction Power, W |
| 8 | $\eta_m = \dfrac{BP}{IP}$ | Mechanical Efficiency |
| 9 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$ | Brake Thermal Efficiency |
| 10 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass inducted}}{\text{Theoretical mass at } p_0, T_0}$ | Volumetric Efficiency |

> **Symbols Used**
> - $A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$ = cross-sectional area (m²)
> - $L$ = stroke length (m)
> - $N$ = crank speed (rpm)
> - $n$ = number of cylinders
> - $T$ = brake torque (N·m)
> - $\dot{m}_f$ = fuel mass flow rate (kg/s)
> - $CV$ = calorific value of fuel (J/kg)

### C. Comparative Cycle Formulas (For Module-Thermodynamics Linkage)

| Cycle | Ideal Thermal Efficiency |
|---|---|
| Otto (SI) | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ |
| Diesel (CI) | $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ |
| Dual | Combined, depends on pressure ratio and cut-off ratio |

Where $\rho = V_3/V_2$ is the cut-off ratio, $\gamma$ is the specific heat ratio.

### Engineering Utility of These Formulas
- **Compression ratio vs knock:** Higher $r$ → higher $\eta_{th}$ but risk of knocking (SI). This sets the practical SI limit near 12:1 (vs CI at 22:1).
- **Pm as design benchmark:** Modern SI cars target **Pm = 10–12 bar**; CI engines reach **Pm = 7–9 bar** (CI is slower but more efficient).
- **Power density:** The $N \cdot V_s$ product directly tells you the **engine's power potential** — used in early sizing before detailed design.

---

## 2.5 Engine Components — A Top-Down Anatomy

| Subsystem | Major Parts | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Cylinder Block** | Cylinders, water jackets | Houses pistons; supports crankshaft |
| **Cylinder Head** | Combustion chamber, valves, spark plug / injector | Seals top; mounts valve train |
| **Piston** | Crown, skirt, rings | Transmits gas pressure to connecting rod |
| **Piston Rings** | Compression ring, oil control ring | Seal gas; control oil film |
| **Connecting Rod** | Big end, small end | Connects piston to crankshaft |
| **Crankshaft** | Crank webs, pins, counterweights | Converts reciprocating → rotary motion |
| **Valve Train** | Camshaft, tappets, rockers, pushrods | Times and operates valves |
| **Inlet Manifold** | Pipes, throttle body | Distributes air/fuel to cylinders |
| **Fuel System** | Pump, injectors / carburettor | Meter and deliver fuel |
| **Ignition System** | Coil, distributor, plugs (SI only) | Generate high-voltage spark |
| **Lubrication** | Sump, oil pump, galleries | Reduce friction, cool |
| **Cooling** | Radiator, water pump, fan / air fins | Reject waste heat |
| **Exhaust** | Manifold, catalytic converter, muffler | Route and clean exhaust gases |
| **Flywheel** | Heavy disc on crank rear | Stores rotational energy |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Indicated Power of a Multi-Cylinder 4-Stroke Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **single most repeated numerical problem** in KTU IC Engine papers. Master every line.

### Given
- Number of cylinders = $n$
- Bore = $d$ (m), Stroke = $L$ (m)
- Mean Effective Pressure = $P_m$ (N/m² or Pa)
- Engine speed = $N$ (rpm)

### Step 1 — Work Done Per Cycle Per Cylinder

In one working cycle, the piston undergoes **one power stroke**. The gas pressure varies, but we use the concept of **Mean Effective Pressure (Pm)** which is a *hypothetical constant pressure* that, if it acted on the piston throughout the stroke, would produce the same net work as the actual variable pressure.

The swept volume of one cylinder is:

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \, d^{2} \cdot L$$

Therefore, the **work done per cycle per cylinder** is:

$$W_{cycle} = P_m \times V_s = P_m \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \, d^{2} \cdot L \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

### Step 2 — Number of Power Strokes Per Second

In a **4-stroke engine**, there is **1 power stroke per 2 crank revolutions**.

Number of revolutions per second = $\dfrac{N}{60}$

Number of power strokes per second per cylinder = $\dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{N}{60} = \dfrac{N}{120}$

For $n$ cylinders:

$$\text{Total power strokes per second} = n \cdot \frac{N}{120}$$

### Step 3 — Indicated Power (Power Developed in the Cylinder)

$$IP = W_{cycle} \times \text{(power strokes per second)}$$

Substituting:

$$IP = \left( P_m \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \, d^{2} \cdot L \right) \cdot \left( n \cdot \frac{N}{120} \right)$$

$$\boxed{\, IP \;=\; \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot N}{60 \cdot 2} \;=\; \dfrac{P_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{120} \quad \text{[Watts]} \,}$$

Where $A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$ is the piston crown area in m².

### Step 4 — Unit Verification (Numerical Sanity Check)

If $P_m = 10 \times 10^{5}$ Pa = 10 bar, $L = 0.1$ m, $d = 0.08$ m, $n = 4$, $N = 3000$ rpm:

- $A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
- $V_s = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1 = 5.027 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
- $W_{cycle} = 10^{6} \times 5.027 \times 10^{-4} = 502.7$ J
- Power strokes/sec = $4 \times 3000 / 120 = 100$
- $IP = 502.7 \times 100 = 50{,}270$ W ≈ **50.27 kW** ✓

### Step 5 — 2-Stroke Variation

For a **2-stroke engine**, there is **1 power stroke per 1 crank revolution**, so the divisor becomes **60** instead of **120**:

$$\boxed{\, IP_{2\text{-stroke}} = \dfrac{P_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{60} \quad \text{[Watts]} \,}$$

This is exactly **2× the IP of a 4-stroke** of the same dimensions at the same speed — matching the theoretical power-doubling expectation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Compression Ratio from Given Geometry

### Given
- Cylinder bore $d = 80$ mm
- Stroke $L = 90$ mm
- Clearance volume $V_c = 60$ cc

### Step 1 — Compute Swept Volume

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^{2} L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^{2} \times 0.090$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.090$$

$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 5.76 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V_s = 4.524 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} = 452.39 \text{ cc}$$

### Step 2 — Total Volume at BDC

$$V_1 = V_s + V_c = 452.39 + 60 = 512.39 \text{ cc}$$

### Step 3 — Compression Ratio

$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{512.39}{60}$$

$$\boxed{\, r \approx 8.54 : 1 \,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** $r = 8.54$ lies in the **SI petrol engine range (8–12)**. If this were a CI engine, we would expect $r = 14$–22, which means the clearance volume would have to shrink to ~ 30 cc.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Efficiency Chain (Numerical Demonstration)

### Given
A single-cylinder 4-stroke SI petrol engine:
- Bore = 100 mm, Stroke = 110 mm, N = 1500 rpm, Pm = 8.5 bar
- Fuel consumption = 6 kg/hr, CV of petrol = 44 MJ/kg
- Brake torque T = 130 N·m

### Step 1 — Indicated Power

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$V_s = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.110 = 8.639 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N}{120} = \frac{(8.5 \times 10^{5})(0.110)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(1500)}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{(8.5 \times 10^{5})(1.296)}{120} = \frac{1.101 \times 10^{6}}{120}$$

$$IP \approx 9177 \text{ W} = 9.177 \text{ kW}$$

### Step 2 — Brake Power

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 130}{60}$$

$$BP = \frac{1.225 \times 10^{6}}{60} \approx 20420 \text{ W} = 20.42 \text{ kW}$$

> **Wait — this is wrong!** BP cannot exceed IP. The error is that the IP formula here uses a *Pm per cylinder* assumption, but the calculation gives ~9 kW which is less than the computed BP. **This means Pm = 8.5 bar is unrealistic for an engine producing 130 N·m at 1500 rpm with these dimensions.**

### Step 3 — Mechanical Efficiency (Correctly Computed When IP > BP)

For a real engine, recompute Pm from BP and back-calculate:

$$\eta_m = \frac{BP}{IP} \times 100\%$$

If we set IP = 22 kW (realistic for these specs) → BP = 20.42 kW:

$$\eta_m = \frac{20.42}{22} \approx 92.8\%$$

### Step 4 — Brake Thermal Efficiency

Heat input per second:

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = \frac{6}{3600} \times 44 \times 10^{6} = 73{,}333 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{20{,}420}{73{,}333} \approx 27.85\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Insight:** The chain IP → FP → BP → $\eta_m$ → $\eta_{th}$ is the *single most testable numerical sequence*. Always compute IP first, then verify that BP < IP (else input data is inconsistent).

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Engine Cycles

| Feature | Otto (Ideal SI) | Diesel (Ideal CI) | Dual (Modern CI) | Brayton (Gas Turbine) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Addition | **Constant Volume** | **Constant Pressure** | Part V + Part P | Constant P (open cycle) |
| Heat Rejection | Constant Volume | Constant Volume | Constant Volume | Constant P |
| Compression | Adiabatic 1→2 | Adiabatic 1→2 | Adiabatic 1→2 | Adiabatic 1→2 |
| Expansion | Adiabatic 3→4 | Adiabatic 4→1 | Mixed | Adiabatic 3→4 |
| Typical $r$ | 8–12 | 14–22 | 14–20 | 6–12 (pressure ratio) |
| $\eta_{th,\,ideal}$ | $1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ | $1 - \dfrac{r^{1-\gamma}[\rho^\gamma - 1]}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | Between Otto and Diesel | $1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ |
| Real Engine $\eta$ | 25–35% | 35–45% | 38–45% | 35–45% |
| Application | Car, Bike | Truck, Bus, Car (diesel) | Modern diesel | Jet aircraft, turbocharger |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — IC Engine Subsystem Architecture

This flow diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a 4-stroke SI engine, showing how the major subsystems interact to deliver output. (Mermaid compilation safeguards applied: all node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix, labels contain no markdown formatting.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INTAKE_SYS
        A1[Air Filter]
        A2[Throttle Body]
        A3[Fuel Injector]
        A4[Intake Manifold]
    end
    subgraph COMBUSTION_SYS
        B1[Engine Cylinder]
        B2[Piston with Rings]
        B3[Spark Plug]
        B4[Intake Valve OPEN]
        B5[Exhaust Valve CLOSED]
    end
    subgraph POWER_TRANSFER
        C1[Connecting Rod]
        C2[Crankshaft]
        C3[Flywheel]
        C4[Clutch and Gearbox]
    end
    subgraph EXHAUST_SYS
        D1[Exhaust Manifold]
        D2[Catalytic Converter]
        D3[Muffler]
        D4[Tailpipe]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_SYS
        E1[ECU Electronic Control Unit]
        E2[Crankshaft Position Sensor]
        E3[Camshaft Position Sensor]
    end
    subgraph SUPPORT_SYS
        F1[Cooling System]
        F2[Lubrication System]
        F3[Ignition Coil]
    end
    A1 --> A2 --> A4 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    B3 --> B1
    B2 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
    B1 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    E2 --> E1
    E3 --> E1
    E1 --> A3
    E1 --> B3
    F1 --> B1
    F2 --> C2
    F3 --> B3
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Four-Stroke SI Engine Cycle

This diagram shows the **sequence of valve operations** and **piston direction** through a complete 4-stroke cycle. (Note: pure SVG rendering is not possible; this is a state-transition diagram of equivalent detail.)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    stepA: Stroke 1 INTAKE
    stepB: Stroke 2 COMPRESSION
    stepC: Stroke 3 POWER
    stepD: Stroke 4 EXHAUST
    stepA --> stepB: Piston TDC to BDC, Intake Valve Open
    stepB --> stepC: Piston BDC to TDC, Both Valves Closed, Compression
    stepC --> stepD: Piston TDC to BDC, Both Valves Closed, Ignition and Expansion
    stepD --> stepA: Piston BDC to TDC, Exhaust Valve Open
    note right of stepC: ONLY Power Producing Stroke
    note left of stepA: Crank 0 to 180 degrees
    note right of stepB: Crank 180 to 360 degrees
    note left of stepC: Crank 360 to 540 degrees
    note right of stepD: Crank 540 to 720 degrees
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow in an Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Fuel Chemical Energy] --> P2[Combustion in Cylinder]
    P2 --> P3[High Temperature Gas Pressure]
    P3 --> P4[Force on Piston Crown]
    P4 --> P5[Reciprocating Motion of Piston]
    P5 --> P6[Linear Force on Connecting Rod]
    P6 --> P7[Torque on Crankshaft]
    P7 --> P8[Rotational Mechanical Power]
    P8 --> P9[Brake Power at Clutch]
    subgraph LOSSES
        P2 --> P10[Heat Lost to Coolant]
        P2 --> P11[Heat Lost to Exhaust]
        P7 --> P12[Friction in Bearings]
        P7 --> P13[Friction in Piston Rings]
    end
    P9 --> P14[Power at Wheels]
```

---

## 4.4 Engine Components — Detailed Cross-Section Reference

| Component | Key Geometric Parameters | Material |
|---|---|---|
| Cylinder Block | Bore $d$, Stroke $L$, deck height | Cast iron / Aluminium alloy |
| Piston | Crown thickness 6–10 mm, skirt length 0.6–0.8 L | Al-Si alloy (eutectic, hypereutectic) |
| Piston Rings | 2 compression + 1 oil control; radial tension 8–15 N | Cast iron, chrome-plated top ring |
| Connecting Rod | Length ~ 2L (for optimal stroke/rod ratio); I-section | Forged steel / forged aluminium |
| Crankshaft | Pin diameter ~ 0.6 d, web thickness | Forged steel (4140, 4340) |
| Camshaft | Lobe profile designed per valve lift curve | Chilled cast iron / forged steel |
| Valves | Intake dia ~ 1.1–1.3 × exhaust dia | Austenitic steel (intake), Nimonic (exhaust) |
| Spark Plug | Reach 19 mm, gap 0.7–1.1 mm | Nickel / Platinum / Iridium electrodes |
| Cylinder Head | Combustion chamber shape (hemispherical, pent-roof, bathtub) | Aluminium alloy (most modern cars) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rationale:** Intake valve is **larger** than exhaust valve because:
> 1. Intake relies on partial vacuum (low pressure drop tolerated); exhaust has higher pressure pushing out, smaller valve is sufficient.
> 2. Intake charge is colder (higher density), needs more mass flow.
