# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`
**Explain with a neat sketch the working of a 4-stroke petrol engine.**

#### Model Answer (Concise Board-Ready Format)

A 4-stroke SI engine operates on the **Otto cycle** and completes one cycle in **two revolutions (720°)** of the crankshaft.

1. **Suction stroke** (0°–180°): Piston moves TDC → BDC. Intake valve opens, fuel-air mixture drawn in.
2. **Compression stroke** (180°–360°): Both valves closed. Mixture compressed to a ratio of 8:1–12:1, temperature rises to ~400 °C.
3. **Power/expansion stroke** (360°–540°): Spark plug ignites mixture at ~10° BTDC. Combustion (constant volume) raises pressure to ~40 bar. High pressure pushes piston from TDC to BDC.
4. **Exhaust stroke** (540°–720°): Exhaust valve opens. Burnt gases expelled as piston moves BDC → TDC.

> Only **one stroke produces work**; the rest consume it (stored temporarily in the flywheel).

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**Compare 2-stroke and 4-stroke engines (any 6 points).**

#### Model Answer

| # | Parameter | 2-Stroke | 4-Stroke |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Strokes per cycle | 2 | 4 |
| 2 | Crank revolutions per cycle | 1 (360°) | 2 (720°) |
| 3 | Power strokes per revolution | 1 | 1/2 |
| 4 | Theoretical power (same size, speed) | Double | Half |
| 5 | Valves / ports | Usually ports | Poppet valves |
| 6 | Lubrication | Oil mixed with fuel (total loss) | Sump-based oil circulation |
| 7 | Weight | Lighter | Heavier |
| 8 | Thermal efficiency | Lower (scavenging loss) | Higher |
| 9 | Emissions | Higher (HC unburnt) | Lower |
| 10 | Applications | Mopeds, chainsaws | Cars, trucks, bikes |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected]`

**(a)** With the help of a P-V and T-S diagram, **explain the working of a 4-stroke diesel engine**. Discuss the function of each component. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 80 mm, stroke of 90 mm, and runs at 3000 rpm. The mean effective pressure is 9 bar, mechanical efficiency is 85 %, and fuel consumption is 12 kg/hr with calorific value 44 MJ/kg. Calculate: **(i) Indicated Power, (ii) Brake Power, (iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency, (iv) Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Working of 4-Stroke Diesel Engine

**Components & Functions:**
1. **Cylinder + Cylinder head** — houses the piston; seals combustion chamber.
2. **Piston** — transmits gas force to connecting rod via piston pin.
3. **Connecting rod** — converts reciprocating motion of piston to rotary motion of crank.
4. **Crankshaft** — rotates about axis; flywheel stores energy.
5. **Intake & exhaust valves** — control gas entry and exit.
6. **Fuel injector** — sprays diesel into hot compressed air at high pressure.
7. **Camshaft** — driven by crankshaft via timing belt/chain; opens valves at correct timing.
8. **Cooling & lubrication systems** — remove heat and reduce friction.

**Working Cycle:**

| Stroke | Piston | Intake Valve | Exhaust Valve | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Intake | TDC→BDC | OPEN | CLOSED | Fresh **air** inducted |
| Compression | BDC→TDC | CLOSED | CLOSED | Air compressed (r = 14–22), T ~ 900 °C |
| Power | TDC→BDC | CLOSED | CLOSED | Diesel injected → auto-ignition → expansion |
| Exhaust | BDC→TDC | CLOSED | OPEN | Burnt gases expelled |

**Ideal P-V Diagram (Diesel Cycle):**
- Process 1–2: Adiabatic compression (vertical-ish curve)
- Process 2–3: Constant pressure heat addition (horizontal line at top)
- Process 3–4: Adiabatic expansion
- Process 4–1: Constant volume heat rejection

**Ideal T-S Diagram:** similar topology, with area under curve representing work and entropy changes.

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:** $n = 4$, $d = 0.080$ m, $L = 0.090$ m, $N = 3000$ rpm, $P_m = 9 \times 10^{5}$ Pa, $\eta_m = 0.85$, $\dot{m}_f = 12$ kg/hr, $CV = 44 \times 10^{6}$ J/kg.

#### (i) Indicated Power

Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.080)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6.4 \times 10^{-3} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Step 2 — Indicated power (4-stroke):
$$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot N}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{(9 \times 10^{5})(0.090)(5.027 \times 10^{-3})(4)(3000)}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{(9 \times 10^{5})(0.090)(5.027 \times 10^{-3})(12000)}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{(9 \times 10^{5}) \times 5.4308}{1}$$

$$\boxed{\, IP \approx 4.888 \times 10^{4} \text{ W} \approx 48.88 \text{ kW} \,}$$

#### (ii) Brake Power

$$BP = \eta_m \cdot IP = 0.85 \times 48.88$$

$$\boxed{\, BP \approx 41.55 \text{ kW} \,}$$

#### (iii) Brake Thermal Efficiency

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}_f \cdot CV = \frac{12}{3600} \times 44 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \frac{12 \times 44 \times 10^{6}}{3600} = 146{,}667 \text{ W} = 146.67 \text{ kW}$$

$$\eta_{th,\,brake} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{in}} = \frac{41.55}{146.67}$$

$$\boxed{\, \eta_{th,\,brake} \approx 28.33\% \,}$$

#### (iv) Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption (ISFC)

$$ISFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{IP \, [\text{kW}]}$$

$$ISFC = \frac{12 \times 3600}{48.88 \times 3600 / 1000} = \frac{12}{0.04888} \times 1000$$

> Using a cleaner form: $ISFC = \dfrac{3600 \times \dot{m}_f}{IP\,[\text{kW}]} = \dfrac{3600 \times 12}{48.88} = \dfrac{43{,}200}{48.88}$

$$\boxed{\, ISFC \approx 884 \text{ g/kWh} \,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT make these mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "120" denominator** for 4-stroke IP. Students often use "60" and double the answer.
> 2. **Mixing up BSFC and ISFC** — BSFC uses BP, ISFC uses IP. ISFC < BSFC.
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — $P_m$ in Pa (not bar); $L$, $d$ in metres (not mm).
> 4. **Forgetting $\eta_m$ link** — if you computed BP from torque, do NOT additionally multiply by $\eta_m$. Use one method consistently.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]`

**(a)** **Explain the working of a 2-stroke petrol engine with a neat sketch. Why is it called a "crankcase-scavenged" engine?** **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define the following with formulas: **(i) Compression ratio, (ii) Mean effective pressure, (iii) Volumetric efficiency, (iv) Mechanical efficiency, (v) Brake thermal efficiency, (vi) Specific fuel consumption, (vii) Indicated power.** Compute the **Indicated Power** of a single-cylinder, 4-stroke engine with bore = 100 mm, stroke = 120 mm, Pm = 8 bar, and speed = 1800 rpm. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — 2-Stroke Petrol Engine

**Construction:** A 2-stroke SI engine has:
- A **cylinder** with three openings (ports) cut into its walls — **intake port, transfer port, exhaust port**.
- A **piston** that alternately covers and uncovers these ports as it moves.
- A **crankcase** that is sealed (unlike 4-stroke) and acts as a small pump.
- **Spark plug** at the cylinder head.

**Working:**

**Upward stroke (BDC → TDC):**
- Piston first uncovers the **exhaust port** → burnt gases rush out (exhaust begins).
- Piston uncovers the **transfer port** → fresh charge from crankcase enters the cylinder (scavenging).
- As piston rises, it compresses the fresh charge above (in cylinder).
- Simultaneously, the upward piston creates a partial vacuum in the crankcase, drawing fresh fuel-air mixture through the **intake port** into the crankcase.

**Near TDC:** Spark plug fires → mixture ignites → high pressure develops.

**Downward stroke (TDC → BDC):**
- Combustion gases expand and push piston down → **power stroke**.
- Piston compresses the fresh mixture in the crankcase.
- Piston uncovers the exhaust port again → exhaust begins.
- As the piston descends further, it **uncovers the transfer port** and the compressed charge in the crankcase flows into the cylinder, pushing out remaining exhaust gases.

**Why "crankcase-scavenged"?**
The crankcase itself acts as the **scavenging pump** — it draws in fresh charge during upward stroke and pushes it into the cylinder via the transfer port during downward stroke. No separate pump is needed, hence the name. This is the simplest and cheapest 2-stroke design (called *Schnürle* or *cross-flow scavenging* in modern forms).

> **Pros:** Simple, lightweight, one power stroke per revolution (high power-to-weight ratio).
> **Cons:** Poor scavenging efficiency → lower thermal efficiency, more HC emissions, oil mixing causes smoke.

### Part (b) — Definitions + Numerical

**Definitions:**

**(i) Compression Ratio (r):** Ratio of total cylinder volume (at BDC) to clearance volume (at TDC).
$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_c} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$$

**(ii) Mean Effective Pressure (Pm):** The hypothetical constant pressure acting on the piston throughout the power stroke that produces the same work as the actual variable pressure.
$$P_m = \frac{W_{cycle}}{V_s}$$

**(iii) Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_{vol}$):** Ratio of actual mass of charge inducted to the theoretical mass that would occupy the displaced volume at ambient conditions.
$$\eta_{vol} = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{actual}}{\rho_{atm} \cdot V_s}$$

**(iv) Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_m$):** Ratio of brake power to indicated power.
$$\eta_m = \frac{BP}{IP}$$

**(v) Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th,\,b}$):** Ratio of brake power output to heat input from fuel.
$$\eta_{th,\,b} = \frac{BP}{\dot{m}_f \cdot CV}$$

**(vi) Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** Fuel consumption per unit brake power per hour.
$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f \times 3600}{BP \, [\text{kW}]} \quad [\text{kg/kWh}]$$

**(vii) Indicated Power (IP):** Power developed by the gas pressure on the piston, before any mechanical losses.
$$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{120} \text{ (4-stroke)}$$

#### Numerical Computation

**Given:** $n = 1$, $d = 100$ mm = 0.1 m, $L = 120$ mm = 0.12 m, $P_m = 8$ bar = $8 \times 10^{5}$ Pa, $N = 1800$ rpm, 4-stroke.

**Step 1 — Piston area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Swept volume:**
$$V_s = A \cdot L = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.12 = 9.425 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3 = 942.5 \text{ cc}$$

**Step 3 — IP Formula:**
$$IP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{120} = \frac{(8 \times 10^{5})(0.12)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(1800)(1)}{120}$$

$$IP = \frac{(8 \times 10^{5})(0.12)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(1800)}{120}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify inner term:**
- $(8 \times 10^{5}) \times 0.12 = 9.6 \times 10^{4}$
- $9.6 \times 10^{4} \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 753.98$
- $753.98 \times 1800 = 1.357 \times 10^{6}$

**Step 5 — Divide by 120:**
$$IP = \frac{1.357 \times 10^{6}}{120} = 11{,}310 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{\, IP \approx 11.31 \text{ kW} \approx 15.17 \text{ hp} \,}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns Observed in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the diagram:** A "neat sketch" carries **2–3 marks** standalone. Always draw the cylinder + piston + valves + crank + spark plug (for SI) or injector (for CI).
> 2. **Not specifying units:** $P_m$ in Pa or bar? $L$ in mm or m? Examiners deduct **½ mark** per missing unit in a 7-mark question.
> 3. **Confusing SI and CI:** Writing "spark plug" for a diesel engine or "fuel injector" for petrol → **−1 mark**.
> 4. **Writing IP = BP without justification:** IP and BP differ by friction losses; this distinction is tested.
> 5. **Wrong stroke-count for IP formula:** Using "60" instead of "120" for 4-stroke → answer doubled → **−2 marks**.
> 6. **Forgetting $n$ (number of cylinders) in multi-cylinder problems:** Single biggest mark-loss in numerical questions.
> 7. **No mention of flywheel:** Any explanation of a 4-stroke engine without mentioning the flywheel's role loses **1 mark** because the flywheel is what makes 1-power-stroke-per-720° operation feasible.
> 8. **Missing valve timing reference:** Examiners love the phrase *"valve timing"* — for full marks, mention IVC (intake valve closes after BDC) and EVC (exhaust valve closes after TDC).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Engines**

### Definition Layer
- ☐ **IC Engine**: Combustion inside cylinder; converts chemical → mechanical.
- ☐ **SI Engine**: Spark Ignition (petrol); uses spark plug; Otto cycle.
- ☐ **CI Engine**: Compression Ignition (diesel); auto-ignition; Diesel cycle.
- ☐ **2-Stroke vs 4-Stroke**: 1 vs 2 crank revolutions per cycle; ports vs valves.

### Geometric Formulas (memorise verbatim)
- ☐ $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 L$
- ☐ $r = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_c}$
- ☐ $V_{total} = n \cdot V_s$ for multi-cylinder.

### Power Formulas
- ☐ 4-stroke IP: $IP = \dfrac{P_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{120}$
- ☐ 2-stroke IP: $IP = \dfrac{P_m \, L \, A \, N \, n}{60}$ (no "120")
- ☐ BP: $BP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$
- ☐ FP = IP − BP
- ☐ $\eta_m = BP / IP$
- ☐ $\eta_{th,b} = BP / (\dot{m}_f \cdot CV)$
- ☐ BSFC in **g/kWh** = $3600 \cdot \dot{m}_f / BP_{[kW]}$

### Comparative Engine Knowledge
- ☐ SI: $r$ = 8–12, Pm = 8–12 bar, $\eta$ = 25–35 %.
- ☐ CI: $r$ = 14–22, Pm = 7–9 bar, $\eta$ = 35–45 %.
- ☐ CI is more efficient → diesel cars have better fuel economy.
- ☐ SI produces less vibration → smoother for passenger cars.

### Cycle Knowledge
- ☐ **Otto cycle**: 2 adiabatic + 1 constant-V heat addition + 1 constant-V heat rejection.
- ☐ **Diesel cycle**: 2 adiabatic + 1 constant-P heat addition + 1 constant-V heat rejection.
- ☐ **Dual cycle**: realistic modern CI; part V + part P.

### Engineering Judgement
- ☐ A 4-stroke engine needs a **flywheel** — only 1 power stroke per 720°.
- ☐ Intake valve is **larger** than exhaust valve.
- ☐ Air-cooled engines are simpler (used in bikes like Royal Enfield, TVS).
- ☐ Water-cooled engines handle higher power (cars, trucks).

### KTU Favourite One-Liners (for 1-mark direct questions)
- ☐ *"Why SI uses lower compression ratio than CI?"* — To avoid knocking (auto-ignition of petrol).
- ☐ *"Why CI is more efficient than SI?"* — Higher $r$ gives higher $\eta_{Otto}$ (since $\eta = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ is increasing in $r$).
- ☐ *"Why is the inlet valve larger?"* — More mass flow at the same pressure drop; intake charge is cooler and denser.
- ☐ *"What is knocking?"* — Uncontrolled auto-ignition of end-gas in SI engines → pressure wave → "ping" sound and engine damage.

### Numerical Question Hot-Zones
- ☐ Compute IP / BP / FP / $\eta_m$ / $\eta_{th}$ in **one chain** — appearing in nearly every KTU paper.
- ☐ Compression ratio computation from bore, stroke, clearance volume.
- ☐ Comparison of BSFC for two fuels (BSFC inversely proportional to $\eta_{th}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra:** Whenever you see "mean effective pressure" in a problem statement → your first reflex must be $W = P_m \cdot V_s$. This single equation unlocks 70 % of numerical questions in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification, Construction, and Materials of Engine Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classification, Construction, and Materials of Engine Components # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition: Engine Component Architecture** > In the context of automotive powerplants, the **classification, construction, and materials of engine components** refers to the systematic categorization of internal combustion (IC) engines based on thermodynamic cycle, ignition method, stroke cycle, and cylinder geometry, coupled with the engineering study of the geometric configuration, mating tolerances, and metallurgical composition of the constituent parts (cylinder block, head, piston, connecting rod, crankshaft, camshaft, valves, and flywheel) that collectively convert chemical energy of fuel into mechanical work. The subject is foundational to Module 1 because every subsequent module — fuel systems, cooling, lubrication, ignition, and emission control — is built upon an understanding of *which* component does *what*, *how* it is built, and *what* material makes it survive the in-cylinder environment. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of an automobile engine as a **human heart in a steel cage**: - The **cylinder block** is the rib cage — the rigid skeleton that holds everything together and survives the pounding. - The **piston** is the beating heart muscle — it moves up and down, pumping energy. - The **connecting rod** is the tendon — it links the muscle (piston) to the bone (crankshaft). - The **crankshaft** is the rotating joint (shoulder) that converts the linear "muscle pump" into rotary motion. - The **valves** are the lips/mouth — they open to let fuel/air in (inhale) and exhaust out (exhale) at exactly the right moment. - The **flywheel** is the body's momentum — keeping motion smooth between heartbeats. Just as a cardiologist must know the anatomy of the heart before treating disease, an automobile engineer must master engine construction and materials before diagnosing, designing, or servicing any subsystem. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Parameter | Standard Value / Range | |---|---| | Combustion chamber peak temperature | **2000°C – 2500°C** | | Combustion chamber peak pressure | **50 bar – 100 bar** (SI) / **80 bar – 200 bar** (CI) | | Piston mean speed (typical) | **9 m/s – 15 m/s** | | Cylinder bore-to-stroke ratio (modern) | **0.8 – 1.2** (square or slightly oversquare) | | Engine speed range (passenger car) | **600 rpm (idle) – 6500 rpm (redline)** | | Material density — Cast Iron | **7.2 g/cm³** | | Material density — Aluminum Alloy | **2.7 g/cm³** | > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (CO1):** The first Course Outcome of PESFT636 demands that students must be able to *“describe the classification, construction, and materials of major engine components.”* This module directly maps to CO1 at **Bloom's Level: Remember (L1) and Understand (L2).** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Engines — Hierarchical Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Primary Classification — Energy Source - **Internal Combustion (IC) Engine:** Combustion of fuel occurs *inside* the working cylinder. (E.g., Petrol/Diesel car engines). - **External Combustion (EC) Engine:** Combustion occurs *outside* the working cylinder. (E.g., Steam engine, Stirling engine). > [!NOTE] > All automobile engines studied under PESFT636 are **IC engines.** ### 2.1.2 Secondary Classification — Ignition Method - **Spark Ignition (SI) Engine:** Uses an externally supplied spark plug. Fuel = Petrol/Gasoline. Air-fuel mixture is prepared *before* or *during* intake. - **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine:** Self-ignites fuel due to high compression temperature. Fuel = Diesel. Only air is compressed; fuel is injected near TDC. ### 2.1.3 Tertiary Classification — Thermodynamic Cycle - **Otto Cycle (Constant Volume Heat Addition):** Theoretical cycle of SI engines. - **Diesel Cycle (Constant Pressure Heat Addition):** Theoretical cycle of CI engines. - **Dual / Sabathe Cycle:** A hybrid; partial heat addition at constant volume, the rest at constant pressure. Models modern high-speed CI engines accurately. ### 2.1.4 Quaternary Classification — Stroke Cycle - **4-Stroke Engine:** Complete cycle in **two crankshaft revolutions (720°)** — Intake, Compression, Power, Exhaust. - **2-Stroke Engine:** Complete cycle in **one crankshaft revolution (360°)** — uses ports instead of poppet valves. ### 2.1.5 Quinary Classification — Cylinder Arrangement | Layout | Description | Application | |---|---|---| | **Inline (I)** | Cylinders in a single row | Most 4-cyl family cars | | **V-Type** | Two banks at 60°/90° angle | 6, 8, 10, 12 cylinder performance cars | | **Flat / Boxer** | Cylinders horizontally opposed | Porsche, Subaru | | **W-Type** | Compact VR-style with 4 banks | Bugatti Veyron (W16) | | **Radial** | Cylinders arranged around crank (aircraft legacy) | Vintage aircraft | ## 2.2 Construction of Engine Components ### 2.2.1 Cylinder Block - The **monolithic main structure** housing the cylinders, coolant jackets, and bearing saddles. - Houses the **crankcase** (lower half) where the crankshaft rotates. - Modern blocks: **closed-deck** (cylinder bores fully surrounded by coolant) for high-boost turbo applications; **open-deck** (open at coolant side) for economy. ### 2.2.2 Cylinder Head - Bolted to the block via **head bolts/studs**; sealed by a **head gasket** (multi-layer steel MLS). - Contains **intake ports, exhaust ports, valve guides, valve seats, spark plug holes (SI), injector holes (CI), and combustion chamber cavities.** - **OHC (Overhead Cam)** and **OHV (Overhead Valve)** architectures reside here. ### 2.2.3 Piston - A **cup-shaped cylindrical component** reciprocating inside the cylinder bore. - Key regions: **Crown (top), Head, Land (ring region), Skirt, Pin boss.** - Seals the cylinder using **compression ring(s)** (gas seal) and **oil control ring(s)** (oil scraping). ### 2.2.4 Piston Rings - **Compression ring (top):** Seals combustion gases above the piston. - **Compression ring (second):** Improves seal; sometimes stepped or napier profile. - **Oil control ring (bottom):** Scrapes excess oil back to the sump, regulating the oil film on the cylinder wall. ### 2.2.5 Connecting Rod - Links the **piston pin (gudgeon pin)** to the **crankshaft journal**. - Consists of: **small end (piston pin bushing)**, **I-beam or H-beam shank**, **big end (bearing cap bolted on)**. - Subject to **tensile, compressive, and buckling loads**; must be light yet rigid. ### 2.2.6 Crankshaft - Converts reciprocating piston motion into rotary motion. - Comprises: **main journals** (supported in block bearings), **crankpins (rod journals)**, **counterweights** (balance), **crank webs**, **nose (front pulley)**, **flange (flywheel mounting)**. - Lobe arrangement determines **firing order.** ### 2.2.7 Camshaft - Operates the **valves** via lobes in SI engines; operates **injectors** in CI engines. - Drive: **chain, belt, or gear.** - Position: **OHV (in block, pushrods)** or **OHC (single/dual overhead cam).** ### 2.2.8 Valves - **Poppet valves** — mushroom-shaped; **intake valve** (larger, often nickel-chrome plated) and **exhaust valve** (smaller, sodium-cooled in high-performance engines). - Components: **head, face, stem, tip, spring retainer, keepers (cotter pins).** ### 2.2.9 Flywheel - A heavy **inertia disc** mounted on the crankshaft flange. - Stores rotational kinetic energy between power strokes, smoothing torque pulsations. - Often the mounting surface for the **clutch disc** (manual) or **flexplate** (automatic). ### 2.2.10 Manifolds - **Intake manifold:** Distributes air (or air-fuel mixture) to cylinders. - **Exhaust manifold:** Collects exhaust gases from cylinders. ## 2.3 Materials of Engine Components | Component | Primary Material | Reason / Property | |---|---|---| | Cylinder Block (cast) | **Gray Cast Iron (GG25)** | Wear resistance, damping, machinability | | Cylinder Block (modern) | **Aluminum Alloy (A356, A319)** | Lightweight (40% mass saving), better heat dissipation | | Cylinder Liner/Sleeve | **Cast Iron, Nikasil-coated Al, Plasma-sprayed** | Wear resistance on bore surface | | Cylinder Head | **Aluminum Alloy (A356, A201)** | Lightweight, high thermal conductivity | | Piston | **Al-Si Alloy (e.g., A4032, A390, Mahle 124)** | Lightweight, low thermal expansion, high wear resistance | | Piston Ring | **Cast Iron, Chrome-plated steel, PTFE-coated** | High wear, spring-back | | Connecting Rod | **Forged Steel (4340, 4140) or Powder Forged** | High fatigue strength | | Crankshaft | **Forged Steel (4340), Nodular Cast Iron, Billet** | High fatigue, wear, and impact strength | | Camshaft | **Chilled Cast Iron, Forged Steel** | Surface hardness for lobe wear | | Valve (Intake) | **Stainless Steel (21-4N), Nimonic alloys** | Heat and corrosion resistance | | Valve (Exhaust) | **Inconel, Sodium-cooled hollow stem Nimonic** | Extreme heat resistance | | Valve Spring | **Chrome-Silicon Spring Steel, Oil-tempered wire** | High fatigue limit | | Flywheel | **Gray Cast Iron** | Mass + damping capacity | | Head Gasket | **Multi-Layer Steel (MLS) with elastomer coating** | Sealing under thermal cycling | | Bearings (Main/Rod) | **Steel backing + Babbitt (Sn-Sb-Cu) lining** | Embeddability, conformability | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why aluminum pistons in cast-iron blocks?** > A cast-iron block is often paired with **aluminum pistons** because aluminum has a **higher thermal expansion coefficient (~23 × 10⁻⁶ /°C)** than cast iron (~11 × 10⁻⁶ /°C). The piston is deliberately made *smaller* at room temperature so that at operating temperature (~200°C), the differential expansion produces the desired running clearance. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^2 \cdot L$ | Swept volume of one cylinder | m³ | | 2 | $V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^2 \cdot L_c$ | Clearance volume | m³ | | 3 | $r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = 1 + \frac{L}{L_c}$ | Compression ratio (dimensionless) | — | | 4 | $N_{cycle} = \frac{N}{2}$ (4-stroke) | Power strokes per second | strokes/s | | 5 | $N_{cycle} = N$ (2-stroke) | Power strokes per second | strokes/s | | 6 | $\text{Piston speed } \bar{U}_p = 2 L N$ | Mean piston speed | m/s | | 7 | $\text{Bore/Stroke } = B/L$ | Engine architecture indicator | — | | 8 | $\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | Otto cycle thermal efficiency | — | | 9 | $\eta_{th,Diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | Diesel cycle thermal efficiency | — | | 10 | $\rho = \frac{V_1}{V_2} = \frac{V_c + V_s}{V_c + V_{cut-off}}$ | Cut-off ratio (Diesel) | — | | 11 | $P_i = \frac{p_{im} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot n}{60}$ | Indicated Power (4-stroke) | kW | | 12 | $p_{im} = \frac{\text{IMEP} \cdot N \cdot n}{2}$ (4-stroke) | Mean indicated pressure relation | bar | Where: - $B$ = Bore diameter, $L$ = Stroke length, $L_c$ = Clearance height - $N$ = RPM, $n$ = number of cylinders, $A$ = cross-section area - $\gamma$ = specific heat ratio (≈ 1.4 for air), $\rho$ = cut-off ratio > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** These equations allow an engineer to size an engine for a target power output, choose between a long-stroke (high torque, low rpm) and short-stroke (high rpm, sporty) architecture, and select a material whose **thermal conductivity ($k$)**, **Young's modulus ($E$)**, and **fatigue limit ($S_f$)** can withstand the calculated stresses. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Swept Volume and Compression Ratio The **swept volume** $V_s$ is the volume displaced by the piston as it travels from Top Dead Center (TDC) to Bottom Dead Center (BDC). **Step 1 — Start with the geometry of a cylinder.** The piston face is circular with diameter $B$ (bore). As it moves a distance $L$ (stroke), it sweeps a cylindrical volume. $$V_s = \text{Area of cross-section} \times \text{Stroke length}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the area of a circle, $A = \frac{\pi}{4} B^2$.** $$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 \cdot L$$ **Step 3 — The clearance volume** $V_c$ is the volume remaining above the piston when the piston is at TDC. It includes the head gasket volume, the piston dish/dome, and the chamber volume. $$V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 \cdot L_c + V_{chamber} + V_{gasket} + V_{piston,dish}$$ For simplicity (and as accepted in KTU problems), we approximate: $$V_c \approx \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 \cdot L_c$$ **Step 4 — The compression ratio** $r$ is the ratio of the *largest* volume (BDC) to the *smallest* volume (TDC). $$r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$$ **Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $V_c$ (where $V_c = \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 L_c$ and $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 L$):** $$\begin{aligned} r &= \frac{\frac{\pi}{4} B^2 L + \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 L_c}{\frac{\pi}{4} B^2 L_c} \\ &= \frac{L + L_c}{L_c} \\ &= 1 + \frac{L}{L_c} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Final simplified form (commonly used in KTU problems):** $$r = 1 + \frac{L}{L_c}$$ **Interpretation:** - A higher $r$ (compression ratio) → higher thermal efficiency (Otto: $1 - r^{1-\gamma}$) but higher knocking tendency in SI engines. - Petrol SI engines: typically $r = 8$ to $12$. - Diesel CI engines: typically $r = 14$ to $22$. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Engine Classification (Rapid Comparison) | Parameter | SI (Petrol) | CI (Diesel) | |---|---|---| | Fuel | Gasoline | Diesel | | Ignition | Spark plug | Compression heat | | Air-fuel mixture | Pre-mixed (homogeneous) | Stratified (heterogeneous) | | Compression ratio | 8 – 12 | 14 – 22 | | Thermodynamic cycle | Otto / Sabathe | Diesel / Sabathe | | Throttling | Yes (pumping loss) | No (air only) | | Peak cylinder pressure | 40 – 60 bar | 80 – 200 bar | | Stroke cycle | 4-stroke (typical) | 4-stroke (typical) | | Component strength | Moderate | High (high pressures) | | Weight | Lighter | Heavier | | Fuel efficiency | Lower | Higher | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material Property Comparison | Material | Density $\rho$ (g/cm³) | Thermal Cond. $k$ (W/m·K) | Young's Mod. $E$ (GPa) | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | Gray Cast Iron | **7.2** | 46 | 100 – 170 | Block, flywheel, brake drum | | Al-Si Alloy (A356) | **2.7** | 150 | 72 | Head, piston, block | | Forged Steel (4340) | **7.85** | 44 | 200 | Crankshaft, con-rod | | Inconel 718 | 8.2 | 11 | 200 | Exhaust valves (high-temp) | | Babbitt (Sn-base) | 7.3 | 50 | 50 | Bearing lining | ## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem (KTU University Exam pattern):** > A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine has a bore of **80 mm** and stroke of **90 mm**. The clearance volume is **12%** of the swept volume. Calculate: > 1. The swept volume of one cylinder. > 2. The compression ratio. > 3. The Otto cycle thermal efficiency if $\gamma = 1.4$. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Swept volume per cylinder:** $$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot B^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.080)^2 \cdot (0.090)$$ $$\begin{aligned} V_s &= 0.7854 \cdot 0.0064 \cdot 0.090 \\ &= 0.7854 \cdot 5.76 \times 10^{-4} \\ &= 4.524 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 \\ &= 452.4\ \text{cm}^3 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Compression ratio:** $$V_c = 0.12 \cdot V_s = 0.12 \cdot 452.4 = 54.29\ \text{cm}^3$$ $$r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{452.4 + 54.29}{54.29} = \frac{506.69}{54.29} = 9.33$$ **Step 3 — Otto thermal efficiency:** $$\eta_{th,Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{(9.33)^{0.4}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} (9.33)^{0.4} &= e^{0.4 \cdot \ln 9.33} = e^{0.4 \cdot 2.2336} = e^{0.8934} = 2.443 \\ \eta_{th,Otto} &= 1 - \frac{1}{2.443} = 1 - 0.4094 = 0.5906 \\ &= 59.06\% \end{aligned}$$ **Sanity Check:** For SI engines, the *actual* (brake) thermal efficiency is 25–35%; the *ideal* Otto efficiency at r ≈ 9.3 should fall in the 55–60% band — ✓ check passed. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Engine Component Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[IC Engine] --> B[Engine Block Assembly] A --> C[Valvetrain Assembly] A --> D[Reciprocating Assembly] A --> E[Rotating Assembly] A --> F[Inlet Exhaust Assembly] B --> B1[Cylinder Block] B --> B2[Cylinder Head] B --> B3[Head Gasket] B --> B4[Oil Pan Sump] B --> B5[Crankcase] C --> C1[Camshaft] C --> C2[Valves Intake Exhaust] C --> C3[Valve Springs] C --> C4[Rocker Arms] C --> C5[Timing Chain or Belt] C --> C6[Push Rods OHV] D --> D1[Piston] D --> D2[Piston Rings] D --> D3[Connecting Rod] D --> D4[Gudgeon Pin Wrist Pin] E --> E1[Crankshaft] E --> E2[Main Bearings] E --> E3[Flywheel] E --> E4[Vibration Damper] F --> F1[Intake Manifold] F --> F2[Exhaust Manifold] F --> F3[Throttle Body] F --> F4[Fuel Injectors] ``` ## 4.2 Engine Classification Flowchart ```mermaid graph TD ENG[Engine] --> EC{Combustion Location} EC -->|External| ECENG[Steam Stirling EC Engine] EC -->|Internal| IC[IC Engine] IC --> IG{Ignition Type} IG -->|Spark Plug| SI[SI Engine Petrol] IG -->|Compression Heat| CI[CI Engine Diesel] SI --> OT[Otto Cycle] CI --> DC[Diesel Cycle] CI --> SAB[Sabathe Dual Cycle] OT --> ST{Stroke} DC --> ST SAB --> ST ST -->|Two Revolutions| F4S[4 Stroke Engine] ST -->|One Revolution| T2S[2 Stroke Engine] F4S --> ARR{Cylinder Arrangement} T2S --> ARR ARR --> IN[Inline] ARR --> VT[V Type] ARR --> FB[Flat Boxer] ARR --> RA[Radial] ARR --> WR[W Type] ``` ## 4.3 4-Stroke Engine Operating Cycle — Block-Level Functional Flow ```mermaid graph LR IN[1 Intake Stroke
IV Open
EV Closed] --> CM[2 Compression Stroke
IV Closed
EV Closed] CM --> PW[3 Power Stroke
IV Closed
EV Closed
Combustion] PW --> EX[4 Exhaust Stroke
IV Closed
EV Open] EX --> IN ``` ## 4.4 Component Material Mapping (Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD MT[Engine Material Selection] --> CAST[Cast Iron Family] MT --> AL[Aluminum Alloys] MT --> ST[Steel Family] MT --> SP[Specialty Alloys] CAST --> CG[Gray Cast Iron] CAST --> CI[Nodular Ductile Iron] CAST --> CHI[Chilled Cast Iron] AL --> AL1[A356 Head and Block] AL --> AL2[A4032 Piston] AL --> AL3[A390 Hypereutectic Piston] ST --> ST1[4340 Crankshaft] ST --> ST2[4140 Con Rod] ST --> ST3[Spring Steel Valve Spring] SP --> SP1[Inconel Exhaust Valve] SP --> SP2[21 4N Stainless Intake Valve] SP --> SP3[Babbitt Bearing Lining] SP --> SP4[MLS Head Gasket] ``` ## 4.5 Cylinder Block Cross-Sectional Schematic (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TD A[Cylinder Block] --> B1[Coolant Jacket Top] A --> B2[Cylinder Bore] A --> B3[Main Bearing Saddle] A --> B4[Cam Bore OHV] A --> B5[Head Bolt Boss] A --> B6[Crankcase Cavity] A --> B7[Oil Drain Back Passage] A --> B8[Block Deck Surface] B2 --> RING[Piston Ring Contact Zone] B2 --> BORE[Honed Surface 0.2 to 0.4 micrometer Ra] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Classify internal combustion engines based on (i) ignition method, (ii) stroke cycle, and (iii) cylinder arrangement. Give one example for each category.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Ignition method:** (1 mark) - **SI (Spark Ignition):** Uses spark plug; example — Maruti Alto K10 petrol engine. - **CI (Compression Ignition):** Uses compression heat; example — Tata Nexon 1.5L Revotorq diesel. 2. **Stroke cycle:** (1 mark) - **4-stroke:** Power stroke every 2 revolutions; example — almost all modern cars. - **2-stroke:** Power stroke every 1 revolution; example — Bajaj Pulsar two-wheeler (older two-stroke scooters like TVS XL100). 3. **Cylinder arrangement:** (1 mark) - **Inline:** Honda City 1.5L i-VTEC (L4). - **V-type:** Audi A6 3.0 TFSI (V6). ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List any three engine components and state the material typically used for each. Justify the material choice in one line.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per component):** 1. **Cylinder Block — Gray Cast Iron (GG25):** (1 mark) - *Justification:* Excellent wear resistance, good damping, low cost, easy to cast in complex shapes. 2. **Piston — Aluminum-Silicon Alloy (A4032):** (1 mark) - *Justification:* Low density (2.7 g/cm³) reduces reciprocating mass; high Si content gives wear resistance and low thermal expansion. 3. **Exhaust Valve — Inconel (Ni-Cr superalloy):** (1 mark) - *Justification:* Withstands exhaust temperatures up to 850°C; resists hot corrosion from combustion products. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and functions of any **five major components** of a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke SI engine. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain why **aluminum alloys** are preferred over cast iron for modern cylinder heads. State any **three properties** that make aluminum suitable. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — 5 Major Components (7 marks, 1.4 marks each):** 1. **Cylinder Block:** (1.4 marks) - The main cast structure housing the cylinder bores, coolant jacket, and crankcase. Made of gray cast iron or aluminum alloy. Provides rigidity and acts as the mounting platform for all other components. 2. **Piston:** (1.4 marks) - Cup-shaped aluminum alloy component reciprocating in the cylinder. Sealed by piston rings. Transmits combustion force to the connecting rod via the gudgeon pin. 3. **Connecting Rod:** (1.4 marks) - Forged steel link between the piston pin and the crankshaft journal. Designed as I-beam for high strength-to-weight ratio. Subject to combined tension, compression, and buckling. 4. **Crankshaft:** (1.4 marks) - Forged steel or nodular cast iron shaft with main journals, crankpins, and counterweights. Converts reciprocating motion to rotation. Determines firing order. 5. **Cylinder Head:** (1.4 marks) - Aluminum alloy casting bolted to the block; houses valves, ports, spark plug holes, and forms the upper half of the combustion chamber. Often incorporates integrated intake/exhaust manifolds. **Part (b) — Why Aluminum for Cylinder Heads? (7 marks):** | Property | Aluminum Alloy | Cast Iron | Advantage | |---|---|---|---| | Density | **2.7 g/cm³** | 7.2 g/cm³ | 60% lighter (3 marks) | | Thermal Conductivity | **150 W/m·K** | 46 W/m·K | Better heat dissipation; reduces knock tendency (2 marks) | | Corrosion Resistance | Excellent (oxide layer) | Moderate | Longer head life, especially in moist climates (1 mark) | | Castability / Machinability | Excellent | Good | Complex port shapes (e.g., pent-roof) easily cast (1 mark) | > **Conclusion:** Modern engines run at higher specific outputs; lower head mass reduces reciprocating inertia; better cooling allows higher compression ratios and superior anti-knock margin. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice **(a)** Compare SI and CI engines in terms of **fuel, ignition, compression ratio, cycle, thermal efficiency, and typical materials** used for critical components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **expression for the swept volume** and the **compression ratio** of an IC engine. A single-cylinder engine has a bore of 85 mm, stroke of 100 mm, and clearance volume of 60 cm³. Calculate (i) swept volume, (ii) compression ratio, and (iii) Otto cycle efficiency (γ = 1.4). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — SI vs CI Comparison (7 marks, 1 mark per row):** | Parameter | SI Engine | CI Engine | |---|---|---| | Fuel | Gasoline (volatile) | Diesel (less volatile) | | Ignition | Spark plug | Compression auto-ignition | | Compression ratio | 8 – 12 | 14 – 22 | | Cycle | Otto / Sabathe | Diesel / Sabathe | | Thermal efficiency | 25 – 35% (brake) | 32 – 45% (brake) | | Throttle | Yes (pumping loss) | No | | Component material | Lighter (Al alloys common) | Stronger (forged steel, heavy cast iron) | **Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical (7 marks):** **Derivation (4 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.1 of these notes: $$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 L, \qquad r = 1 + \frac{L}{L_c} = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$$ **Numerical (3 marks):** (i) Swept volume: $$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.085)^2 \cdot 0.100 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.007225 \cdot 0.1 = 5.675 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3 = 567.5\ \text{cm}^3$$ (ii) Compression ratio: $$r = \frac{V_s + V_c}{V_c} = \frac{567.5 + 60}{60} = \frac{627.5}{60} = 10.46$$ (iii) Otto efficiency: $$\begin{aligned} \eta_{th,Otto} &= 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{(10.46)^{0.4}} \\ (10.46)^{0.4} &= e^{0.4 \cdot \ln 10.46} = e^{0.4 \cdot 2.347} = e^{0.939} = 2.557 \\ \eta &= 1 - \frac{1}{2.557} = 1 - 0.391 = 0.609 = 60.9\% \end{aligned}$$ ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in this Module (KTU Examiner's Notes):** > 1. **Confusing 2-stroke and 4-stroke firing interval** — Many students write "2-stroke engine fires every 360°" but forget to state "*per cylinder*." In a 4-cylinder 4-stroke, the crank rotates 720° for one full engine cycle (firing interval = 180° per cylinder). > 2. **Mixing up "cycle" and "stroke"** — A 2-stroke engine does NOT mean 2 strokes of the piston in one cycle; it means the *cycle is completed* in 2 strokes (one up, one down). > 3. **Compression ratio formula misuse** — In KTU papers, the common error is using $r = V_s / V_c$ instead of $r = (V_s + V_c) / V_c$. Lose **1 mark** easily. > 4. **Skipping units** — Examiner's strict on units. Always write $cm^3$ or $m^3$ explicitly for volume, and bar or Pa for pressure. > 5. **Material-property mismatch** — Don't write "aluminum is used because it's hard." Correct answer: "aluminum is used because of its **low density and high thermal conductivity**." Hardness is *not* the reason — hardness is actually *lower* than cast iron. > 6. **Forgetting the firing order in a multi-cylinder engine** — When asked firing order, the standard SI 4-cyl is **1-3-4-2**; 4-cyl CI is **1-3-2-4** (or **1-2-4-3**). Marks deducted for vague answers. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic 1:** > - ✅ Engine is classified by: **energy source → ignition → cycle → stroke → cylinder layout.** > - ✅ **SI** = Spark Ignition (Otto cycle, r = 8–12); **CI** = Compression Ignition (Diesel cycle, r = 14–22). > - ✅ **4-stroke** = 2 crank revolutions per cycle; **2-stroke** = 1 crank revolution per cycle. > - ✅ **Firing interval** for 4-stroke, n-cylinder engine = **720°/n**. > - ✅ Major components: **block, head, piston, rings, con-rod, crank, cam, valves, flywheel, manifolds.** > - ✅ Cylinder block — **Cast Iron (traditional) / Al Alloy (modern).** > - ✅ Piston — **Al-Si alloy** (lightweight, low expansion); con-rod — **forged steel 4340.** > - ✅ Crankshaft — **forged steel / ductile iron**; camshaft — **chilled cast iron / forged steel.** > - ✅ Exhaust valve — **Inconel / Nimonic** (heat resistant); intake valve — **stainless steel 21-4N.** > - ✅ Bearings — **steel-backing + Babbitt lining** (embeddability). > - ✅ Head gasket — **Multi-Layer Steel (MLS)**. > - ✅ Swept volume $V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} B^2 L$. > - ✅ Compression ratio $r = 1 + L/L_c = (V_s + V_c)/V_c$. > - ✅ Otto efficiency $\eta = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$; Diesel efficiency $\eta = 1 - r^{1-\gamma} \cdot (\rho^\gamma - 1)/[\gamma(\rho - 1)]$. > - ✅ **Aluminum vs Iron for heads:** density (2.7 vs 7.2 g/cm³), thermal conductivity (150 vs 46 W/m·K), and castability. > - ✅ **Firing order** 4-cyl SI: **1-3-4-2**; remember the mnemonic *"One Pointer, the third and fourth are 2B continued."*

topics.title: Prototype Testing as per Central Motor Vehicles Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Prototype Testing as per Central Motor Vehicles Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are the Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR)?

The **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989** are the principal set of statutory rules notified by the Government of India under the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988**. They prescribe the construction, equipment, maintenance, and testing standards for all motor vehicles operating on Indian roads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMVR ≠ Motor Vehicles Act.**
> - The **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** is the parent legislation.
> - The **CMVR, 1989** are the detailed rules made under that Act, specifying the actual engineering tests, dimensions, performance limits, and certifications a vehicle must satisfy before it is legally allowed to be sold or driven in India.

## 1.2 What is Prototype Testing?

**Prototype Testing** is the formal, statutory process by which a *new vehicle model* (a prototype) is subjected to a comprehensive series of laboratory and field tests to verify that it conforms to every applicable rule under the CMVR, 1989. Only after the prototype successfully passes all prescribed tests does the testing agency issue a **Type Approval Certificate**, without which the vehicle cannot be manufactured, sold, or registered in India.

> [!NOTE]
> **In KTU Examination Context:**
> Whenever a question mentions "type approval," "homologation," or "CMVR certification," the examiner is fundamentally pointing to **Prototype Testing**. These three terms are conceptually interchangeable in Indian automobile regulatory practice.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Licence Test" Equivalent

Think of prototype testing like a **driving licence test** for a new car model:

| Human Driving Licence | Vehicle Prototype Testing |
|-----------------------|---------------------------|
| You must pass a vision, sign, and road test | The prototype must pass safety, emission, and performance tests |
| Issued by the RTO after verification | Issued by ARAI/iCAT after verification |
| Without it, you cannot legally drive | Without it, the model cannot be sold or registered |

Just as a person cannot legally drive on Indian roads without a driving licence, **no manufacturer can legally sell a new vehicle model in India without a Type Approval Certificate** obtained through prototype testing.

## 1.4 Key Regulatory Bodies Involved in Prototype Testing

The testing and certification infrastructure in India is built around the following agencies, all recognized under Rule 126 of the CMVR:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 126 of CMVR** designates the following testing agencies as competent to issue type approval:
> - **ARAI** — Automotive Research Association of India, Pune
> - **iCAT** — International Centre for Automotive Technology, Manesar (Haryana)
> - **CIRT** — Central Institute of Road Transport, Pune
> - **VRDE** — Vehicle Research & Development Establishment, Ahmednagar (DRDO)
> - **GARC** — Global Automotive Research Centre, Chennai

The Government of India, through the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)**, retains the ultimate authority to recognize or withdraw recognition from any testing agency.

## 1.5 Why is Prototype Testing Required?

Prototype testing exists in the CMVR framework to ensure that every vehicle entering Indian roads satisfies four fundamental pillars of compliance:

1. **Safety** — Protection of occupants, pedestrians, and other road users.
2. **Emissions** — Compliance with prevailing Bharat Stage (BS) emission norms.
3. **Performance & Durability** — Confirmation that the vehicle can withstand Indian operating conditions (heat, dust, altitude, poor fuel quality).
4. **Anti-Theft & Identification** — Standardization of VIN (Vehicle Identification Number), tampered-proof registration plates, speed governors (for commercial vehicles), etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Origin:** The legal mandate for prototype testing arises from **Section 59 of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** read with **Rule 126 of CMVR, 1989**. Any new model of a motor vehicle must be of a type approved by the testing agency.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Vehicles for Type Approval

Rule 126 of CMVR divides motor vehicles into distinct categories for the purpose of prototype testing. Each category has its own set of applicable tests.

| Vehicle Category (CMVR Class) | Description | Examples |
|-------------------------------|-------------|----------|
| **Category L** | Two and three-wheeled vehicles | Motorcycles, scooters, mopeds, auto-rickshaws |
| **Category M** | Motor vehicles with at least 4 wheels used for passenger carriage | Cars, buses, mini-buses |
| **Category N** | Motor vehicles with at least 4 wheels used for goods carriage | Trucks, tempos, LCVs, HCVs |
| **Category T** | Agricultural and forestry tractors | Tractors, power tillers |
| **Category C** | Off-road vehicles (construction, mining) | Dumpers, bulldozers, earth-movers |
| **Category R** | Trailer / semi-trailer combinations | Tow trucks, tankers, caravans |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Questions frequently ask for the *exact category letter* of a given vehicle. Remember: **M = Motor (Passenger), N = lorry (Goods), L = Light (2/3 wheelers)**.

## 2.2 Statutory Tests Conducted on Prototypes

The CMVR-mandated prototype tests are typically grouped into four broad test families. The complete test plan for a new model is defined by the testing agency based on the vehicle class and applicable standards.

### 2.2.1 Safety-Related Tests

These tests verify the structural and active/passive safety of the prototype:

- **Frontal Impact Test** (as per AIS-008 / ECE R94) — simulates a head-on crash
- **Side Impact Test** (AIS-009 / ECE R95)
- **Offset Deformable Barrier Test** — a more severe frontal test
- **Rollover Test** (AIS-029) — primarily for buses and heavy vehicles
- **Pedestrian Safety Test** (AIS-100)
- **Seat Belt Anchorages Test** (AIS-005)
- **Brake System Test** (AIS-018) — stopping distance, fade, water recovery
- **Steering Impact Test** (AIS-011)
- **Interior Fittings Test** (AIS-007)
- **Door Latch Test** (AIS-002)

### 2.2.2 Emission Tests

These tests verify compliance with the prevailing **Bharat Stage (BS) emission norms**, currently **BS-VI (BS-6) Phase II** as of 2024.

- **Type 1 Test** — Cold-start exhaust emissions over the **MIDC (Modified Indian Driving Cycle)**
- **Type 2 Test** — CO emissions at idle (mandatory for gasoline vehicles)
- **Type 3 Test** — Crankcase emissions (mandatory for gasoline)
- **Type 4 Test** — Evaporative emissions (SHED test)
- **Type 5 Test** — Durability of emission control devices (aging cycles)
- **Type 6 Test** — OBD (On-Board Diagnostics) verification
- **Type 7 Test** — Real Driving Emissions (RDE) under BS-VI Phase 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Students are often asked to list the emission test types. The mnemonic **"C-CR-EV-DO-RD"** helps: **C**old start (Type 1), **C**rankcase (Type 3), **R**est idle CO (Type 2), **EV**aporative (Type 4), **D**urability (Type 5), **O**BD (Type 6), **R**DE (Type 7).

### 2.2.3 Performance and Durability Tests

- **Photometric Tests** — Headlamp, signal lamp, and rear lamp intensity
- **Horn Test** — Sound level (typically 93–112 dB(A) at 7 m)
- **Speedometer Calibration Test**
- **Gradeability Test** (climbing ability on inclines)
- **Maximum Speed Test**
- **Fuel Consumption Test**
- **Endurance Test** (typically 15,000–30,000 km on test tracks for cars)
- **Hood Latch and Door Latch Tests**
- **Wheel and Tyre Performance Test**
- **Rear-view Mirror Test**
- **Windscreen and Defrosting/Demisting Test**
- **Underrun Protection Test** — for trucks and buses

### 2.2.4 Administrative and Identification Tests

- **VIN Stamping Verification** — Vehicle Identification Number
- **Engine Number and Chassis Number Verification**
- **Registration Plate Visibility and Reflectivity Test**
- **Reflector Conformity Test**
- **CNG/LPG Kit Compliance** (if applicable)
- **HV Battery / Electric Powertrain Compliance** (for EVs, per AIS-156 amendments)

## 2.3 The Bharat Stage (BS) Emission Norms — Indian Context

The BS norms are India's equivalent of the European Euro norms, and they define the **maximum permissible exhaust emissions** of pollutants. The progression is:

| Norm | Implementation Year (approx.) | Equivalent Euro Norm |
|------|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| India 2000 | 2000 | Euro 2 |
| BS-II | 2001 (Delhi) / 2005 (all India) | Euro 2 |
| BS-III | 2005 (Delhi) / 2010 (all India) | Euro 3 |
| BS-IV | 2010 (Delhi) / 2017 (all India) | Euro 4 |
| BS-VI | 2020 (all India) — *skipped BS-V* | Euro 6 |
| BS-VI Phase 2 | 2023 (all India) | Euro 6d with RDE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** India **skipped BS-V entirely** and jumped directly from BS-IV to BS-VI in 2020. This required manufacturers to redesign after-treatment systems, especially adding **Diesel Particulate Filters (DPF)** and **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** systems to diesel vehicles.

## 2.4 The Type Approval Certificate (TAC)

After a prototype successfully completes all required tests, the testing agency issues a **Type Approval Certificate (TAC)**. The TAC has the following key features:

- **Issued by:** ARAI / iCAT / VRDE / CIRT / GARC
- **Validity:** Generally **4 years**, with provision for renewal
- **Coverage:** One TAC is issued per *model variant* (e.g., a single car model with multiple engine options or trim levels may need multiple TACs)
- **Contents:** Test reports, approved specifications, applicable standards, and any conditions of approval
- **Cancellation:** If the production model deviates from the approved prototype, the TAC can be cancelled

## 2.5 Single vs. Multi-Stage Type Approval

This is a **high-frequency KTU question**.

| Approval Type | Description | Typical User |
|---------------|-------------|--------------|
| **Single-Stage Type Approval** | The complete vehicle is built and tested by the **final manufacturer** | Indigenous OEM (e.g., Maruti, Tata) |
| **Multi-Stage Type Approval** | The vehicle is built in **stages** by different manufacturers (e.g., chassis maker, body builder, finisher) and each stage is type approved separately | Buses, trailers, special purpose vehicles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bus Body Building:** A chassis from Ashok Leyland (stage 1) → body built by a coach builder (stage 2) → interior fitment by a third party (stage 3). Each stage requires its own type approval under multi-stage approval.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although prototype testing is largely procedural, the following engineering formulas are critical for the *tests* that constitute prototype testing.

| Test / Quantity | Formula | Description / Standard |
|-----------------|---------|------------------------|
| Stopping Distance (Brake Test) | $d = u^2 / (2 a)$ | Distance $d$ from initial speed $u$ to stop with deceleration $a$ |
| Stopping Distance (MFDD-based) | $d = 0.1 \cdot V_b + V_b^2 / (130 \cdot (MFDD / 3.6))$ | Empirical formula used in AIS-018 (V_b in km/h, MFDD in m/s²) |
| Engine Power Correction Factor | $P_{corrected} = P_{observed} \cdot \alpha$ | Where $\alpha$ accounts for ambient temperature and pressure |
| Sound Level dB(A) Reduction | $L_2 = L_1 - 20 \log_{10}(r_2 / r_1)$ | Inverse square law for noise propagation |
| Sound Level Doubling of Distance | $\Delta L = -6$ dB per doubling of distance | Free-field assumption |
| Headlamp Lux Intensity | $E = I / d^2$ | Inverse square law for photometric tests |
| Effective Brake Force Ratio | $EBFR = F_{max} / (M \cdot g)$ | Ratio of maximum deceleration to gravity |
| BS-VI NOx Limit (Diesel Cars) | $\le 80$ mg/km | As per CMVR schedule for BS-VI passenger cars (Diesel) |
| BS-VI CO Limit (Petrol Cars) | $\le 1.0$ g/km | As per CMVR schedule for BS-VI passenger cars (Petrol) |
| Gradeability | $\sin\theta = (F_{tractive} - F_{rolling}) / (M \cdot g)$ | Maximum gradient the vehicle can climb |
| Vehicle Identification Number Digits | 17 characters (per AIS-017) | Standard VIN length in India |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Mandate:** When answering numerical problems in KTU exams, always state the **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards) number** alongside the formula. Examiners reward standard code references.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Prototype testing under CMVR is not merely a legal formality — it is the **engineering gatekeeper** that:

1. **Standardises the Indian Vehicle Fleet** — Ensures every vehicle, regardless of make, meets minimum safety and emission thresholds.
2. **Drives Engineering Innovation** — Forces manufacturers to develop safer, cleaner technology to pass tougher future norms.
3. **Protects the Indian Consumer** — Prevents unsafe imports and low-quality local manufacturing.
4. **Aligns India with Global Standards** — Facilitates export of Indian-made vehicles by demonstrating compliance with international norms (ECE / WP.29).
5. **Enables Industry Regulation** — Provides the legal basis for product recalls if a production vehicle is found to deviate from the approved prototype.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stopping Distance from AIS-018 Brake Test

The AIS-018 standard, which is referenced by the CMVR, prescribes a specific empirical formula for stopping distance. Let us derive it step by step.

### Step 1 — Define the Variables

Let:
- $V_b$ = Speed of the vehicle at the start of braking (in km/h)
- $MFDD$ = Mean Fully Developed Deceleration (in m/s²)
- $d$ = Stopping distance (in metres)

The stopping distance is composed of two parts:
- **Reaction distance** (driver's reaction time) ≈ $0.1 \cdot V_b$
- **Braking distance** (deceleration phase)

### Step 2 — Convert Speed to SI Units

$$
V_{b,ms} = \frac{V_b \times 1000}{3600} = \frac{V_b}{3.6}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Kinematic Equation for Braking Distance

From the second equation of motion, $v^2 = u^2 - 2 a s$, at full stop $v = 0$:

$$
0 = \left(\frac{V_b}{3.6}\right)^2 - 2 \cdot (MFDD) \cdot s_{brake}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Braking Distance $s_{brake}$

$$
s_{brake} = \frac{V_b^2}{(3.6)^2 \cdot 2 \cdot MFDD} = \frac{V_b^2}{12.96 \cdot MFDD}
$$

### Step 5 — Reformulate to Match AIS-018 Format

The AIS-018 standard expresses the denominator as $130 \cdot (MFDD / 3.6)$:

$$
s_{brake} = \frac{V_b^2}{130 \cdot (MFDD / 3.6)} = \frac{V_b^2 \cdot 3.6}{130 \cdot MFDD}
$$

### Step 6 — Combine with Reaction Distance

The total stopping distance becomes:

$$
d = 0.1 \cdot V_b + \frac{V_b^2}{130 \cdot (MFDD / 3.6)}
$$

### Worked Example

A car is tested at $V_b = 100$ km/h with $MFDD = 7$ m/s². Find the stopping distance.

**Step 1:** Reaction distance
$$
s_{reaction} = 0.1 \times 100 = 10 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Braking distance
$$
s_{brake} = \frac{100^2}{130 \times (7 / 3.6)} = \frac{10000}{130 \times 1.9444} = \frac{10000}{252.78} \approx 39.56 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3:** Total stopping distance
$$
d = 10 + 39.56 = 49.56 \text{ m}
$$

This value can be directly compared with the AIS-018 prescribed limits for M1 category vehicles.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Headlamp Photometric Compliance

The CMVR prescribes minimum luminous intensity values (in lux or candelas) at specific test points on a screen placed 25 m in front of the vehicle. The standard mandates certain illumination thresholds at the **headlamp cut-off line** and **hot spot**.

### Step 1 — Inverse Square Law

For a point light source, illuminance $E$ at distance $d$ from a source of intensity $I$ is:

$$
E = \frac{I}{d^2}
$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Find Required Intensity

$$
I = E \cdot d^2
$$

### Step 3 — Apply CMVR Test Distance

For headlamp testing, the standard screen distance is $d = 25$ m. The CMVR / AIS-010 typically specifies a minimum illuminance of $E_{min} = 0.7$ lux at point B50L (left beam, 50 m equivalent point).

$$
I_{min} = 0.7 \times 25^2 = 0.7 \times 625 = 437.5 \text{ candela}
$$

### Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation

If the headlamp produces less than 437.5 cd at the B50L point, it would cause glare to oncoming traffic. If it produces too much at the hot spot (typically > 32,400 cd for high beam), it fails the glare check.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Test Type vs. Applicable Vehicle Category

| Test Type | L (2/3 wheeler) | M (Passenger) | N (Goods) | T (Tractor) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|-----------|-------------|
| Crash Test (Frontal) | No | Yes (M1) | Optional | No |
| Brake Test (AIS-018) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Headlamp Test (AIS-010) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Emission Type 1 (MIDC) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes (limited) |
| Noise Test (Horn) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Tip-over Test | Yes | No | No | Yes |
| PTO Test (Power Take-off) | No | No | No | Yes |
| Rollover Test | No | Yes (M2/M3) | Optional | No |
| Underrun Protection | No | No | Yes | No |
| Side Impact | No | Yes (M1) | No | No |

---

## 3.4 Flow of Prototype Testing — Procedural Walkthrough

The following is the exact procedural sequence a new vehicle model follows to obtain type approval under CMVR Rule 126:

1. **Application Submission** — Manufacturer files an application with the testing agency (ARAI/iCAT/VRDE) along with vehicle specifications, drawings, and component data sheets.
2. **Document Review** — The agency reviews technical documentation, component supplier list, and proposed manufacturing process.
3. **Sample Vehicle Submission** — The manufacturer supplies 2–3 prototype units to the testing agency, depending on the test plan.
4. **Test Scheduling** — The agency prepares a detailed test schedule covering all applicable rules and standards.
5. **Laboratory Testing** — Emission tests (on dynamometer), photometric tests, horn tests, and other component tests are performed.
6. **Field Testing** — Vehicle undergoes road tests including brake, gradeability, durability, and noise tests on the proving ground.
7. **Crash Testing** (if applicable) — Frontal, side, or rollover crash tests are performed at the agency’s crash facility.
8. **Test Report Compilation** — The agency consolidates results into a comprehensive Test Report.
9. **Compliance Verification** — Each test result is checked against the corresponding CMVR / AIS limit.
10. **Type Approval Certificate Issuance** — If all tests pass, TAC is issued with a unique TAC number.
11. **Post-Approval Conformity of Production (CoP)** — Random production samples are tested periodically to ensure the production vehicle matches the approved prototype.
12. **TAC Renewal** — The TAC is renewed every 4 years after fresh tests on current production samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conformity of Production (CoP)** is the *continuous* verification process. The TAC is a one-time approval; CoP is the lifelong check that the production model continues to match the approved prototype.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Decision Tree — Does a Vehicle Need Type Approval?

> [!NOTE]
> This decision tree is a *high-value KTU exam answer*. Replicating such structured logic in your answer script typically earns full marks in 14-mark questions.

**Step 1:** Is the vehicle a *new model* not previously type-approved in India?
- **Yes** → Proceed to Step 2
- **No** → Use existing TAC; only CoP needed

**Step 2:** Is the manufacturer a **single-stage** OEM (i.e., building the complete vehicle)?
- **Yes** → Apply for Single-Stage Type Approval
- **No** → Apply for Multi-Stage Type Approval

**Step 3:** Identify the vehicle category (L / M / N / T / C / R).

**Step 4:** Identify the applicable emission norm (currently BS-VI Phase 2 for new approvals in 2024).

**Step 5:** Identify the *additional* AIS standards applicable to that category (e.g., AIS-008 for frontal impact on M1 vehicles).

**Step 6:** Submit prototype to the testing agency and follow the 12-step procedure outlined in Section 3.4.

**Step 7:** On issue of TAC, mark every production unit with a **VIN** and a **"CMVR Compliant"** plate (or similar regulatory marking).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]**
**Define Type Approval as applicable to motor vehicles under the Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR). Name any two testing agencies authorized to issue Type Approval Certificates in India.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Type Approval is a statutory certification granted under **Rule 126 of CMVR 1989** confirming that a new vehicle model conforms to all prescribed construction, safety, emission, and performance standards. Without a valid Type Approval Certificate, a vehicle model cannot be manufactured, sold, or registered in India.
>
> Two authorized testing agencies: **ARAI (Automotive Research Association of India, Pune)** and **iCAT (International Centre for Automotive Technology, Manesar)**.
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: VRDE, CIRT, GARC.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Category L:** Two and three-wheeled vehicles — *Example: Motorcycle*
> - **Category M:** Motor vehicles with 4+ wheels for passenger transport — *Example: Car or Bus*
> - **Category N:** Motor vehicles with 4+ wheels for goods transport — *Example: Truck*
> - **Category T:** Agricultural and forestry tractors — *Example: Tractor*
> - **Category C:** Off-road construction vehicles — *Example: Dumper*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989**, its legal origin, and its relevance to prototype testing of motor vehicles in India. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Describe the **complete procedure of prototype testing**, listing the major tests conducted on a new car model and the role of the testing agencies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Legal Origin and Hierarchy (2 marks)**
- The CMVR, 1989 are detailed rules framed under the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988**.
- The parent Act defines the broad legal framework; CMVR specifies the engineering rules, standards, and tests.
- The framework is administered by the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)**, Government of India.

**2. Scope and Purpose (2 marks)**
- The CMVR governs every aspect of vehicle design, manufacture, and operation in India.
- It covers constructional standards, safety equipment, emission limits, identification, registration, and inspection.
- The purpose is to ensure uniformity of safety, environmental, and quality standards across the Indian vehicle fleet.

**3. Relevance to Prototype Testing (3 marks)**
- **Rule 126 of CMVR** mandates that every new model of a motor vehicle must be of a *type approved* by an authorized testing agency.
- This makes prototype testing a **statutory prerequisite** before production or import.
- The TAC issued post-testing is valid for **4 years**, after which it must be renewed based on re-testing of production samples.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Application Stage (2 marks)**
- Manufacturer submits technical documentation, CAD drawings, and specifications to the testing agency.
- Multiple prototypes (typically 2–3 units) are supplied for testing.

**2. Major Tests Conducted on a New Car Model (4 marks)**

| Test Family | Specific Tests |
|-------------|----------------|
| Safety | Frontal impact, side impact, seat belt anchorages, brake system (AIS-018), steering impact |
| Emission | MIDC driving cycle (Type 1), idle CO (Type 2), evaporative (Type 4), OBD verification (Type 6), RDE (Type 7) |
| Performance | Gradeability, top speed, fuel consumption, endurance (15,000–30,000 km), photometry (AIS-010) |
| Administrative | VIN stamping, registration plate reflectivity, horn sound level, tamper-proof checks |

**3. Role of Testing Agencies (1 mark)**
- ARAI, iCAT, VRDE, CIRT, and GARC issue TACs after successful completion of tests and conduct CoP audits periodically.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** What is **Bharat Stage (BS) emission norm**? Explain the **BS-VI norms** in detail, highlighting the difference between BS-VI Phase 1 and Phase 2. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the difference between **Single-Stage** and **Multi-Stage Type Approval** with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**1. Definition of BS Norms (2 marks)**
- BS norms are Indian emission standards specifying the maximum permissible levels of pollutants (CO, HC, NOx, PM) from motor vehicles.
- They are aligned with the European Euro norms and are progressively tightened every few years.

**2. BS-VI Phase 1 (Implemented April 2020) (3 marks)**
- Equivalent to Euro 6 norms.
- Introduced advanced emission control technologies: **DPF** (Diesel Particulate Filter) for diesels, **GPF** (Gasoline Particulate Filter) for petrols.
- Reduced NOx limit for diesel cars to **80 mg/km** (from 250 mg/km under BS-IV).
- Introduced OBD Stage II.
- Required **SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction)** for larger diesel engines.

**3. BS-VI Phase 2 (Implemented April 2023) (2 marks)**
- Introduces **Real Driving Emissions (RDE)** testing — emissions measured on actual road, not just laboratory cycle.
- Mandates stricter PN (Particle Number) limits.
- Requires **OBD Stage III** for better in-use compliance monitoring.
- Uses portable emission measurement systems (PEMS) for RDE verification.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**1. Single-Stage Type Approval (3.5 marks)**
- A *single manufacturer* designs, builds, and tests the complete vehicle.
- One Type Approval Certificate covers the entire vehicle.
- **Example:** A car manufacturer like Tata Motors designs the car from scratch and gets a single TAC for the complete vehicle.

**2. Multi-Stage Type Approval (3.5 marks)**
- The vehicle is built in **distinct stages** by different manufacturers.
- Each stage requires its own TAC, and the final stage TAC confirms that all stages have been approved.
- **Example:** A bus where the chassis is built by Ashok Leyland (Stage 1 TAC), the body is fabricated by a coach builder (Stage 2 TAC), and interior fitment is done by a third party (Stage 3 TAC). The final assembled bus can only be registered after the Stage 3 TAC is issued.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Prototype Testing Questions):**
> - **Do not** confuse *Type Approval* with *Roadworthiness Certification* (Fitness Certificate under Section 56). They are entirely different — TAC is one-time for a model, Fitness is periodic for individual vehicles.
> - **Do not** write the names of testing agencies in lower case or use informal abbreviations. Use full names: *Automotive Research Association of India, Pune* — not "Arai" or "pune arai."
> - **Do not** forget to mention **Rule 126 of CMVR** explicitly. KTU examiners award marks for the specific rule reference.
> - **Do not** list emission test types (Type 1, 2, 3...) in the wrong numerical order. Memorize using the mnemonic *C-CR-EV-DO-RD*.
> - **Do not** confuse *AIS* (Automotive Industry Standards) with *BIS* (Bureau of Indian Standards). AIS is the specific auto-industry standards body that drafts the actual test procedures under CMVR; BIS is a broader Indian standards body for general products.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for "Prototype Testing as per CMVR"**

- **CMVR 1989** is the set of statutory rules under the **Motor Vehicles Act 1988**, specifying all engineering tests and standards for Indian motor vehicles.
- **Rule 126 of CMVR** is the specific rule that mandates **Type Approval** for every new vehicle model.
- **Type Approval Certificate (TAC)** is a one-time model-level approval, valid for **4 years**, with renewal based on re-testing of production samples.
- **Five authorized testing agencies:** ARAI (Pune), iCAT (Manesar), VRDE (Ahmednagar), CIRT (Pune), GARC (Chennai).
- **Six vehicle categories:** L, M, N, T, C, R.
- **Single-Stage** vs **Multi-Stage** type approval — a frequent 7-mark question.
- **BS-VI Phase 1 (2020)** introduced DPF, GPF, SCR; **BS-VI Phase 2 (2023)** introduced RDE and OBD Stage III.
- **India skipped BS-V**, jumping from BS-IV to BS-VI in 2020.
- **Test families** under CMVR: Safety, Emission, Performance & Durability, Administrative/Identification.
- **Photometry** is governed by AIS-010; **Braking** by AIS-018; **Frontal impact** by AIS-008; **Side impact** by AIS-009; **VIN** by AIS-017.
- **Conformity of Production (CoP)** is the continuous post-approval check that production vehicles match the approved prototype.
- **AIS-156** is the standard for EV battery safety, increasingly relevant in 2024-scheme questions.
- **Headlamp test** uses the inverse square law: $E = I / d^2$, with the standard test distance of 25 m.
- **Stopping distance** from AIS-018: $d = 0.1 \cdot V_b + V_b^2 / \left(130 \cdot \left(MFDD / 3.6\right)\right)$.
- **VIN** in India is **17 characters** long, as per AIS-017.
- **Multi-stage type approval** is mandatory for buses, trailers, and any vehicle built in distinct stages by different manufacturers.
- **Mnemonic for emission test types:** *C-CR-EV-DO-RD* — Type 1 Cold start, 2 Rest CO, 3 Crankcase, 4 EVaporative, 5 Durability, 6 OBD, 7 RDE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel System (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The **Fuel System** in an automobile is the integrated assembly of components responsible for **storing, filtering, metering, pressurizing, and delivering** the correct quantity of clean fuel (petrol/diesel/CNG) at the correct pressure and correct air-fuel ratio to the engine cylinders for combustion, at all operating speeds and loads.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, the fuel system is broadly classified into:
1. **Petrol (SI Engine) Fuel System** — Uses carburetor or port/direct fuel injection.
2. **Diesel (CI Engine) Fuel System** — Uses injection pump + injectors at very high pressure (200–2000 bar).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Circulatory System**
> Think of the automobile fuel system exactly like the human blood circulation system:
> - **Fuel Tank** = Heart (storage/reservoir)
> - **Fuel Pump** = Heartbeat (pumping action)
> - **Fuel Filter** = Kidneys (purification)
> - **Carburetor / Fuel Injector** = Lungs (mixing fuel with air "oxygen")
> - **Fuel Lines (Pipes)** = Veins and arteries (transportation)
> - **Engine Cylinders** = Body cells where energy is finally used.

Just as a body fails if blood supply is wrong, an engine fails if fuel is not delivered at the **right quantity, right pressure, right time, and right mixture**.

## 1.3 Key Performance Parameters (Standard Metrics)

The following numerical specifications are **KTU 2024 board-favorite values** (must be memorized):

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F):**
  - Petrol (Gasoline): **14.7 : 1** by mass
  - Diesel: **14.5 : 1** by mass
  - LPG: **15.4 : 1** by mass
  - CNG: **17.2 : 1** by mass
- **Calorific Value of Petrol:** **44 MJ/kg** (≈ 10,500 kcal/kg)
- **Calorific Value of Diesel:** **45 MJ/kg** (≈ 10,800 kcal/kg)
- **Injection Pressure (Diesel Common Rail):** **200 – 2000 bar**
- **Injection Pressure (MPFI Petrol):** **3 – 5 bar** (port) / **50 – 350 bar** (GDI)
- **Tank Capacity (typical sedan):** **40 – 65 litres**

## 1.4 Functional Requirements of a Fuel System (KTU Module 1 Syllabus Highlight)

A fuel system **must** satisfy these five conditions:

1. **Supply** the correct quantity of fuel to match engine demand.
2. Maintain a **stoichiometric or near-stoichiometric air-fuel ratio** for complete combustion.
3. Deliver fuel in a **finely atomized form** for rapid mixing with air.
4. **Purify** the fuel by removing dust, water, and contaminants.
5. Vary the **mixture strength** automatically as per load and speed conditions (Idling, Cruising, Acceleration, Full Load).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Main Components of a Typical Petrol Fuel System

The fuel path travels in the following sequence:

**Fuel Tank → Fuel Strainer (Coarse Filter) → Fuel Pump → Fuel Filter (Fine Filter) → Carburetor / Fuel Injector → Combustion Chamber**

### 2.1.1 Fuel Tank
- Made of **pressed steel sheet** (modern cars use **high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** plastic tanks for corrosion resistance and weight saving).
- Contains a **fuel gauge sender unit**, a **vent pipe** (to prevent vacuum formation), and a **drain plug** (to remove water/sediment).
- Mounted at the **rear** of the vehicle to maximize luggage space.

### 2.1.2 Fuel Pump
Two major types used in KTU syllabus:

**A. Mechanical Fuel Pump (older cars)**
- Driven by a cam on the engine camshaft.
- Uses a **diaphragm** with a return spring.
- Pressure delivered: **0.2 – 0.5 bar**.
- Flow rate: **80 – 150 litres/hour**.

**B. Electrical Fuel Pump (modern cars)**
- Submerged in the fuel tank (**in-tank pump**) for cooling and to prevent vapor lock.
- Brushless DC motor driving an impeller (turbine type).
- Pressure delivered: **3 – 5 bar** (MPFI cars).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the fuel pump is inside the tank in modern cars?**
> Because the fuel itself cools the pump motor and prevents **vapor lock** — a phenomenon where petrol vaporizes in the fuel line due to engine bay heat, creating air bubbles that stop fuel flow.

### 2.1.3 Fuel Filter
- **Pleated paper element** inside a metal/plastic canister.
- Filters particles down to **10 – 25 microns**.
- Has a **check valve** in modern filters to maintain residual pressure in the line after engine shutdown, aiding hot restarts.

### 2.1.4 Carburetor (for older / basic SI engines)
A **carburetor** is a mechanical device that mixes fuel with air in the correct proportion using the **Venturi Effect**.

### 2.1.5 Fuel Injection System (modern)
Uses electrically controlled **solenoid injectors** that spray atomized fuel into the intake port (MPFI) or directly into the cylinder (GDI / CRDI).

## 2.2 The Carburetor — Theoretical Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Principle: The Venturi Effect
A **Venturi** is a tube that narrows in the middle. By **Bernoulli's Principle**, when fluid (air) passes through the narrow throat, its **velocity increases** and its **pressure decreases**.

This pressure drop (suction) is used to **draw fuel from the float chamber** through a calibrated **main jet**.

### 2.2.2 Simple Carburetor — Operating Logic

**Operating logic in 5 structured steps:**

1. Piston moves down on **intake stroke** → atmospheric air rushes into the venturi.
2. Air velocity increases at the venturi **throat** → static pressure falls below atmospheric.
3. **Fuel from the float chamber** (which is at atmospheric pressure) is pushed by the pressure difference into the **discharge nozzle** at the throat.
4. Fuel is **atomized** (broken into fine droplets) and mixed with incoming air → forms the air-fuel charge.
5. The mixture travels past the **throttle valve** into the engine cylinder.

### 2.2.3 Main Systems Inside a Carburetor

| System | Function | KTU Marker |
|---|---|---|
| **Float Chamber** | Maintains constant fuel level below the discharge nozzle | Prevents overflow |
| **Main Metering System** | Supplies fuel at cruising/part-throttle | Uses main jet |
| **Idling System** | Supplies rich mixture at low speed / closed throttle | Uses idle jet & pilot screw |
| **Economizer System** | Allows leaner mixture at cruising | Power jet cuts in at high load |
| **Accelerating Pump** | Injects extra fuel during sudden acceleration | Mechanical plunger |
| **Choke** | Provides very rich mixture for cold starting | Butterfly valve at air entry |

## 2.3 Air-Fuel Ratio Requirements at Different Conditions

| Operating Condition | Required A/F Ratio | Mixture |
|---|---|---|
| **Idling** | **10 : 1 to 12 : 1** | Rich |
| **Cruising (Part Throttle)** | **15 : 1 to 17 : 1** | Lean |
| **Full Load (Acceleration)** | **12 : 1 to 13 : 1** | Rich (for power & cooling) |
| **Cold Starting** | **3 : 1 to 9 : 1** | Very Rich |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU FORMULAS — MEMORIZE THESE**

| Formula | Symbol Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|
