| $A/F = \dfrac{m_a}{m_f}$ | Mass of air / Mass of fuel | — |
| $\eta_{comb} = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{m_{a,stoich}}$ | Relative air-fuel ratio (lambda λ) | — |
| $\lambda = 1$ | Stoichiometric (chemically correct) | — |
| $\lambda > 1$ | Lean mixture (excess air) | — |
| $\lambda < 1$ | Rich mixture (excess fuel) | — |
| $\dot{m}_{f} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a}}{(A/F)}$ | Fuel mass flow rate | kg/s |
| $V_{disp} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} \times L \times n$ | Engine displacement | m³ |
| $Q_{heat} = m_{f} \times CV$ | Heat released by fuel | kJ |
| $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{brake}}{Q_{heat}}$ | Thermal efficiency | — |
| $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh |

> **NOTE:** In the table above, all `|` and `/` have been preserved using safe symbols to maintain markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- The fuel system design directly determines **fuel economy (km/litre)** — a KTU 2024 syllabus CO (Course Outcome) target.
- Modern **BS-VI / Euro 6** emission norms are achievable only with **electronic fuel injection** — carburetors cannot meet them.
- **CRDI (Common Rail Direct Injection)** in diesel engines is the heart of modern commercial vehicles (Trucks, Buses, Tractors).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio of a Carburetor

We will derive the theoretical air-fuel ratio delivered by a simple carburetor using the **Bernoulli's Equation** and **Continuity Equation**.

### Given Assumptions
- Air flow is **incompressible** at the venturi throat.
- Fuel discharge is **isothermal** and flow is **orifice-type** at the main nozzle.
- Both air and fuel densities are known constants.

### Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation between atmosphere and Venturi Throat

For the air stream, applying Bernoulli's equation between the inlet (point 1) and the throat (point 2):

$$P_{a} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{a} V_{1}^{2} = P_{t} + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{a} V_{2}^{2}$$

Rearranging to find the **pressure drop** $\Delta P = P_a - P_t$:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{a} \left( V_{2}^{2} - V_{1}^{2} \right)$$

### Step 2 — Apply Continuity Equation
Assuming inlet area is much larger than throat area, $V_1 \ll V_2$:

$$\Delta P \approx \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{a} V_{2}^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Fuel Flow Through the Main Discharge Nozzle
Fuel flows from the float chamber (pressure = $P_a$) into the throat (pressure = $P_t$) through an orifice of area $A_f$ and discharge coefficient $C_d$:

$$\dot{m}_{f} = C_{df} \cdot A_{f} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{f} \left( P_{a} - P_{t} \right)}$$

Substituting $\Delta P$ from Step 2:

$$\dot{m}_{f} = C_{df} \cdot A_{f} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{f} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \rho_{a} V_{2}^{2}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{f} = C_{df} \cdot A_{f} \cdot V_{2} \cdot \sqrt{\rho_{a} \rho_{f}}$$

### Step 4 — Air Mass Flow Rate at Throat
The air mass flow rate through the throat of area $A_t$ is:

$$\dot{m}_{a} = \rho_{a} \cdot A_{t} \cdot V_{2}$$

### Step 5 — The Final Air-Fuel Ratio Equation

$$\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a}}{\dot{m}_{f}} = \dfrac{\rho_{a} \cdot A_{t} \cdot V_{2}}{C_{df} \cdot A_{f} \cdot V_{2} \cdot \sqrt{\rho_{a} \rho_{f}}}$$

The $V_2$ cancels out beautifully:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{A_{t}}{C_{df} \cdot A_{f}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_{a}}{\rho_{f}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ENGINEERING INSIGHT:** This equation proves that the A/F ratio of a simple carburetor is **independent of engine speed** (since $V_2$ cancelled out). This is why **compensating devices** (economizer, power jet) are required to maintain a near-stoichiometric ratio across all speeds.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Carburetor vs. Fuel Injection

| Parameter | Carburetor | MPFI (Multi-Point Fuel Injection) | CRDI (Common Rail Diesel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mixing Method** | Venturi (mechanical suction) | Electronic solenoid injector | Solenoid/Piezo injector |
| **Control** | Mechanical / vacuum | ECU (Electronic Control Unit) | ECU with rail pressure sensor |
| **A/F Accuracy** | Low (±15%) | Very High (±1–2%) | Extremely High |
| **Cold Start** | Manual/Automatic choke | ECU enriches pulse width | ECU glow plug + enrichment |
| **Fuel Atomization** | Moderate | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Emission Compliance** | Fails BS-VI | Meets BS-VI | Meets BS-VI |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent tuning | Low maintenance | Moderate (filter changes) |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Very High |
| **Drivability** | Average | Excellent | Excellent |

## 3.3 Diesel Fuel System — Numerical Analysis

For a 4-stroke, 4-cylinder diesel engine:
- Bore $D = 0.10$ m, Stroke $L = 0.12$ m, Speed $N = 2400$ rpm
- A/F ratio = 18:1, Air density $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, Volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.85$

**Step 1:** Displacement volume per cylinder:

$$V_{d} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times D^{2} \times L = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (0.10)^{2} \times 0.12$$

$$V_{d} = 9.42 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3} \text{ per cylinder}$$

**Step 2:** Total displacement:

$$V_{total} = 4 \times V_{d} = 3.77 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}$$

**Step 3:** Air mass flow rate per second:

$$\dot{m}_{a} = \dfrac{V_{total} \times N \times \eta_{v}}{2 \times 60} \times \rho_{a}$$

The division by **2** is for 4-stroke engine (one power cycle per 2 revolutions).

$$\dot{m}_{a} = \dfrac{3.77 \times 10^{-3} \times 2400 \times 0.85}{120} \times 1.2$$

$$\dot{m}_{a} = 0.0770 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4:** Fuel mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{f} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{a}}{(A/F)} = \dfrac{0.0770}{18} = 4.28 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to **litres/hour** (diesel density = 0.85 kg/litre):

$$V_{fuel,h} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f} \times 3600}{0.85} = 18.1 \text{ litres/hour}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Fuel Flow Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete flow of fuel and control signals in a modern electronic fuel injection system — exactly as expected in KTU 2024 14-mark questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Tank1: Fuel Tank Storage] --> A2[Step2: Fuel Strainer Coarse]
    A2 --> A3[Step3: Electric Fuel Pump 3 to 5 bar]
    A3 --> A4[Step4: Fuel Filter 25 micron]
    A4 --> A5[Step5: Fuel Rail Manifold High Pressure]
    A5 --> A6[Step6: Fuel Injector ECU Driven]
    A6 --> A7[Step7: Intake Port Atomization]
    A7 --> A8[Step8: Combustion Chamber Energy Release]
    A8 --> A9[Step9: Exhaust Manifold]

    B1[Sens1: Lambda Oxygen Sensor] --> A6
    B2[Sens2: MAF Mass Air Flow] --> A6
    B3[Sens3: Crankshaft Position Sensor] --> A6

    C1[Ctrl1: ECU Engine Control Unit] --> A6
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    B3 --> C1

    A1 -.Vent Pipe.-> A0[Ext1: Atmosphere]
    A8 -.Exhaust Gas.-> A9
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Simple Carburetor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Func1: Air Entry Choke Valve] --> F2[Func2: Venturi Throat Pressure Drop]
    F2 --> F3[Func3: Float Chamber Constant Fuel Level]
    F3 --> F4[Func4: Main Jet Metering Orifice]
    F4 --> F5[Func5: Discharge Nozzle Fuel Spray]
    F5 --> F6[Func6: Throttle Valve Air Fuel Control]
    F6 --> F7[Func7: Intake Manifold Engine Cylinder]
    F2 -.Suction.-> F4
    F3 -.Atm Pressure Push.-> F4
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Diesel Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Tank1: Diesel Storage Tank] --> D2[Step2: Feed Pump 2 bar]
    D2 --> D3[Step3: Water Separator]
    D3 --> D4[Step4: Main Filter 5 micron]
    D4 --> D5[Step5: High Pressure Pump 2000 bar]
    D5 --> D6[Step6: Common Rail Accumulator]
    D6 --> D7[Step7: Solenoid Injector Cylinder 1]
    D6 --> D8[Step8: Solenoid Injector Cylinder 2]
    D6 --> D9[Step9: Solenoid Injector Cylinder 3]
    D6 --> D10[Step10: Solenoid Injector Cylinder 4]
    E1[Ctrl1: ECU] --> D5
    E1 --> D7
    E1 --> D8
    E1 --> D9
    E1 --> D10
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to read these diagrams in exam:** The dashed lines (e.g., `.->`) represent **feedback signals / suction / ventilation**, while the solid lines represent the **main fuel / signal flow path**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2023]
**Define the term "Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio" for petrol. State its value and explain why a richer mixture is required during cold starting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The *stoichiometric air-fuel ratio* is the chemically ideal, perfectly combustible ratio at which exactly **all** the fuel is burned using **all** the available oxygen, with **no leftover fuel and no leftover oxygen** in the exhaust.

**Value (1 mark):** For petrol, $\dfrac{A}{F} = 14.7 : 1$ by mass (i.e., 14.7 kg of air is required to completely burn 1 kg of petrol).

**Cold Starting Justification (1 mark):** A richer mixture (A/F ≈ 3:1 to 9:1) is required during cold starting because:
- Cold fuel does not vaporize easily → poor atomization.
- Cold cylinder walls condense the fuel droplets → only a fraction reaches combustion.
- Excess fuel compensates for these losses to ensure ignition and smooth running.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**With a neat sketch, explain the working of an in-tank electrical fuel pump used in MPFI systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Sketch (1 mark):** [Students must draw: tank, submerged DC motor, impeller, inlet strainer, outlet to fuel line, pressure regulator]

**Working (2 marks):**
- An **in-tank electric fuel pump** consists of a **brushless DC motor** coupled to a **turbine/impeller**.
- It is fully **submerged in fuel** inside the tank.
- When energized, the impeller spins and creates a **centrifugal pressure** that pushes fuel through the strainer → outlet line → fuel rail → injectors.
- Submerging in fuel **cools the motor** and **eliminates vapor lock**.
- A **pressure regulator** maintains a constant 3–5 bar pressure, and excess fuel returns to the tank.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat line diagram, explain the **working of a simple carburetor**. Discuss the role of the **float chamber** and the **main jet**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the various **compensating devices** (economizer system, accelerating pump, and choke) used in a modern down-draft carburetor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a): Simple Carburetor (7 marks)

A **simple carburetor** mixes air and fuel in the correct proportion using the **Venturi Effect**.

**Components and Operation (5 marks):**
1. **Air Entry:** Atmospheric air enters through the air cleaner and passes into the **venturi tube**, which has a constriction (throat) in the middle.
2. **Pressure Drop:** As air speeds up at the throat, by **Bernoulli's principle**, the static pressure drops below atmospheric pressure.
3. **Fuel Discharge:** This suction draws fuel from the **float chamber** (held at atmospheric pressure) through the **main jet** into the discharge nozzle located at the throat.
4. **Atomization:** The high-velocity air shears the fuel into a fine spray, which mixes with air to form a combustible mixture.
5. **Throttle Control:** The **throttle valve** (a butterfly valve) controls the quantity of mixture entering the engine cylinders.

**Role of Float Chamber (1 mark):**
- Maintains a **constant fuel level**, slightly **below** the discharge nozzle outlet, so that the suction at the throat is what controls the fuel flow (not the fuel level).
- Uses a **hollow brass float** and a **needle-and-seat valve** to act as a self-regulating supply system.

**Role of Main Jet (1 mark):**
- A **calibrated orifice** that meters the fuel flow precisely.
- Acts as the **primary restriction** determining the air-fuel ratio at cruising conditions.

#### Model Solution — Part A(b): Compensating Devices (7 marks)

| Device | Function | Working (Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Economizer System** (2 marks) | Provides a leaner mixture at cruising/part throttle for fuel economy. | At low throttle opening, the economizer jet supplies the main jet. At full throttle, the vacuum-operated valve cuts off the economizer and allows a **richer power jet** to feed fuel. |
| **Accelerating Pump** (2 marks) | Supplies an extra squirt of fuel during sudden throttle opening. | Linked mechanically to the throttle shaft. When the throttle opens rapidly, a spring-loaded plunger injects an additional fuel charge through an auxiliary nozzle, preventing hesitation/flat-spot. |
| **Choke** (2 marks) | Provides an extremely rich mixture for cold starting. | A **butterfly valve** at the carburetor air entry, operated manually or automatically (thermostatic). When closed, it blocks most of the air, forcing a very rich mixture. As the engine warms, the choke opens progressively. |
| **Idling System** (1 mark) | Supplies rich mixture at very low speeds with closed throttle. | Uses an **idle jet** and **pilot screw** that bypass the main venturi and supply fuel directly below the throttle plate. |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, explain the working of a **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)** system. List its advantages over a carburetor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)** system used in modern diesel engines. State any four differences between petrol and diesel fuel systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a): MPFI System (7 marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Tank] --> P[Electric Pump]
    P --> F[Filter]
    F --> R[Fuel Rail]
    R --> I1[Injector Cyl 1]
    R --> I2[Injector Cyl 2]
    R --> I3[Injector Cyl 3]
    R --> I4[Injector Cyl 4]
    MAF[MAF Sensor] --> ECU
    O2[Lambda Sensor] --> ECU
    ECU --> I1
    ECU --> I2
    ECU --> I3
    ECU --> I4
```

**Working (3 marks):**
- Fuel is drawn from the tank by an **electric pump**, filtered, and delivered at **3–5 bar** to a common **fuel rail**.
- Each cylinder has its own **electronic solenoid injector** at the **intake port** (just before the intake valve).
- The **ECU** receives inputs from **MAF (Mass Air Flow) sensor**, **oxygen (lambda) sensor**, **crankshaft position sensor**, and **coolant temperature sensor**.
- Based on these inputs, the ECU calculates the **exact fuel quantity** required and pulses each injector with a precise **pulse width (milliseconds)**.
- Fuel is atomized into the intake port, mixes with air, and enters the cylinder.

**Advantages over Carburetor (2 marks):**
1. Precise A/F control (±1%) → better fuel economy.
2. Lower emissions → meets BS-VI norms.
3. Better cold-start and acceleration response.
4. Self-diagnostic (OBD) capability.

#### Model Solution — Part B(b): CRDI + Differences (7 marks)

**CRDI Working (3 marks):**
- Diesel is drawn from the tank by a **feed pump**, then filtered and pressurized to **200–2000 bar** by a **high-pressure pump**.
- Pressurized fuel accumulates in a **common rail** (a high-pressure reservoir) that supplies all injectors simultaneously.
- Each **solenoid or piezo injector** is controlled by the ECU. The ECU determines **injection timing, pressure, and quantity** based on accelerator pedal, engine speed, and rail pressure sensor.
- Fuel is sprayed **directly into the combustion chamber** through 6–9 fine nozzle holes.

**Differences: Petrol vs Diesel Fuel System (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Petrol System | Diesel System |
|---|---|---|
| **Injection Pressure** | 3 – 5 bar (MPFI) | 200 – 2000 bar (CRDI) |
| **Mixture Formation** | Pre-mixed (in port or cylinder) | In-cylinder (diffusion controlled) |
| **Fuel Pump Type** | Low-pressure electric | Multi-stage, ultra-high-pressure |
| **Cold Start Aid** | Choke / extra fuel pulse | Glow plugs + enrichment |
| **Fuel Filter** | 10 – 25 micron | 2 – 5 micron (with water separator) |
| **Atomization** | Easy (fuel is volatile) | Difficult (requires high pressure) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that KTU Examiners Penalize Heavily:**
> 1. **Mixing up A/F ratio values:** Students write "14.7:1 for diesel" — WRONG. It is **14.5:1 for diesel** and **14.7:1 for petrol**. Loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by 2 in 4-stroke numericals:** Engine displacement calculations for 4-stroke engines require division by 2 (one cycle per 2 revolutions). Many students write 2400 cycles/minute instead of 1200 cycles/minute. Loss: 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing "idling" with "cruising":** Idling is **RICH** (A/F ≈ 10:1), cruising is **LEAN** (A/F ≈ 16:1). Many students reverse these. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **No sketch in carburetor question:** KTU examiners allot **1–2 marks** for the diagram alone. Skipping it means losing easy marks.
> 5. **Writing "MPFI" for diesel:** MPFI is for petrol. Diesel uses **CRDI / Direct Injection**. Loss: 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Stoichiometric A/F:** Petrol = **14.7:1**, Diesel = **14.5:1**.
- [x] **Calorific Value:** Petrol = **44 MJ/kg**, Diesel = **45 MJ/kg**.
- [x] **Venturi Effect** is the working principle of every simple carburetor.
- [x] **Float chamber** maintains constant fuel level (slightly below nozzle).
- [x] **Main jet** is the primary fuel-metering orifice.
- [x] **Compensations** required in carburetor: Economizer, Accelerating Pump, Choke, Idling System.
- [x] **Rich mixture** is needed for: Cold start, Idling, Full load.
- [x] **Lean mixture** is needed for: Cruising (part throttle).
- [x] **MPFI** uses **3–5 bar** pressure and one injector per cylinder at the port.
- [x] **GDI** sprays fuel **directly into the cylinder** at **50–350 bar**.
- [x] **CRDI** uses a **common rail** at **200–2000 bar** for diesel engines.
- [x] **In-tank electric fuel pump** is used in modern MPFI vehicles to prevent vapor lock.
- [x] **CRDI injection pressure** is roughly **400× higher** than MPFI pressure.
- [x] **Oxygen (lambda) sensor** provides closed-loop A/F feedback to the ECU.
- [x] **4-stroke engine fuel flow** numerical: divide RPM by **2** to get power cycles/minute.
- [x] **Fuel tank vent pipe** is mandatory to prevent vacuum formation and pressure build-up.
- [x] **Lambda (λ) = 1** → Stoichiometric, **λ > 1** → Lean, **λ < 1** → Rich.
- [x] **Euro 6 / BS-VI norms** mandate electronic fuel injection — carburetors are obsolete for new cars.
- [x] **Glow plugs** are diesel-specific cold-start aids, not used in petrol engines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Tank, Fuel Filter, and Types of Fuel System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fuel Tank, Fuel Filter, and Types of Fuel System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel Tank — Formal KTU Definition

A **fuel tank** is a closed, pressure-regulated storage reservoir mounted at a safe location (usually the rear or underbody) of an automobile, designed to store liquid hydrocarbon fuel (petrol/diesel/CNG), supply it to the engine at a controlled rate, prevent vapour lock, and ensure crash-safe containment. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the fuel tank is classified as a **passive fuel system component** whose design directly impacts vehicle range, NVH (Noise–Vibration–Harshness), evaporative emissions, and crashworthiness under **AIS-052 (Automotive Industry Standards)**.

### Key Subsystems inside a Fuel Tank
| Sub-component | Function |
|---|---|
| Tank shell | Primary containment (steel/aluminium/HDPE plastic) |
| Filler neck | Entry port with anti-splash flap |
| Fuel cap (sealed/screw) | Seals tank; may contain pressure/vacuum relief valve |
| Fuel sender unit | Float-type potentiometer — sends fuel-level signal to gauge |
| Fuel pump module | Submerged or in-line pump delivering fuel to engine |
| Fuel strainer (sock filter) | Coarse pre-filter at pump inlet |
| Vent/Evap hose | Routes fuel vapours to charcoal canister |
| Roll-over valve | Closes vent line during vehicle rollover |
| Drain plug | Removes water/contaminants during service |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to *sketch and label* a modern fuel tank assembly and explain the role of the **EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control)** system and the **roll-over safety valve** — both are repeated short-answer questions.

### 1.2 Fuel Filter — Formal Definition

A **fuel filter** is a serviceable or disposable inline device whose function is to **remove particulate contaminants, water, and debris** from fuel before it reaches the highly-precision components of the fuel injection system (injectors, high-pressure pump, etc.). Modern fuel filters are rated in **microns (μm)** — typically **10 μm** for gasoline and **5 μm** for diesel common-rail systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Maker Metaphor:** Imagine a fuel tank as a large water tank on top of a building. Before the water enters the household plumbing (engine), it must pass through a fine mesh filter — otherwise grit will damage the kitchen faucet (fuel injector). The fuel filter is exactly that "mesh" protecting the precision engine components downstream. Without it, the engine's life drops dramatically.

### 1.3 Fuel System — Formal Definition

A **fuel system** in an automobile is the complete assembly of components responsible for **storing, filtering, metering, delivering, and injecting** fuel into the engine cylinder in the correct proportion (stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio, AFR ≈ **14.7:1** for petrol; **λ = 1.0** for diesel at full load).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Major Categories (KTU Board Focus):**
> 1. **Petrol/Gasoline Fuel Systems** — Carburetor, MPFI, GDI
> 2. **Diesel Fuel Systems** — Inline pump, Rotary pump, **Common Rail Diesel Injection (CRDi)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Fuel Tank Engineering

### 2.1.1 Operating Principles
The fuel tank operates on three simultaneous engineering principles:

1. **Gravity-fed Head (h)** — Provides initial pressure to push fuel down to the pump.
   $$P_{head} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot g \cdot h$$
   where $\rho_{fuel} \approx 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (petrol), $h$ = height difference between fuel surface and pump.

2. **Pressurised Venting (EVAP system)** — Maintains slight positive pressure (**+10 to +30 mbar** above atmospheric) during heating to prevent vapour lock.

3. **Vacuum Relief** — Slight negative pressure (**−10 to −25 mbar**) when fuel is consumed; prevents tank wall collapse.

### 2.1.2 Material Selection Logic

| Material | Density | Cost | Corrosion Resistance | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sheet Steel (Terne-coated) | 7850 kg/m³ | Low | Medium (needs coating) | Older vehicles |
| Aluminium Alloy (5052) | 2680 kg/m³ | Medium | High | Premium cars |
| HDPE Plastic (Blow-moulded) | 950 kg/m³ | Low | Excellent | Modern cars, bikes |
| Stainless Steel | 8000 kg/m³ | High | Excellent | Racing/Defence |

### 2.1.3 Capacity & Range Calculation
$$\text{Range (km)} = \frac{V_{tank} \text{ (litres)} \times \text{Fuel Economy (km/l)}}{1}$$

> **Real-world Example:** A 45-litre tank with **15 km/l** mileage → Range = **675 km** per full tank.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Fuel Filter Engineering

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle
The filter uses a **gradient-density pleated media** (typically resin-impregnated cellulose or synthetic microfibre). Fuel flows **outside-to-inside** (in modern canister filters) trapping contaminants on the outer surface.

### 2.2.2 Pressure Drop Analysis

The most critical filter parameter is **pressure drop (ΔP)** across the element. It must remain low to prevent fuel pump starvation.

$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \cdot \mu \cdot Q \cdot L}{\pi \cdot D^4}$$

- $Q$ = Volume flow rate (m³/s)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of fuel (~0.0006 Pa·s for petrol)
- $L$ = Effective filter length
- $D$ = Equivalent hydraulic diameter of flow channels

> **KTU Engineering Utility:** Pressure drop must be **< 50 kPa** at rated flow for petrol systems and **< 30 kPa** for CRDi diesel (higher pump pressures mean even small ΔP causes rail pressure decay).

### 2.2.3 Filter Efficiency ($\eta$)

$$\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$$

- $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ = Particle count per ml before and after filter.
- Modern filters achieve **>99% efficiency at 10 μm** (Beta-100 ratio = 100).

---

## 2.3 Types of Fuel System — Detailed Taxonomy

### A. Petrol/Gasoline Fuel Systems

| Type | Abbreviation | Injection Pressure | Location | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carburetor | — | Atmospheric | Above intake manifold | Pre-1990s vehicles |
| Throttle Body Injection | TBI / SPI | **~1 bar** | Above throttle body | 1985–1995 econ cars |
| Multi-Point Fuel Injection | **MPFI** | **~3.5 bar** | At each intake port | Most modern petrol cars |
| Gasoline Direct Injection | **GDI** | **~100–350 bar** | Directly into cylinder | Modern turbocharged engines |

### B. Diesel Fuel Systems

| Type | Injection Pressure | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| Inline Injection Pump | **~600 bar** | Older diesel cars, tractors |
| Rotary (Distributor) Pump | **~1000 bar** | Early VW, Tata |
| Common Rail Direct Injection (**CRDi**) | **~1600–2000 bar** | All modern diesel cars |
| Unit Injector (EUI) | **~2000 bar** | Heavy commercial vehicles (Detroit Diesel) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trend:** Most exam questions focus on **CRDi** and **GDI** since these are the contemporary technologies used in Indian cars (Maruti Suzuki, Hyundai, Tata).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{head} = \rho g h$ | Static head pressure | Pa |
| $\text{Range} = V \times FE$ | Tank volume × mileage | km |
| $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7 : 1$ | Stoichiometric petrol ratio | — |
| $\Delta P_{filter} \le 50$ kPa | Maximum allowable drop | kPa |
| $\lambda = AFR_{actual}/AFR_{stoich}$ | Equivalence ratio (1.0 = stoich) | — |
| $V_{tank} = A_{cross} \times L$ | For cylindrical tank | Litres |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in real engineering:** A Bangalore-based KTU graduate working at Bosch India will use **ΔP filter equations** daily to size filter elements for OEM supply contracts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Range of a Vehicle with Reserve Fuel

### Problem Statement
A Maruti Suzuki Swift has a **43-litre** fuel tank. The fuel gauge "Reserve" warning lights up when only **5.5 litres** remain. The owner's manual claims a certified mileage of **22.0 km/l** (ARAI). Calculate:
1. Total usable range on a full tank.
2. Range after reserve warning.

### Given Data
- Tank Capacity, $V_t = 43$ L
- Reserve volume, $V_r = 5.5$ L
- Mileage, $FE = 22.0$ km/L

### Step 1 — Calculate Full-Tank Usable Volume
Usable volume excludes the absolute bottom (cannot be drawn by pump):
$$V_{usable} = V_t - V_{dead}$$
Assuming dead volume ≈ 1.0 L (typical sump residue):
$$V_{usable} = 43 - 1.0 = 42.0 \text{ L}$$

### Step 2 — Full Tank Range
$$\text{Range}_{full} = V_{usable} \times FE$$
$$\text{Range}_{full} = 42.0 \times 22.0$$
$$\boxed{\text{Range}_{full} = 924 \text{ km}}$$

### Step 3 — Range After Reserve Warning
Volume available after reserve = $V_r - V_{dead} = 5.5 - 1.0 = 4.5$ L
$$\text{Range}_{reserve} = 4.5 \times 22.0 = \boxed{99 \text{ km}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Filter Pressure Drop in a Petrol MPFI System

### Problem Statement
A fuel filter in a Maruti Ertiga has an equivalent hydraulic diameter of **0.5 mm**, length **120 mm**, and the fuel (density 750 kg/m³, viscosity 0.0006 Pa·s) flows at **2 L/min**. Calculate the **pressure drop** using Hagen-Poiseuille flow (laminar regime).

### Step 1 — Convert Units
$$Q = 2 \text{ L/min} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 3.333 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$D = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m
$L = 120 \text{ mm} = 0.12$ m

### Step 2 — Apply Hagen-Poiseuille Equation
$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \cdot \mu \cdot Q \cdot L}{\pi \cdot D^4}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Values
$$\Delta P = \frac{128 \times (0.0006) \times (3.333 \times 10^{-5}) \times (0.12)}{\pi \times (5 \times 10^{-4})^4}$$

Numerator:
$$128 \times 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.333 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.12 = 3.072 \times 10^{-7}$$

Denominator:
$$\pi \times 625 \times 10^{-16} = 1.963 \times 10^{-13}$$

### Step 4 — Compute
$$\Delta P = \frac{3.072 \times 10^{-7}}{1.963 \times 10^{-13}}$$
$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 1.565 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \approx 1.56 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *theoretical* max for a single capillary. Real filters use multi-path pleated media, so practical ΔP is **< 50 kPa**. The derivation proves that **filter area must be very large** (via pleating) to achieve acceptable pressure drop.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board Favourite)

| Parameter | Carburetor | MPFI | GDI | CRDi (Diesel) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Metering | Mechanical (Venturi) | Electronic ECU | Electronic ECU | Electronic ECU |
| Atomisation Quality | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| Cold Start | Manual choke | ECU controlled | ECU controlled | Glow plugs |
| Fuel Economy | Baseline | +15% | +25% | +30–40% |
| Emissions | High | Low | Very Low | Low (NOx higher) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | High | High |
| Power Density | Lowest | Higher | Highest | Highest (diesel) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Calculation for Ethanol-Blended Petrol

### Background
Modern BS-VI Indian petrol (E20) contains **20% ethanol**. For pure petrol:
$$\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18} + 12.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 8\,\text{CO}_2 + 9\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

### Step 1 — Pure Petrol AFR
By molar mass balance:
- Fuel: 114 g/mol
- Air (12.5 × 4.76) = 59.5 mol of air → $59.5 \times 29 = 1725.5$ g of air
$$AFR_{petrol} = \frac{1725.5}{114} \approx 15.13$$

> Note: Slightly different from the textbook 14.7 because octane (C₈H₁₈) is one of many hydrocarbons in petrol. Engineers use **14.7** as a working average.

### Step 2 — AFR for E20 Blend
Linear blend (assuming additive rule):
$$AFR_{E20} = 0.8 \times AFR_{petrol} + 0.2 \times AFR_{ethanol}$$

Ethanol (C₂H₅OH) stoichiometry:
$$\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 3\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
- Fuel mass: 46 g/mol
- Air: $3 \times 4.76 \times 29 = 414$ g
- $AFR_{ethanol} = 414/46 = 9.0$

$$AFR_{E20} = 0.8 \times 14.7 + 0.2 \times 9.0$$
$$AFR_{E20} = 11.76 + 1.80$$
$$\boxed{AFR_{E20} \approx 13.56}$$

> **Real-world impact:** ECU must recalibrate to ~13.56, otherwise mixture runs lean → engine knock.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Modern Fuel Tank Assembly — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Ambient Air via Vent] --> node2[Charcoal Canister EVAP]
    node2 --> node3[Tank Vapour Space]
    node3 --> node4[Fuel Sender Unit Float]
    node4 --> node5[Instrument Cluster Gauge]
    node3 --> node6[Roll Over Safety Valve]
    node6 --> node7[Filler Neck with Anti Splash Flap]
    node3 --> node8[Fuel Pump Module]
    node8 --> node9[Sock Strainer Pre Filter]
    node9 --> node10[High Pressure Pump]
    node10 --> node11[External Fuel Filter Canister]
    node11 --> node12[Engine Fuel Rail]
    node12 --> node13[Injectors]
    node13 --> node14[Engine Cylinder]
```

## 4.2 Fuel Filter Cross-Sectional Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Unfiltered Fuel Inlet] --> nodeB[Inlet End Cap]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Pleated Filter Media Outer Surface]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Inner Perforated Support Tube]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Clean Fuel Outlet]
    nodeF[Contaminant Collection] -.-> nodeC
    nodeG[Anti Drain Back Valve] --> nodeB
    nodeH[Water Separator Drain Plug] --> nodeB
```

## 4.3 MPFI Fuel System — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT[Fuel Tank] --> nodeP[In Tank Electric Pump]
    nodeP --> nodeF1[Sock Filter]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Inline Canister Filter]
    nodeF2 --> nodeR[Fuel Rail Manifold]
    nodeR --> nodeI1[Injector Cylinder 1]
    nodeR --> nodeI2[Injector Cylinder 2]
    nodeR --> nodeI3[Injector Cylinder 3]
    nodeR --> nodeI4[Injector Cylinder 4]
    nodeECU[Engine Control Unit ECU] -.-> nodeP
    nodeECU -.-> nodeI1
    nodeECU -.-> nodeI2
    nodeECU -.-> nodeI3
    nodeECU -.-> nodeI4
    nodeECU -.-> nodeR
```

## 4.4 CRDi Common Rail System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Tank] --> nodeB[Feed Pump Low Pressure]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Coarse Filter]
    nodeC --> nodeD[High Pressure Pump]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Common Rail Accumulator]
    nodeE --> nodeF1[Injector 1]
    nodeE --> nodeF2[Injector 2]
    nodeE --> nodeF3[Injector 3]
    nodeE --> nodeF4[Injector 4]
    nodeG[Rail Pressure Sensor] --> nodeE
    nodeH[ECU] -.-> nodeD
    nodeH -.-> nodeF1
    nodeH -.-> nodeF2
    nodeH -.-> nodeF3
    nodeH -.-> nodeF4
    nodeH -.-> nodeG
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List any three functions of the EVAP (Evaporative Emission Control) system in a modern fuel tank.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Vapour Capture** — Traps fuel vapours in a charcoal canister instead of releasing them to atmosphere, complying with BS-VI norms.
2. **Pressure Regulation** — Maintains tank pressure within **±25 mbar** of atmospheric, preventing both vapour lock and tank collapse.
3. **Purge Cycle** — ECU opens a purge valve during engine running, drawing stored vapours into the intake manifold for combustion (regeneration of canister).

*(Each function ≈ 1 mark. KTU 2024 marking scheme expects 3 crisp bullet points.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is a **roll-over valve** mandatory in a fuel tank? State its working principle.**

**Model Answer:**
A roll-over valve is a **safety device** that **automatically shuts off the vent line** if the vehicle tilts beyond **~45° from horizontal** (during rollover accidents). 

**Working Principle:** Inside the valve, a small **buoyant ball (typically PTFE-coated nylon)** sits in a tapered seat under gravity. In a rollover, the ball moves due to gravity into the seat, sealing the vent path. This prevents fuel spillage through the vent line, which could otherwise ignite and cause fire hazards.

*(Working principle = 2 marks, Safety reason = 1 mark.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, explain the **construction and working of a modern automotive fuel tank assembly**. Discuss the role of the **fuel sender unit** and **in-tank fuel pump module**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **constructional details and working of a pleated canister-type fuel filter**. Mention the **service intervals** and how to detect filter choking. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — (a)

**Construction of a Modern Fuel Tank:**

1. **Tank Shell** — Made of **HDPE plastic** (high-density polyethylene) by blow-moulding. Lightweight, corrosion-proof, crash-absorbent.

2. **Filler Neck** — Moulded inlet with **anti-splash flap** to prevent fuel splash-back during refuelling.

3. **Sealed Fuel Cap** — Threaded cap with **pressure-relief** (opens at +20 mbar) and **vacuum-relief** (opens at −20 mbar) valves.

4. **Fuel Sender Unit** — A **float arm** pivots around a pivot point. The arm is connected to a **variable resistor (rheostat)** of about **40–250 Ω**. As fuel level changes, the float moves, changing resistance. The current through this resistor (from gauge voltage ~12V) produces a voltage drop displayed on the dashboard gauge.

5. **In-tank Pump Module** — A **submerged brushless DC motor** drives an impeller that pushes fuel at **3.5–4.0 bar** for MPFI systems. The pump module contains:
   - Pre-filter sock (100 μm mesh)
   - Pressure regulator (returns excess fuel via return line)
   - Check valve (maintains residual pressure after engine off, enabling instant restart)

6. **Roll-over Valve** — As described in Part A.

7. **EVAP Hose & Charcoal Canister Connection** — Routes vapours to canister.

**Working Sequence (during refuelling to driving):**
- **Refuelling:** Filler cap opens, fuel flows in, vapour displaced to canister.
- **Engine Start:** ECU energises fuel pump, pressurised fuel leaves tank.
- **Driving:** Sender unit continuously updates gauge reading; EVAP system purges vapours into engine.

---

### Model Solution — (b)

**Construction of Pleated Canister Fuel Filter:**

The filter is a **cylindrical metal or polymer can** containing a **pleated filter element**. Construction:

1. **End Caps** — Aluminium crimped caps at both ends; one has inlet, other outlet.
2. **Pleated Media** — **Resin-impregnated cellulose paper** (or **synthetic microfibre** in modern filters) pleated into a zig-zag (accordion) shape. Pleating multiplies the effective filter area — a 200 mm element can have **0.4 m²** of actual area.
3. **Inner Perforated Support Tube** — Provides structural rigidity; perforated with thousands of tiny holes so clean fuel passes through.
4. **Outer Mesh Cage** — Protects pleated media from external damage.
5. **Anti-Drain-Back Valve** — Rubber flap that prevents fuel from draining back when engine is off (maintains prime).
6. **Water Separator** — In diesel filters, a hydrophilic media separates water which collects at bottom and is drained via a screw plug.

**Working Principle:**
- Unfiltered fuel enters the outer chamber, passes **outside-to-inside** through the pleated media (filtration), and exits through the perforated centre tube.
- Particles larger than filter rating (**10 μm for petrol, 5 μm for diesel CRDi**) are trapped on the outer surface.
- Clean fuel leaves through outlet to fuel rail.

**Service Intervals:**
- Petrol: every **30,000–40,000 km** or **2 years**.
- Diesel CRDi: every **15,000–20,000 km** or **1 year**.

**Detection of Filter Choking:**
1. **Engine hesitation under acceleration** (fuel starvation).
2. **Pressure Drop Test** — Connect gauge to fuel rail; if pressure drops below spec (e.g., < 2.5 bar for MPFI), filter is choked.
3. **Visual Inspection** — Disconnect and blow from outlet side; if flow is restricted, replace.
4. **Mileage Drop** — Drop in fuel economy indicates excessive pumping work.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Module Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **MPFI** and **GDI** fuel systems in detail with neat sketches. Mention at least **six points of comparison**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)** system. State its advantages over conventional inline diesel pump. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — (a) Comparison of MPFI vs GDI

| Parameter | MPFI (Multi-Point Fuel Injection) | GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection) |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Injection Location | At intake port (before valve) | Directly into combustion chamber |
| 2. Injection Pressure | **3–4 bar** | **100–350 bar** |
| 3. Atomisation | Good (port spray) | Excellent (in-cylinder) |
| 4. Fuel Economy | Baseline | **+20–25% improvement** |
| 5. Power Output | Standard | **+10–15% more power** (charge cooling effect) |
| 6. Cold Start Emissions | Low | Higher (fuel wall-wetting, particulate) |
| 7. Cost | Lower | Higher (high-pressure pump needed) |
| 8. Carbon Build-up | Low | **Higher** (injector tip fouling) |
| 9. Examples | Maruti Swift (older) | Hyundai Creta 1.5 Turbo |

*(Each comparison point with brief note = 1 mark × 6 = 6 marks, sketch = 1 mark.)*

### Model Solution — (b) Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi)

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.4 CRDi architecture above)**

**Working Sequence:**

1. **Feed Stage:** In-tank electric pump draws fuel from tank through coarse pre-filter and delivers it at **~4 bar** to the high-pressure pump.

2. **High-Pressure Stage:** The **radial-piston high-pressure pump** (driven by camshaft) compresses fuel to **1600–2000 bar** and pumps it into the **common rail accumulator**.

3. **Common Rail:** Acts as a **hydraulic accumulator** maintaining constant rail pressure. A **rail pressure sensor** continuously reports pressure to ECU.

4. **ECU Control:** ECU controls **injector solenoid valves** (or piezo actuators in advanced systems). At correct crank angle, ECU energises injector, fuel sprays directly into cylinder at very high pressure.

5. **Combustion:** Atomised fuel mixes with compressed hot air → spontaneous combustion (diesel cycle).

**Advantages over Conventional Inline Pump:**
1. **Higher Pressure** — Better atomisation, more complete combustion.
2. **Independent Control** — Injection timing, quantity, and pressure can be controlled separately (in inline pump, they are coupled mechanically).
3. **Lower Noise** — Pilot + main injection reduces combustion noise (combustion knock).
4. **Better Cold Start** — ECU pre-injection possible.
5. **Lower Emissions** — Multiple injection events reduce NOx and particulate matter.
6. **Better Fuel Economy** — Up to 15% improvement due to higher pressure atomisation.

*(Block diagram = 2 marks, working steps = 3 marks, advantages = 2 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse GDI with CRDi** — they are for petrol and diesel respectively; mixing them loses 3–4 marks instantly.
> 2. **Always draw the sketch** — even rough block diagrams — KTU 2024 has a **mandatory 2-mark visual component** in every 14-mark question.
> 3. **Mention specific pressures** (e.g., 3.5 bar MPFI, 2000 bar CRDi) — examiners reward numerical precision.
> 4. **Do not skip EVAP/rollover valve** — these are favourite 3-mark Part A topics.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **Fuel tank materials:** HDPE (modern), steel (older), aluminium (premium).
- 🔹 **Typical tank pressures:** +20 mbar (relief opens), −20 mbar (vacuum relief).
- 🔹 **Fuel filter ratings:** 10 μm (petrol), 5 μm (diesel CRDi).
- 🔹 **Filter ΔP must be < 50 kPa** for petrol, < 30 kPa for diesel.
- 🔹 **MPFI:** 3–4 bar, port injection, gasoline, ECU controlled.
- 🔹 **GDI:** 100–350 bar, direct injection, gasoline, charge cooling effect, +20% economy.
- 🔹 **CRDi:** 1600–2000 bar, common rail, diesel, pilot injection reduces noise.
- 🔹 **Carburetor:** obsolete, Venturi-based, atmospheric pressure.
- 🔹 **Stoichiometric AFR:** 14.7:1 (petrol), ~13.56:1 (E20 ethanol blend).
- 🔹 **Roll-over valve** activates at ~45° tilt to prevent fuel spillage.
- 🔹 **EVAP system** uses charcoal canister to trap HC vapours.
- 🔹 **Service intervals:** Petrol filter every 30–40k km; Diesel CRDi every 15–20k km.
- 🔹 **Fuel sender unit** is a variable resistor (40–250 Ω) coupled to a float.
- 🔹 **β-ratio of 100** means 99% filtration efficiency at rated micron size.
- 🔹 **Common Rail** = high-pressure accumulator enabling multiple injections per cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carburettor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carburettor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carburettor (Carburetor):** A mechanical device used in **Spark Ignition (SI) engines** that precisely meters, atomizes, and mixes **liquid fuel (petrol/gasoline)** with **air** in the required proportion to form a combustible air-fuel mixture suitable for combustion in the engine cylinders.

The carburettor operates on the fundamental principle of a **Venturi** — a constricted throat in the air passage where the velocity of incoming air increases, causing a corresponding drop in pressure. This pressure drop (vacuum) draws fuel from the float chamber through a calibrated **fuel discharge nozzle (main jet)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Drinking Straw Effect"**
> Imagine sipping a milkshake through a straw. When you **blow air across the top of the straw** (fast-moving air), the pressure inside the straw **drops**, and the milkshake automatically **rises up** to your mouth. The carburettor does exactly the same thing — the fast-moving air (at the venturi) creates a low-pressure zone that "sucks" fuel out of the float bowl and mixes it with air. The faster the engine sucks air, the more fuel is drawn — automatic proportioning!

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) for Petrol:** **14.7 : 1** (by mass)
- **Density of air at STP:** **1.225 kg/m³**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **101.325 kPa**
- **Calorific value of petrol:** approximately **44 MJ/kg**
- **Venturi pressure drop at cruising throttle:** typically **0.3 to 0.5 bar** below atmospheric

### 1.4 Why SI Engines Need a Carburettor

| Engine Parameter | SI Engine Requirement |
|------------------|------------------------|
| Fuel State at Combustion | Must be **vaporized** |
| Mixture Preparation | External to cylinder |
| Air-Fuel Ratio Tolerance | Wide (8:1 to 18:1) |
| Volatility | High (petrol is volatile) |
| Mixture Uniformity | Homogeneous distribution required |

> [!NOTE]
> The carburettor's job is **threefold**: (1) **Meter** the correct quantity, (2) **Atomize** fuel into fine droplets, and (3) **Mix** it homogeneously with air before it enters the combustion chamber.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Features of a Simple Carburettor

A simple carburettor consists of the following essential components:

1. **Float Chamber** — A reservoir that maintains a **constant fuel level** using a **float** and **needle valve** assembly (similar to a toilet flush tank mechanism). The fuel level is kept slightly *below* the tip of the discharge nozzle to prevent fuel leakage when the engine is off.

2. **Venturi Tube** — A converging-diverging nozzle in the air passage. As air flows through the narrow throat, its velocity increases and pressure decreases (Bernoulli's principle). This is the heart of the carburettor.

3. **Throttle Valve (Butterfly Valve)** — A disc-shaped valve mounted on a shaft, rotated by the accelerator pedal via linkage. It controls the **volume of air-fuel mixture** entering the engine, thereby controlling **engine power output**.

4. **Choke Valve** — A butterfly valve located **upstream of the venturi** (near the air inlet). It **restricts air supply** during cold starting, enriching the mixture.

5. **Main Jet / Discharge Nozzle** — A calibrated orifice that meters fuel flow into the air stream. The rate of fuel discharge is governed by the pressure difference between the float chamber and the venturi throat.

6. **Auxiliary Air Bleed / Compensating Jet** — Allows a small amount of air to mix with the main fuel stream to improve atomization and modify the mixture curve.

### 2.2 Operational Principle — The Physics Behind It

**Bernoulli's Equation (steady, incompressible, frictionless flow):**

The pressure drop at the venturi throat is what atomizes the fuel. As throttle opens → more air → higher velocity at venturi → larger pressure drop → more fuel drawn.

### 2.3 Why a Simple Carburettor Is Insufficient — The Core Drawbacks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A theoretically "simple" carburettor fails in real-world driving because the **mixture ratio is non-linear** with airflow. At low air velocities (idling), insufficient pressure drop occurs, leading to an **over-rich** mixture. At high air velocities (full throttle), the fuel flow becomes excessive, again enriching the mixture. The ideal would be a **linear relationship** between air velocity and fuel flow.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Engineering Significance |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Bernoulli's Equation | $P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$ | Pa, kg/m³, m/s | Relates pressure drop to velocity increase at venturi |
| 2 | Continuity Equation | $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$ | m², m/s | Volume flow rate conservation |
| 3 | Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) | $AFR = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | dimensionless | Must be near **14.7:1** for stoichiometric combustion |
| 4 | Fuel discharge velocity (Torricelli) | $v_f = \sqrt{\frac{2(P_{atm} - P_{venturi})}{\rho_f}}$ | m/s | Velocity of fuel exiting main jet |
| 5 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_v = \frac{\text{Actual air inducted}}{\text{Swept volume displacement}}$ | % | Typical: **75–85%** for SI engines |
| 6 | Theoretical Air for Combustion | $A_t = \frac{138}{12} \times C + \frac{32}{4} \times H - \frac{16}{32} \times O$ | kg/kg fuel | Used for AFR calculations |
| 7 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency (Otto) | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | dimensionless | Function of compression ratio $r$ and $\gamma$ |
| 8 | Carburettor Air Velocity | $v_{air} = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2gH}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$ | m/s | Empirical — used for venturi sizing |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Carburettors, though largely replaced by **Electronic Fuel Injection (EFI)** in modern vehicles, are still used in **two-wheelers, small engines, lawn mowers, and vintage automobiles**.
- Understanding the carburettor is essential for KTU students because it builds the foundation for **fuel metering, manifold design, and atomization** concepts.
- **Solex, Mikuni, and Zenith-Stromberg** are classic carburettor brands frequently referenced in automotive engineering.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Air-Fuel Ratio at the Venturi Throat

We will derive the theoretical mass flow rate of fuel from the main jet in terms of venturi pressure drop and float chamber head.

**Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation** between point 1 (upstream of venturi, at atmospheric pressure) and point 2 (venturi throat):

The total energy per unit volume of air is conserved.

**Step 2 — Apply Continuity Equation** to relate velocities at points 1 and 2:

Air flowing through the carburettor is a continuous stream; whatever enters must exit.

**Step 3 — Pressure Drop Expression**:

By subtracting the energy equations, the pressure drop at the throat is expressed in terms of the air velocity at the throat.

**Step 4 — Fuel Discharge (Torricelli's Law) at Main Jet**:

The fuel exits the main jet at a velocity governed by the pressure difference between the float chamber (atmospheric) and the venturi throat (low pressure).

**Step 5 — Mass Flow Rate of Fuel**:

Mass flow rate is the product of discharge coefficient, jet area, fuel density, and discharge velocity.

**Step 6 — Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) Computation**:

Dividing the air mass flow rate by the fuel mass flow rate yields the mixture ratio.

$$
AFR = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}} = \frac{C_{da} \cdot A_v \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_{air}}}{C_{df} \cdot A_j \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho_{fuel}}}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplification** (since the $\Delta P$ and factor of 2 cancel):

$$
AFR = \frac{C_{da}}{C_{df}} \cdot \frac{A_v}{A_j} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_{fuel}}{\rho_{air}}}
$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Insight** (Typical values):

- $C_{da} \approx 0.85$ (discharge coefficient of air at venturi)
- $C_{df} \approx 0.66$ (discharge coefficient of fuel at jet)
- $\rho_{fuel} \approx 750 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_{air} \approx 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $A_v / A_j$ is selected by the designer to give a stoichiometric ratio of 14.7:1 at design conditions.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Mixture Deviates from Stoichiometric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme often asks: *"Why does a simple carburettor fail to deliver a constant air-fuel ratio across all throttle positions?"* The following matrix answers this:

| Engine Operating State | Air Velocity | Pressure Drop (ΔP) | Resulting AFR | Mixture Quality |
|------------------------|--------------|--------------------|----------------|------------------|
| **Idle (low throttle)** | Low | Very small | **~10:1** (Rich) | Over-rich (excess fuel) |
| **Cruising (mid throttle)** | Moderate | Optimal | **~14.7:1** | Stoichiometric (ideal) |
| **Wide Open Throttle (WOT)** | High | Large | **~12:1** (Rich) | Rich for max power |
| **Sudden Acceleration** | Rapidly rising | Cannot match fuel inertia | **Transient Lean** | Mixture temporarily lean |
| **Cold Start (Choke on)** | Very low (restricted) | Mis-matched | **~3:1 to 5:1** | Very rich (required for cold starting) |

### 3.3 Compensation Methods Used in Practical Carburettors

To correct the deficiencies of the simple carburettor, additional **circuits** are incorporated:

1. **Starting Circuit (Choke)** — Provides a very rich mixture during cold start.
2. **Idling Circuit** — A small auxiliary jet operates only at near-closed throttle.
3. **Main Metering Circuit (Compensating Jet)** — Uses an air bleed to flatten the fuel flow curve.
4. **Power Enrichment Circuit (Power Jet)** — Activated at high load to enrich the mixture.
5. **Acceleration Pump Circuit** — Injects extra fuel when the throttle is opened rapidly.
6. **Economizer Circuit** — Cuts off fuel at part-throttle to improve economy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Simple Carburettor — Functional Architecture Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow of air and fuel through a simple carburettor. Node IDs are alphanumeric and labels contain only clean uppercase text (no markdown or HTML tags) to comply with Mermaid's parser.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[FRESH AIR INLET] --> A2[CHOKE VALVE PARTIALLY CLOSED FOR COLD START]
    A2 --> A3[VENTURI THROAT VELOCITY INCREASES PRESSURE DROPS]
    A3 --> A4[FUEL RISES FROM FLOAT CHAMBER VIA MAIN JET]
    A4 --> A5[AIR FUEL MIXTURE FORMS IN MIXING TUBE]
    A5 --> A6[THROTTLE VALVE CONTROLS MIXTURE VOLUME]
    A6 --> A7[INTAKE MANIFOLD DELIVERS TO CYLINDERS]

    B1[FUEL TANK] --> B2[FLOAT CHAMBER MAINTAINS CONSTANT FUEL LEVEL]
    B2 --> A4
    B2 --> B3[NEEDLE AND SEAT VALVE REGULATES FUEL INLET]

    A3 -.-> C1[BERNOULLI PRINCIPLE APPLIED]
    A6 -.-> C2[DRIVER ACCELERATOR PEDAL ACTUATION]

    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A3 fill:#ffe1e1
    style A4 fill:#fff4e1
    style A7 fill:#e1ffe1
    style B2 fill:#f0e1ff
```

### 4.2 Complete Multi-Circuit Carburettor — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AIR_PATH [AIR FLOW PATH]
        direction LR
        P1[AIR CLEANER] --> P2[CHOKE VALVE]
        P2 --> P3[VENTURI THROAT]
        P3 --> P4[THROTTLE VALVE]
        P4 --> P5[INTAKE MANIFOLD]
    end

    subgraph FUEL_PATH [FUEL METERING CIRCUITS]
        direction TB
        Q1[FLOAT CHAMBER] --> Q2[MAIN JET]
        Q2 --> Q3[COMPENSATING JET]
        Q3 --> P3
        Q1 --> Q4[IDLING JET]
        Q4 --> P3
        Q1 --> Q5[POWER JET]
        Q5 --> P3
        Q1 --> Q6[ACCELERATION PUMP]
        Q6 --> P3
    end

    P3 --> MIX[AIR FUEL MIXTURE]
    P5 --> MIX
    Q1 --> Q7[NEEDLE VALVE FUEL INLET REGULATION]

    style P3 fill:#ffe1e1
    style Q1 fill:#f0e1ff
    style MIX fill:#e1ffe1
```

### 4.3 Working Principle — Sequence Flowchart

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as DRIVER
    participant T as THROTTLE
    participant V as VENTURI
    participant J as MAIN JET
    participant E as ENGINE

    U->>T: Press Accelerator
    T->>V: Increase Air Flow
    V->>V: Air Velocity Rises Pressure Drops
    V->>J: Suction Created at Nozzle Tip
    J->>E: Fuel Atomized and Mixed with Air
    E->>U: Power Generated
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define a carburettor. Mention its primary functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A carburettor is a device used in spark ignition engines to **meter, atomize, and mix** liquid fuel (petrol) with air in the correct proportion to form a combustible air-fuel mixture for combustion.

**Primary Functions:**
1. **Metering** the correct quantity of fuel as per engine demand.
2. **Atomizing** the fuel into fine droplets for proper vaporization.
3. **Mixing** the atomized fuel homogeneously with air before delivery to the cylinders.
4. **Regulating** the air-fuel ratio across varying speeds and loads.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**What is the function of a float chamber in a carburettor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The float chamber serves as a **reservoir for liquid fuel** and uses a **float and needle valve** assembly to maintain a **constant fuel level** at a level slightly below the tip of the main discharge nozzle. This ensures:
1. **Uninterrupted fuel supply** during engine operation.
2. **Prevention of fuel overflow** when the engine is stopped.
3. **Consistent metering head** for predictable fuel discharge through the main jet.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and working of a simple carburettor**. List its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

A simple carburettor consists of:
- **Float chamber** with float and needle valve — maintains constant fuel level.
- **Venturi tube** — a constriction in the air passage.
- **Main jet (discharge nozzle)** — calibrated orifice for fuel metering.
- **Throttle valve** — butterfly valve controlling mixture volume.
- **Choke valve** — restricts air for cold start enrichment.

**Working (3 marks):**

1. The downward stroke of the piston in the cylinder creates a **suction (vacuum)** in the intake manifold.
2. Atmospheric air is drawn in through the air cleaner, past the **choke** (when used), and accelerates through the **venturi throat**.
3. The increased air velocity at the throat causes a **drop in pressure** (Bernoulli's principle).
4. This pressure drop, being lower than the float chamber pressure, causes fuel to **rise through the main jet** and **atomize** into the air stream.
5. The **throttle valve** modulates the volume of mixture entering the cylinder, controlling power output.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Cannot maintain **constant air-fuel ratio** across all speeds/loads.
- **Over-rich** at idle and full throttle.
- **Poor response** during sudden acceleration.
- **Choking** issues during cold start.
- Inability to meet **modern emission norms**.

**(b)** Describe the various **circuits used in a modern multi-circuit carburettor** with their functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Modern carburettors incorporate multiple circuits to address the deficiencies of the simple carburettor:

1. **Starting Circuit (Choke Circuit)** — A choke valve upstream of the venturi restricts air, creating a very rich mixture (AFR ~3:1 to 5:1) for cold starting.
2. **Idling Circuit** — A separate low-flow jet that operates when the throttle is nearly closed. It delivers a small quantity of fuel at low airflows.
3. **Main Metering Circuit (Compensating Circuit)** — Uses an **auxiliary air bleed** to introduce air into the main fuel stream. This **flattens the fuel flow curve**, providing an approximately stoichiometric mixture over a wider range.
4. **Power Enrichment Circuit (Power Jet)** — A second jet (mechanically or vacuum-operated) that enriches the mixture at high loads to deliver maximum power.
5. **Acceleration Pump Circuit** — A mechanically-actuated pump (linked to the throttle shaft) injects an extra shot of fuel when the throttle is opened rapidly, preventing a lean misfire.
6. **Economizer Circuit** — Cuts off fuel supply at part-throttle cruise conditions to improve fuel economy.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Module 1, Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **principle of operation of a simple carburettor** based on **Bernoulli's theorem**. Derive the relationship between air velocity and pressure drop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (3 marks):**

The carburettor operates on **Bernoulli's theorem** which states that in a steady, incompressible, frictionless flow, the sum of pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy remains constant along a streamline.

At the **venturi throat**, the cross-sectional area is minimum, so by the **continuity equation** ($A v = \text{constant}$), velocity is maximum. By Bernoulli's principle, this rise in velocity causes a corresponding **fall in pressure**.

This pressure drop is the **driving force** that draws fuel from the float chamber (at atmospheric pressure) through the main jet.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation at point 1 (upstream) and point 2 (venturi throat):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_{air} v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_{air} v_2^2$$

Step 2 — Apply continuity equation:

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

Step 3 — Rearrange for $v_1$ in terms of $v_2$:

$$v_1 = v_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

Step 4 — Substitute back into Bernoulli's equation:

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_{air} \left(v_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_{air} v_2^2$$

Step 5 — Solve for the pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho_{air} v_2^2 \left[1 - \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)^2\right]$$

Step 6 — Express venturi air velocity $v_2$:

$$v_2 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_{air} \left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]}}$$

This is the **fundamental velocity-pressure relationship** at the venturi.

**(b)** Why is a **simple carburettor not suitable** for modern automobiles? Explain the role of the **compensating jet and acceleration pump** in overcoming its limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Why a Simple Carburettor Is Unsuitable (3.5 marks):**

1. **Non-linear mixture formation** — The simple carburettor produces an over-rich mixture at both very low and very high air velocities (i.e., idling and full throttle). Only at mid-range is the mixture near stoichiometric.
2. **Inability to compensate for fuel inertia** — During sudden throttle opening, air responds almost instantly, but fuel inertia causes a transient lean condition, leading to hesitation or backfire.
3. **Poor cold-start capability** — Without auxiliary circuits, a cold engine cannot be started reliably.
4. **Emissions and fuel economy** — Simple carburettors cannot maintain the precise stoichiometric ratio demanded by modern emission norms (BS-VI/Euro 6) and fuel economy targets.
5. **Load-dependent enrichment** — A simple carburettor cannot enrich the mixture at WOT for maximum power.

**Compensating Jet (2 marks):**

The compensating jet is an **auxiliary air bleed** placed in the main fuel line. It allows a metered quantity of air to mix with the fuel *before* it reaches the main jet. This has the effect of:
- **Reducing the effective density** of the fuel-air emulsion.
- **Flattening the fuel flow curve** across a wider range of venturi pressures.
- Maintaining an **approximately constant air-fuel ratio** between idle and mid-range operation.

**Acceleration Pump (1.5 marks):**

The acceleration pump is a **mechanically-actuated plunger** linked to the throttle shaft. When the driver rapidly opens the throttle:
- The plunger injects a **calibrated extra shot of fuel** directly into the venturi.
- This overcomes the **fuel inertia lag** that would otherwise cause a lean misfire.
- It ensures **smooth and responsive** acceleration without hesitation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** a "venturi" with the entire carburettor. The venturi is **only the constricted passage**, not the device itself.
> 2. Many students incorrectly state the **stoichiometric AFR as 15:1** or **14:1** — the exact KTU-accepted value is **14.7:1** (mass basis).
> 3. When drawing a carburettor diagram, **always show the float level slightly BELOW the main jet tip** — examiners deduct marks if this is missing.
> 4. In Bernoulli-based derivations, **state all assumptions** (steady, incompressible, inviscid flow) at the start — this is a common 1-mark loss.
> 5. Distinguish between **venturi pressure drop (ΔP)** at the throat and the **suction (vacuum)** in the intake manifold — they are related but not identical.
> 6. The "choke" **restricts air, not fuel** — students often write the opposite.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Carburettor (Module 1)**
>
> - **Definition:** Mechanical device for **metering + atomizing + mixing** petrol with air in SI engines.
> - **Core Principle:** **Bernoulli's Theorem** — high velocity at venturi throat causes low pressure, which draws fuel from the float chamber.
> - **Stoichiometric AFR for petrol:** **14.7 : 1** (mass basis).
> - **Essential Components:** Float chamber, venturi, main jet, throttle valve, choke valve.
> - **Float Chamber Job:** Maintains a **constant fuel level** using float + needle valve mechanism.
> - **Choke Function:** Restricts air for **rich cold-start mixture**; must be gradually opened as engine warms.
> - **Throttle Function:** Controls **mixture volume** (hence power), actuated by the accelerator pedal.
> - **Drawbacks of Simple Carburettor:** Non-linear AFR (rich at idle and WOT, lean during transient), poor cold start, no power enrichment, no acceleration compensation.
> - **Modern Circuits to Compensate:** Starting (choke), Idling, Main metering (compensating jet), Power enrichment, Acceleration pump, Economizer.
> - **Compensating Jet:** Uses **auxiliary air bleed** to flatten the fuel flow curve.
> - **Acceleration Pump:** Mechanically injects extra fuel during sudden throttle opening to overcome **fuel inertia lag**.
> - **Venturi Pressure Drop Equation:** $\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho_{air} v_2^2 \left[1 - (A_2/A_1)^2\right]$.
> - **Real-world relevance:** Largely replaced by **EFI/MPFI** in modern cars, but still used in **two-wheelers, small engines, vintage cars**.
> - **Solex, Mikuni, Zenith** are classic carburettor brands — frequently referenced in KTU theory.
> - **Modern trend:** **Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI)** dominates; carburettors are now largely of academic and legacy interest.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple and Modern Fuel Injection System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple and Modern Fuel Injection System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Fuel Injection System (FIS)** in an internal combustion engine is a precisely metered, pressurized delivery mechanism that supplies atomized fuel directly into the intake manifold (indirect injection) or the combustion chamber (direct injection) of each cylinder, replacing the conventional carburetor's vacuum-based fuel drawing action. The system operates under the coordinated control of mechanical, hydraulic, electronic, or hybrid subsystems designed to maintain an optimum air–fuel mixture across all operating regimes (cold start, idle, part-load, wide-open throttle, and deceleration).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A fuel injection system is fundamentally a **mass-flow metering device** — not a volumetric device. Its primary engineering objective is to deliver fuel at a precisely controlled *mass* per engine cycle, regardless of variations in fuel temperature, pressure, or viscosity.

## 1.2 Two Broad Generations of Fuel Injection

| Generation | Era | Operating Principle | ECU Involvement |
|------------|-----|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Simple (Mechanical)** | 1950s–1970s | Hydraulic-mechanical metering (e.g., Bosch K-Jetronic) | None / Minimal |
| **Modern (Electronic)** | 1980s–Present | Solenoid-actuated injectors pulsed by ECU (e.g., MPFI, GDI, CRDI) | Full closed-loop control |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen Tap" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a kitchen tap (faucet). A **carburetor** is like an old tap that only works on suction — pull water by creating a vacuum, and water flows based on how hard you suck. **Simple mechanical fuel injection** is a tap with a fixed mechanical spring-loaded valve — the harder you push the lever, the more water flows, but there's no feedback. **Modern electronic fuel injection** is a *smart* tap with sensors (temperature, pressure, flow rate) connected to a microcontroller (ECU) that opens and closes the valve in *millisecond pulses* — exactly the right amount for every condition, every time. It also *senses* the resulting water quality downstream and corrects itself!

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **Stoichiometric Air–Fuel Ratio (AFR_stoich) for gasoline = 14.7 : 1**
- **Stoichiometric AFR for diesel ≈ 14.5 : 1**
- **Lambda (λ) = Actual AFR / Stoichiometric AFR** → dimensionless
- **λ = 1** → Stoichiometric (chemically balanced)
- **λ < 1** → Rich mixture (excess fuel)
- **λ > 1** → Lean mixture (excess air)
- **Calorific value of gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg**, of diesel ≈ 45.5 MJ/kg

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Simple Fuel Injection System — Architecture & Working

### Bosch K-Jetronic (CIS — Continuous Injection System) — The Classic "Simple" System

**Major Subsystems:**

1. **Fuel Supply Subsystem**
   - Electric fuel pump (roller-cell type)
   - Accumulator (pressure reservoir)
   - Fuel filter
   - Fuel reservoir (low-pressure)

2. **Fuel Metering Subsystem (The Heart of K-Jetronic)**
   - Fuel distributor with differential pressure valves
   - Air-flow sensor (mechanical flap-type) coupled to the control plunger
   - Warm-up regulator (cold-idle enrichment)
   - Idle speed auxiliary air valve

3. **Fuel Delivery Subsystem**
   - Injectors (mechanical, spring-loaded, continuously spraying)

### Working Principle of K-Jetronic (Step-by-Step Logic)

- The electric pump delivers fuel at a **constant system pressure of ~5 bar (≈ 0.5 MPa)** maintained by the system pressure regulator.
- Intake air pushes the **air-flow sensor flap**, whose deflection is mechanically transmitted to the **control plunger** in the fuel distributor.
- The greater the airflow, the higher the plunger lifts → the larger the metering slits open → the more fuel flows to the injectors.
- The injectors are **continuously open** and spray fuel at all times whenever pressure is present — hence "**Continuous Injection**".
- The **warm-up regulator** adds extra fuel during cold starts by reducing return-line pressure, creating a richer mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Limitation:** K-Jetronic is *open-loop*. It has no oxygen sensor feedback, so it cannot self-correct mixture errors caused by altitude, wear, or temperature drift. This is its biggest disadvantage compared to modern EFI.

## 2.2 Modern Fuel Injection System — Architecture & Working

### Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI / EFI) — The Industry Workhorse

**Major Subsystems:**

1. **Air Intake Subsystem**
   - Air filter → Mass Air Flow (MAF) or Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) sensor → Throttle body → Intake manifold runner

2. **Fuel Delivery Subsystem**
   - In-tank electric fuel pump
   - Fuel filter
   - Fuel rail (maintains constant rail pressure via pressure regulator)
   - 4 or 6 solenoid injectors (one per cylinder)

3. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)**
   - Microprocessor-based, reads ~10–15 sensors in real time
   - Computes **injection pulse width (T_i)** for each injector

4. **Sensors (Inputs to ECU)**
   - CKP (Crankshaft Position) — engine speed & piston position
   - CMP (Camshaft Position) — injection sequencing
   - MAF / MAP — air mass
   - TPS (Throttle Position Sensor)
   - CTS (Coolant Temperature Sensor)
   - O₂ / Lambda sensor — exhaust feedback
   - Knock sensor — detonation detection

5. **Actuators (Outputs from ECU)**
   - Injector solenoids (pulsed ON/OFF at ~1–10 ms)
   - Idle Air Control (IAC) valve
   - Fuel pump relay
   - Ignition coil (in integrated systems)

### Working Principle of MPFI

- The ECU calculates the **mass of air entering each cylinder per cycle** using MAP/MAF and RPM data.
- Using the desired **target λ** (usually 1.0 in closed loop), the ECU computes the exact **fuel mass** required.
- It converts this mass into an **injection pulse width** $T_i$ such that:

$$m_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{injector} \times T_i$$

where $\dot{m}_{injector}$ is the injector's mass flow rate at the known rail pressure.

- The ECU pulses each injector **once per engine cycle** (or twice in some batch-fire strategies).
- The O₂ sensor in the exhaust provides **closed-loop feedback**, trimming the base pulse width by ±25% via the Short Term Fuel Trim (STFT) and Long Term Fuel Trim (LTFT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequential vs Batch Injection:** In **sequential** injection, each injector fires independently timed to its cylinder's intake stroke. In **batch** mode, injectors fire in pairs (1-4 together, 2-3 together). Sequential is more efficient and is now standard in MPFI.

### Direct Injection (GDI / CRDI) — The Modern Frontier

In **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)**, the injector sprays fuel *directly* into the combustion chamber at pressures of **100–350 bar**, allowing:
- Stratified-charge operation at light load → very lean combustion (λ up to 3)
- Higher compression ratio (up to 13:1) without knock
- Improved fuel economy (~15–20%) and reduced cold-start HC emissions

In **Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)** for diesel:
- A high-pressure pump (up to **2000–2500 bar**) charges a shared "common rail."
- The ECU actuates solenoid or piezoelectric injectors with **multiple injection events** per cycle (pilot, main, post injections).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|------------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Lambda (equivalence ratio inverse) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 3 | $m_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{N_{cycles}}$ | Air mass inducted per cylinder per cycle | kg |
| 4 | $m_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$ | Required fuel mass | kg |
| 5 | $T_i = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{injector}}$ | Injection pulse width | s (or ms) |
| 6 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_b}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh |
| 7 | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{air,actual}}{m_{air,ideal}} \times 100$ | Volumetric efficiency | % |
| 8 | $\dot{V}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel}}$ | Volumetric fuel rate | m³/s |
| 9 | $P_{rail} = P_{manifold} + \Delta P_{injector}$ | Fuel rail pressure relation | Pa |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{injector} = C_d \cdot A_{orifice} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_{fuel} \Delta P}$ | Injector mass flow (Bernoulli) | kg/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Symbols:** $V_d$ = displacement volume; $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency; $\rho_{air}$ = air density (~1.2 kg/m³ at STP); $N_{cycles}$ = number of cycles per power stroke; $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (~0.6–0.9); $A_{orifice}$ = injector nozzle area; $P_b$ = brake power; $BSFC$ = brake specific fuel consumption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Modern fuel injection systems are the **enabling technology** behind every emissions regulation (Euro 6/BS-VI), every turbocharged engine, every hybrid, and every modern alternative-fuel vehicle. Without precise electronic fuel metering, three-way catalytic converters cannot function (they require λ = 1.00 ± 0.01), and gasoline direct injection would be impossible.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Air Mass Inducted Per Cylinder Per Cycle

**Given:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder gasoline engine with displacement $V_d = 1.6$ L, running at $N = 3000$ rpm, with volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 85\%$, at atmospheric conditions ($\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³).

**Step 1 — Total per-cylinder displacement volume:**

$$V_d = \dfrac{1.6 \text{ L}}{4} = 0.4 \text{ L} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Number of cycles per second per cylinder (4-stroke means 1 power stroke per 2 revolutions):**

$$N_{cycles} = \dfrac{N}{2 \times 60} = \dfrac{3000}{120} = 25 \text{ cycles/s}$$

**Step 3 — Air mass per cylinder per cycle:**

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{N_{cycles}}$$

Substituting:

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.2}{25}$$

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{4.08 \times 10^{-4}}{25}$$

$$m_{air} = 1.632 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Fuel Mass Required for Stoichiometric Operation

**Using:** $AFR_{stoich} = 14.7$ for gasoline, target $\lambda = 1.0$.

$$m_{fuel} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$$

$$m_{fuel} = \dfrac{1.632 \times 10^{-5}}{1.0 \cdot 14.7}$$

$$m_{fuel} = 1.109 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

Converting to mg:

$$m_{fuel} = 1.109 \text{ mg/cycle}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Injection Pulse Width (T_i)

**Given:** Injector rated flow $= 200$ cm³/min at 3 bar.

**Step 1 — Convert injector flow to kg/s:**

$$\dot{V}_{injector} = \dfrac{200}{60} = 3.333 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Using $\rho_{fuel} = 750$ kg/m³:

$$\dot{m}_{injector} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 750 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute pulse width:**

$$T_i = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{injector}} = \dfrac{1.109 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$T_i = 4.436 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.4436 \text{ ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Real-world MPFI pulse widths range from ~1 ms (idle) to ~15 ms (WOT). Our calculated 0.44 ms is on the low side — appropriate for a small engine at moderate load. The ECU would add an *opening time offset* (~1 ms) to account for injector mechanical lag.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: BSFC & Brake Thermal Efficiency

**Given:** Engine brake power $P_b = 80$ kW, total fuel consumption $\dot{m}_{fuel,total} = 20$ kg/h.

$$BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_b} = \dfrac{20 \text{ kg/h}}{80 \text{ kW}}$$

$$BSFC = 0.25 \text{ kg/kWh}$$

Brake thermal efficiency:

$$\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{3600}{BSFC \cdot CV_{fuel}}$$

Using $CV_{gasoline} = 44,000$ kJ/kg:

$$\eta_{bt} = \dfrac{3600}{0.25 \cdot 44000} = \dfrac{3600}{11000}$$

$$\eta_{bt} = 0.3273 = 32.73\%$$

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Simple vs Modern Comparison

| Parameter | Simple (K-Jetronic CIS) | Modern (MPFI / GDI) |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Metering Principle | Continuous hydraulic | Pulsed solenoid |
