| Control Type | Open-loop mechanical | Closed-loop electronic |
| AFR Accuracy | ±5–8% | ±1% |
| Lambda Range | 0.95–1.05 | 0.7–3.0 (GDI stratified) |
| Cold Start Enrichment | Warm-up regulator + auxiliary air | ECU maps + injector pulse width |
| Emissions Control Capability | Poor (pre-catalyst era) | Excellent (Euro 6/BS-VI ready) |
| Diagnostic Capability | None | OBD-II / OBD-III self-diagnostics |
| Adjustment for Altitude | Manual re-jetting required | Automatic via MAP/MAF correction |
| Typical Rail Pressure | 5 bar | 3.5–5 bar (MPFI) / 100–350 bar (GDI) / 2000 bar (CRDI) |
| Cost & Complexity | Low–Medium | Medium–High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Simple K-Jetronic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery + Ignition Switch] --> B[Electric Roller-Cell Fuel Pump]
    B --> C[Fuel Accumulator]
    C --> D[Fuel Filter]
    D --> E[Fuel Reservoir - Low Pressure]
    E --> F[Fuel Distributor with Control Plunger]
    G[Air Filter] --> H[Air-Flow Sensor Flap - Mechanical]
    H -->|Mechanical Linkage| F
    F --> I[Injector Bank - Continuous Spray]
    I --> J[Intake Manifold - Each Cylinder]
    J --> K[Combustion Chamber]
    K --> L[Exhaust Manifold]
    M[Warm-Up Regulator] -.->|Bypasses excess fuel| E
    N[System Pressure Regulator] -.->|Maintains 5 bar| F
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Modern MPFI System (Closed-Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sensors [Input Sensors to ECU]
        S1[CKP - Crankshaft Position]
        S2[CMP - Camshaft Position]
        S3[MAF or MAP Sensor]
        S4[TPS - Throttle Position]
        S5[CTS - Coolant Temperature]
        S6[O2 Lambda Sensor]
        S7[Knock Sensor]
    end

    ECU[ECU - Electronic Control Unit - 32 bit Microprocessor]

    subgraph Actuators [Output Actuators from ECU]
        AC1[Injector Solenoids x4]
        AC2[Fuel Pump Relay]
        AC3[IAC Valve]
        AC4[Ignition Coil Driver]
    end

    S1 --> ECU
    S2 --> ECU
    S3 --> ECU
    S4 --> ECU
    S5 --> ECU
    S6 -->|Closed-Loop Feedback| ECU
    S7 --> ECU

    ECU -->|Pulse Width Modulated Signal| AC1
    ECU --> AC2
    ECU --> AC3
    ECU --> AC4

    AC1 --> INJ[Intake Ports of 4 Cylinders]
    INJ --> ENG[Combustion]
    ENG --> EXH[Exhaust]
    EXH --> S6
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[In-Tank Electric Pump] --> LP[Low-Pressure Stage ~ 4 bar]
    LP --> HPF[High-Pressure Pump - Radial Piston]
    HPF -->|Pressure up to 2000 bar| CR[Common Rail - Shared Accumulator]
    CR --> PI[Piezoelectric Injectors - 4 or 6 units]
    PI --> CC[Combustion Chamber - Direct]

    ECUd[ECU Diesel] -->|Multiple Pilot + Main + Post Pulses| PI
    ECUd --> HPF
    ECUd -->|VGT Control| TBV[Turbocharger Variable Geometry]

    EXHd[Exhaust] --> DPF[Diesel Particulate Filter]
    DPF --> SCR[Selective Catalytic Reduction - NOx]
    SCR --> LAMBDA2[Lambda / NOx Sensor]
    LAMBDA2 -->|Feedback| ECUd
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow & Decision Logic in the ECU (Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Starts] --> B[Read All Sensor Inputs]
    B --> C{Engine Temperature?}
    C -->|Cold| D[Use Cold-Start Fuel Map - Rich]
    C -->|Warm| E{Engine Load?}
    D --> E
    E -->|Idle| F[Use Idle Lambda Target 1.0]
    E -->|Part Load| G[Use Cruise Lambda Target 1.0]
    E -->|WOT| H[Use Power Lambda Target 0.85 - Rich]
    F --> I[Compute T_i Pulse Width]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Fire Injectors at Correct Timing]
    J --> K[Read O2 Sensor Next Cycle]
    K --> L{O2 Voltage?}
    L -->|High = Lean| M[Increase T_i by +1 percent]
    L -->|Low = Rich| N[Decrease T_i by -1 percent]
    M --> B
    N --> B
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working principle of the Bosch K-Jetronic continuous injection system with a neat sketch.**

**Model Answer:**
The K-Jetronic (CIS) is a mechanically controlled, continuous fuel injection system. Fuel from an electric pump is delivered at a constant system pressure (~5 bar) maintained by the system pressure regulator. Air entering the engine lifts a flap-type airflow sensor, which is mechanically linked to a control plunger inside the fuel distributor. The plunger lift is proportional to air mass flow, and the fuel distributor meters fuel proportionally via differential pressure valves. Injectors are continuously open and spray atomized fuel into the intake ports. A warm-up regulator enriches the mixture during cold operation by reducing return-line back-pressure.

*(3 marks: 1 mark system description, 1 mark working, 1 mark warm-up enrichment)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any six sensors used in a modern MPFI system and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Sensor | Function |
|---|--------|----------|
| 1 | CKP | Crankshaft position and engine RPM |
| 2 | MAP | Manifold absolute pressure for load estimation |
| 3 | MAF | Direct air mass flow measurement |
| 4 | TPS | Driver throttle demand |
| 5 | CTS | Coolant temperature for warm-up correction |
| 6 | O₂ Lambda | Exhaust O₂ for closed-loop AFR feedback |
| 7 | Knock | Detects detonation for spark retard |

*(3 marks: 1 mark listing + 2 marks correct function pairs)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently *list* components without explaining the *signal/data flow*. Marks are lost when the link between a sensor → ECU computation → actuator response is missing. Always draw a clear signal-flow block and state the *physical quantity* each component measures or controls. Also, students often write "ECU controls injection" — you MUST specify **what variable** is controlled (pulse width, timing, pressure, sequencing).

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module 1 Choice A]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the working of a modern Multi-Point Fuel Injection (MPFI) system with the help of a block diagram. List the major sensors and actuators.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2 above for the full mermaid architecture).**

**Working — Stepwise:**
1. The driver presses the accelerator, opening the throttle plate. The TPS sends a voltage signal (typically 0–5 V) to the ECU.
2. Air rushes past the MAF sensor (or the MAP sensor measures manifold vacuum), giving the ECU the instantaneous air mass flow rate.
3. The CKP and CMP sensors provide engine speed and the angular position of each cylinder's piston, allowing the ECU to determine the exact crank-angle window for fuel injection.
4. The ECU computes target air mass per cylinder per cycle using the speed-density equation $m_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{N_{cycles}}$, corrected by CTS (cold enrichment) and barometric pressure.
5. The ECU divides air mass by $\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}$ to obtain the required fuel mass.
6. The ECU calculates the **injection pulse width** $T_i$ such that the solenoid injector delivers exactly the required fuel mass at the known rail pressure.
7. The O₂ sensor in the exhaust provides a voltage (~0.1 V lean, ~0.9 V rich); the ECU compares this to the 0.45 V reference and applies Short Term Fuel Trim (STFT) of ±1% per correction cycle to maintain λ = 1.00 ± 0.01.
8. After extended operation, the ECU adapts via Long Term Fuel Trim (LTFT) to compensate for injector aging, vacuum leaks, or air filter clogging.

**Major Sensors (any 6):** CKP, CMP, MAP/MAF, TPS, CTS, O₂, Knock.

**Major Actuators (any 3):** Injector solenoids, fuel pump relay, IAC valve, ignition coil.

*(7 marks: 2 diagram + 3 working steps + 2 components)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] With neat sketches, explain the working of a Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDI) system for diesel engines. Mention the advantages over conventional inline injection pumps.**

**Model Solution:**

In a CRDI system, fuel is pressurised once in a high-pressure pump and stored in a shared **common rail** at 1500–2500 bar. Solenoid or piezoelectric injectors are individually controlled by the ECU and can perform **multiple injection events per combustion cycle**: pilot, main, and post injections.

**Components:**
- Low-pressure transfer pump
- High-pressure radial piston pump (driven by camshaft)
- Common rail accumulator (with pressure sensor and pressure control valve)
- Piezoelectric injectors (faster response, ~0.1 ms opening)
- ECU with crankshaft and camshaft position sensors
- Turbocharger (often variable geometry)

**Working:**
1. The transfer pump lifts fuel from the tank and delivers it to the high-pressure pump at 4–7 bar.
2. The high-pressure pump compresses fuel to rail pressure (controlled by the ECU via the pressure control valve).
3. The common rail maintains a constant pressure regardless of engine speed or load.
4. At the precise crank angle determined by the ECU, the injector is energised and fuel is sprayed directly into the combustion chamber at extreme pressure, producing very fine atomisation.
5. Multiple injections (pilot injection reduces combustion noise; main injection delivers power; post injection regenerates the DPF) are precisely timed.
6. NOx and particulate sensors provide closed-loop feedback.

**Advantages over Inline/In-line Injection Pumps:**
- **Higher and more flexible injection pressure** independent of engine speed.
- **Multiple injection events** per cycle — pilot, main, post — for lower noise, lower emissions, better cold start.
- **Better atomisation** due to very high pressure → smaller droplets → more complete combustion.
- **ECU-based control** allows adaptive mapping for load, speed, temperature, altitude.
- **Lower maintenance** — no mechanical timing adjustments required.
- **Reduced BSFC** (~5–10%) and lower particulate + NOx emissions.

*(7 marks: 3 working + 2 components + 2 advantages)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 1 Choice B]` (Internal Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks] A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder gasoline engine has a total displacement of 1.6 L, runs at 3000 rpm with volumetric efficiency 85% at standard atmospheric conditions ($\rho_{air}$ = 1.2 kg/m³). Compute: (i) the air mass inducted per cylinder per cycle, (ii) the fuel mass required for stoichiometric operation, and (iii) the injector pulse width if the injector flow rate is 200 cm³/min at 3 bar rail pressure. Take fuel density = 750 kg/m³.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Air mass per cylinder per cycle:**

$$V_d \text{ per cylinder} = \dfrac{1.6}{4} = 0.4 \text{ L} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

$$N_{cycles} = \dfrac{3000}{2 \times 60} = 25 \text{ cycles/s}$$

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.85 \cdot 1.2}{25} = 1.632 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**(ii) Fuel mass (stoichiometric, λ = 1):**

$$m_{fuel} = \dfrac{1.632 \times 10^{-5}}{14.7} = 1.109 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/cycle} = 1.109 \text{ mg/cycle}$$

**(iii) Injector pulse width:**

$$\dot{V}_{injector} = \dfrac{200}{60} = 3.333 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{injector} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 750 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$T_i = \dfrac{1.109 \times 10^{-6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.436 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.444 \text{ ms}$$

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 across sub-parts)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare Simple (K-Jetronic) and Modern (MPFI) fuel injection systems with respect to: (i) metering principle, (ii) closed-loop capability, (iii) cold start enrichment method, (iv) diagnostic capability, (v) lambda range, (vi) typical rail pressure, and (vii) emissions compliance. Which is preferred for a current BS-VI compliant vehicle and why?**

**Model Solution:** (See the KTU Analytical Matrix in Section 3.5 for the structured table.)

**Conclusion:** Modern MPFI (or GDI/CRDI) is the **only acceptable choice** for BS-VI compliance because:
- Closed-loop λ control (±1%) is mandatory for three-way catalytic converter operation.
- On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II) is legally mandated.
- Cold-start HC and NOx emissions can only be reduced with precise, ECU-managed injection timing.
- K-Jetronic has no λ feedback and cannot meet BS-IV norms, let alone BS-VI.

*(7 marks: 1 per comparison point × 6 = 6 marks + 1 mark conclusion)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. Confusing **K-Jetronic (continuous)** with **L-Jetronic (electronic pulsed)** — they share the "Jetronic" family name but are completely different in principle.
> 2. Writing *"ECU controls fuel"* without specifying that the ECU controls **pulse width, timing, sequencing, and rail pressure** — each is a distinct control variable.
> 3. Forgetting the **2-revolution rule** in 4-stroke engines — power stroke occurs once every 2 crank revolutions, so $N_{cycles} = N/2$.
> 4. Using $\rho_{air} = 1.0$ kg/m³ (incorrect STP value) instead of **1.2 kg/m³** — a 20% numerical error that costs full marks on derivation problems.
> 5. Confusing **BSFC units** — KTU expects **kg/kWh**, not g/kWh or L/h.
> 6. Omitting the **O₂ sensor closed-loop feedback** description — this is a KTU-favourite 2-marker.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A fuel injection system is a *mass-flow metering* device, not a volumetric one.
- **Simple System = K-Jetronic CIS** = continuous mechanical hydraulic injection at ~5 bar, open-loop, no ECU.
- **Modern System = MPFI / GDI / CRDI** = solenoid or piezoelectric pulsed injection with full ECU closed-loop control.
- **Stoichiometric AFR** for gasoline = **14.7 : 1**; for diesel ≈ **14.5 : 1**.
- **Lambda (λ) is dimensionless**; closed-loop targets λ = 1.00 ± 0.01.
- **Injection pulse width** $T_i = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{injector}}$ is the primary ECU output.
- **Air mass per cycle** $m_{air} = \dfrac{V_d \cdot \eta_v \cdot \rho_{air}}{N_{cycles}}$ — remember the **÷2 for 4-stroke** and **per-cylinder** displacement.
- **Major sensors in MPFI:** CKP, CMP, MAP/MAF, TPS, CTS, O₂, Knock.
- **Rail pressure:** K-Jet ~5 bar; MPFI ~3.5–5 bar; GDI 100–350 bar; CRDI 1500–2500 bar.
- **GDI** enables stratified-charge lean burn (λ up to 3) and ~15–20% fuel economy gain.
- **CRDI** uses **multiple injection events** (pilot, main, post) per cycle for noise + emissions reduction.
- **Closed-loop feedback** is provided by the O₂ lambda sensor → STFT (±1% per cycle) and LTFT (long-term adaptive).
- **K-Jetronic cannot meet BS-IV or BS-VI norms** due to lack of closed-loop control and OBD capability.
- **BSFC** = brake specific fuel consumption in **kg/kWh** — typically 0.25–0.30 for gasoline engines.
- **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ = (actual air mass inducted) / (theoretical air mass at atmospheric density).
- **OBD-II diagnostic port** is mandatory for BS-VI vehicles and depends entirely on the EFI system architecture.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Emission Standards as per CMV Rules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Emission Standards as per CMV Rules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Emission Standards as per CMV (Central Motor Vehicle) Rules** refer to the legally enforceable maximum permissible limits of regulated pollutants that a motor vehicle is allowed to emit into the atmosphere, as mandated by the Government of India through the **Central Motor Vehicles Rules, 1989** (subsequently amended). The relevant rules governing emissions are primarily **Rule 115** (emission standards for vehicles), **Rule 116** (type approval procedure), and **Rule 117** (pollution under control certification).

In India, these emission limits are implemented through the **Bharat Stage (BS)** classification system, which is broadly harmonized with the European **EURO** emission standards, but adapted for Indian driving conditions, fuel quality, and vehicle population characteristics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bharat Stage (BS)** is the national emission standard set by the Government of India to regulate the output of air pollutants from internal combustion engine equipment, including motor vehicles. India has skipped BS-V and directly moved to **BS-VI** from **BS-IV**, effective 1st April 2020.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large apartment building where each flat (vehicle) has a kitchen (engine) that releases smoke (exhaust gases). If every flat puffs out thick black smoke, the entire building's air becomes unhealthy. The **RWA (Residents Welfare Association)** — analogous to the **Government of India / Ministry of Road Transport & Highways (MoRTH)** — frames rules stating: *"Each flat can release only a fixed maximum amount of smoke, and the smoke must be tested before the flat is allowed to operate."*

These rules are the **CMV Rules (Central Motor Vehicle Rules)**. The **emission standard** (like BS-VI) is the maximum smoke level allowed. The **type approval** is the certificate a new flat must obtain before being occupied, and the **PUC (Pollution Under Control)** certificate is the periodic health check that an existing flat must undergo to ensure it is still within limits.

### 1.3 Why Emission Standards Are Necessary

- **Public health protection**: Vehicular emissions contain **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, unburnt **Hydrocarbons (HC)**, **Oxides of Nitrogen (NOx)**, and **Particulate Matter (PM)** — all of which are toxic and carcinogenic.
- **Environmental protection**: CO₂ and NOx contribute to **global warming** and **acid rain**.
- **Technology forcing**: Emission norms push Original Equipment Manufacturers (OEMs) to innovate in **catalytic converters**, **electronic fuel injection (EFI)**, **exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)**, and **diesel particulate filters (DPF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Automotive Research Association of India (**ARAI**), Pune, is the principal testing and certification agency authorized by the Government of India for verifying compliance with CMV emission rules. The **International Centre for Automotive Technology (ICAT)**, Manesar, and **Vehicle Research and Development Establishment (VRDE)**, Ahmednagar, are the other designated test agencies.

### 1.4 Pollutants Regulated Under CMV Rules

| Pollutant | Chemical Symbol | Primary Source | Health Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Monoxide | **CO** | Incomplete combustion of petrol | Reduces oxygen carrying capacity of blood |
| Hydrocarbons | **HC** | Unburnt fuel | Carcinogenic, contributes to smog |
| Oxides of Nitrogen | **NOx** | High temperature combustion | Respiratory diseases, acid rain |
| Particulate Matter | **PM / PM2.5** | Diesel combustion | Lung cancer, cardiovascular issues |
| Carbon Dioxide | CO₂ | Complete combustion | Greenhouse gas, global warming |

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used

- **g/km** — grams per kilometre (for light-duty vehicles, M1 category)
- **g/kWh** — grams per kilowatt-hour (for heavy-duty engines)
- **mg/km** — milligrams per kilometre (for PM, motorcycles)
- **Number/km** — for particle number (PN) under BS-VI

> [!NOTE]
> The unit **g/km** corresponds to chassis dynamometer tests, while **g/kWh** corresponds to engine dynamometer tests on the **World Harmonized Stationary Cycle (WHSC)** or **World Harmonized Transient Cycle (WHTC)** for heavy-duty engines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Evolution of Bharat Stage Emission Norms in India

The implementation of emission standards in India followed a phased approach:

| Standard | Year of Implementation | Equivalent Euro Standard | Key Highlights |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bharat Stage I (BS-I)** | 2000 | Euro-I | Introduction of unleaded petrol, 2-way catalytic converter for petrol |
| **Bharat Stage II (BS-II)** | 2001 (4-wheelers), 2005 (2-wheelers) | Euro-II | Reduced sulphur in fuel, EGR introduced for diesels |
| **Bharat Stage III (BS-III)** | 2005 (metros), 2010 (nationwide) | Euro-III | Three-way catalytic converter, OBD-I for petrol cars |
| **Bharat Stage IV (BS-IV)** | 2010 (metros), 2017 (nationwide) | Euro-IV | OBD-II mandatory, particulate filters for diesels |
| **Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI)** | **1st April 2020** (direct leap from BS-IV) | Euro-VI | **DPF mandatory**, **SCR / LNT** for NOx, PN limit, **5 years fuel sulphur = 10 ppm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India **skipped BS-V** entirely and transitioned directly from **BS-IV to BS-VI** in April 2020. This was a historic regulatory leap made possible by a Supreme Court directive and a parallel upgrade in fuel quality (BS-VI fuel contains only 10 ppm sulphur, down from 50 ppm in BS-IV).

### 2.2 CMV Rule 115 — Emission Standards for Four-Wheeler Vehicles

**Rule 115** of the CMV Rules prescribes the emission limits that every new vehicle manufactured, imported, or sold in India must comply with. The limits are categorized by:

1. **Vehicle category** — M1 (passenger cars), N1 (light commercial), M2/M3 (buses), N2/N3 (trucks)
2. **Fuel type** — Petrol, Diesel, CNG, LPG, Hybrid, Electric
3. **Reference mass (RM)** — Mass of the vehicle in running order + 25 kg of fuel + driver (75 kg) + 100 kg for L7 category

### 2.3 BS-VI Emission Limits (Selected Examples)

For a **passenger car (M1 category, Petrol)** under BS-VI:

| Pollutant | Limit (g/km) |
|---|---|
| CO | **1.0** |
| HC + NOx (combined) | **0.10** |
| NOx | **0.06** |
| PM (mass) | **0.005** (direct injection only) |
| Particle Number (PN) | **6.0 × 10¹¹ / km** |

For a **passenger car (M1 category, Diesel)** under BS-VI:

| Pollutant | Limit (g/km) |
|---|---|
| CO | **0.50** |
| HC + NOx | **0.17** |
| NOx | **0.08** |
| PM (mass) | **0.0045** |
| Particle Number (PN) | **6.0 × 10¹¹ / km** |

For a **Heavy-Duty Diesel Engine (WHTC test, Steady state portion)**:

| Pollutant | Limit (g/kWh) |
|---|---|
| CO | **1.50** |
| HC | **0.13** |
| NOx | **0.40** |
| PM | **0.01** |
| PN | **8.0 × 10¹¹ / kWh** |

### 2.4 Test Cycles Used for Compliance

The compliance with CMV emission limits is established by running the vehicle/ engine on a standardized **test cycle** on a **chassis dynamometer** (for light vehicles) or **engine dynamometer** (for heavy-duty engines):

- **Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC)** — For 4-wheeler light-duty vehicles
- **Indian Driving Cycle (IDC)** — Earlier predecessor, replaced by MIDC
- **World Motorcycle Test Cycle (WMTC)** — For 2-wheelers
- **World Harmonized Light Vehicles Test Procedure (WLTP)** — A more rigorous test being introduced
- **World Harmonized Transient Cycle (WHTC)** and **World Harmonized Stationary Cycle (WHSC)** — For heavy-duty engines
- **World Harmonized Heavy-Duty On-Board Diagnostics (WWH-OBD)** — Real-driving monitoring

### 2.5 On-Board Diagnostics (OBD) Provisions Under BS-VI

OBD is a self-diagnostic system mandated by CMV Rules that continuously monitors the performance of major emission control components. Under BS-VI:

- **OBD-I**: Threshold limits for triggering a malfunction indicator lamp (MIL) for petrol vehicles.
- **OBD-II**: Stricter thresholds, applies to both petrol and diesel vehicles.
- **Real Driving Emissions (RDE)**: Portable Emissions Measurement Systems (PEMS) are used to measure emissions under real-world driving, supplementing the laboratory tests.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO emission index | $\text{CO} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{CO}}{\dot{m}_{fuel}} \times 1000$ | g/kg fuel | Engine bench test |
| Brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC) | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{f}}{P_{b}}$ | g/kWh | Engine efficiency |
| Specific NOx mass | $m_{NOx} = c_{NOx} \times V_{exh} \times \rho_{exh}$ | g | Emissions calculation |
| Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{it} = \dfrac{P_i \times 3600}{\dot{m}_{f} \times \text{CV}}$ | dimensionless | Engine performance |
| Air-Fuel ratio equivalence | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | dimensionless | Lambda sensor / OBD |
| Particulate mass concentration | $C_{PM} = \dfrac{m_{filter,final} - m_{filter,initial}}{V_{sampled}}$ | mg/m³ | Dilution tunnel |
| Brake specific NOx | $\text{BSNOx} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{NOx}}{P_{b}}$ | g/kWh | HD engine certification |
| SFC to BSFC conversion | $\text{BSFC} = \dfrac{SFC \times 1000}{CV}$ | g/kWh | Energy audit |

> [!NOTE]
> For the MIDC test cycle, the emission result is computed as the **mass-weighted average** over the complete cycle, not a simple arithmetic mean, since different phases have different duration and weighting factors.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CMV emission framework directly impacts:
- **Engine design**: Higher compression ratio for diesels requires precise EGR control to keep NOx within BS-VI.
- **After-treatment systems**: BS-VI mandates **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC)** + **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF)** + **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** + **Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC)** — a complete after-treatment train.
- **Fuel refining**: Refineries had to upgrade from **Hydro-desulphurization (HDS)** units producing 50 ppm sulphur fuel to deep-HDS producing <10 ppm sulphur fuel.
- **On-board monitoring**: OBD-II requirements increase the **ECU** complexity by 2–3× compared to BS-IV.
- **Vehicle resale and scrappage**: BS-VI vehicles attract lower **Green Tax** and are eligible for longer registration life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mass-Weighted Emission Calculation Over a Driving Cycle

During the MIDC (Modified Indian Driving Cycle) test, the vehicle is run on a chassis dynamometer through a defined speed-time profile. The total driven distance and total pollutant mass emitted are measured, and the **emission in g/km** is computed.

#### Step 1: Define the Speed-Time Profile

The MIDC consists of four phases (urban + extra-urban) of a defined duration $\Delta t_i$ and distance $s_i$ in each phase. The total cycle distance is:

$$D_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} s_i$$

For the standard MIDC, the **total distance covered in one complete cycle = 10.84 km**, and the **total cycle duration = 1180 s**.

#### Step 2: Define Modal (Instantaneous) Emission Rate

The modal emission rate of pollutant $j$ at any second $t$ is given by:

$$\dot{m}_{j}(t) = c_{j}(t) \times \dot{V}_{exh}(t) \times \rho_{exh}(t)$$

where $c_{j}(t)$ is the instantaneous concentration (in ppm or %), $\dot{V}_{exh}(t)$ is the exhaust volumetric flow rate, and $\rho_{exh}(t)$ is the exhaust gas density.

#### Step 3: Integrate the Modal Mass

The total mass of pollutant $j$ emitted in one complete cycle is:

$$m_{j,total} = \int_{0}^{T_{cycle}} \dot{m}_{j}(t) \, dt$$

In discrete form (bag analysis, with $N$ samples per second):

$$m_{j,total} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} c_{j,k} \cdot \dot{V}_{exh,k} \cdot \Delta t$$

#### Step 4: Compute the Specific Emission (g/km)

The **specific emission** in grams per kilometre, which is the value compared against the BS-VI limit, is:

$$E_{j} = \dfrac{m_{j,total}}{D_{total}} \quad \text{(g/km)}$$

#### Step 5: Apply to a Numerical Example

Suppose a BS-VI petrol car undergoes MIDC. Test data gives:

- Total HC mass in CVS (Constant Volume Sampling) bag: $m_{HC} = 0.085$ g
- Total NOx mass: $m_{NOx} = 0.045$ g
- Total CO mass: $m_{CO} = 0.65$ g
- Total distance: $D_{total} = 10.84$ km

Specific emissions:

$$E_{HC} = \dfrac{0.085}{10.84} = 7.84 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/km} \approx 0.008 \text{ g/km}$$

$$E_{NOx} = \dfrac{0.045}{10.84} = 4.15 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/km} \approx 0.004 \text{ g/km}$$

$$E_{CO} = \dfrac{0.65}{10.84} = 5.99 \times 10^{-2} \text{ g/km} \approx 0.060 \text{ g/km}$$

**Comparison with BS-VI limit for petrol M1**: $\text{CO limit} = 1.0 \text{ g/km}$; the measured $0.060 \text{ g/km}$ is well within the limit.

#### Step 6: Combined HC + NOx Limit Check

$$E_{HC} + E_{NOx} = 0.008 + 0.004 = 0.012 \text{ g/km}$$

**BS-VI limit for combined HC + NOx (petrol) = 0.10 g/km**. The vehicle passes by a wide margin.

### 3.2 Derivation: Brake-Specific NOx for Heavy-Duty Engines

For heavy-duty diesel engines tested on the **WHTC (World Harmonized Transient Cycle)**, the certification limit is expressed in **g/kWh**, requiring power integration.

#### Step 1: Brake Power Over the Cycle

The engine operates on a transient cycle, and the **brake work per cycle** is:

$$W_{b} = \int_{0}^{T_{cycle}} \tau_{b}(t) \cdot \omega(t) \, dt \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

#### Step 2: Total NOx Mass Per Cycle

$$m_{NOx} = \int_{0}^{T_{cycle}} \dot{m}_{NOx}(t) \, dt \quad \text{(g)}$$

#### Step 3: Brake-Specific NOx (g/kWh)

$$\text{BSNOx} = \dfrac{m_{NOx}}{W_{b}/3600} = \dfrac{m_{NOx} \times 3600}{W_{b}}$$

Converting $W_{b}$ from Joules to kWh by dividing by $3.6 \times 10^6$:

$$\text{BSNOx} = \dfrac{m_{NOx}}{W_{b} \times 10^{-3}/3.6} = \dfrac{3600 \times m_{NOx}}{W_{b} \times 10^{-3}}$$

#### Step 4: Numerical Example

Suppose:
- Total NOx mass per WHTC: $m_{NOx} = 8.5$ g
- Brake work per WHTC: $W_{b} = 76000$ kJ = $7.6 \times 10^7$ J

Then:

$$\text{BSNOx} = \dfrac{8.5 \times 3600}{76000} = \dfrac{30600}{76000} = 0.4026 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**BS-VI WHTC limit (heavy-duty diesel) = 0.46 g/kWh**. The engine marginally passes. Note: The above derivations are textbook simplifications; actual WHTC uses weighting factors for cold-start, hot-start, and final segments per ECE R49 procedure.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of BS-IV vs BS-VI Limits (Petrol M1)

| Pollutant | BS-IV Limit (g/km) | BS-VI Limit (g/km) | Reduction (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CO | 1.00 | 1.00 | 0 % |
| HC + NOx | 0.30 | 0.10 | ~66.7 % |
| NOx | 0.08 | 0.06 | 25 % |
| PM (mass) | 0.025 (GDI only) | 0.005 (GDI) | 80 % |
| PN | Not regulated | $6.0 \times 10^{11}$/km | New metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the addition of the **Particle Number (PN)** limit under BS-VI. This is a non-additive, non-mass metric that counts ultrafine particles, addressing the **non-exhaust emissions** and **nanoparticle toxicity** concerns raised by the WHO.

### 3.4 Type Approval Procedure Under CMV Rule 115 / 116

The flow of compliance from manufacturer to road is:

1. **Application** by manufacturer to the Test Agency (ARAI / ICAT / VRDE).
2. **Vehicle submission** to the test agency with technical specifications.
3. **Type approval tests** conducted (Type I, Type II, Type III, Type IV, Type V, OBD, durability).
4. **Issue of Type Approval Certificate (TAC)** valid for a defined model year.
5. **Conformity of Production (CoP)** — random tests from production line.
6. **Every new model** registration is granted only on the basis of TAC reference.

### 3.5 In-Use Compliance (PUC) Under CMV Rule 115(2)

Every vehicle on the road must carry a valid **Pollution Under Control (PUC) certificate**, issued by an authorized testing station. For petrol vehicles, the PUC limit is:

$$\text{CO} \leq 3 \% \text{ by volume (idling)}$$

$$\text{HC} \leq 2000 \text{ ppm (idling)}$$

For diesel vehicles (free acceleration test):

$$\text{Opacity (Hartridge units)} \leq 65 \text{ HU (for 4-stroke)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bharat Stage Evolution and Compliance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[GOVT OF INDIA MoRTH] --> B1[Issue CMV Rule 115]
    B1 --> C1[Define BS Standard]
    C1 --> D1[BS I 2000]
    C1 --> D2[BS II 2001 2005]
    C1 --> D3[BS III 2005 2010]
    C1 --> D4[BS IV 2010 2017]
    C1 --> D5[BS VI 2020 Direct Leap]
    D1 --> E1[Fuel Quality Upgrade]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[OEM Vehicle Design]
    F1 --> G1[Type Approval Test at ARAI ICAT VRDE]
    G1 --> H1{Compliance Status}
    H1 --> I1[Pass Type Approval Certificate]
    H1 --> I2[Fail Redesign and Retest]
    I1 --> J1[Production and Sales]
    J1 --> K1[In Use PUC Certification]
    K1 --> L1[Random RDE PEMS Check]
    L1 --> M1[BS VI Certified Vehicle]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: BS-VI Diesel After-Treatment System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Diesel Engine Exhaust Outlet] --> B2[DOC Diesel Oxidation Catalyst]
    B2 --> C2[DPF Diesel Particulate Filter]
    C2 --> D2[SCR Selective Catalytic Reduction]
    D2 --> E2[ASC Ammonia Slip Catalyst]
    E2 --> F2[Tailpipe Emission to Atmosphere]
    G2[AdBlue DEF Tank 32.5 percent Urea] --> H2[DEF Injector]
    H2 --> D2
    I2[Lambda NOx Sensor Inlet] --> J2[ECU Engine Control Unit]
    I2b[Lambda NOx Sensor Outlet] --> J2
    J2 --> H2
    J2 --> K2[EGR Valve Control]
    L2[Engine] --> K2
    K2 --> L2
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Type Approval Test Sequence Under CMV Rules

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Manufacturer Submits Prototype] --> Q1[Type I Emission Test on MIDC]
    P0 --> Q2[Type II Idle CO Test]
    P0 --> Q3[Type III Crankcase Emission]
    P0 --> Q4[Type IV Evaporative Emission]
    P0 --> Q5[Type V Durability 160000 km]
    P0 --> Q6[OBD II Verification]
    P0 --> Q7[EMC Electromagnetic Compatibility]
    P0 --> Q8[RDE Real Driving Emissions PEMS]
    Q1 --> R1[Bag Analysis CVS]
    R1 --> S1[Compare with BS VI Limit g per km]
    Q2 --> S1
    Q3 --> S1
    Q4 --> S1
    Q5 --> T1[Apply Deterioration Factor DF]
    T1 --> S1
    S1 --> U1{All Tests Pass}
    U1 --> V1[Issue Type Approval Certificate]
    U1 --> V2[Reject and Require Redesign]
    V1 --> W1[Conformity of Production CoP]
    W1 --> X1[Production and Sale Authorization]
```

### 4.4 Decision Matrix: Pollutant vs After-Treatment Technology for BS-VI

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z1[Pollutant Source] --> Z2[CO]
    Z1 --> Z3[HC]
    Z1 --> Z4[NOx]
    Z1 --> Z5[PM]
    Z2 --> Y1[DOC Oxidation Catalyst]
    Z3 --> Y1
    Z5 --> Y2[DPF Particulate Filter]
    Y1 --> Y2
    Y2 --> Y3[SCR with AdBlue]
    Y4[LNT Lean NOx Trap] --> Y3
    Z4 --> Y3
    Z4 --> Y4
    Y3 --> Y5[ASC Ammonia Slip Catalyst]
    Y5 --> Z6[Tailpipe Clean Exhaust]
    A1[EGR Exhaust Gas Recirculation] --> Z4
```

### 4.5 Logical Architecture: OBD-II Malfunction Detection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O1[Engine Sensors] --> O2[ECU Reads Live Data]
    O2 --> O3[Compare with Threshold Maps]
    O3 --> O4{Fault Detected}
    O4 --> O5[Set DTC Diagnostic Trouble Code]
    O4 --> O6[Illuminate MIL Malfunction Indicator Lamp]
    O5 --> O7[Freeze Frame Data Storage]
    O6 --> O8[Operator Diagnostic Scan]
    O8 --> O9[Service Center Repair]
    O9 --> O10[Clear DTC and Verify]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Bharat Stage (BS) emission standards. How is BS-VI different from BS-IV in terms of NOx limit for a passenger car (M1 category)?**

**Model Answer:**
Bharat Stage (BS) emission standards are the national emission norms laid down by the Government of India under **Rule 115 of the Central Motor Vehicle (CMV) Rules, 1989**, prescribing the maximum permissible levels of pollutants like CO, HC, NOx, and PM from motor vehicles. They are aligned with the European Euro norms.

**Difference for passenger car (M1, petrol):**
- BS-IV NOx limit: 0.08 g/km
- BS-VI NOx limit: 0.06 g/km
- Reduction = 25 % in NOx, and combined HC + NOx is reduced by approximately 66.7 % (from 0.30 to 0.10 g/km).
- BS-VI also introduces **Particle Number (PN) limit** and mandates **OBD-II** for both petrol and diesel.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] What is a Type Approval Certificate under CMV Rules? Which agencies are authorized to issue it in India?**

**Model Answer:**
A **Type Approval Certificate (TAC)** is an official document issued after a prototype vehicle or engine successfully passes all prescribed emission, safety, and performance tests as mandated by CMV Rules. It certifies that the particular model/type of vehicle complies with the prevailing emission and safety standards and is permitted to be manufactured and sold in India.

**Authorized test agencies in India:**
1. **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)**, Pune
2. **International Centre for Automotive Technology (ICAT)**, Manesar
3. **Vehicle Research and Development Establishment (VRDE)**, Ahmednagar (for defence and special vehicles)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the Bharat Stage VI (BS-VI) emission limits for a **diesel passenger car (M1 category)**. Compare these limits with BS-IV limits and highlight the technological challenges faced by OEMs in achieving compliance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete after-treatment system** required for a BS-VI compliant heavy-duty diesel engine. Discuss the function of DOC, DPF, SCR, and ASC in emission control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) BS-VI Limits for Diesel Passenger Car (M1)**

The emission limits applicable to a diesel passenger car (M1 category, Reference Mass up to 2610 kg) under BS-VI, tested on the MIDC chassis dynamometer, are:

| Pollutant | BS-IV Limit (g/km) | BS-VI Limit (g/km) |
|---|---|---|
| CO | 0.50 | 0.50 |
| HC + NOx | 0.30 | 0.17 |
| NOx | 0.18 | 0.08 |
| PM (mass) | 0.025 | 0.0045 |
| PN (particles/km) | Not regulated | $6.0 \times 10^{11}$ |

**Major reductions achieved in BS-VI:**
- NOx reduced by 55.5 % (from 0.18 to 0.08 g/km)
- PM reduced by 82 % (from 0.025 to 0.0045 g/km)
- HC + NOx reduced by 43.3 %
- PN limit introduced for the first time

**Technological challenges for OEMs:**
1. **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF) integration** — Filter capacity, regeneration strategy (passive vs active), and ash management pose significant engineering challenges.
2. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR)** — Requires AdBlue/DEF tank infrastructure, dosing control, and low-temperature activity optimization.
3. **Lean NOx Trap (LNT)** — Sulfur poisoning of the trap due to even trace fuel sulphur is a critical concern; requires ultra-low sulphur fuel (<10 ppm).
4. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** — High-pressure vs low-pressure EGR trade-offs for NOx vs PM balance.
5. **Engine management** — Multiple injection strategies (pilot, main, post), in-cylinder pressure sensors, and complex ECU calibrations.
6. **Cost escalation** — BS-VI vehicles cost approximately 10–15 % more than BS-IV equivalents.
7. **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** — Stricter thresholds require advanced sensor and actuator monitoring.

**(b) BS-VI Heavy-Duty Diesel After-Treatment System**

The after-treatment system is arranged in a series configuration downstream of the engine exhaust manifold. The exhaust gases flow as shown:

$$\text{Engine} \rightarrow \text{Exhaust Manifold} \rightarrow \text{Turbocharger} \rightarrow \text{DOC} \rightarrow \text{DPF} \rightarrow \text{SCR} \rightarrow \text{ASC} \rightarrow \text{Tailpipe}$$

**Function of each component:**

1. **Diesel Oxidation Catalyst (DOC):**
   - Oxidises CO to CO₂: $2\text{CO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2$
   - Oxidises HC to CO₂ and H₂O: $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + (x + y/4)\text{O}_2 \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + (y/2)\text{H}_2\text{O}$
   - Oxidises NO to NO₂, which is then used by the DPF for passive regeneration.
   - Light-off temperature ≈ 200–250 °C.

2. **Diesel Particulate Filter (DPF):**
   - Mechanically traps soot (PM) on a porous ceramic wall-flow substrate (SiC or cordierite).
   - Soot loading increases back-pressure; periodic **regeneration** is required.
   - **Passive regeneration**: Continuous oxidation of trapped soot using NO₂ (from DOC) at 300–450 °C.
   - **Active regeneration**: ECU raises exhaust temperature to 600 °C by post-injection of fuel to burn off accumulated soot.

3. **Selective Catalytic Reduction (SCR):**
   - Injects **AdBlue (32.5 % aqueous urea solution)** into the exhaust stream.
   - Urea decomposes: $\text{(NH}_2)_2\text{CO} \rightarrow \text{NH}_3 + \text{HNCO} \rightarrow \text{NH}_3 + \text{CO}_2$
   - NH₃ reduces NOx on a Cu-zeolite or Vanadium catalyst:
     - $4\text{NH}_3 + 4\text{NO} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 4\text{N}_2 + 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$
     - $8\text{NH}_3 + 6\text{NO}_2 \rightarrow 7\text{N}_2 + 12\text{H}_2\text{O}$
   - Operates effectively between 200–500 °C.

4. **Ammonia Slip Catalyst (ASC):**
   - Positioned downstream of SCR to capture any unreacted NH₃ that may escape the SCR catalyst.
   - Converts excess NH₃ to N₂ and H₂O.
   - Prevents ammonia odour and secondary pollution.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer Section 4.2 for the Mermaid block diagram)

**Efficiency of the complete train:**
- CO reduction ≈ 90–95 %
- HC reduction ≈ 90 %
- PM reduction ≈ 99 % (with DPF)
- NOx reduction ≈ 90–95 % (with SCR + EGR)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a)** Describe the **Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC)** used for emission testing of light-duty vehicles in India. How is the specific emission (g/km) calculated from the test data? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II)** system mandated under BS-VI. List the major monitored components, the threshold criteria, and the role of the Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Modified Indian Driving Cycle (MIDC)**

The **MIDC** is a chassis dynamometer test cycle representing typical Indian urban and extra-urban driving conditions. It is derived from real-world traffic data collected across major Indian cities (Delhi, Mumbai, Chennai, Kolkata, Bangalore).

**Key characteristics of MIDC:**
- Total cycle duration: **1180 seconds (≈ 19.67 minutes)**
- Total distance covered: **10.84 km**
- Average speed: **33.6 km/h**
- Maximum speed: **90 km/h**
- Idle time fraction: ≈ 19 %
- Consists of **4 phases**:
  1. Phase 1 (0–195 s): Low-speed urban driving
  2. Phase 2 (195–400 s): Stop-and-go traffic
  3. Phase 3 (400–810 s): Medium-speed urban
  4. Phase 4 (810–1180 s): High-speed extra-urban

**Calculation of specific emission (g/km):**

Step 1: The vehicle exhaust is diluted with ambient air in a **Constant Volume Sampling (CVS) tunnel**, and a sample bag collects the diluted exhaust throughout the cycle.

Step 2: The mass of pollutant $j$ in the bag is determined by measuring the concentration and the total diluted exhaust volume:

$$m_j = \int_{0}^{T_{cyc}} c_j(t) \cdot \dot{V}_{CVS}(t) \, dt \quad \text{(g)}$$

Step 3: The total distance $D$ travelled in the cycle is computed by integrating the speed profile:

$$D = \int_{0}^{T_{cyc}} v(t) \, dt \quad \text{(km)}$$

Step 4: The specific emission is:

$$E_j = \dfrac{m_j}{D} \quad \text{(g/km)}$$

Step 5: A **Deterioration Factor (DF)** is applied to account for emission increase over the vehicle's lifetime (typically 160,000 km for BS-VI):

$$E_{j,final} = E_j \times DF_j$$

The final $E_{j,final}$ is compared with the BS-VI limit specified in CMV Rule 115. The vehicle is approved only if:

$$E_{j,final} \leq \text{Limit}_j \quad \forall j \in \{\text{CO, HC, NOx, PM, PN}\}$$

**(b) On-Board Diagnostics (OBD-II) under BS-VI**

**OBD-II** is a vehicle self-diagnostic and reporting system mandated under BS-VI for all light-duty vehicles (M and N categories). Its primary purpose is to detect emission-related malfunctions in real time and alert the driver.

**Major monitored components / systems:**

1. **Engine misfire** — Per-cylinder combustion monitoring via crankshaft speed fluctuation.
2. **Catalytic converter efficiency** — Comparison of pre-cat and post-cat O₂/NOx sensor signals.
3. **Oxygen sensor (Lambda sensor) health** — Heater circuit, response time, and voltage range.
4. **Evaporative Emission Control System (EVAP)** — Leak detection in fuel system.
5. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR)** — Flow rate and valve position monitoring.
6. **DPF / PM filter loading** — Differential pressure and temperature monitoring.
7. **SCR / LNT efficiency** — NOx sensor before and after SCR.
8. **Fuel system pressure** — Low-pressure and high-pressure fuel rail monitoring.
9. **Throttle position sensor** — Range and rationality check.
10. **Battery / voltage management** — Sensor supply voltage monitoring.

**Threshold criteria for MIL activation:**

For **petrol vehicles** (BS-VI OBD-II), a Malfunction Indicator Lamp (MIL) is illuminated when:
- Catalyst efficiency drops below 50 % of its nominal value
- Engine misfire rate exceeds 1 % to 2 % of total combustion events
- HC emissions exceed 1.5 × the OBD threshold (typically around 0.25 g/km for HC)
- NOx emissions exceed 1.5 × the OBD threshold
- A confirmed sensor failure (open circuit, short circuit, rationality failure) is detected

For **diesel vehicles** (BS-VI OBD-II):
- PM filter loading exceeds a calibrated threshold (typically 120 % of soot mass capacity)
- NOx sensor readings deviate from expected range
- EGR flow error > 25 %

**Role of MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp):**

- Located on the instrument cluster; typically amber/orange in colour.
- **Steady illumination** → Confirmed fault; vehicle should be serviced; emissions may exceed limits.
- **Flashing illumination** → Severe fault (typically misfire causing catalyst damage); immediate service required.
- When the MIL is lit, a **Diagnostic Trouble Code (DTC)** is stored in the ECU memory, along with **freeze-frame data** capturing the engine operating conditions at the time of fault detection.

**Communication Protocol:**
- OBD-II uses **ISO 15765 (CAN bus)** protocol for communication with external scan tools.
- Standardized **DTC format** (e.g., P0301 = Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected).
- The DTC remains stored even after the MIL is cleared, allowing post-repair diagnosis.

**Real Driving Emissions (RDE) supplement:**
Under BS-VI, OBD-II is further supplemented by RDE checks using **Portable Emissions Measurement Systems (PEMS)** to ensure that real-world emissions do not exceed the regulatory limits by more than a **conformity factor (CF)** of 1.43 (for NOx) and 1.5 (for PN).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make in this topic — and where you lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing EURO and BS standards**: EURO-VI is **not** identical to BS-VI in the test cycle. BS-VI uses **MIDC** for light-duty vehicles (4-wheelers), while EURO-VI uses the **NEDC / WLTP**. Always state the **Indian** test cycle in your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting the PN limit**: Many students ignore the **Particle Number (PN)** metric introduced in BS-VI, which carries significant marks.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion**: HD engine emissions are in **g/kWh**, not g/km. Confusing the two loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 4. **Missing Deterioration Factor (DF)**: BS-VI limits are applied **after** multiplying by DF. Skipping this step is a frequent error.
> 5. **Skipping OBD-II / RDE**: Part B answers often ignore OBD-II, but under BS-VI it is a compulsory element.
> 6. **Not mentioning the test agency**: Whenever you discuss Type Approval, **always name ARAI/ICAT/VRDE** — this is a 1-mark differentiator.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CMV Rule 115** is the primary rule for emission standards; **Rule 116** covers type approval; **Rule 117** covers in-use compliance (PUC).
- **Bharat Stage (BS)** norms are India's version of European **Euro** norms, with India-aligned test cycles.
- India **skipped BS-V** and directly implemented **BS-VI on 1st April 2020**.
- BS-VI mandates **DPF for diesel**, **SCR/AdBlue for NOx reduction**, and **PN limit for the first time**.
- **Test cycles**: MIDC (4-wheelers), WMTC (2-wheelers), WHTC/WHSC (HD engines), WLTP (introducing for LDV).
- **OBD-II** is mandatory for all BS-VI vehicles, with **RDE/PEMS** as a real-world supplement.
- **Authorized test agencies**: ARAI (Pune), ICAT (Manesar), VRDE (Ahmednagar).
- **Emission limits**:
  - Petrol M1 (BS-VI): CO = 1.0, HC+NOx = 0.10, NOx = 0.06 g/km
  - Diesel M1 (BS-VI): CO = 0.50, HC+NOx = 0.17, NOx = 0.08, PM = 0.0045 g/km, PN = 6.0 × 10¹¹/km
  - HD Diesel (BS-VI, WHTC): NOx = 0.46 g/kWh, PM = 0.01 g/kWh
- **Deterioration Factor (DF)** is applied for the full useful life (160,000 km for LDV, 700,000 km for HD engines).
- **Fuel sulphur** dropped from 50 ppm (BS-IV) to **10 ppm (BS-VI)**, enabling advanced after-treatment.
- **Type Approval Certificate (TAC)** is mandatory before any model can be sold in India.
- **PUC certificate** is mandatory for every in-use vehicle under Rule 115(2).
- **Euro-VI ≠ BS-VI in test cycle** — always use MIDC in answers unless specifically asked about HD engines (WHTC/WHSC).
- **RDE (Real Driving Emissions)** uses PEMS with a conformity factor of 1.43 for NOx and 1.5 for PN.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical System - Storage Battery Operations and Maintenance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical System - Storage Battery Operations and Maintenance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
A **Storage Battery** (also called an **Accumulator** or **Secondary Cell**) is an electrochemical energy storage device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy through reversible redox (reduction–oxidation) reactions. Unlike a primary cell, a storage battery can be **recharged** by passing a reverse current through it, restoring the active materials to their original state. In an automobile, the storage battery serves three primary functions:
1. **Starting** the internal combustion engine via the starter motor.
2. **Lighting** the vehicle's electrical and electronic loads.
3. **Ignition** of the air–fuel mixture in the combustion chamber (SLI Battery).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The term **SLI Battery** (Starting, Lighting, Ignition) is the most commonly asked definition in KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize it verbatim.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reusable Water Tank"
Think of a storage battery as a **rechargeable water tank**:
- A **fully charged battery** is a full tank ready to deliver water (current).
- A **discharged battery** is an empty tank — it can still be filled up again by pumping water **backwards** (charging).
- The **pipe diameter** represents the battery's internal resistance — narrower pipes restrict flow and cause voltage drop.
- The **tank size** represents the **capacity (Ampere-hour, Ah)**.

This analogy helps in understanding concepts like *self-discharge* (a slow leak in the tank), *sulfation* (sludge buildup reducing usable volume), and *overcharging* (overflow damaging the tank walls).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Memorize for KTU)
- **Nominal Voltage:** **12 V** (modern passenger cars), 6 V (older vehicles).
- **Electrolyte:** Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄) + Distilled Water (specific gravity ≈ 1.28 at full charge).
- **Capacity Unit:** **Ampere-hour (Ah)**.
- **Cold Cranking Amps (CCA):** Current deliverable at **−18 °C** for 30 seconds while maintaining ≥ **7.2 V** (for a 12 V battery).
- **Reserve Capacity (RC):** Minutes a fully charged battery can deliver **25 A** at **27 °C** before voltage drops below **10.5 V**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Electromotive Force (EMF)** is the open-circuit voltage of the cell. **Terminal Voltage** is the voltage under load and is always less than EMF due to internal resistance.

### 1.4 Classification of Automotive Batteries
| Type | Anode | Cathode | Electrolyte | Voltage/Cell |
|------|-------|---------|-------------|--------------|
| **Lead-Acid (Flooded)** | Pb | PbO₂ | H₂SO₄ (Liquid) | 2.1 V |
| **VRLA / Sealed (SMF)** | Pb | PbO₂ | H₂SO₄ (Gel/AGM) | 2.1 V |
| **Lithium-Ion (EV)** | Graphite | LiCoO₂ | LiPF₆ (Organic) | 3.6–3.7 V |
| **Nickel-Metal Hydride (Hybrid)** | MH | NiOOH | KOH (Alkaline) | 1.2 V |

For the KTU 2024 Automobile syllabus, the **Lead-Acid battery** is the primary focus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Lead-Acid Battery (Cell-by-Cell)
A **12 V automotive battery** consists of **6 cells** connected in series, each producing **2.1 V**.

**Key Components:**
- **Positive Plate:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂) — dark brown in color.
- **Negative Plate:** Sponge lead (Pb) — gray in color.
- **Separator:** Insulating porous sheet (PVC/rubber) preventing short circuit while allowing ion flow.
- **Electrolyte:** Dilute sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) with specific gravity **1.28** at full charge.
- **Container:** Hard rubber or polypropylene, divided into 6 compartments.
- **Vent Plugs:** Allow escape of gases (H₂, O₂) during charging.
- **Terminal Posts:** Marked **(+)** and **(−)** for external connection.

### 2.2 Electrochemical Reactions (Discharge Cycle)

During **discharge**, both plates convert toward **lead sulfate (PbSO₄)**:
- **Negative plate:** $\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$
- **Positive plate:** $\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Overall reaction:** $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> During discharge, sulfuric acid is **consumed** and water is **produced** → specific gravity **decreases**.

### 2.3 Electrochemical Reactions (Charge Cycle)

During **charging**, the reverse reaction occurs (an external DC source drives current backwards):
- **Overall reaction:** $2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$
- Gases liberated: $\text{H}_2$ (at negative plate), $\text{O}_2$ (at positive plate) — hence the term "gassing".

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Use |
|-------------------|------------|-------------------|-----|
| **Cell EMF** | $E = 2.1\ \text{V}$ (per cell) | — | Standard lead-acid cell voltage |
| **Battery Voltage** | $V_b = n \times E$ | $n$ = number of cells (6) | Total battery voltage |
| **Capacity** | $C = I \times t$ | $C$ in Ah, $I$ in A, $t$ in hours | Energy storage rating |
| **Specific Energy** | $SE = \dfrac{V \times C}{m}$ | Wh/kg | Energy per unit mass |
| **Peukert's Law** | $I^n \cdot t = C_p$ | $n \approx 1.1$–$1.3$ for lead-acid | Capacity at high discharge rates |
| **State of Charge (SoC)** | $\text{SoC} = \dfrac{\rho - 1.10}{1.28 - 1.10} \times 100\%$ | $\rho$ = specific gravity | Battery charge level |
| **Ampere-hour Efficiency** | $\eta_{Ah} = \dfrac{I_d \cdot t_d}{I_c \cdot t_c} \times 100$ | $d$=discharge, $c$=charge | Charging effectiveness |
| **Watt-hour Efficiency** | $\eta_{Wh} = \eta_{Ah} \times \dfrac{V_d}{V_c} \times 100$ | Voltages in V | True energy efficiency |
| **Internal Resistance** | $r_i = \dfrac{E - V}{I}$ | All in SI units | Voltage drop calculation |
| **CCA Rating Conversion** | $I_{MCA} \approx I_{CCA} \times 1.2$ | At 0 °C | Marine Cranking Amps equivalent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The ampere-hour efficiency of a lead-acid battery is typically **90–95%**, while watt-hour efficiency is **70–80%**. This difference is due to the **higher charging voltage** (≈2.3 V/cell) compared to discharge voltage (≈2.0 V/cell).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Cranking Power:** A typical 12 V, 40 Ah battery can deliver **~200 A** for 12 minutes (CCA ≈ 300 A).
- **Energy Storage in HEV/EV:** Lead-acid is used as a **12 V auxiliary battery** even in electric vehicles.
- **Load Balancing:** Modern cars use **AGM (Absorbent Glass Mat)** batteries to support regenerative braking energy recovery.
- **Sustainability:** Over **95%** of lead-acid battery components are **recyclable** — highest among all battery chemistries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Battery Capacity in Ampere-Hours

**Problem Context:** A vehicle battery delivers a constant current of $I$ amperes for $t$ hours before its terminal voltage drops below the cutoff threshold. Derive the **capacity formula**.

**Step 1 — Definition of Charge**
By the definition of electric current, charge is the product of current and time:
$$Q = I \times t$$

**Step 2 — Define the Unit**
Since the SI unit of charge is Coulomb (C) and $1\ \text{C} = 1\ \text{A} \cdot 1\ \text{s}$, we have:
$$Q\ (\text{in Coulombs}) = I\ (\text{in A}) \times t\ (\text{in s})$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Ampere-Hours**
Since $1\ \text{hour} = 3600\ \text{seconds}$, the total charge in Coulombs becomes:
$$Q = I \times (t \times 3600)\ \text{C} = I \times t\ \text{Ah} \times 3600$$

**Step 4 — Definition of Capacity**
The **Ampere-hour Capacity** is defined as the total charge a battery can deliver:
$$\boxed{C\ (\text{Ah}) = \dfrac{I \times t_{seconds}}{3600}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: State of Charge (SoC) Using Specific Gravity

**Step 1 — Establish Reference Values**
- Full charge specific gravity: $\rho_{fc} = 1.28$ at **27 °C**
- Discharged specific gravity: $\rho_{dc} = 1.10$ at **27 °C**
- Linear interpolation is valid because the change in acid concentration is **proportional** to SoC.

**Step 2 — Linear Interpolation Formula**
The SoC is given by the ratio of available acid density drop to the total possible drop:
$$\text{SoC} = \dfrac{\rho_{measured} - \rho_{dc}}{\rho_{fc} - \rho_{dc}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Substituting Values**
$$\text{SoC} = \dfrac{\rho_{measured} - 1.10}{1.28 - 1.10} \times 100\% = \dfrac{\rho_{measured} - 1.10}{0.18} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Apply Temperature Correction**
Specific gravity varies by **−0.0007 per °C** from 27 °C:
$$\rho_{corrected} = \rho_{measured} + 0.0007 \times (T - 27)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always apply the **temperature correction** before calculating SoC. Marks are frequently lost for this in KTU valuations.

### 3.3 Derivation: Ampere-Hour Efficiency

**Step 1 — Define Inputs and Outputs**
- **Input (Charging):** $Q_c = I_c \times t_c$
- **Output (Discharging):** $Q_d = I_d \times t_d$

**Step 2 — Loss Mechanism**
Losses occur due to:
- **Gassing** (electrolysis of water at the end of charge)
- **Local action** (self-discharge from impurities in plates)
- **Ohmic heating** ($I^2 r$ losses in electrolyte)

**Step 3 — Efficiency Expression**
$$\eta_{Ah} = \dfrac{Q_d}{Q_c} \times 100\% = \dfrac{I_d \cdot t_d}{I_c \cdot t_c} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Typical)**
A battery is charged at $I_c = 10\ \text{A}$ for $t_c = 10\ \text{h}$ and discharged at $I_d = 8\ \text{A}$ for $t_d = 11\ \text{h}$. Calculate $\eta_{Ah}$.

Applying the formula:
$$\eta_{Ah} = \dfrac{8 \times 11}{10 \times 10} \times 100\% = \dfrac{88}{100} \times 100\% = 88\%$$

**Step 5 — Watt-Hour Efficiency**
If the average discharge voltage is $V_d = 11.85\ \text{V}$ and average charge voltage is $V_c = 13.8\ \text{V}$:
$$\eta_{Wh} = 88 \times \dfrac{11.85}{13.8} = 88 \times 0.8587 = 75.57\%$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Battery Fault Diagnosis

| Symptom | Probable Cause | Corrective Action | Marks Weightage |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Low cranking speed | **Sulfation** (hard PbSO₄) | Slow charge at 2.4 V/cell for 24–48 h | High |
| Electrolyte level low | Water loss due to gassing | Top-up with **distilled water only** | High |
| Battery over-heating | Overcharging / high current | Reduce charging current; check regulator | Medium |
| Specific gravity uneven across cells | Internal short / cell failure | Replace cell; check for sediment | Medium |
| White powder on terminals | **Corrosion** (sulfate deposit) | Clean with baking soda + water, apply grease | Low |
| Swollen / bulging case | Excessive gassing / heat | Replace battery; check alternator voltage | High |
| Dark brown electrolyte | Active material shedding | Battery near end of life — replace | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Lead-Acid Battery Cell Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[POSITIVE TERMINAL POST] --> B1[POSITIVE PLATE GRID PbO2]
    A2[NEGATIVE TERMINAL POST] --> B2[NEGATIVE PLATE GRID Pb]
    B1 --> C1[SEPARATOR POROUS PVC]
    C1 --> B2
    B1 --> D1[ELECTROLYTE H2SO4 1.28 SG]
    B2 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[VENT PLUG GAS ESCAPE]
    E1 --> F1[H2 and O2 RELEASE DURING CHARGE]
    D1 --> G1[CONTAINER POLYPROPYLENE 6 CELLS]
    G1 --> H1[INTERCELL CONNECTORS SERIES]
    H1 --> I1[BATTERY OUTPUT 12V DC]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Battery Maintenance Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J1[START VISUAL INSPECTION] --> J2{CRACKS OR BULGE}
    J2 -->|YES| J3[REPLACE BATTERY]
    J2 -->|NO| J4[CHECK ELECTROLYTE LEVEL]
    J4 --> J5{BETWEEN LOWER AND UPPER MARK}
    J5 -->|NO| J6[TOP UP WITH DISTILLED WATER]
    J5 -->|YES| J7[MEASURE SPECIFIC GRAVITY]
    J7 --> J8{BETWEEN 1.21 AND 1.28}
    J8 -->|NO| J9[CHARGE BATTERY]
    J8 -->|YES| J10[LOAD TEST]
    J10 --> J11{VOLTAGE ABOVE 9.6V AT HALF CCA}
    J11 -->|NO| J12[BATTERY WEAK RECHARGE OR REPLACE]
    J11 -->|YES| J13[CLEAN TERMINALS APPLY GREASE]
    J13 --> J14[END BATTERY HEALTHY]
    J6 --> J7
    J9 --> J10
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Battery Charging Method Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[CONSTANT CURRENT CHARGING] --> K2[SIMPLE INEXPENSIVE]
    K2 --> K3[HIGH INITIAL HEAT RISK]
    K4[CONSTANT VOLTAGE CHARGING] --> K5[MODERN ALTERNATOR TYPE]
    K5 --> K6[LOW WATER LOSS]
    K7[TAPER CHARGING] --> K8[VOLTAGE AND CURRENT TAPER]
    K8 --> K9[USED IN GARAGE WORKSHOP]
    K10[TRICKLE CHARGING] --> K11[LOW CURRENT LONG DURATION]
    K11 --> K12[PREVENTS SULFATION]
    K13[PULSE CHARGING] --> K14[INTERMITTENT PULSES]
    K14 --> K15[DESULFATES OLD BATTERIES]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Discharge Reaction Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Pb NEGATIVE PLATE] --> L2[LOSES ELECTRONS]
    L2 --> L3[PbSO4 FORMS]
    M1[PbO2 POSITIVE PLATE] --> M2[GAINS ELECTRONS]
    M2 --> M3[PbSO4 FORMS]
    M3 --> N1[H2O PRODUCED]
    L3 --> N1
    N1 --> O1[SPECIFIC GRAVITY DROPS TO 1.10]
    O1 --> P1[TERMINAL VOLTAGE FALLS BELOW 10.5V]
    P1 --> Q1[BATTERY DISCHARGED RECHARGE REQUIRED]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define an SLI battery. List its three primary functions in an automobile.**

**Model Answer:**
An **SLI Battery** (Starting, Lighting, Ignition) is a rechargeable lead-acid battery used in automobiles to provide the necessary electrical energy for the vehicle's electrical systems. Its three primary functions are:
1. **Starting** the internal combustion engine by powering the starter motor.
2. **Lighting** the vehicle — headlamps, tail lamps, interior lights, indicators.
3. **Ignition** — providing high-voltage pulses to the spark plugs in petrol engines.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks functions)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State two differences between a primary cell and a secondary cell. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Primary Cell | Secondary Cell |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Rechargeable | No (irreversible reaction) | Yes (reversible reaction) |
| Internal resistance | High | Low |
| Example | Dry cell (Leclanché), Alkaline AA | Lead-acid, Lithium-ion |

*(3 marks: 2 marks differences + 1 mark example)*

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction of a lead-acid storage battery. Label all major components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A lead-acid storage battery consists of the following components:
1. **Container:** Made of hard rubber or polypropylene, divided into 6 separate cells by partitions.
2. **Positive Plates:** Lead dioxide (PbO₂) coated on a lead-antimony grid, dark brown in color.
3. **Negative Plates:** Sponge lead (Pb) coated on a lead-antimony grid, gray in color.
4. **Separators:** Porous insulating sheets (PVC, rubber, or fiberglass) placed between + and − plates to prevent short circuit while allowing ionic flow.
5. **Electrolyte:** Dilute sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) with specific gravity 1.28 at full charge.
6. **Vent Plugs:** Provide exit for hydrogen and oxygen gases released during charging.
7. **Terminal Posts:** Two external posts — positive (larger) and negative (smaller).
8. **Cell Connectors:** Lead straps connecting 6 cells in series to give 12 V output.

**Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above for Mermaid block schematic):** A 12 V battery = 6 cells × 2.1 V each.

*(7 marks: 2 for components list + 2 for diagram + 2 for material description + 1 for voltage derivation)*

---

**(b)** Explain the chemical reactions that take place during the charging and discharging of a lead-acid battery. How does the specific gravity of the electrolyte change in each case? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Discharge Reactions:**
- Negative plate: $\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$
- Positive plate: $\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
- Overall: $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy}$

**Observation:** Sulfuric acid is consumed and water is formed → **specific gravity decreases** from 1.28 to 1.10.

**Charge Reactions:** (Reverse of discharge — DC source applied)
- Overall: $2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$

**Observation:** Water is consumed and sulfuric acid is regenerated → **specific gravity increases** back to 1.28. **Gases** (H₂ at negative, O₂ at positive) are liberated near the end of charge due to electrolysis.

**Specific Gravity Change Summary:**
$$\rho_{discharge}: 1.28 \rightarrow 1.10 \quad ; \quad \rho_{charge}: 1.10 \rightarrow 1.28$$

*(7 marks: 2 for discharge reactions + 2 for charge reactions + 1 for overall reaction + 2 for SG change explanation)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Define **Ampere-hour efficiency** and **Watt-hour efficiency** of a battery. A battery is charged at 12 A for 8 hours and discharged at 10 A for 9 hours. If the average charge voltage is 13.5 V and the average discharge voltage is 11.8 V, calculate the Ah and Wh efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**
- **Ampere-hour efficiency** ($\eta_{Ah}$): The ratio of output charge during discharge to input charge during charge.
$$\eta_{Ah} = \dfrac{I_d \times t_d}{I_c \times t_c} \times 100\%$$

- **Watt-hour efficiency** ($\eta_{Wh}$): The ratio of output energy during discharge to input energy during charge.
$$\eta_{Wh} = \dfrac{V_d \times I_d \times t_d}{V_c \times I_c \times t_c} \times 100\%$$

**Given Data:**
- $I_c = 12\ \text{A}$, $t_c = 8\ \text{h}$, $V_c = 13.5\ \text{V}$
- $I_d = 10\ \text{A}$, $t_d = 9\ \text{h}$, $V_d = 11.8\ \text{V}$

**Calculation of $\eta_{Ah}$:**
$$\eta_{Ah} = \dfrac{10 \times 9}{12 \times 8} \times 100\% = \dfrac{90}{96} \times 100\% = 93.75\%$$

**Calculation of $\eta_{Wh}$:**
$$\eta_{Wh} = \dfrac{11.8 \times 10 \times 9}{13.5 \times 12 \times 8} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1062}{1296} \times 100\% = 81.94\%$$

**Why $\eta_{Wh} < \eta_{Ah}$:** The charging voltage (13.5 V) is higher than the discharge voltage (11.8 V) due to internal resistance and overpotential, so the energy loss is greater than the charge loss.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions + 3 for Ah calculation + 2 for Wh calculation + bonus for explanation)*

---

**(b)** List and briefly explain **any five routine maintenance practices** for an automotive lead-acid battery. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Visual Inspection:** Check the case for cracks, bulges, and leakage. Replace if damaged. *(1.4 marks)*

2. **Electrolyte Level Check:** Ensure electrolyte is between the lower and upper level marks. If low, top up with **distilled water only** — never add acid. *(1.4 marks)*

3. **Specific Gravity Measurement:** Use a **hydrometer** to measure the specific gravity. Reading should be 1.21–1.28 at full charge. Variation > 0.05 between cells indicates cell failure. *(1.4 marks)*

4. **Terminal Cleaning:** Remove corrosion (white sulfate deposits) using a solution of **baking soda and water**. Dry thoroughly and apply petroleum jelly or terminal grease to prevent re-corrosion. *(1.4 marks)*

5. **Voltage / Load Test:** Use a voltmeter or load tester. A healthy 12 V battery should show ≥ 12.6 V open-circuit and ≥ 9.6 V under load test. *(1.4 marks)*

*(7 marks: 5 × 1.4 marks distributed across 5 practices)*

---

### 5.2 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Confusing EMF and Terminal Voltage** — Students often write both as the same. EMF is open-circuit; terminal voltage is under load. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting Temperature Correction** in specific gravity measurements. Always apply $\rho_{corrected} = \rho_{measured} + 0.0007 \times (T - 27)$. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Wrong use of Distilled Water vs. Sulfuric Acid** — Only distilled water is added to top up. Adding acid is a critical error. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Not labeling the diagram** — KTU evaluators deduct up to **2 marks** for unlabelled sketches in construction questions.
> 5. **Mixing up Charge/Discharge reactions** — Memorize that during discharge, **PbSO₄ forms on both plates**; during charge, **H₂SO₄ is regenerated**. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 6. **Unit Inconsistency** — Capacity in Ah, Energy in Wh, Specific Gravity is dimensionless. (Loss: 0.5 mark per error)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Reference Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ✅ **SLI Battery** = Starting, Lighting, Ignition. *(Definition question favorite)*
- ✅ A 12 V battery = **6 cells × 2.1 V** connected in series.
- ✅ **Positive plate:** PbO₂ (dark brown) | **Negative plate:** Sponge Pb (gray).
- ✅ **Discharge:** Both plates → PbSO₄. Specific gravity **drops** to 1.10.
- ✅ **Charge:** PbSO₄ → Pb and PbO₂. Specific gravity **rises** to 1.28. Gases H₂ & O₂ released.
- ✅ **Top up with distilled water ONLY** — never add sulfuric acid.
- ✅ **Ah efficiency** = $I_d t_d / I_c t_c$ ≈ 90–95%.
- ✅ **Wh efficiency** < Ah efficiency because $V_c > V_d$.
- ✅ **CCA** measured at **−18 °C** for **30 seconds** at ≥ 7.2 V (12 V battery).
- ✅ **Sulfation** is the #1 cause of lead-acid battery failure.
- ✅ **Temperature correction:** $\Delta\rho = -0.0007\ \text{per °C}$ from 27 °C reference.
- ✅ **AGM / VRLA** = sealed maintenance-free lead-acid batteries used in modern vehicles.
- ✅ **Lithium-ion** = dominant chemistry in EVs (3.6–3.7 V/cell), but lead-acid remains for 12 V auxiliary.
- ✅ **Specific gravity** of pure water = 1.00. Electrolyte SG = 1.10 (discharged) to 1.28 (full charge).
- ✅ Always mention **internal resistance** when discussing voltage drop under load.
- ✅ A healthy cell shows SG variation < **0.05** between cells.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ignition System - Coil and Magneto Ignition System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ignition System - Coil and Magneto Ignition System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Ignition System — Foundation Definition

In an internal combustion (I.C.) engine, the air-fuel mixture inside the combustion chamber must be raised above its **auto-ignition temperature** (≈ 250 °C for petrol, 150 °C for diesel) for combustion to occur. In a **Spark Ignition (SI)** engine, this thermal threshold is not reached by compression alone; instead, an external energy source delivers a precisely timed, high-voltage electric spark across the spark plug gap to initiate combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> An **Ignition System** is defined as the aggregate of electromechanical components in a spark-ignition engine that generates, stores, and delivers a high-voltage surge to the spark plug at the correct instant (a few degrees before Top Dead Centre — TDC) so as to ignite the compressed air-fuel mixture.

The **two principal classifications** mandated by your KTU Module 2 syllabus are:
1. **Coil Ignition System (CIS)** — also called **Battery Ignition System (BIS)**
2. **Magneto Ignition System (MIS)**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Lighting a Campfire in the Dark

Imagine you are trying to light a campfire using dry wood:
- The **wood** = compressed air-fuel mixture in the cylinder.
- The **spark** = the tiny flame that initiates combustion.
- A **matchstick** is unreliable — it can be blown out. You need a **piezo-electric lighter** that delivers a focused, timed spark.

In an engine, this "lighter" is the **ignition system**. The **coil** acts like a step-up transformer, taking a low 12 V battery supply and "boosting" it to **20,000–30,000 V** — enough to ionize the spark plug gap. In a **magneto system**, the engine itself *becomes* the electricity generator (like a bicycle dynamo), eliminating the need for a separate battery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Difference in One Line:**
> - **Coil/Battery Ignition** = Energy Source = External **Battery** (12 V).
> - **Magneto Ignition** = Energy Source = Self-Contained **Magneto Generator**.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Standard |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Battery Voltage | **12 V DC** | SAE J537 |
| Secondary Voltage Required | **20,000 – 30,000 V** | Bosch Standard |
| Spark Plug Gap | **0.6 – 1.0 mm** | NGK Standard |
| Spark Duration | **0.001 – 0.003 s** (1–3 ms) | Industry Empirical |
| Ignition Advance | **5° – 45° BTDC** (varies with RPM) | Heywood Reference |
| Primary Inductance (L) | **5 – 10 mH** | Empirical |
| Energy in Spark | **20 – 50 mJ** | Empirical |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature (Petrol) | **≈ 250 °C** | Combustion Standard |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Coil Ignition System (CIS) — Architecture & Logic

The **Coil Ignition System** is a low-voltage primary circuit, energy-storage circuit, and a high-voltage secondary circuit working in synchrony. Let us break down the operational logic step-by-step:

### Logic Flow — How a Coil Ignition System Fires

- **Step 1 — Energy Storage in the Inductor (Collapsing Field Stage):**
  When the ignition switch is ON and the contact breaker (CB) points are **closed**, current from the **12 V battery** flows through the **primary winding** ($N_p$ turns, low resistance ≈ **1–4 Ω**). This current builds a strong **magnetic field** around the iron core, storing energy in the form of magnetic flux.

- **Step 2 — Sudden Interruption (Field Collapse):**
  The **cam-driven contact breaker** is mechanically opened by a rotating cam (driven by the camshaft at half engine speed for a 4-stroke SI engine). The exact moment of opening is timed to occur **a few degrees before TDC** on the compression stroke of the corresponding cylinder.

- **Step 3 — High-Voltage Induced EMF (Faraday's Law in Action):**
  The sudden collapse of the magnetic flux in the primary winding induces a high-voltage EMF in the **secondary winding** ($N_s$ turns, high resistance ≈ **5,000–25,000 Ω**), in accordance with **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

- **Step 4 — Spark Delivery:**
  This induced high voltage (typically 20,000–30,000 V) jumps the spark plug gap, ionizing the air-fuel mixture and creating a spark that initiates combustion.

- **Step 5 — Distribution:**
  In a multi-cylinder engine, the **rotor arm of the distributor** directs this high voltage to the **correct cylinder's spark plug** in the correct firing order.

### 2.2 The Magneto Ignition System (MIS) — Architecture & Logic

A **Magneto** is essentially a self-contained **permanent-magnet AC generator** coupled with an ignition coil. It is the system of choice for **two-wheelers (motorcycles), aircraft piston engines, lawnmowers, and outboard motors** because it does not require a separate battery.

### Logic Flow — How a Magneto Ignition System Fires

- **Step 1 — Flux Generation by Permanent Magnets:**
  As the engine crankshaft rotates, a **multi-pole permanent magnet** (rotor) spins inside a stationary **stator winding**, generating a low-voltage **alternating EMF** (typically 50–100 V AC).

- **Step 2 — Rectification or Direct Coupling:**
  Depending on design, the AC is either used directly (in older designs) or **rectified** to DC (in modern CDI — Capacitor Discharge Ignition systems). For a classical **magneto** (non-CDI), the AC flows through the **primary winding** of an ignition coil.

- **Step 3 — Interruption by Contact Breaker:**
  At the precise firing instant, the **contact breaker** opens, abruptly interrupting the primary current. This causes a sudden flux collapse.

- **Step 4 — High-Voltage Induction:**
  The collapsing flux induces a very high voltage (15,000–20,000 V) in the secondary winding, which is delivered to the spark plug via a **HT cable** and a **distributor** (in multi-cylinder applications).

- **Step 5 — Sparking:**
  The spark plug fires, igniting the mixture.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations form the **analytical core** of every numerical problem KTU examiners set on this topic. Memorize them.

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Meaning | Engineering Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | **Faraday's Law** — $E$ = induced EMF, $N$ = turns, $\Phi$ = magnetic flux (Weber) | Foundation of transformer action in ignition coil |
| 2 | $\dfrac{E_s}{E_p} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | **Turns Ratio** — $E_s, E_p$ = secondary/primary voltages; $N_s, N_p$ = turns | Determines voltage step-up in coil |
| 3 | $E_s = E_p \times \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$ | Rearranged form of (2) | Used to compute secondary voltage |
| 4 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | **Energy Stored in Inductor** — $L$ = primary inductance, $I$ = primary current at break | Total available spark energy |
| 5 | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | **Energy Stored in Capacitor** — used in CDI (Capacitor Discharge Ignition) | Energy delivered in CDI systems |
| 6 | $V_s = I_p \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | **LC Oscillation Peak Voltage** — for CDI systems | Peak secondary voltage in capacitor discharge |
| 7 | $I_p = \dfrac{V_b}{R_p} \left(1 - e^{-R_p t / L}\right)$ | **Primary Current Build-up** — exponential charging of inductor | Determines current at moment of break |
| 8 | $E_{spark} = \eta \times E_{stored}$ | **Spark Energy Efficiency** — $\eta$ = efficiency factor (typically 0.6–0.8) | Actual energy at plug vs. stored |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Modern Passenger Car (PC) and Utility Vehicle (UV) engines universally use **Coil Ignition** because they have a reliable 12 V battery from the alternator.
