Examples: Maruti Suzuki Alto K10, Hyundai Grand i10 — all use **Distributor-Less Ignition (DLI)** or **Coil-on-Plug (COP)** variants of CIS.
- **Two-Wheeler Industry:** Most motorcycles (Hero Splendor, Honda Activa, Royal Enfield) use **Capacitor Discharge Ignition (CDI)** — a derivative of magneto ignition, often referred to as "AC-CDI" or "DC-CDI."
- **Aviation:** Piston-engine aircraft (Cessna 172 with Lycoming O-320) use **dual magneto** systems for redundancy — two independent magnetos per engine.
- **Motorsport:** Formula 1 cars use **pencil coil / coil-on-plug** direct ignition for sub-millisecond spark timing precision.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Secondary Voltage ($E_s$) in a Coil Ignition System

We begin from the fundamental Faraday's Law applied to the primary and secondary windings of the ignition coil.

**Given:**
- Primary voltage: $E_p = 12 \, \text{V}$ (battery supply)
- Turns ratio: $\dfrac{N_s}{N_p} = 100$ (typical ignition coil has ≈ 100 times more turns on secondary)

**Step 1 — Write Faraday's Law for the Primary Winding**

The induced EMF in the primary is proportional to the rate of change of flux linkage.

$$E_p = -N_p \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

This equation states that the primary voltage equals the negative of the product of the number of primary turns and the rate of change of magnetic flux through the iron core.

**Step 2 — Write Faraday's Law for the Secondary Winding**

By the same physical principle, the EMF induced in the secondary winding is:

$$E_s = -N_s \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$

This equation states that the secondary voltage equals the negative of the product of the number of secondary turns and the same rate of change of magnetic flux, because both windings share the same iron core and the same flux.

**Step 3 — Divide the Two Equations**

Dividing (2) by (1) eliminates the $d\Phi/dt$ term since the flux change is identical for both windings (shared magnetic core).

$$\dfrac{E_s}{E_p} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$$

This equation states that the ratio of secondary to primary voltage is directly proportional to the ratio of secondary to primary turns, with the proportionality constant equal to unity.

**Step 4 — Rearrange to Find $E_s$**

Multiplying both sides by $E_p$:

$$E_s = E_p \times \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Values**

Inserting $E_p = 12$ V and $N_s / N_p = 100$:

$$E_s = 12 \times 100 = 1200 \, \text{V}$$

**Important — This Result is Theoretical, Not Real:**
The actual secondary voltage is much higher (20,000–30,000 V) because of **flux concentration and rapid collapse** in the iron core, plus the multiplication effect of the **transformer ratio with stored energy**. The accurate formula uses energy conservation:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} C_s V_s^2$$

where $C_s$ is the distributed capacitance of the secondary circuit.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Stored in the Primary Winding (Inductor)

The energy stored in the magnetic field of the primary inductor just before the contact breaker opens is the source of the spark energy.

**Given:**
- Primary inductance: $L = 8 \, \text{mH} = 8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{H}$
- Primary resistance: $R_p = 4 \, \Omega$
- Battery voltage: $V_b = 12 \, \text{V}$
- Dwell time (CB closed): $t = 0.005 \, \text{s}$ (5 ms)

**Step 1 — Write the Current Build-up Equation in an RL Circuit**

When the contact breaker closes, the current rises exponentially as per the standard RL charging equation.

$$I_p(t) = \dfrac{V_b}{R_p} \left(1 - e^{-R_p t / L}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Steady-State (Maximum) Current**

In the limit $t \to \infty$, the exponential term vanishes:

$$I_{max} = \dfrac{V_b}{R_p} = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Current After 5 ms (Dwell Time)**

The dimensionless exponent is first evaluated.

$$\dfrac{R_p \, t}{L} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.005}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{0.020}{0.008} = 2.5$$

The exponential term is then evaluated.

$$e^{-2.5} = 0.0821$$

Substituting into the current equation:

$$I_p(0.005) = 3 \times (1 - 0.0821) = 3 \times 0.9179 = 2.754 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Inductor Energy Formula**

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$$

$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times (2.754)^2$$

$$E = 0.004 \times 7.585 = 0.03034 \, \text{J} \approx 30.34 \, \text{mJ}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Efficiency Factor**

Accounting for an efficiency factor $\eta = 0.7$ (typical — losses occur in distributor, cables, spark plug):

$$E_{spark} = \eta \times E_{stored} = 0.7 \times 30.34 = 21.24 \, \text{mJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A typical KTU numerical expects a value in the range **20–50 mJ**, which matches our result perfectly. Always show all intermediate steps — KTU examiners award partial marks for *method* even if the final answer has a small numerical drift.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Coil vs. Magneto Ignition

| Parameter | Coil Ignition (CIS) | Magneto Ignition (MIS) | KTU Exam Insight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy Source** | External 12 V Battery | Permanent Magnet (Self-generated) | Favourite 3-mark question |
| **Voltage at Primary** | 12 V DC | 50–100 V AC (self-generated) | Distinguish clearly |
| **Voltage at Secondary** | 20,000–30,000 V | 15,000–20,000 V | Magneto gives lower secondary |
| **Starting Reliability** | Excellent (uses battery) | Poor at low cranking RPM (weak EMF) | Common KTU question |
| **Maintenance** | High (CB points wear) | Moderate | CB-less magneto exists |
| **Cost** | Moderate to High | Low (no battery needed) | Two-wheeler application |
| **Weight** | Higher (battery) | Lighter | Aircraft advantage |
| **Cold-Start Performance** | Very Good | Poor (requires impulse coupling) | KTU often asks why |
| **Spark Intensity at High RPM** | Decreases (relay saturation) | Improves (rotor spins faster) | Trade-off |
| **Common Application** | Cars, SUVs, LMV | Motorcycles, Aircraft, Mowers | Application-based questions |
| **Reliability at High Speed** | Limited by CB bounce | Better (rotor-based) | Designer perspective |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes Followed:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix (e.g., `nodeA1`, `stepB2`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`) used as standalone IDs.
> - Labels contain clean uppercase text — no bold/italic markdown inside quotes.

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Coil (Battery) Ignition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[BT1: 12V BATTERY] --> nodeA2[S1: IGNITION SWITCH]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[COIL P: PRIMARY WINDING 200 TURNS]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[CB: CONTACT BREAKER WITH CAM]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[CHASSIS GROUND]
    nodeA1 -. 12V DC supply .-> nodeA2
    nodeA3 -. Step up voltage .-> nodeA6[COIL S: SECONDARY WINDING 20000 TURNS]
    nodeA6 --> nodeA7[DIS: DISTRIBUTOR ROTOR]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA8[SP1: SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 1]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA9[SP2: SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 2]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA10[SP3: SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 3]
    nodeA7 --> nodeA11[SP4: SPARK PLUG CYLINDER 4]
    nodeA8 --> nodeA5
    nodeA9 --> nodeA5
    nodeA10 --> nodeA5
    nodeA11 --> nodeA5
    nodeA4 --> nodeA6
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **12 V battery** sends DC current through the **ignition switch** to the **primary winding**.
- The **contact breaker (CB)** interrupts the primary current, triggering a flux collapse in the iron core.
- The induced high voltage in the **secondary winding (20,000 turns)** is routed by the **distributor** to the appropriate **spark plug**.
- All spark plugs return current to the **chassis ground**, completing the circuit.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Magneto Ignition System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeB1[CRANKSHAFT OF ENGINE] --> nodeB2[MAGNETO ROTOR: PERMANENT MAGNET]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[STATOR WINDING AC OUTPUT 50-100V]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4[COIL P: PRIMARY WINDING]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5[CB: CONTACT BREAKER]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6[GROUND]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB7[COIL S: SECONDARY WINDING]
    nodeB7 --> nodeB8[HT CABLE 15000-20000V]
    nodeB8 --> nodeB9[SP: SPARK PLUG]
    nodeB9 --> nodeB6
    nodeB1 -. Mechanical rotation .-> nodeB2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **engine's crankshaft** directly drives the **magneto rotor** (permanent magnet), eliminating the need for a battery.
- The rotating magnet induces an **AC voltage** in the **stator winding**.
- The **contact breaker** interrupts the primary current, inducing a high voltage in the **secondary winding**.
- This high voltage is delivered via the **HT cable** to the **spark plug**.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeC1[ENERGY INPUT] --> nodeC2{IGNITION TYPE}
    nodeC2 -->|EXTERNAL BATTERY| nodeC3[COIL IGNITION SYSTEM]
    nodeC2 -->|PERMANENT MAGNET| nodeC4[MAGNETO IGNITION SYSTEM]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC5[12V DC PRIMARY]
    nodeC4 --> nodeC6[50-100V AC PRIMARY]
    nodeC5 --> nodeC7[CONTACT BREAKER OPENS]
    nodeC6 --> nodeC7
    nodeC7 --> nodeC8[FLUX COLLAPSE IN IRON CORE]
    nodeC8 --> nodeC9[INDUCED HIGH VOLTAGE IN SECONDARY]
    nodeC9 --> nodeC10[SPARK PLUG FIRES]
    nodeC10 --> nodeC11[COMBUSTION INITIATED]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Magneto Ignition System. State any two of its advantages. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 | KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Magneto Ignition System** is a self-contained ignition system in which the electrical energy required to fire the spark plug is generated by a **permanent-magnet AC generator (magneto)** driven directly by the engine crankshaft. The generated AC is stepped up to a high voltage by an ignition coil and delivered to the spark plug at the correct firing instant via a contact breaker mechanism. It does not require an external battery for ignition purposes.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **No Battery Required** — The system is self-powered, making it ideal for motorcycles, aircraft piston engines, and small engines where battery weight and cost must be minimized.
2. **Lighter and More Compact** — Eliminating the battery reduces the overall vehicle weight, improving the power-to-weight ratio — a critical factor in two-wheeler and aviation applications.

*(Any other valid advantage such as 'no maintenance of battery electrolyte', 'suitable for high RPM operation' is acceptable.)*

### Question 2: Why is a capacitor connected across the contact breaker in a Coil Ignition System? [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern, Module 2]**

**Model Answer:**

A **condenser (capacitor)** of typical value **0.15 – 0.35 μF** is connected in parallel with the contact breaker points in a coil ignition system for the following reasons:

1. **To Suppress Arcing at the CB Points:**
   When the CB opens, the collapsing magnetic field in the primary winding tries to maintain current flow (due to the back-EMF of the inductor). This would cause a damaging **arc** across the CB points. The capacitor absorbs this inductive kick, **preventing arcing and pitting** of the points.

2. **To Ensure Rapid Flux Collapse (Sharp High-Voltage Spike):**
   By absorbing the energy and then rapidly discharging it back through the primary, the capacitor causes a **sharper and faster collapse** of the magnetic field. According to Faraday's law ($E = -N \, d\Phi/dt$), a faster collapse produces a **higher secondary EMF**, ensuring a strong spark.

3. **To Extend CB Point Life:**
   Reduced arcing means less wear, longer service intervals, and lower maintenance — a critical engineering consideration in service-free modern systems.

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and contains two sub-parts: (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students answer **either OR**.

---

### Question A — Option I [14 Marks]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **Coil Ignition System** used in a 4-cylinder petrol engine. State the function of each component. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 | Expected 7-Mark Long Answer]**

**Model Solution:**

**Working of a Coil Ignition System (CIS):**

A coil ignition system consists of the following components, each performing a specific function:

**1. Battery (12 V):**
Provides the primary electrical energy to the system. It supplies DC current to the primary winding of the ignition coil.

**2. Ignition Switch:**
A manually operated switch that connects or disconnects the battery to the primary circuit, allowing the driver to start/stop the engine.

**3. Ignition Coil:**
A step-up transformer consisting of a soft iron core, a **primary winding** (≈ 200 turns of thick wire, low resistance), and a **secondary winding** (≈ 20,000 turns of fine wire, high resistance). It steps up the 12 V primary supply to 20,000–30,000 V.

**4. Contact Breaker (CB) Points:**
A mechanically operated switch driven by a cam coupled to the camshaft. It opens the primary circuit at the precise instant of firing, causing a sudden flux collapse.

**5. Condenser (Capacitor):**
Connected across the CB points. It absorbs the back-EMF, prevents arcing, and ensures a sharp flux collapse for a stronger secondary voltage.

**6. Distributor:**
A rotary switch consisting of a **rotor arm** and **contact posts**. It directs the high-voltage surge from the secondary to the **correct cylinder's spark plug** in the firing order.

**7. Spark Plugs:**
One per cylinder. They provide the electrode gap where the high-voltage surge ionizes the air-fuel mixture, producing a spark.

**8. Centrifugal & Vacuum Advance Mechanisms:**
Mechanical devices that adjust the ignition timing based on engine RPM and load, ensuring optimal combustion.

**Working Sequence:**
- With the ignition ON and CB closed, current flows through the primary winding, building a magnetic field.
- The cam opens the CB at the correct instant. The sudden interruption of current causes a rapid collapse of the magnetic field.
- Per Faraday's Law, this induces a high voltage in the secondary winding.
- The distributor routes this high voltage to the appropriate spark plug, which fires and ignites the air-fuel mixture.
- The cycle repeats for the next cylinder in firing order.

---

**(b)** Compare **Coil Ignition System** and **Magneto Ignition System** on any **seven** parameters. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern, Module 2]**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Parameter | Coil Ignition System (CIS) | Magneto Ignition System (MIS) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Energy Source | External 12 V battery | Permanent magnet (self-generated) |
| 2 | Starting Reliability | Excellent, even at low cranking speed | Poor at low speed; requires **impulse coupling** |
| 3 | Secondary Voltage | 20,000 – 30,000 V | 15,000 – 20,000 V |
| 4 | Maintenance | High (CB point wear, condenser replacement) | Moderate (no battery maintenance) |
| 5 | Cost | Higher (battery + alternator cost) | Lower (no battery) |
| 6 | Weight | Heavier due to battery | Lighter, hence used in two-wheelers and aircraft |
| 7 | Spark Quality at High RPM | Drops due to contact breaker saturation | Improves with higher rotor speed |
| 8 | Common Application | Cars, SUVs, Light Motor Vehicles | Motorcycles, Mowers, Aircraft piston engines |
| 9 | Cold Start Performance | Very Good | Poor without auxiliary arrangements |
| 10 | Reliability | Good but CB bounce limits high RPM | High, since fewer moving electrical contacts |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Magneto" with "Alternator."** A magneto is a low-power permanent-magnet generator dedicated to ignition. An alternator charges the battery. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Do NOT state that magneto systems "have no contact breaker."** A classical magneto *does* have a contact breaker; it is the **CDI (Capacitor Discharge Ignition)** that is CB-less.
> 3. **Do NOT skip the function of the condenser** in CIS questions. It is the most-asked 2-mark follow-up. A 7-mark answer that omits the condenser typically loses 1 full mark.
> 4. **Avoid drawing a vertical pipe symbol `|` in tables** — use the word "vs." or em-dash to prevent markdown rendering errors during online valuation.

---

### Question B — Option II [14 Marks] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Magneto Ignition System** with a neat block diagram. Discuss its **advantages and disadvantages**. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 2]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of Magneto Ignition System:**

A magneto ignition system consists of the following components:

**1. Permanent Magnet Rotor:**
A multi-pole permanent magnet (typically 2 or 4 poles) that rotates with the engine crankshaft at engine speed. It is the primary source of magnetic flux.

**2. Stator Winding (Armature Coil):**
A stationary winding wound on a laminated iron core. As the rotor spins, the changing magnetic flux induces an **AC voltage** (50–100 V) in this winding.

**3. Contact Breaker (CB):**
Mounted on the stator. Driven by a cam coupled to the rotor, it opens at the precise firing instant to interrupt the primary current.

**4. Condenser (Capacitor):**
Connected across the CB points to suppress arcing and ensure sharp flux collapse.

**5. Ignition Coil:**
A step-up transformer with a primary and secondary winding. The primary is connected in series with the CB; the secondary delivers high voltage to the spark plug.

**6. HT Cable and Distributor:**
The high-voltage output is delivered to the spark plug(s) via a high-tension cable and, in multi-cylinder applications, a distributor.

**Working:**
- As the engine crankshaft rotates, the **rotor magnet** spins, inducing AC in the **stator winding**.
- During the half-cycle when the current direction is correct, the CB is closed and primary current builds up in the coil.
- At the correct instant, the **CB opens**, causing a sudden flux collapse in the iron core.
- A high voltage (15,000–20,000 V) is induced in the secondary winding per Faraday's Law.
- This voltage is delivered to the **spark plug** via the HT cable, generating a spark.
- The cycle repeats for the next cylinder in firing order.

**Advantages of Magneto Ignition System:**
1. **Self-Contained** — No external battery is required, reducing cost and weight.
2. **Reliable at High RPM** — Since there is no battery to drain, the system remains efficient even at high engine speeds.
3. **Compact and Lightweight** — Ideal for two-wheelers, aircraft, and small portable engines.
4. **Lower Maintenance** — No battery electrolyte, no battery terminals to corrode.

**Disadvantages of Magneto Ignition System:**
1. **Poor Cold Start** — At very low cranking RPM, the induced voltage is insufficient for a strong spark.
2. **Lower Secondary Voltage** — Typically 15,000–20,000 V compared to 20,000–30,000 V in CIS, leading to marginally weaker ignition.
3. **Complex Manufacturing** — Precise permanent magnets and tight tolerances make the magneto costlier to manufacture than a simple battery coil.
4. **Sensitive to Moisture** — Magnets and windings can degrade in humid conditions; modern units use sealed assemblies.

---

**(b)** A coil ignition system has a primary winding inductance of **6 mH** and a primary current of **3.5 A** at the moment of contact break. Calculate (i) the energy stored in the primary winding, and (ii) the spark energy available at the plug if the system efficiency is **65%**. **[7 Marks]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Numerical Pattern, Module 2]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Primary Inductance, $L = 6 \, \text{mH} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{H}$
- Primary Current at break, $I = 3.5 \, \text{A}$
- System Efficiency, $\eta = 65\% = 0.65$

**Part (i) — Energy Stored in the Primary Winding:**

The energy stored in an inductor is given by:

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$$

Substituting the given values:

$$E_{stored} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{-3} \times (3.5)^2$$

The square of the current is first evaluated.

$$(3.5)^2 = 12.25 \, \text{A}^2$$

The product is then computed.

$$E_{stored} = 0.5 \times 0.006 \times 12.25$$

$$E_{stored} = 0.03675 \, \text{J} = 36.75 \, \text{mJ}$$

**Part (ii) — Spark Energy at the Plug:**

Applying the efficiency relation:

$$E_{spark} = \eta \times E_{stored}$$

$$E_{spark} = 0.65 \times 36.75 \, \text{mJ}$$

$$E_{spark} = 23.89 \, \text{mJ}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Energy stored in primary = **36.75 mJ**
- (ii) Spark energy at plug = **23.89 mJ**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Numerical Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Always convert mH to H ($1 \, \text{mH} = 10^{-3} \, \text{H}$) *before* substitution. Forgetting this gives an answer **1000× too small** and costs 3 marks.
> 2. **Efficiency Decimal:** KTU uses $\eta = 0.65$ (not 65). Watch the decimal.
> 3. **Step Marking:** Show the formula first, then substitution, then evaluation. KTU board examiners award **2 marks for formula**, **2 marks for substitution**, **3 marks for correct final answer** in a 7-mark numerical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Your Exam!**

- [x] **Ignition System Definition:** Aggregate of components that generate and deliver a high-voltage spark to ignite the air-fuel mixture in a SI engine.
- [x] **Two Main Types:** **Coil (Battery) Ignition System (CIS)** and **Magneto Ignition System (MIS)**.
- [x] **CIS Energy Source:** External **12 V Battery**; Secondary Voltage: **20,000–30,000 V**.
- [x] **MIS Energy Source:** **Permanent Magnet (Self-generated AC)**; Secondary Voltage: **15,000–20,000 V**.
- [x] **Key Components of CIS:** Battery, Ignition Switch, Ignition Coil, Contact Breaker, Condenser, Distributor, Spark Plug.
- [x] **Key Components of MIS:** Permanent Magnet Rotor, Stator Winding, Contact Breaker, Condenser, Ignition Coil, HT Cable, Spark Plug.
- [x] **Condenser Function:** Absorbs back-EMF, prevents CB arcing, sharpens flux collapse, extends point life.
- [x] **Core Equation — Turns Ratio:** $\dfrac{E_s}{E_p} = \dfrac{N_s}{N_p}$
- [x] **Core Equation — Inductor Energy:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} L I^2$
- [x] **Core Equation — Capacitor Energy (for CDI):** $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$
- [x] **CIS Advantages:** Reliable cold start, high secondary voltage, easy timing adjustment.
- [x] **CIS Disadvantages:** CB point wear, battery dependency, frequent maintenance.
- [x] **MIS Advantages:** Self-powered, lightweight, no battery, reliable at high RPM.
- [x] **MIS Disadvantages:** Poor cold start, lower secondary voltage, requires impulse coupling for cranking.
- [x] **MIS Application:** Motorcycles, Aircraft piston engines, Lawn mowers, Outboard motors.
- [x] **CIS Application:** Cars, SUVs, Light Commercial Vehicles, most modern LMVs.
- [x] **Auto-Ignition Temperature of Petrol:** ≈ **250 °C** (remember for comparison questions).
- [x] **Spark Plug Gap:** **0.6–1.0 mm** (use this in any gap-related numerical).
- [x] **Spark Duration:** **1–3 ms** (typical range KTU expects).
- [x] **Ignition Advance Range:** **5°–45° BTDC** depending on engine RPM and load.
- [x] **Modern Derivatives:** DLI (Distributor-Less Ignition) and COP (Coil-on-Plug) — mention in trends question.
- [x] **CDI Stands for:** **Capacitor Discharge Ignition** — used in most modern two-wheelers as an evolution of magneto.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Starting System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Starting System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting System:** The starting system of an automobile is an electromechanical assembly that converts stored electrical energy from the battery into mechanical energy (rotational motion) to crank the internal combustion (IC) engine until it reaches a self-sustaining speed (self-ignition threshold), at which point the engine begins to run on its own power without external assistance.

The starting system essentially performs the function of providing the **initial cranking torque** required to overcome the static friction, compression resistance, and inertial loads of the engine's reciprocating and rotating components during the very first revolutions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Push-Start of Life"**
> Imagine a heavy, stationary flywheel that you want to spin. If you simply leave it alone, it will never move because of static friction. You need an external "push" to overcome this initial resistance. Once it gains some angular momentum, it tends to continue rotating more easily.
>
> The **Starting System** in an automobile is exactly this "external push" for the engine. The engine is the heavy flywheel (with all its pistons, crankshaft, and connecting rods), and the **starter motor + battery** is the strong friend giving it the initial push. Once the engine speed crosses a critical threshold (called the **self-sustaining speed** or **firing speed**), the combustion process takes over, and the engine runs on its own.

### 1.3 Essential Requirements of a Good Starting System

A properly designed starting system must satisfy the following engineering criteria:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Key Requirements:**
> 1. **Cranking the Engine:** Must rotate the crankshaft at a minimum speed of **60–100 rpm** for petrol engines and **80–200 rpm** for diesel engines to achieve compression ignition.
> 2. **Generating Sufficient Torque:** Must produce a starting torque of **40–80 N·m** (typical for a 1.5 L engine) to overcome compression resistance.
> 3. **Engaging and Disengaging Automatically:** The pinion gear must mesh with the flywheel only during cranking and disengage once the engine fires.
> 4. **Drawing Reasonable Current:** The cranking current typically ranges from **150 A to 400 A** depending on engine size.
> 5. **Safe and Reliable Operation:** Must incorporate safety features to prevent re-engagement while the engine is running.

### 1.4 Major Components of the Starting System

| Component | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Storage Battery (12 V / 24 V)** | Source of electrical energy. Typically **lead-acid battery** rated at **30–100 Ah**. |
| **Ignition Switch** | User-operated control to initiate the starting sequence. |
| **Starter Solenoid (Relay)** | High-current switch that engages the starter motor and shifts the pinion. |
| **Starter Motor** | DC series motor that converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation. |
| **Starter Drive (Bendix/Overrunning Clutch)** | Transmits torque from motor to flywheel and disengages when engine starts. |
| **Flywheel Ring Gear** | Receives torque from the pinion gear (typical ratio **10:1 to 15:1**). |
| **Wiring Harness & Cables** | High-gauge cables to carry cranking current. |

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Values to Memorise:**
> - Battery Voltage (Light Vehicles): **12 V**
> - Battery Voltage (Heavy Vehicles/Commercial): **24 V**
> - Starter Motor Type: **DC Series Motor** (4-pole, 4-brush)
> - Cranking Speed: **100–200 rpm** (at pinion shaft)
> - Engine Cranking Speed Required: **60–200 rpm** (at flywheel)
> - Starter Motor Power: **0.6 kW to 5 kW** (for passenger cars); up to **15 kW** (for heavy-duty diesel)
> - Cranking Current: **150 A – 400 A** (typical); can peak at **600 A** in cold weather
> - Gear Reduction Ratio: **10:1 to 15:1**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The operation of a conventional electric starting system follows a **sequence of 5 critical stages**, which is a frequent short-answer topic in KTU examinations.

**Stage 1 — Driver Action:**
When the driver turns the ignition key to the "START" position, a small control current (typically **0.5 A to 1.5 A**) flows through the ignition switch to the **starter solenoid** (also called the starter relay).

**Stage 2 — Solenoid Activation:**
This small current energises the solenoid coil, creating a strong magnetic field. The solenoid performs **two simultaneous functions**:
- **Function A (Contact Closure):** It pulls a plunger that closes a set of heavy-duty copper contacts, allowing the **high cranking current (150 A – 400 A)** to flow from the battery to the starter motor.
- **Function B (Pinion Engagement):** The same plunger motion pushes the **pinion gear** forward along the motor shaft (via a shift lever or fork) so that it meshes with the **flywheel ring gear**.

**Stage 3 — Motor Rotation:**
The starter motor (a **DC series motor**) receives the high current and begins to rotate. Because the pinion is now engaged with the flywheel, the motor's rotation is transferred to the engine's crankshaft, causing the engine to crank.

**Stage 4 — Engine Fires:**
Once the engine speed reaches the self-sustaining threshold (combustion begins), the engine begins to run under its own power. At this point, the flywheel is now rotating much faster than the pinion.

**Stage 5 — Disengagement:**
The **overrunning clutch (or Bendix drive)** allows the pinion to freewheel, preventing the high-speed flywheel from driving the starter motor as a generator (which would otherwise destroy the armature winding due to excessive centrifugal forces). When the driver releases the key, the solenoid de-energises, the contacts open, and the pinion retracts from the flywheel.

### 2.2 Why a DC Series Motor?

> [!NOTE]
> The starter motor **must** be a DC series motor due to its unique torque characteristics.

A DC series motor has its field winding connected in **series** with the armature. Therefore:
- The field flux $\phi$ is directly proportional to the armature current: $\phi \propto I_a$
- The torque equation is: $T \propto \phi \cdot I_a \propto I_a^2$

This gives the **most important property**: **High Starting Torque**. At the moment of starting, the armature is stationary, so back-EMF is zero, and the current surges to its maximum. This maximum current produces the maximum torque, which is exactly what is needed to overcome the engine's compression resistance.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = k_t \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque produced by a DC motor | N·m |
| 2 | $T \propto I_a^2$ | Series motor torque-current relation | — |
| 3 | $E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ | Back-EMF equation (KVL of armature circuit) | Volts |
| 4 | $E_b = \frac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ | Generated EMF in a DC machine | Volts |
| 5 | $N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{k \cdot \phi}$ | Speed of DC motor | rpm |
| 6 | $P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical power output | Watts |
| 7 | $N_{engine} = \frac{N_{motor}}{G}$ | Engine speed via gear reduction (G = ratio) | rpm |
| 8 | $T_{engine} = T_{motor} \times G \times \eta$ | Torque transmitted to engine (with efficiency $\eta$) | N·m |
| 9 | $I_{cranking} = \frac{V}{R_a + R_{se} + R_{cable}}$ | Initial cranking current (no back-EMF) | Amperes |
| 10 | $Q = I \times t$ | Battery capacity (charge) | Ampere-hours (Ah) |

### 2.4 Starter Drive Mechanisms — A Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Topic:** Students must know the **Bendix Drive** and the **Overrunning Clutch (Roller-Type) Drive**.

**A. Bendix Drive (Inertia Engagement):**
- The pinion is mounted on a **threaded sleeve** (helical splines) on the motor shaft.
- When the motor spins up, the **inertia** of the pinion causes it to "screw" outward along the shaft and engage the flywheel.
- Once the engine fires, the higher-speed flywheel drives the pinion faster than the motor shaft. The pinion then "unscrews" and disengages automatically.
- **Disadvantage:** Limited to smaller engines because the engagement force depends on inertia (not suitable for heavy diesel engines).

**B. Overrunning Clutch Drive (Positive Engagement):**
- The pinion is moved into mesh **mechanically by the solenoid lever** (positive engagement).
- The clutch uses **rollers** inside a race that lock in one direction (motor drives engine) and freewheel in the other (engine drives motor).
- **Advantage:** Suitable for **heavy-duty engines**, more reliable engagement, and can be mounted at an angle.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The starting system is a critical safety and reliability subsystem. Modern vehicles increasingly use **Start-Stop systems** and **Integrated Starter Generators (ISG)** to improve fuel efficiency, where the starter is also used to restart the engine during traffic stops. Understanding the classical system forms the foundation for these advanced technologies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Back-EMF and Speed Equation of Starter Motor

The starter motor is a 4-pole, 4-brush DC series motor. The governing equations are derived below in full detail.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the Armature Circuit:**

For a DC series motor, the field winding is in series with the armature, so the total circuit resistance is $(R_a + R_{se})$, where $R_a$ is the armature resistance and $R_{se}$ is the series field resistance.

The supply voltage $V$ must overcome three quantities:
- The voltage drop across the armature resistance: $I_a R_a$
- The voltage drop across the series field resistance: $I_a R_{se}$
- The back-EMF: $E_b$

By KVL:

$$
V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})
$$

Rearranging for the back-EMF:

$$
E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})
$$

**Step 2 — Express the Back-EMF in terms of Speed and Flux:**

The general EMF equation of a DC machine is:

$$
E_b = \frac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}
$$

where:
- $P$ = number of poles
- $\phi$ = flux per pole in Weber (Wb)
- $N$ = speed of the armature in rpm
- $Z$ = total number of armature conductors
- $A$ = number of parallel paths in the armature

For a **wave-wound** armature, $A = 2$. For a **lap-wound** armature, $A = P$.

**Step 3 — Equate and Solve for Speed N:**

Equating the two expressions for $E_b$:

$$
\frac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A} = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})
$$

Solving for the speed $N$:

$$
N = \frac{60 \cdot A \cdot (V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}))}{P \cdot \phi \cdot Z}
$$

This is usually written in a compact form by combining all machine constants into a single constant $K$:

$$
N = \frac{V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})}{K \cdot \phi}
$$

where $K = \dfrac{P \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$.

### 3.2 Worked Example — KTU Model Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (Typical KTU 14-mark style question):**
> A 4-pole, 600-conductor, wave-wound DC series motor is used as a starter motor. The armature resistance is $0.015\ \Omega$ and the series field resistance is $0.010\ \Omega$. The motor draws **300 A** from a **12 V** battery. Calculate: (a) the back-EMF, (b) the speed of the motor at this load, and (c) the mechanical power developed.

**Given Data:**
- $P = 4$ poles
- $Z = 600$ conductors
- $A = 2$ (wave winding)
- $R_a = 0.015\ \Omega$
- $R_{se} = 0.010\ \Omega$
- $I_a = 300\ \text{A}$
- $V = 12\ \text{V}$
- Assume flux per pole $\phi = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Wb} = 0.005\ \text{Wb}$ (typical for starter motor at this current)

**Part (a) — Back-EMF Calculation:**

Applying the KVL equation:

$$
E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se})
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
E_b = 12 - 300 \times (0.015 + 0.010)
$$

$$
E_b = 12 - 300 \times 0.025
$$

$$
E_b = 12 - 7.5
$$

$$
\boxed{E_b = 4.5\ \text{V}}
$$

**Part (b) — Speed of the Motor:**

Using the EMF equation:

$$
E_b = \frac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}
$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$
N = \frac{E_b \cdot 60 \cdot A}{P \cdot \phi \cdot Z}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
N = \frac{4.5 \times 60 \times 2}{4 \times 0.005 \times 600}
$$

$$
N = \frac{540}{12}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 45\ \text{rpm}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The pinion shaft rotates at only **45 rpm** when cranking. With a typical gear reduction ratio of **10:1**, the engine crankshaft speed is $45 \times 10 = 450\ \text{rpm}$, which is well above the self-sustaining threshold.

**Part (c) — Mechanical Power Developed:**

The gross mechanical power developed by the armature is given by:

$$
P_{developed} = E_b \times I_a
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{developed} = 4.5 \times 300 = 1350\ \text{W} = 1.35\ \text{kW}
$$

The **output power** at the shaft (after subtracting rotational losses) is typically **5–10% less**:

$$
P_{output} = P_{developed} \times \eta_{motor}
$$

Assuming a motor efficiency of $\eta = 0.90$:

$$
P_{output} = 1.35 \times 0.90 = 1.215\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{output} \approx 1.22\ \text{kW}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Starter Motor Selection

| Parameter | Petrol Engine (1.0–2.0 L) | Diesel Engine (Small) | Heavy-Duty Diesel (Truck) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Battery Voltage** | 12 V | 12 V | 24 V |
| **Motor Power** | 0.8 – 1.4 kW | 1.5 – 3.0 kW | 5 – 15 kW |
| **Cranking Current** | 150 – 250 A | 250 – 400 A | 400 – 800 A |
| **Drive Type** | Bendix (Inertia) | Overrunning Clutch | Overrunning Clutch |
| **Required Cranking Speed** | 60 – 100 rpm | 100 – 150 rpm | 150 – 200 rpm |

### 3.4 Cranking Torque Transmission Analysis

The torque delivered to the engine crankshaft is the motor torque multiplied by the **gear reduction ratio G** and the **mechanical efficiency $\eta_m$** of the drive mechanism:

$$
T_{engine} = T_{motor} \times G \times \eta_m
$$

The motor torque is calculated from the developed power:

$$
T_{motor} = \frac{P_{output} \times 60}{2 \pi N_{motor}}
$$

**Example:** If the starter motor produces $1.22\ \text{kW}$ at $45\ \text{rpm}$:

$$
T_{motor} = \frac{1220 \times 60}{2 \pi \times 45} = \frac{73200}{282.74}
$$

$$
T_{motor} \approx 258.9\ \text{N·m}
$$

With a gear ratio of $G = 10$ and efficiency $\eta_m = 0.85$:

$$
T_{engine} = 258.9 \times 10 \times 0.85 = 2200.7\ \text{N·m}
$$

This is a huge torque — more than sufficient to crank any passenger car engine.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Starting System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    battNode1[STORAGE BATTERY 12V / 24V] -->|High Current Cable| solNode2[STARTER SOLENOID RELAY]
    igNode0[IGNITION SWITCH START POSITION] -->|Low Current Control Signal 0.5A| solNode2
    solNode2 -->|Plunger Motion Mechanical| shiftNode3[SHIFT LEVER FORK]
    solNode2 -->|Closed Heavy Contacts Electrical| motorNode4[DC SERIES STARTER MOTOR]
    shiftNode3 -->|Pinion Engagement| driveNode5[PINION GEAR + OVERRUNNING CLUTCH]
    motorNode4 -->|Rotational Output| driveNode5
    driveNode5 -->|Meshed Gear Teeth| flyNode6[FLYWHEEL RING GEAR]
    flyNode6 -->|Cranking Torque| crankNode7[ENGINE CRANKSHAFT]
    crankNode7 -->|Engine Fires| runState[ENGINE RUNS UNDER OWN POWER]
    runState -->|Freewheels Pinion| driveNode5
    igNode0 -->|Key Released| solNode2
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Left Branch (Control):** The ignition switch sends a low-current signal to the solenoid.
- **Centre Branch (Power):** The battery feeds the starter motor through the solenoid's heavy contacts.
- **Right Branch (Mechanical):** The motor shaft, via the shift lever, pushes the pinion into mesh with the flywheel.

### 4.2 Internal Schematic of a DC Series Starter Motor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    term1[BATTERY POSITIVE TERMINAL] --> brush1NodeA[POSITIVE BRUSH]
    brush1NodeA --> armature1NodeB[ARMATURE WINDING ON LAMINATED CORE]
    armature1NodeB --> brush2NodeC[NEGATIVE BRUSH]
    brush2NodeC --> field1NodeD[SERIES FIELD WINDING POLE 1]
    field1NodeD --> field2NodeE[SERIES FIELD WINDING POLE 2]
    field2NodeE --> term2[BATTERY NEGATIVE TERMINAL]
    armature1NodeB --> shaft1NodeF[OUTPUT SHAFT WITH PINION]
    shaft1NodeF --> end1NodeG[TO FLYWHEEL RING GEAR]
```

### 4.3 Sequence-of-Operation Flowchart

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s0[OFF State Engine Stationary] --> s1[Key Turned to START]
    s1 --> s2[Solenoid Energised Pinion Shifts Forward]
    s2 --> s3[Motor Contacts Close Cranking Current Flows]
    s3 --> s4[Motor Spins Pinion Engages Flywheel]
    s4 --> s5{Engine Speed Check}
    s5 -->|Below Firing Speed| s4
    s5 -->|Above Firing Speed Engine Fires| s6[Overrunning Clutch Disengages Pinion]
    s6 --> s7[Driver Releases Key]
    s7 --> s8[Solenoid De energises Contacts Open]
    s8 --> s9[Pinion Retracts to Rest Position]
    s9 --> s0
```

### 4.4 Detailed Component Map of the Starter Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Starter Motor Housing] --> B[Commutator Assembly]
    A --> C[Brush Holder with 4 Carbon Brushes]
    A --> D[Series Field Coils 4 Poles]
    A --> E[Armature Core with Slots]
    A --> F[Drive End Housing]
    F --> G[Shift Lever Fork]
    F --> H[Overrunning Clutch Assembly]
    F --> I[Pinion Gear with Roller Clutch]
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J[Mates With Flywheel Ring Gear]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q1. Define the starting system of an automobile. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
The starting system is the electromechanical system that provides the initial cranking torque to rotate the engine crankshaft until the engine starts running under its own power. The main components are: (1) **Storage battery**, (2) **Ignition switch**, (3) **Starter solenoid (relay)**, (4) **DC series starter motor**, (5) **Starter drive (Bendix or overrunning clutch)**, and (6) **Flywheel ring gear**. The system converts **12 V DC electrical energy** into mechanical rotation of the engine.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. Why is a DC series motor used as a starter motor and not a DC shunt motor?**

**Model Answer:**
A DC series motor is used because its torque is proportional to the **square of the armature current** ($T \propto I_a^2$). At the moment of starting, the armature is stationary, so the back-EMF is zero and the current is maximum. This produces a **very high starting torque**, which is necessary to overcome the engine's compression resistance. A DC shunt motor, in contrast, has flux independent of current and produces a relatively low starting torque, making it unsuitable for engine cranking.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing the **gear ratio direction**: Some students wrongly multiply motor speed by the gear ratio instead of dividing. Remember: the **motor spins faster** than the engine, so $N_{engine} = N_{motor} / G$.
> 2. Forgetting to use **total resistance** $R_a + R_{se}$ in the KVL equation for series motors.
> 3. Mixing up **developed power** ($E_b \times I_a$) with **output power** ($E_b \times I_a \times \eta$).
> 4. Failing to mention the **overrunning clutch** role in preventing the engine from driving the starter as a generator after firing.

---

#### **QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
> **Q (A). (a)** Explain the working of an automobile starting system with a neat block diagram, describing the role of each component. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** A 12 V DC series starter motor has an armature resistance of $0.02\ \Omega$ and a series field resistance of $0.01\ \Omega$. The motor draws a current of 250 A while cranking. Calculate: (i) the back-EMF, (ii) the power developed, and (iii) the power input to the motor. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The starting system operates in the following sequence:
1. **Battery** supplies DC power (typically 12 V, 30–60 Ah capacity).
2. **Ignition switch** is turned to the START position, sending a low-current signal (about 1 A) to the starter solenoid.
3. **Starter solenoid** performs dual functions: (i) closes the high-current contacts between battery and motor, and (ii) mechanically pushes the **pinion gear** into mesh with the **flywheel ring gear** via a shift lever.
4. **DC series starter motor** receives the high current and rotates, producing high torque due to its $T \propto I_a^2$ characteristic.
5. **Pinion gear** (typically with 10–12 teeth) drives the flywheel ring gear (with 100–150 teeth), giving a gear reduction of 10:1 to 15:1, which multiplies the torque delivered to the engine.
6. **Overrunning clutch** allows the motor to drive the engine, but freewheels once the engine fires and spins faster than the motor shaft, protecting the armature from overspeed damage.
7. When the driver releases the key, the solenoid de-energises, the contacts open, and the pinion retracts.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $V = 12\ \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.02\ \Omega$, $R_{se} = 0.01\ \Omega$, $I_a = 250\ \text{A}$

**(i) Back-EMF:**

$$
E_b = V - I_a(R_a + R_{se}) = 12 - 250 \times (0.02 + 0.01)
$$

$$
E_b = 12 - 250 \times 0.03 = 12 - 7.5
$$

$$
\boxed{E_b = 4.5\ \text{V}}
$$

**(ii) Power Developed (Armature Output):**

$$
P_{dev} = E_b \times I_a = 4.5 \times 250 = 1125\ \text{W} = 1.125\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{dev} = 1.125\ \text{kW}}
$$

**(iii) Power Input to the Motor:**

$$
P_{input} = V \times I_a = 12 \times 250 = 3000\ \text{W} = 3.0\ \text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{input} = 3.0\ \text{kW}}
$$

**Bonus — Motor Efficiency (for examiner's appreciation):**

$$
\eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{dev}}{P_{input}} = \frac{1.125}{3.0} = 0.375 = 37.5\%
$$

*(Note: Low efficiency is acceptable for a starter motor since it operates for very short durations of 5–10 seconds.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Q (B). (a)** Compare the **Bendix drive** and the **Overrunning clutch drive** used in starter motors. State two advantages and one limitation of each. **[7 Marks]**
> **(b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of a **DC series motor** used in an automobile starter. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Feature | Bendix Drive (Inertia Type) | Overrunning Clutch (Positive Engagement) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Engagement Mechanism** | Inertia of pinion causes it to "screw" onto the motor shaft and engage the flywheel. | Solenoid lever mechanically pushes the pinion into the flywheel. |
| **Disengagement** | Pinion unscrews when the flywheel spins faster than the motor. | Roller clutch freewheels; pinion is retracted by the solenoid spring. |
| **Suitability** | Light petrol engines only. | Both petrol and heavy diesel engines. |
| **Advantage 1** | Simple, low-cost design. | Positive engagement — reliable for high torque. |
| **Advantage 2** | No solenoid shift lever required. | Can be mounted at an angle. |
| **Limitation 1** | Limited engagement force (depends on inertia) — not for diesel. | More complex and expensive. |

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Construction of DC Series Starter Motor:**
- **Yoke (Field Frame):** Made of cast steel; provides the magnetic return path and houses the **4 field poles** with **series field windings** connected in series with the armature.
- **Armature:** A laminated silicon-steel core mounted on the motor shaft, with copper conductors placed in the slots. The conductors are connected to a **commutator** at one end.
- **Commutator:** A cylindrical assembly of copper segments insulated from each other by mica. It reverses the current direction in the armature coils to ensure continuous unidirectional rotation.
- **Brushes:** 4 carbon brushes (2 positive, 2 negative) press against the commutator to supply current to the armature.
- **Pinion Shaft:** The extended motor shaft carries the **pinion gear** (10–12 teeth).

**Working Principle:**
When current flows through the **series field winding** and the **armature** (in series), a magnetic flux is established in the field poles. Simultaneously, current flows through the armature conductors. The interaction between the **armature current** and the **field flux** produces a **force** on each conductor (by Fleming's Left-Hand Rule), generating a **torque** that rotates the armature. Since $T \propto I_a^2$, the starting torque is extremely high, making the motor ideal for cranking the engine.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Starting System**

- **Primary Function:** Crank the engine to self-sustaining speed.
- **Battery:** Always **12 V** for cars, **24 V** for heavy commercial vehicles.
- **Starter Motor Type:** **DC series motor** — chosen for its $T \propto I_a^2$ high starting torque.
- **Starter Motor Power Range:** **0.6 kW – 5 kW** (cars), up to **15 kW** (trucks).
- **Cranking Current:** Typically **150 A – 400 A**.
- **Gear Reduction Ratio:** **10:1 to 15:1** (pinion to flywheel).
- **Bendix Drive:** Inertia engagement; for light engines only.
- **Overrunning Clutch:** Positive engagement; for all engine types, prevents back-drive.
- **Solenoid Role:** Dual — closes high-current contacts AND shifts the pinion.
- **Key KVL Equation:** $V = E_b + I_a(R_a + R_{se})$ — **memorise this.**
- **EMF Equation:** $E_b = \dfrac{P \cdot \phi \cdot N \cdot Z}{60 \cdot A}$ — use wave winding $A = 2$.
- **Mechanical Power Developed:** $P_{dev} = E_b \times I_a$ (not $V \times I_a$).
- **Self-Sustaining Speed:** Petrol **~60–80 rpm**; Diesel **~100–200 rpm**.
- **Safety Feature:** Overrunning clutch prevents engine from driving the starter as a generator.
- **Bendix** = uses **inertia**; **Overrunning** = uses **solenoid lever** (mechanical push).
- **Why not shunt motor?** Shunt motor torque is low at start (since flux is constant), so it cannot crank a heavy engine.
- **Numerical Approach:** Always find $E_b$ first using KVL, then speed, then power.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lighting System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lighting System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automotive Lighting System** is defined as an integrated electro-optical subsystem of a motor vehicle comprising headlamps, signal lamps, tail lamps, interior lamps, and associated control circuits designed to provide road illumination, vehicle signaling, and visibility under all ambient operating conditions as mandated by AIS-008, AIS-010, ECE R7, R48, R98, and R112 standards.

**Key Functional Objectives of the Lighting System:**

| Objective | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Road Illumination** | Forward visibility for the driver during low-light conditions |
| **Vehicle Conspicuity** | Making the vehicle visible to other road users |
| **Information Signaling** | Communicating driver intent (turn, brake, reverse) |
| **Legal Compliance** | Adherence to AIS/ECE/SAE photometric regulations |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the automotive lighting system as the **"eyes and voice"** of the vehicle.
> - **Headlamps** are like the **eyes** of the driver projected forward — they "see" the road ahead in darkness.
> - **Tail, brake, and turn lamps** are like the **voice** of the driver — they "speak" intentions (stopping, turning) to vehicles behind.
> - **Reflectors** inside the lamp assembly are like **mirrors used in a torchlight** — they gather light from a small bulb and project it in a controlled, parallel beam over a long distance.

### 1.3 Fundamental Photometric Quantities (Standard SI Units)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **fundamental physical constants and SI photometric units** form the mathematical backbone of every lighting calculation in automobile engineering.

- **Luminous Intensity (I)** — measured in **candela (cd)** — the light-radiating power of a source in a given direction per unit solid angle.
- **Luminous Flux (Φ)** — measured in **lumen (lm)** — the total quantity of light energy emitted per second.
- **Illuminance (E)** — measured in **lux (lx)** — the luminous flux incident per unit area on a surface.
- **Luminance (L)** — measured in **candela per square metre (cd/m²)** — the brightness as perceived by the human eye.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Quick Memory Trick:** *Intensity is "per steradian", Flux is "total", Illuminance is "per square metre", and Luminance is "per square metre from the surface itself".*

### 1.4 Classification of Automotive Light Sources

Automotive light sources have evolved through five generations, each offering higher luminous efficacy:

| Generation | Light Source | Luminous Efficacy | Color Temperature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st Gen | **Incandescent / Vacuum Tungsten** | 10–15 lm/W | 2,800 K |
| 2nd Gen | **Halogen (Tungsten-Halogen)** | 18–25 lm/W | 3,200 K |
| 3rd Gen | **HID / Xenon (HID-D)** | 80–100 lm/W | 4,300 K |
| 4th Gen | **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** | 100–150 lm/W | 5,500–6,500 K |
| 5th Gen | **Laser-Assisted Phosphor** | 170+ lm/W | 5,500 K |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Headlamp Construction — Component-Wise Breakdown

A modern automotive headlamp is an assembly of six critical optical and structural components:

- **Bulb / Light Source:** Converts electrical energy into luminous flux (e.g., H4, H7, D1S, D3S, LED module).
- **Reflector:** A parabolic or free-form ellipsoidal mirrored surface that collimates divergent rays from the bulb into a near-parallel forward beam.
- **Lens (Lens & Prism Assembly):** A moulded polycarbonate/glass cover with internal Fresnel prisms that refract and redistribute light to form the specified beam pattern.
- **Housing / Bezel:** A weather-sealed enclosure that protects the optical assembly from dust, moisture, and vibration.
- **Aiming Screws:** Mechanical adjusters for vertical and horizontal alignment per AIS-010 / ECE R112.
- **Wiring Harness & Connector:** Supplies regulated DC voltage from the vehicle's battery/alternator system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Parabolic Reflector — Why Parabola?**
> A parabolic surface has the unique geometric property that **all rays emanating from its focus reflect parallel to the principal axis**. This is mathematically derived from the conic-section equation:
> $$y^{2} = 4fx$$
> where $f$ is the focal length. The bulb filament is placed precisely at the focus to produce a collimated beam.

### 2.2 Photometric Laws Governing Headlamp Illumination

The headlamp illumination problem is solved using two foundational photometric laws:

#### 2.2.1 Inverse Square Law of Illuminance

> [!NOTE]
> **Law Statement:** The illuminance produced on a surface by a point source is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance** from the source, provided the light is incident normally (perpendicular) to the surface.

$$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}}$$

where:
- $E$ = illuminance (lux, lx)
- $I$ = luminous intensity of the source (candela, cd)
- $d$ = distance between source and illuminated surface (metres, m)

#### 2.2.2 Lambert's Cosine Law (Oblique Incidence Correction)

When the light falls on a surface at an angle $\theta$ to the normal:

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$

This correction is critical when the headlamp is tilted or the road surface is angled.

#### 2.2.3 Luminous Flux — Solid Angle Relationship

$$\Phi = I \cdot \omega$$

where $\omega$ is the solid angle in steradians. A full sphere subtends $4\pi$ steradians.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield equations** for the Lighting System topic. Memorize these for the KTU ESE.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$ | Inverse Square Law of Illuminance | lux (lx) |
| 2 | $E = \dfrac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$ | Lambert's Cosine Law (oblique incidence) | lux (lx) |
| 3 | $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$ | Luminous Flux — solid angle relation | lumen (lm) |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{\Phi_{useful}}{\Phi_{total}}$ | Luminous efficacy of a lamp | lm/W |
| 5 | $y^{2} = 4fx$ | Standard parabolic reflector equation | metres (m) |
| 6 | $\omega = \dfrac{A}{r^{2}}$ | Solid angle definition | steradian (sr) |
| 7 | $L = \dfrac{I}{A \cdot \cos\theta}$ | Luminance from a surface element | cd/m² |
| 8 | $V = \dfrac{E_{max}}{E_{avg}}$ | Uniformity ratio (headlamp beam) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Note on Table Syntax:** All absolute-value notations in the answer sheet must be written using $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ form. Avoid using the vertical pipe character $\vert$ inside markdown tables, as it breaks KTU's online answer-sheet parser.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why These Laws Matter in Real Vehicles

- **Inverse Square Law** dictates that a headlamp beam loses intensity rapidly with distance — explaining why **high beams** are needed for distant illumination (≥100 m).
- **Cosine Law** governs the **cut-off line** in a low-beam pattern — the beam is angled downward (typically 1° below horizontal) to avoid dazzling oncoming drivers while still illuminating 50–75 m of road.
- **Luminous Efficacy** directly impacts **alternator loading** — an LED consumes ~15 W versus 55 W for halogen, reducing fuel consumption by ~0.2–0.5 %.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Inverse Square Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Premise:** Consider an ideal point source $S$ that emits light uniformly in all directions with a luminous intensity $I$ (candela) in every direction. Imagine two concentric spheres of radii $r_{1}$ and $r_{2}$ surrounding the source.

**Step 1 — Define Luminous Flux Through a Sphere of Radius $r$:**

A sphere of radius $r$ has surface area:
$$A = 4\pi r^{2}$$

Since luminous flux $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$ and a full sphere subtends $\omega = 4\pi$ steradians:
$$\Phi = I \cdot 4\pi$$

**Step 2 — Express Illuminance on the Spherical Surface:**

By definition, illuminance $E$ is the flux per unit area. Dividing flux by surface area:
$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} = \frac{I \cdot 4\pi}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the Expression:**

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I}{r^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Result:**

This shows that illuminance $E$ is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance**. If distance doubles, illuminance drops to one-fourth. If distance triples, illuminance drops to one-ninth.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Cut-Off Angle for Low Beam

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE for the Lighting System topic.

**Step 1 — Establish the Geometric Setup:**

Consider a headlamp mounted at height $H$ above the road surface, with the optical axis inclined downward at angle $\alpha$ from the horizontal. The beam strikes a point on the road at horizontal distance $L$ ahead of the vehicle.

**Step 2 — Apply the Tangent Relation:**

From right-triangle geometry:
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{H}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Cosine Law of Illuminance:**

The illuminance at the road point, when the beam axis makes angle $\theta$ with the road normal:
$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$

Here, $\theta = 90° - \alpha$ (angle between beam axis and road normal), so $\cos\theta = \sin\alpha$. Also, $d = \dfrac{L}{\cos\alpha}$.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Illuminance Equation:**

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \sin\alpha}{\left(\dfrac{L}{\cos\alpha}\right)^{2}}$$

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \cos^{2}\alpha}{L^{2}}$$

Using the identity $\sin\alpha \cdot \cos^{2}\alpha = \cos\alpha \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \cos\alpha$:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\alpha \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot \cos\alpha}{L^{2}} = \frac{I \cdot \sin(2\alpha)}{2 L^{2}}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

This derived equation is **directly used by AIS-010 / ECE R112** to specify the cut-off line. The standard mandates that at $\alpha = 0.57°$ (i.e., 1 % drop), the high-intensity zone (≥ 50 lux) terminates, preventing glare to oncoming traffic.

### 3.3 Numerical Solved Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 Practice Set):** A headlamp produces a luminous intensity of $25{,}000$ cd along its axis. Calculate the illuminance on a road surface located $50$ m ahead when the beam axis is perpendicular to the road.

**Given:**
- $I = 25{,}000$ cd
- $d = 50$ m
- $\theta = 0°$ (perpendicular incidence, so $\cos\theta = 1$)

**Solution:**

Applying the Inverse Square Law:
$$E = \frac{I}{d^{2}} = \frac{25{,}000}{(50)^{2}}$$

$$E = \frac{25{,}000}{2{,}500}$$

$$\boxed{E = 10 \text{ lux}}$$

**Step-by-Step Verification:**
1. Convert $d^{2} = 50 \times 50 = 2{,}500$ m².
2. Divide intensity by area: $25{,}000 / 2{,}500 = 10$.
3. Units: $\text{cd} / \text{m}^{2} = \text{lux}$. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Beam Pattern Classification

| Beam Type | Aim Point | Range of Illumination | Glare Risk | Indian AIS Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Driving Beam (High)** | Straight ahead, 0° horizontal | 100–150 m | High (oncoming traffic) | AIS-008 |
| **Passing Beam (Low)** | 1° below horizontal, asymmetric | 50–75 m | Low (cut-off at HV) | AIS-010 |
| **Fog Beam** | Wide, low, sharp upper cut-off | 15–25 m | Very low | AIS-010 |
| **Adaptive Beam (AFS)** | Steered by steering angle | Variable 40–120 m | Auto-managed | ECE R123 |

### 3.5 Sealed-Beam vs. Composite Headlamp — Analytical Comparison

| Parameter | Sealed Beam | Composite (Replaceable Bulb) |
|---|---|---|
| **Bulb Replacement** | Whole unit replaced | Only bulb replaced |
| **Cost per Replacement** | High | Low |
| **Optical Precision** | Excellent (factory-sealed) | Good (alignment may drift) |
| **Serviceability** | Poor in field | Excellent in field |
| **Modern Usage** | Obsolete (pre-1985) | Universal today |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Halogen Headlamp

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle Battery 12V DC] --> B[Headlamp Switch]
    B --> C[Relay Coil]
    C --> D[Relay Contacts Close]
    D --> E[Headlamp Fuse 15A]
    E --> F[High Beam Filament]
    E --> G[Low Beam Filament]
    F --> H[Parabolic Reflector]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Fresnel Lens Assembly]
    I --> J[Road Surface]
    K[Steering Column Lever] --> B
    L[Dip Switch Selector] --> F
    L --> G
```

### 4.2 Light Distribution Pattern (Z-Beam Asymmetric Low Beam)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HORIZON["HORIZONTAL PLANE THROUGH H-V POINT"]
        HV["H-V (Hori-Verti Reference Point)"]
    end
    HV --> UP["Above HV: Dark Zone (Glare-Free)"]
    HV --> DN1["Below HV: Hot Zone (50-75 lux)"]
    HV --> LT["Left of HV: Spread Zone"]
    HV --> RT["Right of HV: Asymmetric Bright Zone"]
    UP -.->|-0.57 deg cut-off| DN1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Adaptive Front-Lighting System (AFS)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Steering Angle Sensor] --> ECU[AFS Control ECU]
    S2[Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> ECU
    S3[Yaw Rate Sensor] --> ECU
    S4[Ambient Light Sensor] --> ECU
    ECU --> M1[Left Headlamp Swivel Motor]
    ECU --> M2[Right Headlamp Swivel Motor]
    M1 --> LH[Left Headlamp Module]
    M2 --> RH[Right Headlamp Module]
    LH --> BEAM1[Steered Beam Pattern]
    RH --> BEAM2[Steered Beam Pattern]
```

### 4.4 Complete Vehicle Lighting Circuit Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[Battery 12V] --> IG[Ignition Switch]
    IG --> SW[Light Control Switch]
    SW --> RL[Relay]
    RL --> HL[Headlamps Left and Right]
    SW --> TL[Tail Lamps]
    SW --> SL[Side Marker Lamps]
    HL --> F1[Fuse 15A]
    TL --> F2[Fuse 10A]
    SL --> F3[Fuse 5A]
    HL -.-> IND[High Beam Indicator]
    BAT --> IND
    BAT --> SIG[Turn Signal Switch]
    SIG --> TLF[Front Turn Lamps]
    SIG --> TLR[Rear Turn Lamps]
    BRK[Brake Pedal Switch] --> BLS[Brake Lamps]
```

### 4.5 Headlamp Aiming Setup (Screen Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Vehicle on Level Ground] --> H[Headlamp Center Marked H]
    H --> SCR[Screen 10 m Ahead Vertical]
    SCR --> HV[H-V Reference Cross Marked]
    SCR --> TT[Top Tangent Line]
    SCR --> Q1[Quarter Line Left 25%]
    SCR --> Q2[Quarter Line Right 25%]
    SCR --> Z[Z Beam Hot Zone]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
> **Define luminous intensity and state its SI unit. Mention one automotive application where luminous intensity is the controlling photometric parameter.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Luminous intensity is the luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a given direction.
- **Mathematical form (1 Mark):** $I = \dfrac{d\Phi}{d\omega}$, where $\omega$ is the solid angle.
- **SI Unit (½ Mark):** candela (cd).
- **Application (½ Mark):** **Headlamp forward beam** — the photometric specification is given in candela along the optical axis (e.g., AIS-010 mandates ≥ 30,000 cd on the high-beam reference axis).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **State the Inverse Square Law of Illuminance. Mention one limitation of this law when applied to automobile headlamps.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement (1 Mark):** The illuminance on a surface is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the point source — $E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$.
- **Derivation reference (1 Mark):** For a uniform point source, $\Phi = 4\pi I$, and surface area of a sphere $= 4\pi d^{2}$, giving $E = \dfrac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^{2}} = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}$.
- **Limitation (1 Mark):** The law assumes the source is a **true point source**. In real headlamps, the filament has finite dimensions (e.g., H4 bulb filament is ~5 mm), so at close range (< 5 m), the law underestimates illuminance. Additionally, the law assumes a clear, unobstructed medium with no atmospheric absorption.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **sealed-beam halogen headlamp**. Discuss the role of the parabolic reflector and Fresnel lens in producing the desired beam pattern. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Construction (3 Marks):**
   - **Bulb:** Tungsten filament enclosed in a quartz halogen-filled capsule (H4 type uses iodine/bromine vapour at 4–5 atm pressure).
   - **Reflector:** Paraboloidal glass with aluminized inner surface; focal length 25–35 mm.
   - **Lens:** Borosilicate glass with moulded Fresnel prisms (typically 60–80 prism rings).
   - **Sealed Housing:** Argon-arc welded steel flange; hermetic seal prevents moisture ingress.
   - **Aiming Pads:** Three lugs for mechanical alignment.

2. **Working Principle (2 Marks):**
   - When energized at 12 V DC, the filament reaches ~2,900 K.
   - The halogen regenerative cycle: evaporated tungsten combines with halogen vapour → tungsten halide → redeposits on filament at hot zones → prevents blackening of bulb wall.
   - Filament positioned **at focus of parabola** → rays emerge parallel to axis.

3. **Role of Parabolic Reflector and Fresnel Lens (2 Marks):**
   - **Reflector:** Converts divergent rays from the point filament into **collimated parallel rays**, maximising throw distance.
   - **Fresnel Lens:** Refracts collimated rays to produce the specified beam pattern — bright spot centred slightly below the horizontal axis (asymmetric low beam), with horizontal spread for lane illumination.

**(b)** A headlamp produces **45,000 cd** along its axis. Calculate the illuminance on a road surface at **75 m** ahead when the beam strikes at an angle of **30°** to the surface normal. Apply Lambert's Cosine Law. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Luminous intensity, $I = 45{,}000$ cd
- Distance, $d = 75$ m
- Angle of incidence to normal, $\theta = 30°$
- $\cos 30° = 0.866$

**Applying Lambert's Cosine Law:**

$$E = \frac{I \cdot \cos\theta}{d^{2}}$$

$$E = \frac{45{,}000 \times 0.866}{(75)^{2}}$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**
$$E = \frac{45{,}000 \times 0.866}{5{,}625}$$

$$E = \frac{38{,}970}{5{,}625}$$

$$\boxed{E = 6.928 \text{ lux (approximately 7 lux)}}$$

**Verification:**
- Units check: $\text{cd} / \text{m}^{2} = \text{lux}$ ✓
- Magnitude check: For an oncoming driver's eye at ~75 m, AIS-010 mandates ≤ 0.7 lux to avoid glare. Our calculated value is on the **road** (different geometry), and is consistent with the AIS-010 specification of 10–15 lux on the road at 75 m for low beam.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Past Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Compare the construction, working, and performance of **Halogen, HID (Xenon), and LED headlamps**. Justify why LED is the preferred choice for modern electric vehicles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Halogen | HID (Xenon) | LED |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Luminous Efficacy** | 18–25 lm/W | 80–100 lm/W | 100–150 lm/W |
| **Color Temperature** | 3,200 K (yellowish) | 4,300 K (white) | 5,500–6,500 K (cool white) |
| **Lifespan** | 500–1,000 hrs | 2,000–3,000 hrs | 10,000–20,000 hrs |
| **Power Consumption** | 55–65 W | 35 W | 15–25 W |
| **Warm-up Time** | Instant | 5–15 seconds | Instant |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium |
| **Voltage Requirement** | 12 V DC | 25 kV ignition pulse + 85 V AC ballast | 12 V DC with constant-current driver |

**Justification for LED in EVs (2 Marks):**
- **Energy Efficiency:** EV battery range is highly sensitive to auxiliary load. LED saving of ~40 W vs. halogen directly extends range by ~5 km per charge cycle.
- **Compactness:** LED modules are < 30 mm tall, enabling flexible headlamp styling (slim DRL signatures).
- **Solid-State Reliability:** No filament → vibration-resistant → critical for EV regenerative-braking torque pulses.
- **Matrix-Laser Compatibility:** LED is the foundation for Adaptive Driving Beam (ADB) and matrix-pixel lighting.

**(b)** A vehicle's headlamp is mounted at a height of **0.75 m** above the road. If the low-beam axis is aimed at a point **75 m** ahead and **0.75 m** below the headlamp centre, calculate the **angle of depression** $\alpha$ of the beam. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Mounting height: $H = 0.75$ m
- Forward distance: $L = 75$ m
- Vertical drop of aim point: $0.75$ m

**Geometric Setup:**

The aim point is at horizontal distance $L$ and vertical drop $H$ relative to the headlamp. The depression angle $\alpha$ satisfies:
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{H}{L}$$

$$\tan\alpha = \frac{0.75}{75} = 0.01$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.01)$$

**Step-by-step evaluation:**
$$\alpha = 0.5729°$$

$$\boxed{\alpha \approx 0.57°}$$

**Verification against AIS-010 / ECE R112:**
- The mandated cut-off depression for low beam is **1 % downward**, which equals $\tan^{-1}(0.01) = 0.57°$.
- This matches exactly with the AIS-010 regulation. ✓

**Engineering Insight (½ Mark):**
- The "1 % rule" means: for every 1 metre of forward distance, the beam drops by 10 mm — ensuring the bright zone falls on the road, not in the eyes of oncoming drivers.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Lighting System (KTU ESE):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cosine correction in Lambert's Law problems** — Students frequently apply $E = I/d^{2}$ when the angle of incidence is given as non-zero. **Always check** whether the problem states "perpendicular incidence" or gives an angle.
>
> 2. **Confusing candela with lumen** — Candela is *directional* (per steradian), while lumen is *total flux*. Markers deduct 1 mark if the wrong unit is written.
>
> 3. **Skipping the unit verification step** — In 7-mark numerical problems, KTU examiners allocate ½ mark specifically for **dimensional/unit verification**. Always end with: *"Units: cd/m² = lux ✓"*.
>
> 4. **Writing Sealed Beam as "sealed-beam unit" without explaining the parabolic geometry** — A neat sketch of the parabola and the focal-point placement is mandatory for full marks.
>
> 5. **Failing to mention AIS-010 / ECE R112 standards** — Photometric regulations carry 1 mark in any construction-type question. Always cite the standard.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Lighting System (Module 2)**

- **Photometric Quantities (4):** Candela (cd), Lumen (lm), Lux (lx), cd/m².
- **Core Equation:** $E = I/d^{2}$ (Inverse Square Law) and $E = I \cos\theta / d^{2}$ (Lambert's Cosine Law).
- **Luminous Flux Equation:** $\Phi = I \cdot \omega$.
- **Parabola Equation:** $y^{2} = 4fx$ — filament placed at focus $f$.
- **Five Generations of Light Source:** Incandescent → Halogen → HID/Xenon → LED → Laser.
- **Cut-off Depression for Low Beam:** Exactly **1 % downward = 0.57°** (AIS-010 / ECE R112).
- **Asymmetric Low Beam:** Hot zone is shifted to the **right** (right-hand-drive countries shift it to the **left**) to illuminate road signs and avoid glare.
- **Sealed Beam vs. Composite:** Sealed = whole-unit replacement; Composite = replaceable bulb (current industry standard).
- **Adaptive Front-lighting System (AFS):** Uses steering angle, speed, and yaw sensors to swivel beam up to **15°** horizontally.
- **DRL (Daytime Running Lamps):** Mandatory in India since 2017 — uses LED modules at 6–12 W per side.
- **Headlamp Aiming Methods:** Mechanical (screen), Photometric (goniophotometer), and Optical (laser alignment).
- **Standard Lux Values for Roads:** Urban street = 10–30 lux, Highway = 30–50 lux, AIS-010 low beam on road = ≥ 10 lux at 50 m.
- **Fuse Rating:** Headlamp circuit typically 15 A; Tail lamp 10 A.
- **Beam Patterns:** High beam (driving), Low beam (passing — asymmetric), Fog beam (wide, sharp cut-off).
- **Always mention AIS / ECE standard** in any descriptive answer for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Horn System - Wind Shield Wiper Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Horn System - Wind Shield Wiper Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Horn System

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Automotive Horn** is an electromechanical sound-signalling device that converts electrical energy from the vehicle's battery into acoustic energy, producing a loud, directional warning tone. Per KTU 2024 syllabus, the horn system comprises the **horn unit (sound generator)**, a **horn relay (electromagnetic switch)**, **wiring harness with fuse protection**, and a **horn-push switch** mounted on the steering wheel. The system is engineered to deliver a sound pressure level of **100 dB to 110 dB** at a distance of **2 meters** as per AIS (Automotive Industry Standards) regulations.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a horn as the **"voice" of a vehicle**. Just as a person uses vocal cords (vibrated by air from lungs) to produce sound, a car horn uses an **electromagnet** to vibrate a metal diaphragm rapidly. A small push on the steering-wheel button acts like a "throat tap" that completes an electrical circuit, allowing current to flow and "shout" on behalf of the driver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a relay is mandatory:** A horn draws high current (typically **5 A to 15 A**). Routing this through the small steering-column switch would melt the contacts. The relay allows a tiny control current to switch a much larger horn current — a classic application of low-power control of high-power loads.

---

## 1.2 Windshield Wiper Motors

### Formal KTU Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Windshield Wiper Motor** is a fractional-horsepower DC electric motor (usually **Permanent Magnet DC type**, rated **12 V / 24 V**) that, through an internal worm-gear reduction and a mechanical linkage (3-bar or 4-bar parallelogram), converts rotary motion into oscillating, synchronized arcuate motion of two wiper arms, thereby clearing rain, snow, and debris from the windshield glass. Modern systems incorporate **self-parking**, **multi-speed operation** (low/high/intermittent), and **crank-arm angle sensors** for ECU control.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine holding two small windshield squeegees and moving them back-and-forth in unison with your hands — that's the wiper's job. The **motor** is the muscle, the **gear train** is the elbow (slows down the speed), and the **linkage** is the two arms of a person sweeping in perfect harmony. The "park" feature is like automatically returning the squeegees to the bottom-rest position after you stop sweeping.

> [!NOTE]
> **PMDC vs Series Wound:** Almost all modern wipers use **PMDC motors** because the direction of rotation must reverse for self-parking using a simple cam-and-switch mechanism — something easily achieved with PMDC by flipping polarity, but impossible with series-wound motors that need reversed field and armature simultaneously.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Parameters (Highlighted Standards)

| Parameter | Horn System | Wiper Motor |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Voltage | **12 V DC** (cars), **24 V DC** (commercial) | **12 V DC** (cars), **24 V DC** (commercial) |
| Current Draw | **5 A – 15 A** | **3 A – 6 A** (low) / **4 A – 8 A** (high) |
| Sound Level | **100 dB – 110 dB @ 2 m** | N/A (acoustic device) |
| Frequency | **400 Hz – 500 Hz** (fundamental) | N/A (mechanical device) |
| Speed (cycles/min) | N/A | **45 cycles/min (low)**, **60 cycles/min (high)** |
| Service Life | **> 50,000 operations** | **> 1,500,000 cycles** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Horn System — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 The Vibrating-Type Electromagnetic Horn (Most Common)

The horn operates on the principle of **electromagnetic attraction and mechanical resonance**.

**Step 1 — Initial Energisation:**
When the driver presses the horn push, current flows: **Battery → Fuse → Horn Relay Coil → Ground**. The relay coil becomes an electromagnet and pulls the relay contact closed.

**Step 2 — Power Circuit Completion:**
With the relay contact closed, heavy current flows: **Battery → Fuse → Relay Contact → Horn Winding → Ground**.

**Step 3 — Diaphragm Attraction:**
The horn winding becomes a strong electromagnet that attracts the flexible **steel diaphragm** toward the armature core. As the diaphragm moves, it flexes and produces the first half of the sound wave.

**Step 4 — Contact Breaker Action:**
