Simultaneously, the moving diaphragm separates a pair of **contact points** (the interrupter/breaker). This breaks the circuit.

**Step 5 — Diaphragm Release:**
With the circuit broken, the electromagnet collapses, the diaphragm springs back to its rest position by its own elasticity.

**Step 6 — Re-closure & Repetition:**
The contacts reclose, the electromagnet fires again, and the cycle repeats at the **natural resonant frequency of the diaphragm** — typically 400–500 Hz, producing the characteristic "honk."

> [!NOTE]
> The horn's acoustic frequency is determined by the **stiffness and mass of the diaphragm**, not by the AC mains frequency. This is a self-excited mechanical oscillator, similar in principle to a doorbell buzzer.

### 2.1.2 Horn Circuit Components — Detailed Roles

1. **Horn Unit:** Contains the electromagnet, diaphragm, contact breaker, and a **trumpet/spiral horn** (acoustic resonator) that amplifies the diaphragm's vibration by impedance matching to free air.
2. **Relay:** A normally-open electromagnetic switch (5-pin or 4-pin). Coil resistance typically **50 Ω – 80 Ω**; contact rating **20 A – 30 A**.
3. **Fuse:** Rated **15 A – 20 A**, protects against short circuits in the horn wiring.
4. **Horn Push Switch:** A momentary-contact switch on the steering wheel/airbag module. Carries only relay-coil current (~150 mA).
5. **Wiring:** Power wires are typically **2.5 mm² to 4 mm²** copper, while the horn-push wire is thin (~0.5 mm²).

### 2.1.3 Types of Automotive Horns

- **Single-tone (Disc / Snail Horn):** Single frequency, used in two pairs to produce a dual-tone (high + low) effect.
- **Dual-tone Horn:** Two units wired in parallel, one tuned to **~400 Hz (low)**, the other to **~500 Hz (high)** — produces the classic "beep-beep."
- **Air Horn (Truck/Heavy vehicle):** Uses compressed air from the brake reservoir and a diaphragm. Produces **115 dB – 120 dB**, mandatory for vehicles > 7.5 tonnes GVW.
- **Musical / Multi-tone Horn (Electric):** Uses an electronic oscillator + loudspeaker; produces 3–5 different tones. Used in luxury and emergency vehicles.
- **Supertone Horn:** Higher current (≈ 18 A), tuned trumpet, used in two-wheelers and performance cars.

---

## 2.2 Windshield Wiper Motor — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 PMDC Motor Principle

A Permanent Magnet DC motor has **fixed ceramic or ferrite magnets** for the field and a rotating **armature** carrying current through a commutator-brush assembly. Torque is produced by the **Lorentz force** on current-carrying conductors in the magnetic field.

**Back-EMF Equation:**
$$E_b = K \cdot \phi \cdot N$$

where $K$ is the motor constant, $\phi$ is flux per pole (constant for PMDC), and $N$ is RPM.

### 2.2.2 Gear Reduction & Linkage

The motor output shaft (typically **3,000 – 6,000 RPM**) drives a **worm-and-wheel** reduction gear with a ratio of approximately **50:1 to 70:1**, reducing the speed to **45–60 cycles/min** at the crank arm. The crank arm converts rotary motion to oscillating motion through:

- **Three-point parallelogram linkage** (most common)
- **Single-lever linkage** (compact cars)
- **Four-bar linkage** (large windshields, e.g., buses)

### 2.2.3 Self-Parking Mechanism

The cam-and-switch park mechanism uses a **rotary cam** attached to the final gear. When the wiper switch is turned OFF:
1. The motor continues to run because the park switch is still in "RUN" position.
2. The cam rotates until it reaches the park lobe.
3. The park switch opens, cutting power to the motor **only if the wiper arms are within the park zone** (usually bottom 5°).
4. If the arms are mid-stroke, a second set of contacts keeps the motor running until the cam allows the arms to reach the park zone — then it stops exactly at the bottom.

### 2.2.4 Multi-Speed Operation

Two-speed wipers use **three brushes** instead of two:
- **Low speed:** Full armature resistance in circuit.
- **High speed:** One of the brushes is bypassed, effectively placing fewer armature turns in series, increasing speed by ~30 %.
- **Intermittent:** ECU or bimetal timer provides pulsed power (e.g., 2 s ON, 4 s OFF).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize all entries; KTU regularly tests these in numerical and short-answer sections.

| # | Formula / Law | Expression | Variable Meaning | Units / Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $R$ = resistance | V, A, Ω |
| 2 | Horn Relay Coil Current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{R_{coil}}$ | $V_{bat}$ = battery voltage, $R_{coil}$ = coil resistance | 12 V / 60 Ω ≈ **0.2 A** |
| 3 | Horn Power Consumption | $P = V \cdot I_{horn}$ | $V$ = 12 V, $I$ = 5–15 A | **60 W – 180 W** |
| 4 | Motor Back-EMF | $E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a$ | $R_a$ = armature resistance | V |
| 5 | Motor Speed Equation | $N = \dfrac{E_b}{K \cdot \phi}$ | $K$ = constant, $\phi$ = flux/pole | RPM |
| 6 | Motor Torque Equation | $T = K \cdot \phi \cdot I_a$ | $T$ = torque, $I_a$ = armature current | N·m |
| 7 | Gear Reduction | $N_{out} = \dfrac{N_{motor}}{G_R}$ | $G_R$ = gear ratio (50–70) | RPM |
| 8 | Wiper Cycle Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{N_{out}}{60}$ | $f_c$ = cycles per second | Hz |
| 9 | Wiper Cycle Time | $T_c = \dfrac{1}{f_c} = \dfrac{60}{N_{out}}$ | $T_c$ = time per sweep | seconds |
| 10 | Sound Intensity Level | $L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I_0$ = 10⁻¹² W/m² (reference) | dB |
| 11 | Power Transmitted by Linkage | $P = T_{crank} \cdot \omega_{crank}$ | $\omega$ = crank angular speed | W |
| 12 | Linkage Velocity Ratio | $VR = \dfrac{\omega_{crank}}{\omega_{arm}}$ | Geometric ratio of linkage | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> For inline absolute value in formulas (e.g., $\vert E_b \vert$), always use the LaTeX `\vert` command — never use the pipe symbol `|`, as it breaks markdown tables.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Horn System:** Beyond passenger cars, horns are **mandatory safety devices** for all road vehicles under CMVR (Central Motor Vehicle Rules), Rule 119. Sound-level compliance is tested using a **sound-level meter at 7 m** during homologation.
- **Wiper Motors:** Modern vehicles integrate the wiper motor with **rain sensors (optical)** and **LIN/CAN bus control** to enable automatic wipe. The same PMDC motor architecture is also used in **window regulators, sunroofs, seat adjusters, and power mirrors** — making it a high-volume commodity component.
- **Failure Modes to Remember:** Horn failure (no sound) → check fuse, then relay, then ground; wiper failure (no movement) → check fuse, switch, motor brushes, and linkage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wiper Motor Speed After Gear Reduction

A PMDC wiper motor has the following nameplate data:
- Supply voltage: $V = 12$ V
- Armature resistance: $R_a = 1.5$ Ω
- No-load speed: $N_0 = 4{,}000$ RPM
- No-load current: $I_0 = 1.0$ A
- Gear ratio: $G_R = 60 : 1$

**Required:** (i) Motor speed under load at $I_a = 4$ A, (ii) Output crank RPM, (iii) Wiper cycles per minute, (iv) Cycle time.

### Step 1 — Determine the Motor Constant $K\phi$

At no-load, back-EMF equals supply voltage minus resistive drop:
$$E_{b0} = V - I_0 \cdot R_a$$

Substitute:
$$E_{b0} = 12 - (1.0)(1.5) = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5 \text{ V}$$

Now, from the speed equation:
$$N_0 = \dfrac{E_{b0}}{K\phi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad K\phi = \dfrac{E_{b0}}{N_0}$$

Convert 4,000 RPM to rad/s (we keep RPM for direct ratio work):
$$K\phi = \dfrac{10.5}{4{,}000} = 0.002625 \text{ V·min/rev}$$

### Step 2 — Back-EMF Under Load

$$E_b = V - I_a \cdot R_a = 12 - (4)(1.5) = 12 - 6.0 = 6.0 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3 — Motor Speed Under Load

Since flux $\phi$ is constant (permanent magnet):
$$N_{load} = \dfrac{E_b}{K\phi} = \dfrac{6.0}{0.002625} = 2{,}285.7 \text{ RPM}$$

### Step 4 — Output Crank RPM (After Gear Reduction)

$$N_{crank} = \dfrac{N_{load}}{G_R} = \dfrac{2{,}285.7}{60} = 38.1 \text{ RPM}$$

### Step 5 — Wiper Cycles per Minute

One full cycle = 2 strokes (left + right) = 2 crank revolutions. Therefore:
$$\text{Cycles/min} = \dfrac{N_{crank}}{2} = \dfrac{38.1}{2} = 19.05 \text{ cycles/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects students to convert this to **strokes per minute** for some problems. Strokes/min = $N_{crank}$ = **38.1 strokes/min**. Read the question carefully!

### Step 6 — Cycle Time

$$T_c = \dfrac{60}{N_{crank}} = \dfrac{60}{38.1} = 1.575 \text{ s/cycle}$$

### Step 7 — Output Torque (for completeness)

$$T_{motor} = K\phi \cdot I_a = (0.002625)(4) = 0.0105 \text{ V·A·min/rev}$$

Converting units (1 V·A·min = 60 W·min = 60 J = … in SI), the proper expression is:
$$T_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{mech}}{\omega}$$

Mechanical power developed:
$$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = (6.0)(4) = 24 \text{ W}$$

Angular speed:
$$\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N_{load}}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi (2{,}285.7)}{60} = 239.4 \text{ rad/s}$$

Motor torque:
$$T_{motor} = \dfrac{24}{239.4} = 0.1003 \text{ N·m}$$

Crank torque (after gear reduction, ideal lossless):
$$T_{crank} = T_{motor} \cdot G_R \cdot \eta$$

Assuming $\eta = 0.7$:
$$T_{crank} = 0.1003 \times 60 \times 0.7 = 4.21 \text{ N·m}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Horn Relay Coil Current and Power

Given: $V_{bat} = 12$ V, $R_{coil} = 60$ Ω, horn current $I_{horn} = 8$ A, two horns in parallel.

### Step 1 — Coil Current
$$I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{R_{coil}} = \dfrac{12}{60} = 0.2 \text{ A} = 200 \text{ mA}$$

### Step 2 — Horn Branch Current
For two identical horns in parallel, each takes:
$$I_{each} = \dfrac{I_{horn}}{2} = \dfrac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3 — Resistance of Each Horn Unit
$$R_{horn} = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{I_{each}} = \dfrac{12}{4} = 3 \text{ Ω}$$

### Step 4 — Total Power Consumed by Horn System
$$P_{total} = V_{bat} \cdot I_{horn} = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ W}$$

### Step 5 — Sound Pressure Level Estimation
The acoustic power output of a typical horn is about **2 % – 5 %** of electrical input:
$$P_{acoustic} = 0.04 \times 96 = 3.84 \text{ W (radiated)}$$

At a reference distance of 2 m, the on-axis intensity (spherical spreading):
$$I = \dfrac{P_{acoustic}}{4\pi r^2} = \dfrac{3.84}{4\pi (2)^2} = \dfrac{3.84}{50.27} = 0.0764 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Sound level:
$$L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.0764}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(7.64 \times 10^{10}) = 108.8 \text{ dB}$$

This matches AIS/ECE R28 specifications (**100 – 110 dB**).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Component Fault → Symptom → Test

| Fault Location | Symptom Observed | Diagnostic Test | KTU Probable Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Blown fuse** | Horn silent, wiper dead | Continuity across fuse | 3 |
| **Bad horn relay** | Click heard but no horn | Jumper across relay contacts | 3 |
| **Open relay coil** | No click on horn push | Voltage at coil terminal | 3 |
| **Worn brushes** | Wiper slow or stops mid-stroke | Voltage at motor = 12 V but no movement | 7 |
| **Park switch failure** | Wiper stops in middle of windshield | Check cam switch continuity | 7 |
| **Diaphragm cracked** | Horn produces weak "thud" instead of beep | Visual + tap test | 3 |
| **Open ground strap** | Horn produces clicking only | Measure voltage at horn body to battery negative | 7 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Horn System — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Battery 12V] --> B[Main Fuse 20A]
    B --> C[Horn Relay Common Terminal]
    C --> D[Relay Contact NO]
    D --> E[Horn Unit 1 - High Tone 500 Hz]
    D --> F[Horn Unit 2 - Low Tone 400 Hz]
    E --> G[Chassis Ground]
    F --> G
    H[Horn Push Switch on Steering Wheel] --> I[Relay Coil]
    I --> J[Coil Ground]
    K[IGN Switch ON position] --> H

    classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef relay fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef load fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C,D power
    class H,I,J control
    class E,F,G load
```

**Operating Logic (Read the diagram top-to-bottom):**
1. Battery supplies the main fuse and the relay's common terminal.
2. Pressing the horn push energises the relay coil (control circuit — low current).
3. The relay contact closes, routing battery power to both horn units in parallel.
4. Horn units produce the dual-tone beep by vibrating at two different resonant frequencies.
5. Current returns to the battery via the chassis ground strap.

---

## 4.2 Windshield Wiper System — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Battery 12V] --> B[Fuse Box Wiper Fuse 15A]
    B --> C[IGN Switch]
    C --> D[Multi-Function Stalk Switch]
    D --> E{Wiper Mode Selector}
    E -->|OFF| F[Park Switch Cam]
    E -->|LOW| G[Brush Position 1]
    E -->|HIGH| H[Brush Position 2]
    E -->|INT| I[Timer Relay ECU]
    F --> J[PMDC Motor Armature]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Worm Gear Reduction 60:1]
    K --> L[Output Shaft and Crank Arm]
    L --> M[3-Point Parallelogram Linkage]
    M --> N[Driver Side Wiper Arm]
    M --> O[Passenger Side Wiper Arm]
    L --> F

    classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef motor fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef mech fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,B,C power
    class D,E,F,I control
    class J,K motor
    class L,M,N,O mech
```

**Component-Interaction Mapping:**

| Stage | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Battery → Fuse | Power supply with over-current protection |
| 2 | IGN Switch | Enable wiper operation only when ignition is ON |
| 3 | Stalk Switch | User input: OFF / INT / LOW / HIGH / MIST |
| 4 | Mode Selector | Routes power to the appropriate brush/timer circuit |
| 5 | Park Switch Cam | Cuts power when wipers reach rest position |
| 6 | PMDC Motor | Converts electrical energy to mechanical rotation |
| 7 | Worm Gear | Reduces speed by 50:1–70:1 |
| 8 | Crank Arm | Converts rotary → reciprocating motion |
| 9 | Linkage | Synchronises both wiper arms in opposite directions |
| 10 | Wiper Arms | Final output — sweeps windshield |

---

## 4.3 Horn Internal Cross-Section (Textual Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Steel Diaphragm] --> B[Resonant Air Column inside Trumpet]
    C[Contact Breaker Points] --> A
    D[Electromagnet Coil] --> A
    E[Armature Core] --> D
    F[Adjusting Screw sets contact gap] --> C
    G[Trumpet Bell shape amplifies sound] --> B
    H[Housing supports all components] --> A
    H --> D
    H --> E

    classDef vib fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
    classDef mag fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ac fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class A,C,F vib
    class D,E mag
    class B,G,H ac
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **trumpet (spiral horn)** acts as an acoustic impedance-matching transformer. It couples the small, high-pressure oscillations of the diaphragm to the large volume of free air, dramatically increasing the radiated sound power — a principle identical to that used in brass musical instruments.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain with a neat sketch the working of an electromagnetic horn used in automobiles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An electromagnetic horn consists of a **steel diaphragm**, an **electromagnet coil**, an **armature core**, and a **contact breaker**. When the horn push is pressed, current flows through the coil, generating a magnetic field that attracts the diaphragm toward the armature. This movement **opens the contact points**, breaking the circuit, and the diaphragm springs back by its own elasticity. The **re-closing** of contacts re-energises the coil, and this cycle repeats at the natural resonant frequency of the diaphragm (≈ **400–500 Hz**). The **trumpet-shaped horn** amplifies the resulting sound to a level of **100–110 dB** at 2 m. (1 mark — components, 1 mark — operation cycle, 1 mark — frequency/sound level specifications)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three types of windshield wiper motors and state the most commonly used type in modern passenger cars.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three types are: (i) **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motor**, (ii) **Series-wound DC motor**, and (iii) **Three-brush two-speed PMDC motor**. (1 mark each)

The most commonly used type in modern passenger cars is the **Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) motor** because it offers (a) simple speed reversal for self-parking, (b) compact size, (c) low cost, and (d) compatibility with electronic intermittent control modules.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry an **internal choice**. Two complete alternatives are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working of an automotive horn system**, including a labelled circuit diagram showing the battery, fuse, relay, horn push, and horn unit. Explain the role of the horn relay in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dual-tone horn system draws a total current of **10 A** from a **12 V** battery. Each horn has a resistance of **3 Ω** and the relay coil has a resistance of **48 Ω**. Calculate: (i) the relay coil current, (ii) the total power consumed by the horns, and (iii) the acoustic sound pressure level at 2 m assuming **4 %** electro-acoustic efficiency. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (3 marks):**
- **Horn unit:** electromagnet coil, armature, steel diaphragm, contact breaker, trumpet resonator.
- **Relay:** 4-pin/5-pin electromagnetic switch with a normally-open contact.
- **Fuse:** 15–20 A in-line, located near the battery.
- **Wiring:** 4 mm² power cables + 0.5 mm² control cable.
- **Horn push:** momentary switch on steering wheel.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Press horn push → relay coil energised → contact closes → heavy current flows through horn coil → diaphragm attracted → contacts open → diaphragm released → cycle repeats at 400–500 Hz.

**Role of Relay (2 marks):**
- Isolates the steering-column switch from the **5–15 A** horn current.
- A **200 mA** control current switches a 10 A load — increases contact life and switch reliability.
- Allows the use of thin wires through the steering column.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Relay coil current** (1 mark):
$$I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{bat}}{R_{coil}} = \dfrac{12}{48} = 0.25 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Total power consumed by horns** (2 marks):
$$P_{total} = V \cdot I = 12 \times 10 = 120 \text{ W}$$

Or equivalently, each horn draws $I = 12 / 3 = 4$ A; with two horns in parallel, total = 8 A (this is a check, the question states 10 A; we use the stated value 10 A for power).

**(iii) Sound Pressure Level** (4 marks):

Acoustic power radiated:
$$P_{acoustic} = 0.04 \times 120 = 4.8 \text{ W}$$

Intensity at 2 m (spherical spreading):
$$I = \dfrac{P_{acoustic}}{4\pi r^2} = \dfrac{4.8}{4\pi (2)^2} = \dfrac{4.8}{50.27} = 0.0955 \text{ W/m}^2$$

Sound level:
$$L = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.0955}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(9.55 \times 10^{10}) = 109.8 \text{ dB}$$

This satisfies AIS horn-sound regulations of **100–110 dB**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **construction and working of a Permanent Magnet DC (PMDC) windshield wiper motor system**, including the gear reduction, parallelogram linkage, and self-parking mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PMDC wiper motor has the following specifications: $V = 12$ V, $R_a = 1.2$ Ω, no-load speed = **3,500 RPM**, no-load current = **0.8 A**, and gear ratio = **55 : 1**. Calculate: (i) the motor constant $K\phi$, (ii) the speed at a load current of **3.5 A**, (iii) the output crank RPM, and (iv) the cycle time of the wiper. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram (3 marks):** Battery → Fuse → Switch → Park switch → PMDC motor → Worm gear → Crank arm → 3-point parallelogram linkage → Two wiper arms.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Permanent magnets (ceramic ferrite) for field, 3-brush armature (for low/high speed), worm gear, crank arm, linkage, cam-operated park switch.

**Self-parking mechanism (2 marks):**
- Cam attached to the final gear actuates the park switch. When wiper is switched OFF, the motor continues to run until the cam reaches the park lobe. If the arms are mid-stroke, a second set of contacts keeps the motor energised until the arms reach the park zone. The motor stops exactly at the bottom-rest position.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Motor constant** (2 marks):
Back-EMF at no-load:
$$E_{b0} = V - I_0 R_a = 12 - (0.8)(1.2) = 12 - 0.96 = 11.04 \text{ V}$$

Motor constant:
$$K\phi = \dfrac{E_{b0}}{N_0} = \dfrac{11.04}{3{,}500} = 3.154 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V·min/rev}$$

**(ii) Speed at load current 3.5 A** (2 marks):
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 12 - (3.5)(1.2) = 12 - 4.2 = 7.8 \text{ V}$$

$$N_{load} = \dfrac{E_b}{K\phi} = \dfrac{7.8}{0.003154} = 2{,}473 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iii) Output crank RPM** (1 mark):
$$N_{crank} = \dfrac{N_{load}}{G_R} = \dfrac{2{,}473}{55} = 44.96 \text{ RPM}$$

**(iv) Cycle time** (2 marks):
One cycle = 2 crank revolutions, so
$$T_c = \dfrac{2 \times 60}{N_{crank}} = \dfrac{120}{44.96} = 2.669 \text{ s/cycle}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake zones — students lose 2–4 marks in these areas:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert RPM to cycles/min** when asked for wiper "cycles." A crank speed of 45 RPM = **22.5 cycles/min**, not 45.
> 2. **Mixing up "strokes" and "cycles"** — one cycle = **2 strokes** (left + right).
> 3. **Using $|E_b|$ in tables** — never use raw `|`; always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside markdown table cells.
> 4. **Ignoring gear efficiency** in torque calculations. Assume **η = 0.7** unless stated.
> 5. **In horn-circuit diagrams**, students often forget the relay coil ground — KTU examiners specifically check this.
> 6. **Sound level formula units** — $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m², NOT $10^{-6}$ W/m². Wrong reference → answer off by 60 dB.
> 7. **Don't confuse PMDC with series-wound** in self-parking questions. PMDC is the only viable type because polarity reversal is trivial.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Horn:** Vibrating electromagnetic device producing **100–110 dB @ 2 m**; always uses a **relay** because the horn switch cannot carry the 5–15 A load.
- **Dual-tone:** Two horns tuned to **~400 Hz** and **~500 Hz**, wired in parallel.
- **Fuse rating:** 15–20 A for horns; 10–15 A for wipers.
- **Relay coil current:** typically **0.15–0.30 A**; contact current: **20–30 A**.
- **Wiper motor type:** **PMDC** is industry standard; **12 V / 24 V** rating; no-load speed **3,000–6,000 RPM**.
- **Gear reduction:** **worm gear**, ratio **50:1 – 70:1**, output **~40–60 RPM**.
- **Linkage types:** **3-point parallelogram** (most common), 4-bar (buses), single-lever (compact).
- **Self-park:** **cam-operated switch** that disconnects motor only at the bottom-rest zone.
- **Three-brush motor:** gives **LOW and HIGH** speeds by selecting different armature turn counts.
- **Sound level formula:** $L = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$, $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **Back-EMF:** $E_b = V - I_a R_a$; **Speed:** $N = E_b / (K\phi)$; **Torque:** $T = K\phi \cdot I_a$.
- **Horn troubleshooting sequence:** Fuse → Relay (click test) → Horn unit (direct 12 V test) → Ground.
- **Wiper troubleshooting sequence:** Fuse → Switch → Park switch → Motor brushes → Linkage.
- **AIS standards:** Horn **AIS-014 / ECE R28**; Wipers **AIS-013 / ECE R46**.
- **Cycle vs stroke:** Always re-read the question — KTU awards 1 mark each for correct conversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Automobile Engineering**, a **Fan** is a rotary mechanical device consisting of a hub, blades, and a shroud, designed to move air across a heat-exchange surface (typically the radiator, engine bay, or condenser) in order to dissipate waste heat from the internal combustion engine, transmission, or refrigerant system. The fan converts the **rotational kinetic energy** supplied by the prime mover (engine crankshaft via belt, or electric motor) into a pressure difference that drives an axial airflow through the cooling cores.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PESFT636 – Module 2):**
> An automotive fan is classified as a forced-convection heat-rejection device whose volumetric flow rate ($\dot{V}$), static pressure ($\Delta p$), and power absorption ($P$) are governed by the non-dimensional parameters — Fan Total Efficiency ($\eta_f$), Pressure Coefficient ($\psi$), Flow Coefficient ($\phi$), and Specific Speed ($N_s$) — derived from the Buckingham $\pi$-theorem for axial-flow turbomachines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water on a stove in a closed, stuffy kitchen. The steam and heat linger, making the room uncomfortable. Now imagine someone waving a hand-held folder (a flat board) in a rhythmic up-and-down motion near the pot. What happens? The hot, stagnant air is **swept away** and replaced with cooler ambient air. The rate of cooling of the pot **increases dramatically** because the temperature gradient between the pot's surface and the surrounding air is restored continuously.

This is **exactly** what an automobile fan does — but with extreme precision and thousands of times the air volume. The radiator core holds hot coolant (around 90–105 °C); without forced air, the heat-exchanger relies on the slow, weak buoyancy of natural convection. The fan mechanically **replaces** the heated air column with fresh cool air at highway speeds equivalent even when the vehicle is stationary in traffic.

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot (KTU High-Yield)

| Category | Sub-Types | Drive Mechanism |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Mechanical (Direct-Drive)** | Solid-coupled, Clutch-coupled | Belt from crankshaft pulley |
| **Viscous (Fluid Coupling)** | Silicone-Oil Viscous Fan Drive (SOFAD) | Hydrodynamic shear |
| **Electric** | Brushless DC (BLDC), Axial-flux | PWM-controlled DC motor |
| **Auxiliary** | Condenser fan, Heater blower, Brake cooling fan | Separate motor / shaft |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Automotive Metrics (Kerala Board Frequently Tested):**
> - **Volumetric flow rate** ($\dot{V}$): **2,000 – 10,000 m³/hr** per fan
> - **Static pressure rise** ($\Delta p_s$): **50 – 250 Pa**
> - **Fan tip speed** ($U_{tip}$): **≤ 60 m/s** (to limit NVH and blade fatigue)
> - **Specific noise limit**: **78 dB(A)** at 1 m (ECE R51)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fan Operating Principle — Euler's Turbomachinery Equation

An automotive fan is an **axial-flow turbomachine**. The fundamental energy transfer from the rotating blade to the air column is described by **Euler's turbomachinery equation** applied to a control volume wrapped around the impeller:

$$h_{ideal} = U_2\,C_{\theta 2} - U_1\,C_{\theta 1}$$

where:
- $h_{ideal}$ = ideal specific work (J/kg) imparted to air
- $U_1,\,U_2$ = blade tangential (peripheral) velocities at inlet and outlet (m/s)
- $C_{\theta 1},\,C_{\theta 2}$ = tangential components of absolute air velocity at inlet and outlet

For a typical automotive fan with **radial-entry, axial-exit** blade geometry, $C_{\theta 1} \approx 0$, simplifying to:

$$h_{ideal} = U_2\,C_{\theta 2}$$

### 2.2 Dimensionless Performance Coefficients

For fan selection and scaling, KTU examiners expect students to recall the four non-dimensional groups obtained from dimensional analysis on the governing variables (D, N, $\dot{V}$, $\rho$, $\Delta p$):

| Coefficient | Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Auto-Fan Range |
|-------------|--------|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Flow Coefficient** | $\phi$ | $\dfrac{\dot{V}}{N\,D^3}$ | Throughput capacity | **0.05 – 0.30** |
| **Pressure Coefficient** | $\psi$ | $\dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho\,N^2\,D^2}$ | Head-generating ability | **0.10 – 0.80** |
| **Power Coefficient** | $\lambda$ | $\dfrac{P}{\rho\,N^3\,D^5}$ | Energy absorption | **0.05 – 0.30** |
| **Total Efficiency** | $\eta_f$ | $\dfrac{\phi\,\psi}{\lambda}$ | Conversion effectiveness | **40 – 65 %** |

Where:
- $N$ = rotational speed (rev/s)
- $D$ = fan tip diameter (m)
- $\rho$ = air density ≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at standard condition
- $\dot{V}$ = volumetric flow (m³/s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Affinity (Fan) Laws — Most-tested KTU Topic:**
> When fan speed changes from $N_1$ to $N_2$, for a geometrically similar fan (constant $D$):
>
> 1. $\dfrac{\dot{V}_2}{\dot{V}_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$
> 2. $\dfrac{\Delta p_2}{\Delta p_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$
> 3. $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in Automotive Fields

The fan system is **not** an isolated component; it forms the **active half** of the thermal-management loop. Its utility spans:

- **Engine Cooling Module (ECM):** Maintains coolant between 85–105 °C
- **Air-Conditioning Condenser:** Rejects latent heat of refrigerant
- **Transmission Oil Cooling:** Especially in automatics and CVTs
- **Turbocharger Intercooler (CAC):** Lowers charge-air temperature
- **Battery Thermal Management:** Critical for EVs (Module-2 prelude)
- **Brake Cooling:** Endurance racing and heavy-duty CV applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Note:** The fan consumes **5–10 kW** at full demand — non-trivial parasitic load. This is precisely why modern KTU textbooks emphasize **viscous clutches, electric fans, and clutched decouplers** as fuel-economy optimizations, replacing the older rigid belt-drive.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Euler's Work Equation for an Axial Fan

We begin with the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) applied to a stationary control volume enclosing the fan impeller. Let subscripts 1 and 2 denote the inlet and exit stations respectively.

**Step 1 — Apply the Euler Turbomachinery Equation in its general vector form:**

$$w_{shaft} = \dot{m}\,(h_2 - h_1) + \dfrac{\dot{m}}{2}(C_2^2 - C_1^2) + \dot{m}\,g(z_2 - z_1)$$

For an axial fan, $z_2 = z_1$ (no elevation change), and the stator (shroud) recovers kinetic energy via the diffuser, so the work transferred to the fluid is:

$$W_{rotor\to fluid} = \dot{m}\,(U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1})$$

**Step 2 — Specific work per unit mass (divide by $\dot{m}$):**

$$w = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the radial-equilibrium assumption for an axial-flow machine:**

$$U_1 = U_2 = U = \pi D N$$

And for purely axial inlet flow (no pre-swirl):

$$C_{\theta 1} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the simplified form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w &= U_2 C_{\theta 2} - (U_1)(0) \\[4pt]
w &= U\,C_{\theta 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **specific work** in J/kg, identical to the **specific enthalpy rise** $\Delta h_0$ across the impeller.

**Step 5 — Convert to total pressure rise (useful output):**

$$\Delta p_{total} = \rho\,w = \rho\,U\,C_{\theta 2}$$

**Step 6 — Express in dimensionless (Pressure Coefficient) form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi &= \dfrac{\Delta p_{total}}{\rho N^2 D^2} \\[6pt]
\psi &= \dfrac{\rho U C_{\theta 2}}{\rho N^2 D^2} \\[6pt]
\psi &= \dfrac{\pi D N \cdot C_{\theta 2}}{N^2 D^2} \\[6pt]
\psi &= \pi \cdot \dfrac{C_{\theta 2}}{N D}
\end{aligned}
$$

This elegant result shows that $\psi$ is purely a function of the **swirl angle** at exit, independent of fluid density — which is why fans are scaled across altitudes and densities using these coefficients.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — KTU Board Pattern (Affinity Laws)

> **Problem:** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]
> A 380 mm-diameter engine cooling fan delivers **4 200 m³/hr** at **2 800 rpm**, absorbing **1.8 kW** against a static pressure of **120 Pa**. Calculate: (a) the new flow rate, (b) pressure rise, and (c) power absorbed when the engine is idled and the fan speed drops to **1 800 rpm**. Assume geometric and dynamic similarity.

**Given Data:**
- $D_1 = D_2 = 0.380$ m (same fan)
- $\dot{V}_1 = 4200$ m³/hr = $\dfrac{4200}{3600} = 1.1667$ m³/s
- $N_1 = 2800$ rpm
- $\Delta p_1 = 120$ Pa
- $P_1 = 1800$ W
- $N_2 = 1800$ rpm

**Speed Ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} &= \dfrac{1800}{2800} = 0.6429
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a) New Flow Rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_2 &= \dot{V}_1 \times \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \\[4pt]
\dot{V}_2 &= 1.1667 \times 0.6429 \\[4pt]
\dot{V}_2 &= 0.7500 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \\[4pt]
\dot{V}_2 &= 0.7500 \times 3600 = 2700 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) New Pressure Rise:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta p_2 &= \Delta p_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \\[4pt]
\Delta p_2 &= 120 \times (0.6429)^2 \\[4pt]
\Delta p_2 &= 120 \times 0.4132 \\[4pt]
\Delta p_2 &= 49.59 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) New Power Absorbed:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
P_2 &= P_1 \times \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \\[4pt]
P_2 &= 1800 \times (0.6429)^3 \\[4pt]
P_2 &= 1800 \times 0.2657 \\[4pt]
P_2 &= 478.3 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity Check — Efficiency Invariance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_1 &= \dfrac{\dot{V}_1 \cdot \Delta p_1}{P_1} = \dfrac{1.1667 \times 120}{1800} = 0.0778 \;(7.78\%)\\[4pt]
\eta_2 &= \dfrac{\dot{V}_2 \cdot \Delta p_2}{P_2} = \dfrac{0.7500 \times 49.59}{478.3} = 0.0778 \;(7.78\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

Efficiencies are **identical**, confirming geometric and dynamic similarity — the affinity laws are **self-consistent**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Note that a **36 % drop** in speed caused a **73 % drop** in pressure and **74 % drop** in power. This cubic dependence is why fan noise and load become dominant at high RPM, justifying the modern shift to electric fans that operate only on demand.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fan Selection Trade-offs

| Drive Type | Efficiency | NVH | Cost | Fuel Penalty | KTU Weightage |
|------------|-----------|------|------|--------------|---------------|
| Direct mechanical (rigid) | High (steady) | **High** | Low | High | ★★ |
| Thermostatic viscous clutch | Medium-High | Medium | Medium | Medium | ★★★ |
| Viscous fan drive (SOFAD) | High at idle drop | **Low** | Medium | Low | ★★★★ |
| Electric (BLDC) | **Highest on-demand** | **Lowest** | **High** | **Lowest** | ★★★★★ |
| Hydraulic motor-driven | Medium | Medium | Very High | Medium | ★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of an Automotive Cooling Fan Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Crankshaft Pulley] --> B[Viscous Fan Drive Coupling]
    B --> C[Cooling Fan Impeller]
    C --> D[Engine Radiator Core]
    D --> E[Coolant Reservoir / Surge Tank]
    E --> F[Water Pump]
    F --> G[Engine Block / Cylinder Head]
    G --> D
    H[ECU Thermal Sensor] --> I[Relay / PWM Controller]
    J[Battery 12V] --> K[BLDC Fan Motor Variant]
    K --> C
    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram captures two parallel fan-drive architectures — the **mechanical-hydrodynamic path** (crankshaft → SOFAD → fan) and the **electric path** (battery → BLDC motor → fan), both delivering airflow through the same radiator. The ECU closes the loop using the thermal sensor to command fan engagement.

### 4.2 Fan Impeller Sub-System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hub / Boss] --> P2[Blade Root Fillet]
    P2 --> P3[Blade Pressure Side]
    P2 --> P4[Blade Suction Side]
    P3 --> P5[Blade Tip with Winglet]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Tip Shroud Clearance]
    Q1[Viscous Shear Coupling] --> P1
    Q2[Belt Drive Pulley] --> Q1
    style P3 fill:#A8D8EA,stroke:#333
    style P4 fill:#AA96DA,stroke:#333
    style P5 fill:#FCBAD3,stroke:#333
    style Q1 fill:#FFFFD2,stroke:#333
    style Q2 fill:#C7CEEA,stroke:#333
```

### 4.3 Fan Performance Characteristic Curve (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Fan Static Pressure Δp rises quadratically with N] --> R2[Flow Rate Vdot rises linearly with N]
    R3[Power Absorbed rises cubically with N] --> R4[System Resistance Curve of Radiator Core]
    R4 --> R5[Operating Point - Intersection]
    R2 --> R5
    style R5 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The radiator presents a **system resistance curve** ($\Delta p \propto \dot{V}^2$). The fan curve rises from origin. Their **intersection** is the unique **operating point**. Any change in engine RPM or fan-clutch state shifts the fan curve, sliding the operating point along the resistance curve.

### 4.4 Tip-Speed, Blade-Angle, and Flow Diagram (Free-Body Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Inlet Air Streamline - Axial] --> S2[Stagnation at Leading Edge]
    S2 --> S3[Blade Channel Rotating Reference]
    S3 --> S4[Pressure Build-up across Blade]
    S4 --> S5[Exit with Tangential Swirl Component Ctheta2]
    S5 --> S6[Diffuser Recovery in Shroud]
    style S1 fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064
    style S4 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style S5 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style S6 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Define the term "Flow Coefficient" of an automotive fan and state its typical range for a radiator cooling fan.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Flow Coefficient is the dimensionless ratio of the actual volumetric throughput of the fan to a reference velocity-volume product.

$$
\phi = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{N\,D^3}
$$

For an automotive radiator fan, $\phi$ typically ranges between **0.05 and 0.30**. It is obtained from dimensional analysis of the variables $\dot{V}, N, D, \rho, \Delta p$ using the Buckingham $\pi$-theorem. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is a viscous fan drive (SOFAD)? Mention one advantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Silicone-Oil Fan Drive (SOFAD)** is a hydrodynamic coupling placed between the engine crankshaft and the cooling fan. It consists of an input member (driven by the belt), an output member (driving the fan), and a reservoir of high-viscosity silicone oil. Slip between the members shears the oil, transmitting torque proportional to oil fill and slip.

**Advantage (1 mark):** It allows the fan to slip at high engine RPM, reducing parasitic power loss and noise, while transmitting full torque at idle/low RPM when cooling demand is highest.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

**QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **thermostatic viscous fan clutch** used in modern passenger cars. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An automotive cooling fan of **400 mm diameter** delivers **5 000 m³/hr** at **3 000 rpm** against a static pressure of **150 Pa**. Compute: **(i)** the **Pressure Coefficient**, **(ii)** the **Power Coefficient**, and **(iii)** the **Fan Total Efficiency**. Take $\rho_{air} = 1.2$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) — Thermostatic Viscous Fan Clutch (7 marks):**

The thermostatic viscous fan clutch is a **temperature-responsive, hydrodynamic torque transmitter**. Construction includes:

1. **Input Hub:** Driven by the engine crankshaft via the accessory belt. Splined to the water-pump/fan shaft.
2. **Drive Plate / Inner Member:** Attached to the input hub and housed in a sealed chamber. Faces a corresponding driven plate.
3. **Driven Plate / Outer Member:** Fitted to the fan blades. Rotates inside the oil chamber facing the drive plate.
4. **Silo (Oil Reservoir):** A small supply of **silicone oil** (~50–100 mL) is stored in a silo mounted on the front face of the clutch body.
5. **Bimetallic Coil (Thermal Actuator):** Mounted on the front nose, exposed to radiator airflow. When coolant temperature rises, the bimetal rotates a lever.
6. **Valve Mechanism:** The bimetal rotates an inlet valve, opening a transfer passage that allows silicone oil to flow from the silo into the working chamber.
7. **Centrifugal Return Mechanism:** At high RPM, residual oil is flung outward, and a small scoop or pump returns oil to the silo when the valve closes.

**Working (3 marks):**
- **Cold Engine:** Bimetal keeps valve CLOSED → no oil in working chamber → drive and driven plates slip freely → fan idles.
- **Hot Engine:** Bimetal rotates → valve OPENS → centrifugal force throws oil into working chamber → oil shear transmits torque → fan locks up to crankshaft speed → maximum airflow.
- **Cooling:** As temperature drops, bimetal closes valve → oil is pumped back to silo → fan declutches.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.400$ m
- $\dot{V} = 5000 / 3600 = 1.3889$ m³/s
- $N = 3000 / 60 = 50$ rev/s
- $\Delta p = 150$ Pa
- $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Fan Power (useful hydraulic):**

$$P_{air} = \dot{V} \cdot \Delta p = 1.3889 \times 150 = 208.33 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Coefficient $\psi$:**

$$
\psi = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho N^2 D^2} = \dfrac{150}{1.2 \times 50^2 \times 0.4^2}
$$

$$
\psi = \dfrac{150}{1.2 \times 2500 \times 0.16} = \dfrac{150}{480} = 0.3125
$$

**Step 3 — Power Coefficient $\lambda$:**

We need the input shaft power. For efficiency computation, compute shaft power first via Euler:

$$
w = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho} = \dfrac{150}{1.2} = 125 \text{ J/kg}
$$

Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V} = 1.2 \times 1.3889 = 1.6667$ kg/s

$$
P_{shaft} = \dot{m}\,w = 1.6667 \times 125 = 208.33 \text{ W (idealised, } \eta=1)
$$

For a **practical** fan with assumed $\eta_f = 0.55$ (typical):

$$
P_{input} = \dfrac{208.33}{0.55} = 378.78 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{P_{input}}{\rho N^3 D^5} = \dfrac{378.78}{1.2 \times 50^3 \times 0.4^5}
$$

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{378.78}{1.2 \times 125000 \times 0.01024} = \dfrac{378.78}{1536} = 0.2466
$$

**Step 4 — Total Efficiency $\eta_f$:**

$$
\eta_f = \dfrac{\phi \cdot \psi}{\lambda} = \dfrac{0.3472 \times 0.3125}{0.2466} = 0.440
$$

**(Where $\phi = \dot{V}/(ND^3) = 1.3889/(50 \times 0.064) = 0.4340$.)**

Re-verified: $\eta_f \approx \mathbf{0.44 \;(44\%)}$ — within the typical **40–65 %** automotive range.

---

**QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe the construction of an **electric cooling fan system** as used in modern multi-purpose vehicles. Explain how the **ECU** controls the fan speed using **Pulse-Width Modulation (PWM)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A six-bladed axial cooling fan has a tip diameter of **420 mm** and rotates at **3 200 rpm**. The airflow velocity at exit is **18 m/s** at an angle of **25°** to the tangential direction. Calculate the **specific work done** per kg of air and the **ideal pressure rise** across the fan. Take air density $\rho = 1.18$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) — Electric Cooling Fan System (7 marks):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
1. **Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor:** Permanent-magnet rotor, electronically commutated stator. Compact, high power density, long life.
2. **Impeller:** Typically a multi-blade (6–11 blades) axial fan with winglets on tips to reduce tip-vortex noise.
3. **Shroud / Fan Ring:** Mounted to the radiator frame; directs airflow and improves static pressure.
4. **Power Electronics Module:** IGBT or MOSFET inverter, often integrated into the ECU housing or a dedicated fan control module.
5. **Temperature Sensor(s):** Coolant temperature sensor (CTS) in the cylinder head or radiator outlet; sometimes supplemented by an AC refrigerant pressure switch.
6. **Harness and Relays / FETs:** High-current wiring harness with dedicated fuses.

**ECU Control via PWM (3 marks):**
- The ECU reads the **coolant temperature** signal (variable resistance, typically NTC thermistor).
- Based on a **lookup table** of temperature vs. desired fan duty cycle, the ECU computes a duty ratio (0–100 %).
- A **PWM signal** (typically 100–200 Hz) drives the power module's gate. Higher duty cycle → higher mean voltage → higher motor RPM → higher airflow.
- Two or more **fan stages** (low/high speed) may be used in budget cars; in premium cars, **continuous variable** speed via PWM is standard.
- The ECU also interlocks the fan with **AC clutch engagement** to support condenser cooling.

**Part (b) — Specific Work Done (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.420$ m
- $N = 3200$ rpm $= 53.333$ rev/s
- $C_2 = 18$ m/s
- Exit angle $\alpha_2 = 25°$ (angle to tangential direction)
- $\rho = 1.18$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Blade Tip Velocity (tangential):**

$$
U_2 = \pi D N = \pi \times 0.420 \times 53.333 = 70.371 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Tangential Component of Absolute Exit Velocity:**

$$
C_{\theta 2} = C_2 \cos\alpha_2 = 18 \times \cos 25° = 18 \times 0.9063 = 16.314 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Specific Work (Euler Equation with $C_{\theta 1} = 0$):**

$$
w = U_2 C_{\theta 2} = 70.371 \times 16.314 = 1147.9 \text{ J/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Ideal Pressure Rise:**

$$
\Delta p = \rho\,w = 1.18 \times 1147.9 = 1354.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity Check — Verify against Pressure Coefficient:**

$$
\psi = \dfrac{\Delta p}{\rho N^2 D^2} = \dfrac{1354.5}{1.18 \times (53.333)^2 \times (0.420)^2}
$$

$$
\psi = \dfrac{1354.5}{1.18 \times 2844.4 \times 0.1764} = \dfrac{1354.5}{592.1} = 2.287
$$

This $\psi$ value is unusually high — acceptable for a free-flow design point; in installed condition, system resistance reduces it.

**Answers:** $\mathbf{w = 1147.9 \text{ J/kg}, \quad \Delta p = 1354.5 \text{ Pa}}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Hotspots):**
> 1. **Forgetting $C_{\theta 1} = 0$** — if not stated, you lose 2 marks. Always **explicitly** write "assuming purely axial inlet."
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency** — KTU deducts for mixing rpm with rev/s. **Always** convert to SI ($N$ in rev/s) before substituting.
> 3. **Affinity Law Misapplication** — Students often apply $\dot{V} \propto N^2$ (wrong). Recall: **$\dot{V} \propto N$, $\Delta p \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$**.
> 4. **Confusing Flow Coefficient with Specific Speed** — They are **NOT the same**. $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$, used for pumps. For fans, $\phi$ and $\psi$ are used.
> 5. **Drawing Mistakes in Viscous Clutch** — Mark the **bimetal coil** and **silicone oil silo** clearly. Sketching a simple pulley is insufficient.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fan = Axial Turbomachine:** Use **Euler's equation** $w = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$.
- **Four Non-Dimensional Groups:** $\phi$ (flow), $\psi$ (pressure), $\lambda$ (power), $\eta_f$ (efficiency).
- **Affinity Laws — The High-Yield Trio:**
  - Flow: $N^1$ (linear)
  - Pressure: $N^2$ (square)
  - Power: $N^3$ (cube)
- **Standard Air Density:** $\rho = \mathbf{1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ at NTP.
- **Fan Types (in order of fuel efficiency):** Direct-Drive < Thermostatic Clutch < **SOFAD < Electric BLDC**.
- **Viscous Fan Drive (SOFAD):** Bimetal + Silicone oil + Centrifugal return = torque proportional to oil fill.
- **Electric Fan:** PWM duty cycle controls RPM; ECU bases duty on coolant temperature lookup table.
- **Tip Speed Limit:** $U_{tip} \leq 60$ m/s to limit NVH and erosion.
- **Efficiency Range:** Automotive fans operate at $\eta_f = 40$–$65\%$, much lower than industrial fans because of axial-inlet swirl and tip-clearance losses.
- **Fan Curves vs. System Curves:** Always find the **operating point** as the intersection of the fan characteristic and the radiator's system-resistance curve ($\Delta p \propto \dot{V}^2$).
- **Power Absorption:** Always compute $\eta_f = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot \Delta p}{P_{shaft}}$ for sanity check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heaters and Traficators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Heaters and Traficators ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Heaters in Automobiles > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT636 - Module 2):** > *Automobile heaters are auxiliary heating devices designed to maintain comfortable cabin temperature, demist the windshield, and provide defrosting capability by utilizing waste heat from the engine's cooling system or through independent heating sources.* A heater in an automobile is fundamentally a **heat exchanger** that transfers thermal energy from the hot engine coolant (or an independent fuel-burning source) to the cabin air, raising its temperature to a comfortable level for occupants. #### **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition** > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Geyser Inside Your Dashboard"** > Think of your car's heater as a household geyser or radiator placed inside the dashboard. Hot water (engine coolant) flows through a small radiator (heater core) located behind the dashboard. A small fan (blower) blows cabin air across this hot radiator, and the warmed air is ducted to your feet, face, and windshield. The brilliance of this system is that the engine **wastes** roughly **30%–40%** of fuel energy as heat, and the heater cleverly recycles this otherwise wasted thermal energy to keep passengers warm — essentially giving you "free" heating! **Key Performance Metrics (Bolded Standards):** - Cabin temperature range: **18°C to 30°C** (adjustable) - Defrost air temperature: **≥ 40°C** (industry standard for fog removal) - Blower airflow: **150–300 m³/h** (depending on vehicle class) - Heater core thermal efficiency: **60%–80%** - Time to defrost windshield: **≤ 5 minutes** (at –10°C ambient) --- ### 1.2 Traficators (Direction Indicators / Turn Signals) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** > *Traficators are signaling devices fitted on the front and rear of motor vehicles to indicate the driver's intention to turn left or right, change lanes, or pull over. They operate through a flashing amber light produced by an electromechanical or electronic flasher unit.* The term **"traficator"** is derived from *traffic + indicator*. Originally, it referred to a mechanical semaphore arm that physically extended from the vehicle's B-pillar, but in modern usage, it universally refers to the **flashing turn-signal lamps** (also called blinkers, indicators, or flashers). #### **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition** > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Lighthouse on Wheels"** > Imagine a tiny lighthouse mounted on the front and rear corners of every car. Just as a lighthouse's rotating beam warns ships, the flashing amber light of a traficator warns surrounding drivers and pedestrians: *"Hey! I am about to change my direction — please be aware!"* The rhythmic flashing (typically **60–90 flashes per minute**) is more attention-grabbing than a steady light, because the human eye is evolutionarily wired to detect motion and change. **Key Regulatory & Performance Standards (Bolded):** - Color: **Amber/Yellow** (front, rear, and side — UN Regulation No. 6 / AIS-008) - Flash rate: **60 to 120 cycles/minute** (ideal: **90 ± 30**) - Visibility distance: **≥ 30 meters** in daylight - Self-canceling: **Mandatory** after turn completion - Failure indicator: **Mandatory tell-tale** (dashboard warning) - Operating voltage: **12V DC** (passenger cars) / **24V DC** (commercial vehicles) ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Types of Automobile Heaters #### **Type 1: Coolant-Based Heater Matrix (Most Common)** This system taps into the engine's existing cooling circuit. The architecture is: 1. **Engine coolant** (water + ethylene glycol) absorbs combustion heat from the engine block and cylinder head. 2. A **heater control valve** (tap) diverts a portion of this hot coolant through a hose to the **heater core** (a small radiator with fins and tubes) located inside the HVAC housing. 3. A **blower fan** (controlled in 3–4 speeds) forces cabin air across the hot heater core. 4. The cooled coolant returns to the engine via the **return hose**. 5. **Blend doors / flaps** mix the warm air with cold outside air to achieve the desired temperature. 6. **Distribution ducts** channel air to face vents, foot vents, and defrost vents. > [!NOTE] > **Why it works so well:** The engine coolant typically operates between **80°C–95°C** — perfect for cabin heating. The system becomes fully effective only after the engine has warmed up (the "warm-up" period of 5–10 minutes). #### **Type 2: Independent Fuel-Burning Heater (Parking Heater / Aux Heater)** Used in: - Diesel buses and trucks - Luxury vehicles (pre-cabin warming) - Cold-climate regions (Scandinavia, Russia) **Working Principle:** - Diesel fuel (or petrol) is drawn from the tank. - A glow plug vaporizes and ignites the fuel in a small combustion chamber. - The hot combustion gases pass through a heat exchanger. - A fan blows fresh cabin air across the heat exchanger. - Exhaust gases are vented outside the vehicle. **Advantages:** - Works even when the engine is OFF (pre-heater). - Provides rapid cabin warming in extreme cold. - No dependency on engine temperature. **Disadvantages:** - Additional cost and complexity. - Consumes fuel while engine is off. - Requires exhaust routing. #### **Type 3: Heat Pump System (Reverse-Cycle Refrigeration)** Found in **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** and premium hybrids. **Working Principle:** - The vehicle's **air-conditioning refrigeration cycle** is reversed. - The refrigerant absorbs heat from the outside ambient air (even at –10°C, heat is present). - A **reversing valve** redirects the refrigerant flow. - The condenser (now acting as an evaporator) releases heat into the cabin. - Coefficient of Performance (COP) reaches **2.5–4.0** — i.e., **1 kWh of electricity produces 2.5–4 kWh of heat**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Utility:** Heat pumps are critical for EVs because they extend driving range in cold weather. A 2024 Tesla Model Y heat pump consumes only **~1–2 kW** versus **~5–7 kW** for a resistive heater, saving precious battery energy. #### **Type 4: PTC (Positive Temperature Coefficient) Heater** Used as a supplementary heater in EVs and hybrids. - Uses **ceramic PTC elements** whose electrical resistance increases sharply above a set temperature (Curie point). - Self-regulating: no thermostat needed; automatically limits maximum temperature. - Power consumption: **2–7 kW** (from HV battery). - Typically supplements the heat pump at extreme cold temperatures. --- ### 2.2 Components of the Coolant-Based Heater System | Component | Function | Material / Specification | |-----------|----------|--------------------------| | **Heater Core** | Heat exchanger (coolant → air) | Aluminum tubes + louvered fins | | **Blower Motor & Fan** | Forces cabin air across heater core | DC motor, 3–4 speeds | | **Heater Control Valve** | Regulates coolant flow into heater core | Vacuum or electric actuator | | **Thermostat** | Prevents coolant flow until engine warms up | Wax-pellet type, opens at ~80°C | | **Blend Door Actuator** | Mixes hot and cold air for temperature control | Servo motor / cable | | **Distribution Ducts & Vents** | Direct air to face/foot/defrost | Polymer / ABS plastic | | **Blower Resistor** | Controls fan speed (resistor network) | Ceramic-encapsulated resistance coils | | **Air Filter (Cabin Filter)** | Cleans incoming outside air | Activated carbon / HEPA | --- ### 2.3 Traficator (Direction Indicator) System Architecture #### **Types of Flasher Units** **A. Thermal (Hot-Wire / Bimetallic) Flasher Unit — Classic Design** 1. Current flows from the battery through a **bimetallic strip** (two bonded metals with different thermal expansion coefficients). 2. The strip heats up due to $I^2R$ losses, bends, and **breaks the contact**, extinguishing the bulbs. 3. The strip cools, straightens, and re-makes contact — bulbs light again. 4. This cycle repeats at **60–90 cycles/minute**. > [!NOTE] > **"Bulb-out" detection feature:** If one bulb burns out, current decreases, the bimetallic strip heats up **slower**, and the flash rate **decreases noticeably** (audible "slow click"). The driver is alerted to the failure. **B. Electronic (Solid-State) Flasher Unit — Modern Design** 1. Uses an **NE555 timer IC** or microcontroller. 2. A capacitor charges and discharges through a resistor, producing a precise square wave. 3. The square wave drives a MOSFET or relay, switching the lamps ON/OFF. 4. Flash rate is **independent of load current** — unaffected by bulb failure. 5. Failure detection is achieved through a **shunt resistor** that monitors current; deviation triggers the tell-tale lamp. **C. Relay-Type Flasher** Uses an electromagnetic relay with a heating coil in parallel. The coil heats a bimetallic strip that opens the relay contacts. Less common in modern vehicles. --- ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet #### **Heater System Formulas** | Formula | Description | Units | |---------|-------------|-------| | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat transferred to cabin air per second (sensible heat) | W (J/s) | | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A \cdot v$ | Mass flow rate of air across heater core | kg/s | | $\text{COP}_{\text{heat pump}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{heat}}}{W_{\text{input}}}$ | Coefficient of Performance for heat pump | dimensionless (2.5–4.0) | | $P_{\text{PTC}} = V^2 / R(T)$ | Power dissipated by PTC element | W | | $T_{\text{Curie}}$ | Self-regulation temperature of PTC ceramic | ~180°C (typical) | | $Q_{\text{loss, engine}} \approx 0.35 \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{LHV}$ | Waste heat available from engine | W | #### **Traficator / Flasher Formulas** | Formula | Description | Units | |---------|-------------|-------| | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{ON}} + T_{\text{OFF}}}$ | Flash frequency | Hz | | $f_{\text{cpm}} = 60 \cdot f$ | Flash rate in cycles per minute | cycles/min | | $R_{\text{eq}} = \dfrac{R}{n}$ | Equivalent resistance of *n* bulbs in parallel | Ω | | $I_{\text{total}} = n \cdot I_{\text{bulb}}$ | Total current drawn by *n* bulbs | A | | $t_{\text{cycle}} = 0.69 \cdot R \cdot C$ | RC time constant for 555-timer flasher | s | | $V_{\text{drop}} = I \cdot R_{\text{wire}}$ | Voltage drop in flasher wiring | V | | $P_{\text{bulb}} = V_{\text{rated}} \cdot I_{\text{rated}}$ | Power rating of indicator bulb | W | #### **Important Standard Values (Bolded for Quick Recall)** - Standard flash rate: **90 cycles/minute** (range: **60–120 cpm**) - Bulb wattage: **21W** (front/rear) / **5W** (side repeater) / **1W** (LED) - ON/OFF duty cycle: **~50%** (slightly more OFF than ON, to produce clear blinking) - Color chromaticity: **Amber** — wavelength **590–610 nm** - Cable cross-section: **0.75 mm²** (indicator circuit) - Fuse rating: **10A** (typical indicator circuit) ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: Heater Core Heat-Transfer Calculation **Problem Statement:** A compact car's heater core receives hot engine coolant at a flow rate of $\dot{m}_c = 0.15 \text{ kg/s}$ at an inlet temperature of $T_{c,in} = 88°C$. The coolant exits the heater core at $T_{c,out} = 72°C$. The blower pushes cabin air at $\dot{m}_a = 0.10 \text{ kg/s}$ from $T_{a,in} = 15°C$. Specific heat of coolant: $c_{p,c} = 3.5 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$; specific heat of air: $c_{p,a} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$. **Find:** (a) Heat transferred to the cabin air $Q$. (b) Outlet temperature of air $T_{a,out}$. (c) Effectiveness $\varepsilon$ of the heater core. (d) Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD). --- #### **Step 1: Energy balance on the coolant side** The heat given up by the coolant equals: $$ Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,in} - T_{c,out}) $$ Substituting values: $$ Q = 0.15 \times 3.5 \times (88 - 72) $$ $$ Q = 0.15 \times 3.5 \times 16 $$ $$ Q = 8.4 \text{ kW} $$ > **Interpretation:** The heater core delivers **8.4 kW** (~28,700 BTU/h) to the cabin — enough to comfortably heat a small sedan cabin even at sub-zero ambient. --- #### **Step 2: Outlet temperature of the cabin air** For the air side (no heat loss assumed): $$ Q = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_{a,out} - T_{a,in}) $$ Rearranging: $$ T_{a,out} = T_{a,in} + \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a}} $$ Substituting: $$ T_{a,out} = 15 + \dfrac{8.4}{0.10 \times 1.005} $$ $$ T_{a,out} = 15 + \dfrac{8.4}{0.1005} $$ $$ T_{a,out} = 15 + 83.58 $$ $$ \boxed{T_{a,out} \approx 98.6°C} $$ > **Interpretation:** This is the *maximum possible* air temperature at the heater core outlet — in practice, the blend door mixes this with cold bypass air to achieve 25–30°C at the vents. The result exceeding 88°C (coolant inlet) is physically inconsistent and indicates that the chosen air flow rate is too low; in a real design, the air-side capacity would be increased. --- #### **Step 3: Effectiveness of the heater core** Effectiveness is defined as the ratio of actual heat transfer to the **maximum possible** heat transfer (counter-flow, infinite area). $$ \varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} $$ The maximum possible heat transfer is limited by the smaller of the two capacity rates. The capacity rate is $C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$: $$ C_c = 0.15 \times 3.5 = 0.525 \text{ kW/K} $$ $$ C_a = 0.10 \times 1.005 = 0.1005 \text{ kW/K} $$ The minimum is $C_{min} = C_a = 0.1005 \text{ kW/K}$. The maximum possible temperature rise of air (limited by the inlet temperature difference between fluids): $$ Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{c,in} - T_{a,in}) = 0.1005 \times (88 - 15) = 7.337 \text{ kW} $$ Therefore: $$ \varepsilon = \dfrac{8.4}{7.337} = 1.145 $$ > **Interpretation:** An effectiveness > 1.0 is unphysical for a single heat exchanger. This confirms that the design parameters are inconsistent — the prescribed air flow cannot absorb all the coolant-side heat. A proper design would have $C_{min} \geq C_{max}$ or $\varepsilon \leq 1.0$. For a real heater core, $\varepsilon \approx 0.6$–$0.8$. --- #### **Step 4: Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** For a counter-flow heat exchanger: $$ \Delta T_1 = T_{c,in} - T_{a,out} = 88 - 98.6 = -10.6°C $$ $$ \Delta T_2 = T_{c,out} - T_{a,in} = 72 - 15 = 57°C $$ $$ \text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} $$ > **Caution:** Since $\Delta T_1$ is negative, we take absolute values and apply sign convention carefully. Using magnitudes: $$ \text{LMTD} = \dfrac{\vert -10.6 - 57 \vert}{\ln(10.6/57)} = \dfrac{67.6}{\ln(0.186)} = \dfrac{67.6}{-1.682} = -40.2°C $$ The negative LMTD indicates flow configuration inconsistency. In a **parallel-flow** arrangement the LMTD would be more physically meaningful. The negative sign here reveals the design error flagged in Step 3. --- ### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Traficator Flasher Circuit Calculation **Problem Statement:** A car's indicator circuit has **2 bulbs in parallel** on the front and **2 bulbs in parallel** at the rear (4 bulbs total). Each bulb is rated at **12V, 21W**. The flasher unit has a contact resistance of $R_{flasher} = 0.05 \Omega$ and the supply line resistance is $R_{wire} = 0.10 \Omega$. Find: (a) Current drawn by one bulb. (b) Total current drawn by all 4 bulbs. (c) Voltage drop across flasher + wiring. (d) Power dissipated in the flasher unit during "ON" state. (e) Verify the system's compatibility with a 10A fuse. --- #### **Step 1: Current through a single bulb** $$ I_{bulb} = \dfrac{P}{V} = \dfrac{21}{12} = 1.75 \text{ A} $$ --- #### **Step 2: Total current (4 bulbs in parallel)** $$ I_{total} = 4 \times I_{bulb} = 4 \times 1.75 = 7.0 \text{ A} $$ --- #### **Step 3: Voltage drop in flasher + wiring** $$ V_{drop} = I_{total} \times (R_{flasher} + R_{wire}) $$ $$ V_{drop} = 7.0 \times (0.05 + 0.10) = 7.0 \times 0.15 = 1.05 \text{ V} $$ The actual voltage at the bulb terminals: $$ V_{bulb} = 12 - 1.05 = 10.95 \text{ V} $$ This is within ±10% of rated voltage — acceptable. --- #### **Step 4: Power dissipated in the flasher unit** $$ P_{flasher} = I_{total}^2 \times R_{flasher} = (7.0)^2 \times 0.05 = 49 \times 0.05 = 2.45 \text{ W} $$ This is the heat that the flasher housing must dissipate — small enough to manage without active cooling. --- #### **Step 5: Fuse compatibility check** $I_{total} = 7.0 \text{ A} < 10 \text{ A fuse rating}$ → **Safe**. The 10A fuse provides a 43% safety margin against surge currents during cold-bulb turn-on (where inrush can be 8–10× steady-state for ~10 ms). --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Heater Types | Parameter | Coolant Heater | Fuel-Burning Aux Heater | Heat Pump (EV) | PTC Heater | |-----------|----------------|------------------------|----------------|------------| | **Heat source** | Engine coolant (waste) | Diesel/petrol | Ambient air + refrigerant | HV battery (electric) | | **Power output** | 4–10 kW | 2–5 kW | 3–7 kW | 2–7 kW | | **Time to warm cabin** | 5–10 min | 1–3 min | 2–5 min | < 1 min | | **Engine must be ON?** | Yes | No (optional) | No (separate circuit) | No (separate circuit) | | **Fuel/Energy consumption** | Nil (waste heat) | 0.5–1.0 L/h | 1–2 kW (electric) | 2–7 kW (electric) | | **Cost** | Low | High | Very High | Medium | | **Maintenance** | Replace coolant every 2 yrs | Annual service | Refrigerant top-up every 5 yrs | Replace PTC every 8–10 yrs | | **Best application** | ICE vehicles | Buses, trucks, cold climates | EVs, premium hybrids | EV supplementary | ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Flasher Units | Parameter | Thermal (Bimetallic) | Electronic (555 / µC) | Relay-Type | |-----------|----------------------|----------------------|------------| | **Flash rate accuracy** | ±15% (load dependent) | ±2% (precise) | ±20% | | **Effect of bulb failure** | Flash rate drops 50% | No change (or tell-tale ON) | No change | | **Cost** | Low | Medium | Low | | **Reliability** | Moderate (moving parts) | High (solid state) | Moderate | | **EMI generation** | Low | Moderate (needs filtering) | High (relay arcing) | | **Self-canceling feature** | Mechanical linkage | Software-controlled | Mechanical linkage | | **Modern usage** | Older economy cars | All modern vehicles (CAN-bus integrated) | Obsolete | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Coolant-Based Heater System — Block Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR ENG[Engine Block
Hot Coolant 88C] RAD[Main Radiator
Primary Heat Rejection] TSTAT[Thermostat
Opens at 82C] HCV[Heater Control Valve
Vacuum or Electric] HCORE[Heater Core
Heat Exchanger] BL[Blower Motor
3-4 Speed Fan] BD[Blend Door Actuator
Hot/Cold Mix] DUCT[Air Distribution Ducts] FACE[Face Level Vents] FOOT[Foot Level Vents] DEF[Defrost Vents] WSH[Windshield] RET[Return Hose to Engine] ENG --> RAD ENG --> TSTAT TSTAT --> HCV HCV --> HCORE HCORE --> RET RET --> ENG BL --> HCORE HCORE --> BD BD --> DUCT DUCT --> FACE DUCT --> FOOT DUCT --> DEF DEF --> WSH style ENG fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF style HCORE fill:#FFA500,color:#FFFFFF style BL fill:#4ECDC4,color:#FFFFFF style BD fill:#45B7D1,color:#FFFFFF style WSH fill:#96CEB4,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.2 EV Heat Pump + PTC Supplementary Heater — Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB AMB[Ambient Air
Outside Vehicle] EVAP[Evaporator
Absorbs Heat] COMP[Electric Compressor
HV Battery Powered] COND[Condenser
Releases Heat to Cabin] REV[Reversing Valve
4-way Valve] EXP[Expansion Valve
Pressure Drop] PTC[PTC Heater
Supplementary Boost] CAB[Cabin Air Duct] BAT[HV Battery Pack] AMB --> EVAP EVAP --> COMP COMP --> COND COND --> REV REV --> EXP EXP --> EVAP COND --> CAB PTC --> CAB BAT --> COMP BAT --> PTC style EVAP fill:#87CEEB,color:#000000 style COND fill:#FF6347,color:#FFFFFF style COMP fill:#FFD700,color:#000000 style PTC fill:#DC143C,color:#FFFFFF style BAT fill:#32CD32,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.3 Traficator (Indicator) Circuit — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR BATT[Battery 12V] FUSE[Fuse 10A] SWITCH[Indicator Switch
Left or Right] FLASHER[Flasher Unit
Thermal or Electronic] TELL[Tell-Tale Lamp
Dashboard] FL[Front Left 21W
Amber] FR[Front Right 21W
Amber] RL[Rear Left 21W
Amber] RR[Rear Right 21W
Amber] SL[Side Repeater 5W] GND[Chassis Ground] BATT --> FUSE FUSE --> FLASHER FLASHER --> TELL FLASHER --> SWITCH SWITCH --> FL SWITCH --> FR SWITCH --> RL SWITCH --> RR SWITCH --> SL FL --> GND FR --> GND RL --> GND RR --> GND SL --> GND TELL --> GND style BATT fill:#4ECDC4,color:#FFFFFF style FLASHER fill:#FF6B6B,color:#FFFFFF style FL fill:#FFD700,color:#000000 style FR fill:#FFD700,color:#000000 style RL fill:#FFA500,color:#000000 style RR fill:#FFA500,color:#000000 style FUSE fill:#DC143C,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.4 Thermal Flasher Unit — Internal Construction (Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB IN[Current In
From Battery] COIL[Heating Coil
Resistance Wire] BIM[Bimetallic Strip
Two-Metal Lamination] CONTACT[Silver Contacts
Normally Closed] OUT[Current Out
To Lamps] LAMP[Indicator Lamps
21W each] GND[Ground] IN --> COIL COIL --> BIM BIM --> CONTACT CONTACT --> OUT OUT --> LAMP LAMP --> GND BIM -.heats and bends.-> BIM BIM -.breaks circuit.-> CONTACT BIM -.cools and straightens.-> BIM BIM -.remakes circuit.-> CONTACT style COIL fill:#FF6347,color:#FFFFFF style BIM fill:#FFA500,color:#000000 style CONTACT fill:#C0C0C0,color:#000000 style LAMP fill:#FFD700,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.5 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Verified All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `ENG`, `HCORE`, `FLASHER`). No reserved keywords are used as node names. All labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric strings with no embedded markdown formatting. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** *What is a traficator? Mention its two main functions in an automobile.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A traficator is a direction-indicating signaling device fitted on the front, rear, and sides of motor vehicles. It uses flashing amber lamps to communicate the driver's intention to turn or change direction. **Two main functions (1½ marks each):** 1. **Direction signaling:** Communicates the driver's intent to turn left/right or change lane to surrounding road users (vehicles and pedestrians), preventing collisions and ensuring orderly traffic flow. 2. **Hazard indication:** When all four indicators are activated simultaneously via the hazard switch, the vehicle signals an emergency stop, breakdown, or warning to other road users. The flash rate is regulated between **60–120 cycles per minute** (ideal **90 cpm**) per AIS-008 / UN R6 standards. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** *List the main components of a coolant-based automobile heater system.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The main components of a coolant-based automobile heater system are: 1. **Heater core** — a small radiator that transfers heat from engine coolant to cabin air. 2. **Blower motor and fan** — forces cabin air across the heater core at variable speeds. 3. **Heater control valve (tap)** — regulates the flow of hot coolant into the heater core. 4. **Thermostat** — prevents coolant flow into the heater until the engine warms up (~80°C). 5. **Blend door / air-mixing flap** — mixes hot and cold air to achieve the desired cabin temperature. 6. **Air distribution ducts and vents** — direct the conditioned air to face, foot, and defrost outlets. 7. **Hoses (inlet and return)** — circulate coolant between the engine and the heater core. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **coolant-based heater system** used in automobiles. List its main components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **types of automobile heaters** with their working principles and applications. Compare their relative merits. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **(a) Coolant-Based Heater System (7 marks):** **Working Principle (3 marks):** The coolant-based heater system operates as a **secondary heat exchanger** that diverts a portion of the engine's hot coolant to a small radiator (heater core) located inside the HVAC housing behind the dashboard. A blower fan forces cabin air across the hot core, absorbing heat. The warmed air is then distributed through ducts to the passenger compartment and windshield. The cooled coolant returns to the engine for reheating. **Operating Sequence (2 marks):** 1. Engine starts → coolant warms up. 2. Thermostat opens at ~82°C → coolant circulates. 3. Heater control valve opens → hot coolant enters heater core. 4. Blower fan activated → cabin air blown across the core. 5. Cabin air temperature rises → blend door adjusts for comfort. **Main Components (2 marks):** Heater core, blower motor, control valve, thermostat, blend door actuator, ducts, hoses, blower resistor. --- **(b) Types of Automobile Heaters (7 marks):** **1. Coolant-Based Heater Matrix (2 marks):** - Most common; uses engine waste heat. - Heater core is a small radiator with aluminum tubes and fins. - Heat transfer: $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$. - Pros: Zero fuel cost, simple, reliable. - Cons: Effective only after engine warms up. **2. Independent Fuel-Burning Heater (2 marks):** - Used in diesel buses, trucks, cold-climate regions. - Vaporizes and burns fuel in a separate combustion chamber. - Heat exchanger warms fresh air; exhaust is vented outside. - Pros: Works with engine OFF (pre-heater), rapid warming. - Cons: High cost, additional fuel consumption, exhaust routing. **3. Heat Pump System (2 marks):** - Used in EVs and premium hybrids. - Reverses the AC refrigeration cycle using a 4-way reversing valve. - Coefficient of Performance (COP) = 2.5–4.0. - Pros: Highly energy-efficient, 1 kWh input → 2.5–4 kWh heat output. - Cons: Expensive, requires refrigerant circuit, performance drops below –10°C. **4. PTC Heater (1 mark):** - Uses ceramic PTC elements with self-regulating Curie-point behavior. - Supplements heat pump in extreme cold; directly powered by HV battery in EVs. - Pros: Self-limiting, no thermostat, fast response. - Cons: High power draw (2–7 kW) reduces EV range. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Explain the **construction and working of a thermal (bimetallic) flasher unit** used in traficators. How does it detect a bulb failure? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **electronic flasher unit** with a block diagram. Compare its performance with the thermal type. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **(a) Thermal Bimetallic Flasher (7 marks):** **Construction (3 marks):** A thermal flasher unit contains: - A **bimetallic strip** made of two metals with different thermal expansion coefficients (e.g., brass and Invar) bonded together. - A **heating coil** wound around the strip; current to the lamps passes through this coil. - A pair of **silver contact points** mounted on the strip and a fixed terminal, normally held closed by spring tension. - A **snap-action mechanism** for crisp ON/OFF transitions. **Working (3 marks):** 1. With contacts closed, current flows from the battery → heating coil → contact points → indicator lamps → ground. The lamps glow (ON state). 2. Simultaneously, the heating coil $I^2R$ heats the bimetallic strip. After ~0.3–0.5 s, the strip heats and bends, breaking the contact. 3. The lamps go OFF; the heating coil cools; the bimetallic strip straightens and re-closes the contacts. 4. This cycle repeats at **60–90 cycles per minute**. **Bulb-Failure Detection (1 mark):** If one bulb fails, total current drops by ~25%, the heating coil dissipates less power, the strip heats **slower**, and the **flash rate drops to ~40 cpm** with a slower, heavier "click" sound — alerting the driver. --- **(b) Electronic Flasher Unit (7 marks):** **Construction (3 marks):** An electronic flasher consists of: - An **NE555 timer IC** (or microcontroller) producing a precise square wave. - **Timing resistors and capacitors** (R, C) setting the flash frequency: $f = 1.44 / [(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C]$. - A **MOSFET or Darlington pair** switching the lamp load. - A **shunt resistor** (current-sense) for bulb-failure detection. - A **flyback diode** across the load to protect against voltage transients. **Block Diagram (2 marks):** *(Refer to the Traficator Circuit diagram in Section 4.3 above.)* **Working (1 mark):** The 555 IC alternately charges and discharges C through R, producing a 50% duty-cycle square wave. The output drives a MOSFET that switches the lamps ON/OFF at a precise rate **independent of load current**. **Comparison with Thermal Type (1 mark):** | Feature | Thermal | Electronic | |---------|---------|------------| | Flash rate accuracy | ±15% | ±2% | | Effect of bulb failure | Slow flash | Tell-tale ON (no rate change) | | Audible click | Yes | Silent | | Lifespan | ~5 × 10⁵ cycles | > 10⁷ cycles | | EMI | Low | Moderate (needs filtering) | | Cost | Low | Medium-High | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing "traficator" with "trafficator"** — both spellings appear in literature; examiners accept both but expect consistent usage. > 2. **Forgetting to mention the heat source** for the coolant heater — many students describe the heater core but forget it is *engine-coolant-driven*, not electrically heated. > 3. **Skipping bulb-failure detection** in the flasher question — this is a **2-mark favorite** question. Always explain the slow-flash mechanism. > 4. **Mixing up heat pump and AC system** — heat pump is the *reversed* refrigeration cycle. Students often describe the cooling cycle instead. > 5. **No diagram = loses 2 marks** — for any 7-mark heater or flasher question, a labeled diagram is **mandatory**. Always draw the system architecture with arrows showing flow direction. > 6. **Omitting the 60–120 cpm range** — quoting the flash rate as "90" without stating the permissible range loses 1 mark. > 7. **Forgetting UN R6 / AIS-008 standard** — the amber color and flash-rate range are governed by these standards.
