Citing them adds value. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember #### **Heaters — Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Heater core** = small radiator using engine coolant as heat source. - **Engine coolant** typically at **80–95°C** powers the heater. - **Blower motor** speeds: 3–4 stages via blower resistor. - **Thermostat** prevents heater activation until engine warms up. - **Types**: Coolant-based, Fuel-burning, Heat pump, PTC. - **Heat pump COP** = 2.5–4.0 (1 kWh electricity → 2.5–4 kWh heat). - **PTC heaters** are self-regulating at Curie temperature (~180°C). - **Heat transfer formula**: $Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$. - **Defrost time target**: ≤ 5 minutes at –10°C ambient. - **EVs** use heat pumps + PTC to extend driving range in cold. #### **Traficators — Rapid Revision Checklist** - **Traficator** = direction indicator / turn signal. - **Color**: Amber (590–610 nm wavelength) per UN R6 / AIS-008. - **Flash rate**: **60–120 cycles/minute** (ideal: **90 ± 30**). - **Bulb rating**: 21W (front/rear), 5W (side repeater). - **Flasher types**: Thermal (bimetallic), Electronic (NE555), Relay. - **Thermal flasher** uses a bimetallic strip heated by $I^2R$ loss. - **Bulb failure** → thermal flasher slows down; electronic flasher activates tell-tale. - **Self-canceling mechanism** mandatory after turn completion. - **Voltage**: 12V DC (passenger), 24V DC (commercial). - **Fuse rating**: 10A (standard indicator circuit). - **LED indicators** are increasingly common (1–3 W, longer life, faster response). - **Hazard lights** = all four indicators + tell-tale flashing simultaneously. #### **Cross-Comparison Heuristics (High-Frequency Exam Hooks)** | Quick Hook | Answer | |------------|--------| | Heater core fluid | Engine coolant (water + glycol) | | Heat pump working fluid | Refrigerant (R-1234yf / R-134a) | | PTC stands for | Positive Temperature Coefficient | | COP of ideal heat pump | > 1 (typically 2.5–4.0) | | Color of indicator | Amber/Yellow | | Standard flash rate | 90 cpm | | Heater based on waste heat | Coolant heater matrix | | Independent heater fuel | Diesel/Petrol | | Bulb-failure in thermal flasher | Flash rate drops | | Bulb-failure in electronic flasher | Tell-tale lamp glows steadily |

topics.title: Automobile Air Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Automobile Air Conditioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automobile Air Conditioning** is an integrated vehicular environmental control system designed to regulate cabin air temperature, humidity, purity, and circulation to provide thermal comfort to occupants under all external ambient conditions. It operates on the thermodynamic principle of the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)**, in which a refrigerant undergoes phase change to absorb heat from the cabin (low-pressure evaporator) and reject it to the atmosphere (high-pressure condenser), driven by a belt-driven or electrically driven compressor coupled to the engine or a high-voltage bus.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Automobile air conditioning refers to the system that conditions the air inside the passenger compartment by controlling temperature, humidity, dust load and air movement using a refrigeration cycle integrated with the vehicle's powertrain.*

---

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Sweating Glass of Water"**
>
> Imagine pulling a chilled water bottle out of the refrigerator on a humid Kerala afternoon. Within seconds, droplets form on its surface. Where did this water come from? The warm, humid cabin air (hot gas) **released heat** into the cold bottle surface (cool surface), causing the water vapour in the air to **condense** into liquid.
>
> An automobile air conditioner does the **opposite intentionally**: it makes one component (the evaporator) artificially cold using a phase-changing refrigerant. When hot cabin air is blown across this cold evaporator, the refrigerant **absorbs heat** from the air and carries it away to the outside world (condenser at the front of the car). The cooled, dehumidified air is then delivered back into the cabin. The compressor is essentially the "heart" that pumps this refrigerant around, just as the heart pumps blood.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used

The following benchmark values are standard references for KTU automobile AC problems:

| Parameter | Standard Metric |
|---|---|
| Cabin design temperature | **22 – 25 °C** |
| Cabin relative humidity target | **40 – 60 %** |
| Typical vehicle cooling load | **3 – 6 kW** |
| Refrigerant R-134a boiling point (1 atm) | **−26.3 °C** |
| Refrigerant R-1234yf boiling point (1 atm) | **−29.4 °C** |
| High-side pressure (typical) | **1.4 – 1.8 MPa** |
| Low-side pressure (typical) | **0.15 – 0.30 MPa** |
| Refrigerant global warming potential (R-134a) | **1430 GWP** |
| Refrigerant global warming potential (R-1234yf) | **4 GWP** |
| Coefficient of Performance (COP) target | **2.0 – 3.5** |

---

## 1.4 Functional Objectives of an Automotive AC System

> [!NOTE]
> A properly engineered automobile AC must simultaneously satisfy **four** comfort parameters inside the cabin:
>
> 1. **Temperature control** (cooling in summer, heating in winter)
> 2. **Humidity control** (dehumidification in monsoon)
> 3. **Air purity** (filtration of dust, pollen, PM2.5)
> 4. **Air distribution** (proper velocity, vents, defrost)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC) — Stepwise Logic

The automobile AC operates on a closed-loop Rankine cycle reversed in direction (refrigeration cycle). The refrigerant R-134a circulates through **four** irreversible-but-near-ideal components:

### Step 1 — Compression (1 → 2)
- The refrigerant enters the compressor as **low-pressure, low-temperature, vapour** (state 1).
- The compressor (driven by the engine via belt, or electrically by the HV battery in EVs) compresses it to **high-pressure, high-temperature, superheated vapour** (state 2).
- This is an **isentropic compression** in the ideal cycle.
- **Why?** Raising pressure raises the saturation temperature so that the refrigerant can dump heat to the outside ambient air, which is hotter than cabin air.

### Step 2 — Condensation (2 → 3)
- The hot, high-pressure vapour flows into the condenser (mounted in front of the radiator).
- Outside ambient air (or fan-forced air) absorbs heat from the refrigerant.
- The refrigerant **rejects latent heat** and condenses into **high-pressure, sub-cooled liquid** (state 3).
- Heat rejected $Q_{out} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$.

### Step 3 — Expansion / Throttling (3 → 4)
- The high-pressure liquid passes through the **Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV)** or **Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT)**.
- This is an **isenthalpic throttling** process: $h_3 = h_4$.
- Pressure drops sharply; some liquid flashes into vapour, cooling the remaining liquid to a **low-pressure, low-temperature, two-phase mixture** (state 4).

### Step 4 — Evaporation (4 → 1)
- The cold two-phase mixture enters the evaporator (located inside the HVAC box, behind the dashboard).
- Warm cabin air is blown across the evaporator fins by the blower motor.
- The refrigerant **absorbs latent heat** from the air and fully evaporates into low-pressure vapour (state 1).
- Cooled, dehumidified air is delivered into the cabin.
- Heat absorbed $Q_{in} = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation the KTU paper-setter may use for a 14-mark numerical or descriptive question on this topic. Memorise the symbol definitions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{in} = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$ | Refrigeration effect (cooling provided) | kW |
| 2 | $Q_{out} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$ | Heat rejected at condenser | kW |
| 3 | $W_{comp} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | Compressor work input | kW |
| 4 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{W_{comp}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of Performance (dimensionless) | — |
| 5 | $h_3 = h_4$ | Isenthalpic expansion through TXV | kJ/kg |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Refrigerant mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 7 | $h_2 = h_1 + \dfrac{h_2^{s} - h_1}{\eta_{isen}}$ | Actual compressor outlet enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Reversible / theoretical COP limit | — |
| 9 | $T_{dew} = T_{sat}(P_{evap})$ | Evaporator saturation temperature | °C |
| 10 | $D = \dfrac{h_2 - h_3}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Coefficient of heat rejection ratio | — |
| 11 | $\rho_{air} \approx 1.20$ | Density of cabin/outside air at 25 °C | kg/m³ |
| 12 | $\dot{V}_{air} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{\rho_{air} \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ | Required cabin air volumetric flow | m³/s |

**Where:**
- $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$ = refrigerant specific enthalpies at the four state points (kJ/kg)
- $\dot{m}$ = refrigerant mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $T_L$ = evaporator saturation temperature (K)
- $T_H$ = condenser saturation temperature (K)
- $\eta_{isen}$ = isentropic efficiency of compressor
- $c_p$ of air $\approx$ **1.005 kJ/kg·K**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Passenger cars | Cabin cooling + heating + defrost |
| Buses & coaches | Roof-mounted integrated HVAC |
| Refrigerated trucks | Reefer units for cold-chain logistics |
| Construction equipment | Operator cabin climate control |
| Electric vehicles (EVs) | Heat-pump systems to extend range |
| Motorsport | Driver cooling suits + cabin AC |
| Aerospace | Cabin pressure + temperature control |

The same VCRC principle scales from a 2 kW Maruti-Suzuki cabin unit up to a 60 kW Volvo bus HVAC system — only the component sizes, refrigerant quantity and control sophistication change.

---

## 2.4 Why and How Each Step Works (Intuitive Layer)

| Step | Why does this happen? | How is it achieved in the car? |
|---|---|---|
| Compression | Pressure must be raised so condensation temperature exceeds ambient | Belt-driven swash-plate / scroll compressor |
| Condensation | Heat must be dumped to outside air to reuse the refrigerant | Tube-and-fin condenser in front of radiator |
| Expansion | Pressure drop causes partial flash evaporation, dropping temperature | Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) or Fixed Orifice Tube |
| Evaporation | Cold refrigerant absorbs cabin heat to provide cooling | Plate-fin evaporator behind dashboard |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Balance Across the Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation Principle**
> For a closed, steady-flow refrigeration cycle, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied over one complete cycle requires that all energy entering as work equals the net difference between heat rejected and heat absorbed.

**Statement:**

$$\dot{W}_{comp} + \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{Q}_{out}$$

(Energy In = Energy Out, since the working fluid returns to its original state after one cycle, $\Delta U_{cycle} = 0$.)

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Apply SFEE (Steady Flow Energy Equation) to each component, neglecting KE and PE:

*Compressor (1 → 2):*

$$\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$$

*Condenser (2 → 3):*

$$\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$$

*Expansion valve (3 → 4):*

$$h_3 = h_4 \quad \text{(isenthalpic, no work, no heat transfer)}$$

*Evaporator (4 → 1):*

$$\dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$$

**Summing around the cycle** (work input positive, heat input positive at evaporator, heat output positive at condenser):

$$\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{Q}_{out} - \dot{Q}_{in}$$

**Substitute expressions:**

$$\dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3) - \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$$

This simplifies identically, confirming energy conservation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Coefficient of Performance (COP)

**Definition:**

$$COP = \dfrac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{\dot{W}_{comp}}$$

**Substitute the expressions from Section 3.1:**

$$COP = \dfrac{\dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)}{\dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)}$$

Cancel $\dot{m}$:

$$COP = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

This is the **ideal (reversible) COP** corresponding to **isentropic compression**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Actual Compressor Work Using Isentropic Efficiency

The isentropic efficiency of the compressor is defined as:

$$\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{h_2^{s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$$

Rearranging for the actual outlet enthalpy $h_2$:

$$h_2 = h_1 + \dfrac{h_2^{s} - h_1}{\eta_{isen}}$$

**Work out (actual):**

$$\dot{W}_{actual} = \dot{m}\left[h_1 + \dfrac{h_2^{s} - h_1}{\eta_{isen}} - h_1\right] = \dfrac{\dot{m}(h_2^{s} - h_1)}{\eta_{isen}}$$

**Actual COP:**

$$COP_{actual} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_2^{s} - h_1)/\eta_{isen}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Question (14-mark style):**
> *An automotive AC system using R-134a operates between an evaporator pressure of 0.18 MPa (saturation temperature −12 °C) and a condenser pressure of 1.4 MPa (saturation temperature 52 °C). The refrigerant enters the compressor as saturated vapour at 0.18 MPa and leaves at 1.4 MPa, 60 °C. It leaves the condenser as saturated liquid. The cabin cooling load is 4 kW. Assuming isentropic compression and a compressor isentropic efficiency of 0.78, determine: (a) refrigerant mass flow rate, (b) compressor power, (c) COP, (d) heat rejected at the condenser.*

### Given Enthalpies (R-134a Property Tables)

| State Point | Description | Enthalpy $h$ (kJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saturated vapour at 0.18 MPa, −12 °C | $h_1 = 243.34$ |
| 2 (ideal) | Superheated vapour at 1.4 MPa, $s_2 = s_1$ | $h_2^{s} = 281.50$ |
| 3 | Saturated liquid at 1.4 MPa, 52 °C | $h_3 = 117.77$ |
| 4 | After isenthalpic expansion | $h_4 = h_3 = 117.77$ |

### Solution

**(a) Refrigeration effect per kg:**

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 243.34 - 117.77 = 125.57 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Refrigerant mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{RE} = \dfrac{4 \; \text{kW}}{125.57 \; \text{kJ/kg}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 0.03186 \; \text{kg/s}}$$

**(b) Actual compressor outlet enthalpy (using $\eta_{isen}$ = 0.78):**

$$h_2 = h_1 + \dfrac{h_2^{s} - h_1}{\eta_{isen}} = 243.34 + \dfrac{281.50 - 243.34}{0.78}$$

$$h_2 = 243.34 + \dfrac{38.16}{0.78} = 243.34 + 48.92 = 292.26 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Actual compressor work per kg:**

$$w_{comp} = h_2 - h_1 = 292.26 - 243.34 = 48.92 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**Compressor power:**

$$\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot w_{comp} = 0.03186 \times 48.92$$

$$\boxed{\dot{W}_{comp} = 1.559 \; \text{kW}}$$

**(c) Actual COP:**

$$COP_{actual} = \dfrac{RE}{w_{comp}} = \dfrac{125.57}{48.92}$$

$$\boxed{COP_{actual} = 2.567}$$

**Ideal (Carnot) COP for comparison:**

$$COP_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \dfrac{261 \; \text{K}}{(325 - 261) \; \text{K}} = \dfrac{261}{64} = 4.078$$

This confirms the Second Law: $COP_{actual} < COP_{Carnot}$.

**(d) Heat rejected at condenser:**

$$\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3) = 0.03186 \times (292.26 - 117.77)$$

$$\dot{Q}_{out} = 0.03186 \times 174.49$$

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{out} = 5.560 \; \text{kW}}$$

**Energy balance check:**

$$\dot{W}_{comp} + \dot{Q}_{in} = 1.559 + 4.000 = 5.559 \; \text{kW} \approx \dot{Q}_{out} \;\checkmark$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: TXV vs Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT)

| Parameter | Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) | Fixed Orifice Tube (FOT) |
|---|---|---|
| Control type | Modulating, feedback-controlled | Fixed area, non-modulating |
| Response to load | Excellent (adjusts opening) | Moderate (relies on compressor cycling) |
| Stability | High at part-load | Can hunt at low loads |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Failure mode | Sticking open/closed | Clogging with debris |
| Typical vehicles | Premium sedans, SUVs | Economy hatchbacks (older designs) |
| Sensing element | Remote bulb at evaporator outlet | None |

---

## 3.6 Component-Function Analytical Matrix

| Component | Function | Failure Symptoms |
|---|---|---|
| Compressor | Pump refrigerant, raise pressure | No cooling, clutch not engaging, noise |
| Condenser | Reject heat to ambient | High head pressure, lukewarm air |
| TXV / FOT | Expand refrigerant, drop P & T | Frost on evaporator, poor cooling |
| Evaporator | Absorb cabin heat | Warm air, fogging, odours |
| Receiver-Drier | Filter, dehydrate, store refrigerant | Moisture freeze, no cooling |
| Blower motor | Circulate cabin air through evaporator | Weak airflow |
| Cabin filter (Pollen filter) | Filter dust, PM2.5 | Musty smell, foggy windows |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VCRC Block Diagram (Automotive AC Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Compressor\nBelt-driven or Electric\nRaises P & T"] --> B["Condenser\nFront of radiator\nRejects heat to ambient"]
    B --> C["Receiver-Drier\nFilters moisture\nStores refrigerant"]
    C --> D["Expansion Valve\nTXV or FOT\nDrops P isenthalpically"]
    D --> E["Evaporator\nInside HVAC box\nAbsorbs cabin heat"]
    E --> A
    F["Cabin Air\nHot humid"] -->|"blown by blower"| E
    E -->|"cold dry air"| G["Cabin\n22-25 C, 50% RH"]
    B -->|"heat dumped"| H["Ambient Air\nOutside vehicle"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The refrigerant loops clockwise (compressor → condenser → receiver-drier → expansion valve → evaporator → back to compressor). The cabin air only touches the evaporator — it never mixes with the refrigerant.

```mermaid
    P2 -->|"Actual compression\neta = 0.78"| P2a["State 2\nActual Superheat\nh2 = 292.26 kJ/kg"]
```

## 4.4 Refrigerant Type Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R12["R-12 CFC\nBanned 1996\nGWP 10900"] -->|replaced by| R134["R-134a HFC\n1996-2021\nGWP 1430"]
    R134 -->|replaced by| R1234["R-1234yf HFO\n2021 onwards\nGWP 4"]
    R134 -->|alternative| R744["R-744 CO2\nTranscritical\nGWP 1"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the change?** The Montreal Protocol (1987) phased out CFCs (R-12) due to ozone depletion. The EU F-Gas Regulation (2014) is now phasing down HFCs (R-134a) due to high global warming potential. New vehicles use R-1234yf or CO₂ (R-744).

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, May 2024]**

*State the four basic components of any vapour compression refrigeration system and write the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Awarded on Key Words):**

1. **Compressor** — Raises the pressure and temperature of the refrigerant by compressing low-pressure vapour to high-pressure vapour. *(1 mark)*
2. **Condenser** — Rejects the latent heat of the refrigerant to the outside atmosphere, condensing it into a high-pressure liquid. *(1 mark)*
3. **Expansion valve (TXV/FOT)** — Performs isenthalpic throttling, dropping the pressure and temperature of the refrigerant to create a cold two-phase mixture. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Evaporator** — Absorbs heat from the cabin air by evaporating the liquid refrigerant, providing cool, dehumidified air to the cabin. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Dec 2023]**

*Why is R-1234yf replacing R-134a in new-generation automobile air conditioners? Mention two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Environmental compliance** — R-1234yf has a **Global Warming Potential (GWP) of 4**, compared to R-134a's **GWP of 1430**. This aligns with the EU F-Gas Regulation and Kyoto Protocol targets. *(1.5 marks)*
2. **Energy efficiency** — R-1234yf has a slightly higher coefficient of performance at typical automotive operating conditions, marginally improving fuel economy. *(0.75 mark)*
3. **Slight toxicity advantage** — R-1234yf has a lower atmospheric lifetime (~11 days vs 14 years for R-134a), making it a near-drop-in replacement. *(0.75 mark)*

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, May 2024 | Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a vapour compression refrigeration system used in an automobile air conditioner, with a neat block diagram. List the four state points and the corresponding processes. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An automotive AC system using R-134a has an evaporator at 0.20 MPa (saturation temperature −10 °C) and a condenser at 1.6 MPa (saturation temperature 54 °C). The refrigerant enters the compressor as saturated vapour and leaves as superheated vapour at 1.6 MPa, 65 °C. The refrigerant leaves the condenser as saturated liquid. The cabin cooling load is 5 kW. Using the following enthalpies:

- $h_1 = 244.50 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated vapour at 0.20 MPa)
- $h_2 = 295.10 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ (superheated vapour at 1.6 MPa, 65 °C)
- $h_3 = 127.25 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid at 1.6 MPa)

Compute:
(i) Refrigeration effect *(1 mark)*
(ii) Refrigerant mass flow rate *(2 marks)*
(iii) Compressor power input *(2 marks)*
(iv) Coefficient of performance *(1 mark)*
(v) Heat rejected at the condenser *(1 mark)*

---

### **Model Answer A**

#### (a) Working of VCRC (7 Marks)

The vapour compression refrigeration cycle operates as a closed loop with the following sequence:

**Step 1 — Compression (1 → 2):** The compressor (typically a swash-plate type, belt-driven by the engine) sucks low-pressure vapour from the evaporator at state 1 and compresses it isentropically to a high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapour at state 2.

**Step 2 — Condensation (2 → 3):** The hot, high-pressure vapour enters the condenser (tube-and-fin, mounted in front of the radiator). Ambient air blown by the fan and the ram effect of vehicle motion absorbs heat from the refrigerant. The refrigerant rejects latent heat and condenses into a saturated liquid at state 3.

**Step 3 — Throttling (3 → 4):** The high-pressure liquid then passes through the Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV). This is an isenthalpic process ($h_3 = h_4$). The pressure drops sharply, and a portion of the refrigerant flashes into vapour, cooling the remaining liquid to a low-temperature two-phase mixture at state 4.

**Step 4 — Evaporation (4 → 1):** The cold two-phase mixture enters the evaporator (plate-fin heat exchanger inside the HVAC box). The blower motor drives warm cabin air across the evaporator fins. The refrigerant absorbs heat and completely evaporates back to saturated vapour at state 1, returning to the compressor.

[Block diagram showing all four components with state points 1, 2, 3, 4 — *(award 2 marks for diagram with labels)*]

**State-Point Summary Table:**

| State | Description | Phase |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Compressor inlet | Low-P saturated vapour |
| 2 | Compressor outlet | High-P superheated vapour |
| 3 | Condenser outlet | High-P saturated liquid |
| 4 | Evaporator inlet | Low-P two-phase mixture |

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\dot{Q}_{in} = 5 \; \text{kW}$
- $h_1 = 244.50 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $h_2 = 295.10 \; \text{kJ/kg}$
- $h_3 = 127.25 \; \text{kJ/kg}$

**From isenthalpic expansion:**
$$h_4 = h_3 = 127.25 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(i) Refrigeration Effect (RE):**
$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 244.50 - 127.25 = 117.25 \; \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Mass Flow Rate:**
$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{RE} = \dfrac{5}{117.25} = 0.04264 \; \text{kg/s}$$

**(iii) Compressor Power:**
$$\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1) = 0.04264 \times (295.10 - 244.50)$$
$$\dot{W}_{comp} = 0.04264 \times 50.60 = 2.158 \; \text{kW}$$

**(iv) Coefficient of Performance:**
$$COP = \dfrac{RE}{w_{comp}} = \dfrac{117.25}{50.60} = 2.317$$

**(v) Heat Rejected at Condenser:**
$$\dot{Q}_{out} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3) = 0.04264 \times (295.10 - 127.25)$$
$$\dot{Q}_{out} = 0.04264 \times 167.85 = 7.158 \; \text{kW}$$

**Verification:** $\dot{W}_{comp} + \dot{Q}_{in} = 2.158 + 5.000 = 7.158 \; \text{kW} = \dot{Q}_{out}$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *ALTERNATIVE CHOICE*
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Dec 2023 | Module 2]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working of the following components used in an automobile air conditioning system: *(i) Swash-plate compressor *(3.5 marks)*, (ii) Thermostatic expansion valve *(3.5 marks)*.

**(b)** Discuss the recent developments in automobile air conditioning with reference to: *(i) Climate control systems (automatic AC) *(3.5 marks)*, (ii) Use of R-1234yf and CO₂ (R-744) refrigerants *(3.5 marks)*.

---

### **Model Answer B**

#### (a)(i) Swash-Plate Compressor (3.5 Marks)

A **swash-plate compressor** is the most common automotive AC compressor. Its construction includes:

- A **cylindrical housing** with a driving shaft along the axis.
- A **swash-plate** (angled disc) mounted on the shaft, whose tilt angle is often variable.
- **Pistons** (typically 5, 6, 7, or 10) housed in bores arranged axially around the shaft.
- **Suction and discharge reed valves** at the head end of each cylinder.

**Working:** When the engine rotates the drive shaft, the inclined swash-plate converts rotational motion into reciprocating piston motion. On the upstroke, refrigerant is drawn into the cylinder through the suction reed valve. On the downstroke, the refrigerant is compressed and pushed out through the discharge valve into the high-pressure line. The continuous reciprocation produces a near-pulsation-free flow. The compressor is engaged/disengaged from the engine via an **electromagnetic clutch** controlled by the thermostat.

#### (a)(ii) Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) (3.5 Marks)

The TXV is a precision flow-control device that regulates refrigerant flow into the evaporator to match the cabin cooling load.

**Construction:**
- A **diaphragm** at the top, acted upon by a remote bulb sensing the evaporator outlet temperature.
- A **spring** providing opposing force.
- A **needle/pin** controlling the orifice area.
- A **remote bulb** charged with the same refrigerant, strapped to the evaporator outlet pipe.

**Working:** When cabin heat load is high, evaporator outlet temperature rises → bulb pressure rises → diaphragm deflects downward → needle opens wider → more refrigerant flows in → cooling increases. Conversely, when the evaporator becomes too cold (risk of icing), the bulb pressure drops → spring force pushes needle up → flow is restricted → cooling reduces. This negative feedback loop maintains **constant superheat** (~4–8 °C) at the evaporator outlet, optimising performance.

#### (b)(i) Automatic Climate Control (3.5 Marks)

Modern vehicles feature **Automatic Temperature Control (ATC)** systems that go beyond manual AC by:

- Using **multiple cabin temperature sensors** (sun sensor, in-car sensor, ambient sensor, humidity sensor).
- A **microprocessor-based control unit** that compares the set temperature with the actual cabin temperature.
- A **servo motor-driven air-mix door** that continuously modulates the proportion of air passing through the heater core vs. the evaporator to achieve the precise target temperature.
- A **variable-displacement compressor** or **clutch cycling** to modulate cooling capacity.
- **Dual-zone or tri-zone** variants allow driver and passenger to set different temperatures independently.

The driver simply sets "22 °C AUTO" and the system automatically manages blower speed, temperature, air distribution, compressor engagement and recirculation.

#### (b)(ii) R-1234yf and R-744 (CO₂) (3.5 Marks)

**R-1234yf (HFO):**
- GWP ≈ 4, ODP = 0, atmospheric lifetime ≈ 11 days.
- Near-drop-in replacement for R-134a.
- Slight mild flammability (ASHRAE A2L classification) — requires safety mitigations in the refrigerant line.
- Adopted by all major OEMs (Toyota, Honda, VW, GM, Hyundai) since ~2017–2021.

**R-744 (CO₂, Transcritical Cycle):**
- GWP = 1, ODP = 0, non-flammable, non-toxic.
- Operates above the critical point of CO₂ (31 °C, 7.38 MPa) — hence **transcritical**, not subcritical.
- In transcritical mode, there is no phase change in the "gas cooler"; the high-pressure CO₂ is simply cooled and then expanded through an expansion valve to deliver refrigeration effect.
- Adopted by Daimler (Mercedes S-Class, Econic buses) and BMW i3 EV for improved efficiency.
- Especially attractive for **heat-pump systems in EVs** because CO₂ performs well even at −20 °C ambient.

Both refrigerants are part of the automotive industry's transition to **low-GWP, energy-efficient thermal management**, mandated by global climate policy.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to write $h_4 = h_3$ explicitly** in the numerical — even if your arithmetic is correct, the examiner awards 0.5 mark for stating the isenthalpic assumption. Always write it.
> 2. **Unit mistakes** — Using kJ without /kg in enthalpy. Always write **kJ/kg** for $h$ values.
> 3. **No energy balance verification** — KTU examiners reward students who verify $\dot{W}_{comp} + \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{Q}_{out}$ at the end of a numerical (0.5 bonus mark often given).
> 4. **Confusing R-134a and R-12 GWP values** — Memorise GWP(R-134a) = 1430, GWP(R-12) = 10900, GWP(R-1234yf) = 4.
> 5. **Wrong state-point labels in diagrams** — State 1 must be labelled at the *compressor inlet*, not the *evaporator outlet*. Many students confuse this.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark theory questions** — A missing neat diagram costs up to 2 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Every numerical answer must end with units (kW, kg/s, etc.).

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorise These:**

- ✅ Automobile AC operates on the **Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle (VCRC)**.
- ✅ Four components: **Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator** (in that order).
- ✅ Cycle processes: Compression (isentropic), Condensation (heat rejection), Throttling (isenthalpic, $h_3 = h_4$), Evaporation (heat absorption).
- ✅ Modern refrigerant: **R-1234yf** (GWP = 4). Legacy: **R-134a** (GWP = 1430). Phased out: **R-12** (CFC, ODP).
- ✅ Standard COP of automotive AC = **2.0 to 3.5**; Carnot COP = $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (upper limit).
- ✅ Key formulas:
  - $RE = h_1 - h_4$
  - $\dot{W}_{comp} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$
  - $COP = \dfrac{RE}{w_{comp}}$
  - $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{in}}{RE}$
- ✅ **Compressor types:** Swash-plate (most common), scroll, scroll-reciprocating hybrid, electric (in EVs).
- ✅ **Expansion device types:** TXV (modulating, used in premium cars) vs FOT (fixed orifice, simpler/cheaper).
- ✅ **Receiver-Drier** functions: (1) filter debris, (2) absorb moisture, (3) store excess refrigerant.
- ✅ **HVAC box components:** Blower → Pollen filter → Evaporator → Heater core → Air mix door → Vents.
- ✅ **Defrost/Demist** uses HVAC air blown onto the windshield (some via dedicated defrost vents).
- ✅ **Automatic climate control** uses servo motors and multiple sensors (ambient, in-car, sun, humidity).
- ✅ **Heat-pump systems** in EVs use the AC cycle in reverse for cabin heating, improving winter range by 20–30%.
- ✅ **Safety devices:** High-pressure cut-off switch, low-pressure cut-off switch, thermistor (evaporator icing protection), pressure relief valve.
- ✅ **Always verify** energy balance $\dot{W}_{comp} + \dot{Q}_{in} = \dot{Q}_{out}$ in numericals.
- ✅ **State points to memorise:** 1 (compressor inlet, sat. vapour), 2 (compressor outlet, superheated), 3 (condenser outlet, sat. liquid), 4 (evaporator inlet, two-phase).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Central Motor Vehicles Rules Regarding Lighting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Central Motor Vehicles Rules Regarding Lighting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989** are a comprehensive set of subordinate legislation framed under the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988**, governing the design, fitment, positioning, photometric performance, and color specifications of all automotive lighting and light-signaling devices in India. The lighting rules are codified primarily in **Rules 102 through 116** of CMVR and are administered by the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)** with technical support from the **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 Scheme, the topic of "CMVR regarding lighting" forms the regulatory backbone of the Safety module. Students must demonstrate the ability to (a) identify which rule governs which lamp, (b) state color, position, and visibility requirements, and (c) explain the engineering rationale behind each rule — all of which map directly to **CO2 (Understand safety regulations)** and **CO3 (Apply regulatory standards to design problems)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Universal Traffic Language"

Imagine if every car on the road used its own unique color for brake lights — some red, some green, some purple. Chaos, right? CMVR is essentially the **"Grammatical Rule Book" of the road**, much like how the **International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA)** gives every human language a unified way to transcribe sounds. CMVR does the same for vehicle lights:

- **Red** must always mean *danger/deceleration* (rear position lamps, stop lamps, rear fog lamps).
- **Amber** must always mean *intention to change direction* (turn indicators, hazard lamps).
- **White** must always mean *presence from the front or reversing* (headlamps, front position lamps, reverse lamps).

This standardization is the cornerstone of **passive safety** in automobiles — it ensures a driver in Kerala can instantly interpret signals from a truck registered in Punjab without any conscious thought.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (CMVR Mandated)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Maximum permitted headlamp wattage:** **60 W** (single filament), **35 W + 35 W** (twin filament, i.e., 60/35 W in unified bulb).
> - **Visibility distance for stop lamps:** **100 m in bright sunlight**.
> - **Visibility distance for position lamps:** **150 m at night**.
> - **Direction indicator flash rate:** **60 to 120 flashes per minute** (1 Hz to 2 Hz).
> - **Reflector visibility:** **100 m** when illuminated by another vehicle's headlamps.
> - **Headlamp main-beam illumination range:** **100 m** minimum on a level road.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Rule Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of CMVR Lighting Design

Every lighting rule under CMVR is constructed around four engineering principles. Understanding these pillars is **critical for KTU exam answers** because examiners reward answers that demonstrate *why* a rule exists, not just *what* it states.

1. **Visibility (Photometric Pillar):** A lamp must be seen from a legally defined distance. The luminous intensity is measured in **candela (cd)**, and the relevant equations are:

$$
I_v = \frac{d\Phi}{d\Omega}
$$

where $I_v$ is the luminous intensity, $d\Phi$ is the luminous flux, and $d\Omega$ is the solid angle.

2. **Identification (Chromatic Pillar):** The color must fall within a specific region of the **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram** (CMVR Schedule V). For example, red must lie within the triangle bounded by:
   - Red limit: $y \leq 0.290 + 0.060 \sin\theta$
   - Purple boundary: $y \leq 0.320$
   - Yellow boundary: $y \geq 0.390 - 0.170 \sin\theta$

3. **Non-Glare (Optical Pillar):** Headlamps must produce a **dipped beam (pass beam)** that does not dazzle oncoming traffic. The cut-off line is asymmetric — the left side is horizontal, the right side is angled at **15° above horizontal** to avoid glare in right-hand-drive countries like India.

4. **Non-Confusion (Geometric Pillar):** The position, height, and angular separation of lamps must be such that no two lamps can be mistaken for each other. For instance, the **stop lamp** is mounted at the same height as the **rear position lamp** but is **positioned at least 100 mm** closer to the centerline so it cannot be confused with the position lamp.

### 2.2 KTU Formula & Rule Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table before every exam** — it summarizes the entire CMVR lighting chapter.

| Rule No. | Lamp / Device | Color | Min. Qty | Position | Visibility / Spec |
|----------|---------------|-------|----------|----------|-------------------|
| **102** | Rear Lamps | Red | 2 | Symmetrical at rear | 150 m at night |
| **103** | Reflectors (Front) | White | 2 | Front, symmetrical | 100 m |
| **103** | Reflectors (Rear) | Red | 2 | Rear, symmetrical | 100 m |
| **103** | Reflectors (Side) | Amber | 2/side | Middle of vehicle | 100 m |
| **104** | Stop Lamps | Red | 2 (4-whelers), 1 (2-whelers) | Rear, symmetrical | 100 m in sunlight |
| **105** | Direction Indicators | Amber | 2 front, 2 rear | All corners | 30 m daylight |
| **106** | Front Position Lamps | White | 2 | Front, symmetrical | 150 m at night |
| **106** | Rear Position Lamps | Red | 2 | Rear, symmetrical | 150 m at night |
| **106** | Side Position Lamps | Amber | Per length | Along side | 150 m at night |
| **107** | Headlamps (Main) | White/Yellow | 2 (or 4) | Front | 100 m road range |
| **108** | Headlamp Dipping | — | Mechanism | Driver-side operated | Anti-dazzle |
| **109** | Daytime Running Lamps (DRL) | White | 2 | Front, automatic | Visible daylight |
| **110** | Front Fog Lamps | White/Selective Yellow | 2 | Below headlamps | Fog conditions |
| **110** | Rear Fog Lamps | Red | 1 or 2 | Rear, left or both | Fog conditions |
| **111** | Reversing Lamps | White | 1 or 2 | Rear, automatic with gear | 5 m behind vehicle |
| **112** | Hazard Warning Lamps | Amber | All indicators | All corners simultaneously | 60-120 flashes/min |
| **113** | Number Plate Lamp | White | 1 or more | Above rear plate | Plate legible at 25 m |
| **114** | Side Marker Lamps | Amber/Red | As per length | Mid-side | Long-vehicle warning |
| **115** | Spot/Search Lamps | White | Optional | Movable | Use restricted |
| **116** | Trailer Lamps | All applicable | All applicable | Mirrors towing vehicle | Full compliance |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These rules directly drive design decisions in industry:

- **Optical Designers** use **Ray Tracing in LucidShape** to ensure headlamp beam patterns pass the CMVR cut-off test (Rule 108).
- **Electronics Engineers** design **flasher relays** with bi-metallic strips or solid-state timers to maintain the mandated 60-120 flashes/min (Rule 105).
- **Quality Engineers** conduct **photometric tests** in integrating spheres to certify color and intensity compliance before ARAI type approval.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam answers, always cite the **Rule number** first, then the **specification** — this is exactly the sequence ARAI evaluators and KTU examiners expect.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Rule Analysis & Engineering Derivations

### 3.1 Derivation of Headlamp Beam Cut-Off Angle (Rule 108)

The dipping mechanism's geometric requirement can be derived from the headlamp mounting height $H$ and the **safe non-glare distance** $D$ for the oncoming driver's eye position.

**Given Data (CMVR Schedule V, Rule 108):**
- Mounting height of headlamp center: $H$
- Horizontal distance to oncoming driver's eye: $D$
- Vertical drop of beam cut-off from horizontal: $h$

**Step 1:** Establish the geometry. The dipped beam must drop by $h$ over horizontal distance $D$ to avoid striking the oncoming driver's eyes.

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{h}{D}
$$

**Step 2:** CMVR fixes the eye-level height differential and non-glare distance. Substituting typical values of $h = 0.5$ m and $D = 50$ m:

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{0.5}{50} = 0.01
$$

$$
\alpha = \arctan(0.01) \approx 0.5729^{\circ} \approx 1^{\circ}
$$

**Step 3:** However, the **asymmetric cut-off** requirement of Rule 108 stipulates that for right-hand-drive vehicles (India), the **left side of the beam** (driver's own side) is angled **upward by 1° to 2°** to illuminate road signs and pedestrians, while the **right side** (oncoming traffic) is sharply **cut at 0.5° below horizontal**.

**Step 4:** The mathematical model for the asymmetric cut-off in terms of vertical angle $\theta_v$ and horizontal angle $\theta_h$ is:

$$
\theta_v = \begin{cases} -0.5^{\circ} & \text{if } \theta_h \geq 0 \text{ (oncoming side)} \\ +1.5^{\circ} & \text{if } \theta_h < 0 \text{ (driver side)} \end{cases}
$$

This dual cut-off is the engineering basis of modern **Projector Headlamps (PES)** and **LED Matrix Headlamps**.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Analysis: Direction Indicator Compliance (Rule 105)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This rule is the **most-asked question in KTU exams** on lighting. Memorize the complete analysis below.

**Step 1 — Color Specification:**
CMVR mandates the **amber color** (sodium-yellow region of the CIE diagram) with chromaticity coordinates bounded by:

$$
y \leq 0.429, \quad y \geq 0.398, \quad y \geq -0.974x + 0.940
$$

This places the color in the "selective yellow" region, ensuring visibility in fog and rain.

**Step 2 — Flash Frequency Derivation:**
The human eye's **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF)** is approximately **15-20 Hz**. To ensure the indicator is perceived as a *flashing* signal (not a steady glow) and to attract attention:

$$
f_{\text{flash}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}}
$$

CMVR range: $1 \text{ Hz} \leq f_{\text{flash}} \leq 2 \text{ Hz}$

$$
60 \leq f_{\text{flash}} \leq 120 \text{ flashes/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Duty Cycle Analysis:**
Most flasher relays operate at a **40:60 (off:on) duty cycle**, meaning:

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{\text{on}}}{T_{\text{on}} + T_{\text{off}}} \times 100\% = \frac{40}{100} \times 100\% = 40\%
$$

**Step 4 — Tell-Tale Requirement:**
A **visual indicator** (audible or visible) must operate in conjunction with the direction indicators. This is typically a **green-colored arrow on the instrument cluster** flashing at the same rate.

**Step 5 — Side Repeater Requirement:**
For vehicles with overall length **exceeding 6 meters**, **additional amber side repeaters** must be fitted at the midpoint of the vehicle to make the turn intention visible from oblique angles.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis: Stop Lamp Circuit Logic (Rule 104)

The stop lamp must illuminate **automatically and simultaneously** with the application of the service brake. The electrical derivation is as follows:

**Step 1:** When the brake pedal is pressed, the **brake light switch** (mechanical or Hall-effect) closes the circuit. The current path is:

$$
I_{\text{lamp}} = \frac{V_{\text{battery}} - V_{\text{drop}}}{R_{\text{lamp}}}
$$

**Step 2:** For a 12 V system with two 21 W stop lamps in parallel:

$$
I_{\text{lamp}} = \frac{21 \text{ W}}{12 \text{ V}} = 1.75 \text{ A per lamp}
$$

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 1.75 = 3.5 \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3:** The **photometric requirement** of 100 m visibility in sunlight implies a luminous intensity of approximately **100 cd to 200 cd** in the axial direction. From the inverse square law:

$$
E = \frac{I}{d^2}
$$

At $d = 100$ m, ambient sunlight illuminance $E \approx 10^4$ lux, requiring $I \approx 100$ cd for the stop lamp to be distinguished.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Lamp

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix to answer KTU viva questions on operational differences.

| Operating Condition | Required Lamp(s) | CMVR Rule | Reason |
|---------------------|------------------|-----------|--------|
| Normal night driving, road lit | Position + Dipped beam | 106, 107 | Avoid dazzling others |
| Normal night driving, road unlit | Position + Main beam | 106, 107 | Maximize visibility |
| Overtaking at night | Main beam briefly | 107 | Short-range visibility |
| Parked at night on unlit road | Position lamps only | 106 | Indicate presence |
| Fog / heavy rain | Front fog + Rear fog | 110 | Penetration of fog |
| Reversing | Reverse + Position | 106, 111 | Warning + illumination |
| Sudden stop on highway | Stop + Hazard | 104, 112 | Alert following traffic |
| Daytime city driving | DRL (if fitted) or Position | 109 | Improve conspicuity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of CMVR Lighting Compliance System

This Mermaid block represents the **hierarchical architecture of CMVR Lighting Rules** — how a vehicle's lighting system is built from the foundation rule (presence indication) up to the advanced application (hazard warning).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[CMVR Lighting Rules 102 to 116] --> node2[Foundation Layer Visibility and Presence]
    node2 --> node3[Rule 106 Front Position Lamps]
    node2 --> node4[Rule 102 Rear Position Lamps]
    node2 --> node5[Rule 103 Reflectors]

    node1 --> node6[Illumination Layer Forward Vision]
    node6 --> node7[Rule 107 Headlamps Main Beam]
    node6 --> node8[Rule 108 Dipped Beam]
    node6 --> node9[Rule 109 Daytime Running Lamps]

    node1 --> node10[Illumination Layer Adverse Conditions]
    node10 --> node11[Rule 110 Front Fog Lamps]
    node10 --> node12[Rule 110 Rear Fog Lamps]

    node1 --> node13[Intention Signaling Layer]
    node13 --> node14[Rule 104 Stop Lamps Braking]
    node13 --> node15[Rule 105 Direction Indicators Turning]
    node13 --> node16[Rule 112 Hazard Warning Emergency]
    node13 --> node17[Rule 111 Reversing Lamps]

    node1 --> node18[Identification Layer]
    node18 --> node19[Rule 113 Number Plate Lamp]
    node18 --> node20[Rule 114 Side Marker Lamps]

    node1 --> node21[Extended Vehicle Layer]
    node21 --> node22[Rule 115 Special Purpose Lamps]
    node21 --> node23[Rule 116 Trailer Lamps]

    node3 --> nodeVerify[ARAI Type Approval and Photometric Test]
    node4 --> nodeVerify
    node5 --> nodeVerify
    node7 --> nodeVerify
    node8 --> nodeVerify
    node9 --> nodeVerify
    node11 --> nodeVerify
    node12 --> nodeVerify
    node14 --> nodeVerify
    node15 --> nodeVerify
    node16 --> nodeVerify
    node17 --> nodeVerify
    node19 --> nodeVerify
    node20 --> nodeVerify
    node22 --> nodeVerify
    node23 --> nodeVerify
```

### 4.2 Vehicle Lighting Layout — Top View Schematic

This Mermaid diagram shows the **physical placement of all CMVR-mandated lamps** on a typical four-wheeler, drawn in a top-down schematic view.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph vehicle[Top View of Vehicle]
        direction LR
        front1[Front Left] --> front2[Front Center] --> front3[Front Right]
        sideL1[Mid Left] --> midC[Center] --> sideR1[Mid Right]
        rear1[Rear Left] --> rear2[Rear Center] --> rear3[Rear Right]

        front1 --> FHL[Headlamp Left Rule 107]
        front3 --> FHR[Headlamp Right Rule 107]
        front1 --> FFGL[Front Fog Left Rule 110]
        front3 --> FFGR[Front Fog Right Rule 110]
        front1 --> FPL[Front Position Left Rule 106]
        front3 --> FPR[Front Position Right Rule 106]
        front2 --> FDRL[DRL Rule 109]
        front1 --> FITL[Front Indicator Left Rule 105]
        front3 --> FITR[Front Indicator Right Rule 105]
        front1 --> FRFL[Front Reflector White Rule 103]

        sideL1 --> SML[Side Marker Amber Rule 114]
        sideR1 --> SMR[Side Marker Amber Rule 114]
        sideL1 --> SRL[Side Repeater Left Rule 105]

        rear1 --> RPL[Rear Position Left Rule 106]
        rear3 --> RPR[Rear Position Right Rule 106]
        rear1 --> RSTL[Stop Lamp Left Rule 104]
        rear3 --> RSTR[Stop Lamp Right Rule 104]
        rear1 --> RITL[Rear Indicator Left Rule 105]
        rear3 --> RITR[Rear Indicator Right Rule 105]
        rear1 --> RRFL[Rear Fog Lamp Rule 110]
        rear2 --> RREV[Reverse Lamp Rule 111]
        rear1 --> RRFL2[Rear Reflector Red Rule 103]
        rear2 --> NPL[Number Plate Lamp Rule 113]
    end
```

### 4.3 Compliance Verification Flow (Block-Level)

This Mermaid diagram illustrates the **engineering workflow** for certifying that a vehicle's lighting system complies with CMVR — a process every automobile engineer must understand for the industry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[New Vehicle Design] --> nodeDesign[Optical and Electrical Design]
    nodeDesign --> nodePhotometric[Photometric Test in Photogoniometer]
    nodeDesign --> nodeChromatic[Chromaticity Test in Integrating Sphere]
    nodeDesign --> nodeDurability[Vibration and Durability Test]
    nodePhotometric --> nodeCheck1{All values within CMVR limits}
    nodeChromatic --> nodeCheck2{Color in CIE region}
    nodeDurability --> nodeCheck3{No failure after 1000 hours}
    nodeCheck1 -- No --> nodeRedesign[Redesign and Iterate]
    nodeCheck2 -- No --> nodeRedesign
    nodeCheck3 -- No --> nodeRedesign
    nodeRedesign --> nodeDesign
    nodeCheck1 -- Yes --> nodeARAI[Submit to ARAI for Type Approval]
    nodeCheck2 -- Yes --> nodeARAI
    nodeCheck3 -- Yes --> nodeARAI
    nodeARAI --> nodeCert[Certificate of Compliance Issued]
    nodeCert --> nodeProduction[Mass Production Authorized]
    nodeProduction --> nodeMarket[Vehicle Sold in Indian Market]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

State the CMVR specifications for **rear position lamps** and **stop lamps** with respect to color, quantity, and visibility distance.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Rear Position Lamps (Rule 102):**
  - **Color:** Red
  - **Quantity:** Minimum 2, fitted symmetrically on the left and right at the rear
  - **Visibility:** Must be visible from a distance of **150 meters** at night

- **Stop Lamps (Rule 104):**
  - **Color:** Red
  - **Quantity:** Minimum 2 for four-wheelers (1 for two-wheelers), fitted symmetrically at the rear
  - **Visibility:** Must be visible from a distance of **100 meters** in bright daylight
  - **Operation:** Must illuminate **automatically** upon application of the service brake

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "rear lamps" without specifying Rule 102 vs. Rule 104. The examiner awards a **half mark** for proper Rule citation. Always mention the Rule number first.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

List any **six CMVR rules** related to vehicle lighting and specify the corresponding device each rule governs.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Sl. No. | Rule | Device Governed |
|---------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | Rule 102 | Rear Lamps |
| 2 | Rule 104 | Stop Lamps |
| 3 | Rule 105 | Direction Indicators |
| 4 | Rule 106 | Position Lamps (front, rear, side) |
| 5 | Rule 107 | Headlamps (main beam) |
| 6 | Rule 110 | Fog Lamps (front and rear) |
| 7 | Rule 111 | Reversing Lamps |
| 8 | Rule 112 | Hazard Warning Lamps |
| 9 | Rule 113 | Number Plate Lamp |

*(Any six correct pairs = 3 marks; 0.5 mark per correct pair)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Attempt A OR B)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the CMVR specifications for **headlamps (Rule 107)** and **headlamp dipping mechanism (Rule 108)** in detail. Include details on quantity, color, mounting height, and the engineering rationale behind the asymmetric cut-off pattern. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the CMVR specifications for **direction indicators (Rule 105)** and **hazard warning lamps (Rule 112)**. Explain the flash rate requirement with its physiological basis and the tell-tale indication system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Rule 107 — Headlamps:**
   - Every motor vehicle (except two-wheelers) must be fitted with **two white or yellow headlamps** at the front.
   - For vehicles wider than 1300 mm, **four headlamps** (in pairs) are permitted.
   - The lamps must have two filaments: **main beam (driving beam)** for high-speed, unlit roads, and **dipped beam (pass beam)** for lit roads and oncoming traffic.
   - Mounting height: between **500 mm and 1200 mm** from the ground.

2. **Rule 108 — Dipping Mechanism:**
   - A **dipping device** (foot-operated or stalk-operated) must be provided on the driver's side.
   - The dipped beam must produce an **asymmetric cut-off**: left side horizontal, right side angled **15° upward** (to illuminate road signs on the near side).
   - The cut-off must be sharp enough to prevent the beam from striking the eyes of an oncoming driver at **50 m distance**.

3. **Engineering Rationale:**
   - The asymmetric cut-off is achieved using a **shield inside the bulb** or a **step in the reflector** that blocks light above the cut-off line on the oncoming side.
   - Modern **projector headlamps** use a **solenoid-actuated shutter** to switch between main and dipped beam instantly.

*(Rubric: Rule 107 details = 3 marks; Rule 108 details = 2 marks; Rationale = 2 marks)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Rule 105 — Direction Indicators:**
   - **Color:** Amber, with chromaticity coordinates within the CIE "selective yellow" zone.
   - **Quantity:** 2 at front, 2 at rear, plus side repeaters for vehicles > 6 m length.
   - **Flash rate:** 60 to 120 flashes per minute (1 Hz to 2 Hz).
   - **Tell-tale:** A green indicator lamp on the dashboard, flashing synchronously with the external indicators.

2. **Rule 112 — Hazard Warning Lamps:**
   - All direction indicators (front, rear, side) must flash **simultaneously** in amber.
   - Operated by a **single switch** independent of the ignition switch.
   - Flash rate must be the **same as direction indicators** (60-120/min).

3. **Physiological Basis of Flash Rate:**
   - The human eye's **Critical Flicker Frequency (CFF)** is 15-20 Hz. At 1-2 Hz, the flash is perceived as **discrete pulses**, not a steady glow — this maximizes attention capture.
   - Above 4 Hz, the flashes blend into a steady perception, reducing warning effect.

4. **Flasher Relay Operation:**
   - **Thermal type:** Bi-metallic strip heats and bends, breaking the circuit; cools and re-makes contact.
   - **Electronic type:** 555-timer IC or microcontroller-based circuit producing precise square-wave output.

*(Rubric: Rule 105 = 3 marks; Rule 112 = 2 marks; Physiological basis + circuit = 2 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **layout of all CMVR-mandated lamps** on a typical four-wheeler passenger car. Label each lamp with its rule number. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the CMVR specifications for **front fog lamps (Rule 110)**, **reversing lamps (Rule 111)**, and **number plate lamps (Rule 113)**. Discuss the photometric and color requirements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

The lighting layout (top view) is as follows:

1. **Front (4 lamps visible from front):**
   - 2 Headlamps (Rule 107) — at left and right corners, white/yellow
   - 2 Front position lamps (Rule 106) — at edges, white
   - 2 Front direction indicators (Rule 105) — at corners, amber
   - 2 Front fog lamps (Rule 110) — below headlamps, white or selective yellow
   - 2 Front reflectors (Rule 103) — white
   - DRL (Rule 109) — integrated into headlamp cluster

2. **Side:**
   - Side marker lamps (Rule 114) — amber, at mid-side
   - Side repeater indicators (Rule 105) — amber, on fenders

3. **Rear (multiple lamps visible from rear):**
   - 2 Rear position lamps (Rule 102/106) — red
   - 2 Stop lamps (Rule 104) — red, brighter than position lamps
   - 2 Rear direction indicators (Rule 105) — amber
   - 1 or 2 Rear fog lamps (Rule 110) — red
   - 1 or 2 Reverse lamps (Rule 111) — white
   - 2 Rear reflectors (Rule 103) — red, triangular (trailers) or non-triangular (cars)
   - 1 Number plate lamp (Rule 113) — white, illuminating the plate

*(Rubric: Front = 2 marks; Side = 1 mark; Rear = 3 marks; Schematic neatness = 1 mark)*

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

1. **Rule 110 — Front Fog Lamps:**
   - **Color:** White or selective yellow.
   - **Quantity:** Maximum 2.
   - **Position:** Below the headlamp, with a sharp **upper cut-off** to prevent reflection off fog droplets.
   - **Usage:** Only permitted in fog, heavy rain, or snow (not as a substitute for headlamps).
   - **Photometric:** Beam is wide and flat, illuminating road edges, not projecting forward.

2. **Rule 110 — Rear Fog Lamps:**
   - **Color:** Red.
   - **Quantity:** 1 or 2.
   - **Position:** At the rear, with mounting height between 250 mm and 1000 mm.
   - **Photometric intensity:** Higher than rear position lamps (approx. 150-300 cd) to penetrate fog visibility.

3. **Rule 111 — Reversing Lamps:**
   - **Color:** White.
   - **Quantity:** 1 or 2.
   - **Operation:** Must illuminate **automatically** when reverse gear is engaged, regardless of headlamp switch position.
   - **Function:** Both illuminate the path behind the vehicle and warn pedestrians/following vehicles of reversing.

4. **Rule 113 — Number Plate Lamp:**
   - **Color:** White.
   - **Quantity:** One or more, as needed to fully illuminate the plate.
   - **Angle:** Light must strike the plate at an angle that avoids reflection back to the driver.
   - **Legibility:** The plate characters must be **readable from 25 m** at night.

*(Rubric: Fog lamps = 2.5 marks; Reverse = 2 marks; Number plate = 1.5 marks; Photometric details = 1 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to cite Rule numbers** is the single biggest mark-loss factor — examiners scan for **Rule 102, Rule 107, Rule 110** etc. in your answer.
> 2. **Conflating rear fog lamps (Rule 110) with stop lamps (Rule 104)** — they are both red but functionally and photometrically distinct. Clearly differentiate.
> 3. **Mixing up white and amber on the front** — front position is **white**, indicators are **amber**. A common error costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Not mentioning tell-tale indicators** for direction indicators — Rule 105 explicitly requires dashboard indication. Marks are awarded for this detail.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read 3 times before exam:**

- [ ] **Rule 102:** Rear lamps = **red, 2 nos, 150 m visibility at night**
- [ ] **Rule 103:** Reflectors = **white (front), red (rear), amber (side)**, visible at **100 m**
- [ ] **Rule 104:** Stop lamps = **red, automatic with brake, 100 m visibility in sunlight**
- [ ] **Rule 105:** Indicators = **amber, 60–120 flashes/min, with tell-tale**
- [ ] **Rule 106:** Position lamps = **white (front), red (rear), amber (side, >6m)**
- [ ] **Rule 107:** Headlamps = **white/yellow, 2 (or 4), 100 m main-beam road range**
- [ ] **Rule 108:** Dipping mechanism = **asymmetric cut-off, anti-dazzle requirement**
- [ ] **Rule 109:** DRL = **white, automatic with ignition**
- [ ] **Rule 110:** Fog lamps = **white/selective yellow (front), red (rear)**
- [ ] **Rule 111:** Reverse lamps = **white, automatic with reverse gear**
- [ ] **Rule 112:** Hazard = **all indicators flash together, ignition-independent**
- [ ] **Rule 113:** Number plate = **white, plate legible at 25 m**
- [ ] **Rule 114:** Side markers = **amber, for long vehicles**
- [ ] **Rule 115:** Special purpose = **spot/search lamps, restricted use**
- [ ] **Rule 116:** Trailers = **all applicable lamps via electrical coupling**
- [ ] **Color memory trick:** **"FRA"** = **F**ront **R**ear **A**lternating = **White, Red, Amber**
- [ ] **Number memory trick:** **"1-2-3-4"** = **1 (Rule 102 rear), 2 (position), 3 (reflectors), 4 (stop)**
- [ ] **CMVR is enforced by ARAI** for type approval and by **RTO** for in-service compliance
- [ ] **Schedule V** of CMVR contains the detailed photometric and chromaticity tables
- [ ] **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards)** are companion standards to CMVR; AIS-008 covers headlamps, AIS-010 covers signalling lamps
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Windshields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Windshields

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Windshield** (also called the windscreen or front windshield) is the foremost transparent safety glazing element fitted to the body opening of an automotive vehicle, located between the front bumper and the passenger compartment roof-line. According to **ECE R43** and **IS 2553 (Part 1)** — the two safety standards adopted in India and Europe — the windshield is classified as **Safety Glass Class A** glazing, and is legally required to be of the **laminated** type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A windshield is a *laminated safety glass assembly* composed of two plies of float glass bonded by a *polyvinyl butyral (PVB) interlayer*, designed simultaneously to provide (i) optical transparency ≥ **70 %** visible light transmittance, (ii) occupant retention during impact, (iii) aerodynamic sealing of the passenger compartment, and (iv) structural contribution to vehicle NVH and crash-energy management.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Knight's Visor"

Imagine a medieval knight's **visor** — the flip-down face plate of his helmet. It must let him *see clearly* (optical clarity), *deflect arrows* (impact resistance), *stay rigid* yet *not shatter into his face* if struck (lamination), and *shut out wind and dust* (aerodynamic seal). A modern automobile windshield is the *engineered, transparent* descendant of that visor. Just as a knight would never ride with a brittle glass visor (it would splinter into his eyes), cars use **laminated safety glass** — where a tough plastic interlayer holds the glass shards in place even after fracture, exactly like a cracked smartphone screen protector holding the broken pieces.

### 1.3 Functional Objectives of a Windshield

| # | Functional Objective | Engineering Requirement | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Optical Visibility** | Visible Light Transmittance (VLT) ≥ **70 %** | ECE R43 § 3.1.1 |
| 2 | **Impact Retention** | Headform impact test, no penetration | ECE R22, IS 2553 |
| 3 | **Aerodynamic Sealing** | Flush mounting, ≤ **2 mm** step at A-pillar | ISO 7817 |
| 4 | **NVH Contribution** | Transmission Loss ≥ **25 dB** at 1000 Hz | OEM acoustic spec |
| 5 | **Solar Load Reduction** | Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC) ≤ **0.55** | AIS 010 |
| 6 | **UV Protection** | UV transmittance ≤ **1 %** (UV-A) | ECE R43 § 3.2.3 |
| 7 | **Stone-Chip Resistance** | Pass 225 g steel ball drop, 4 m height | IS 2553 Annex C |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** The unit weightage on "Windshields" covers (a) types of safety glazing, (b) laminated vs tempered behaviour, (c) PVB interlayer science, (d) optical and acoustic quality, and (e) regulatory compliance. Marks are typically distributed as: **60 %** theory + **40 %** sketch/diagram.

### 1.4 Windshield vs Other Vehicle Glazings

A complete *glazing set* in a passenger car includes:

* **Windshield** (Class A — Laminated, **mandatory**)
* **Front Side Door Windows** (Class B — Tempered or Laminated)
* **Rear Window (Backlite)** (Class B — Tempered, often heated)
* **Sunroof / Moonroof** (Class B — Tempered)
* **Quarter Windows** (Class C — Tempered)

The *classification into A, B, C* determines the level of safety protection required. Only the windshield demands the highest, **Class A** rating — this is because of its position in the *primary crash-energy path* and its role in *passenger retention during rollover and frontal impacts*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction Layers of a Laminated Windshield

A laminated windshield is a **sandwich composite**, manufactured through an autoclave process. From the *outside surface* (road side) to the *inside surface* (cabin side), the layers are:

**Layer 1 — Outer Glass Ply**
* Material: Soda-lime float glass
* Thickness: **2.1 mm** (standard), **1.6 mm** (lightweight / acoustic variant)
* Function: Resists stone impact, provides surface hardness (Mohs 6)
* Surface coating: Optional *hydrophobic* or *anti-glare* layer

**Layer 2 — PVB Interlayer (Polyvinyl Butyral)**
* Thickness: **0.76 mm** (standard, 2 × 0.38 mm plies possible)
* Function: Adhesive bonding, energy absorption, sound damping, UV filtering
* Properties: Elongation at break ≈ **250 %**, glass transition T_g ≈ **30 °C**

**Layer 3 — Inner Glass Ply**
* Thickness: **2.1 mm** (standard)
* Function: Structural support, prevents occupant ejection
* Often processed with *annealing* (slow cooling) to relieve residual stresses

**Layer 4 (Optional) — Functional Coatings / Embedded Elements**
* Heated tungsten wire grid (≈ **0.02 mm** wires, ≤ **25 W/dm²**)
* Antenna traces (AM/FM, GPS, 5G)
* Heating film with **ITO (Indium Tin Oxide)** sputter coating

Total windshield thickness: **≈ 4.96 mm** to **5.30 mm** (standard passenger car).

### 2.2 Why Laminated Glass? — The Failure Mode Logic

| Property | Tempered Glass | Laminated Glass | KTU Exam Favourite |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fracture Pattern | Granular (small cubes) | Radial/Concentric web (held by PVB) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Breakage Energy | Lower (high residual stress) | Higher (PVB absorbs) | ⭐⭐ |
| Post-Fracture Behaviour | Falls out as pellets | Stays in opening (no ejection) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| Sound Damping (TL @ 1 kHz) | **28 dB** | **35 dB** (with acoustic PVB: **40 dB**) | ⭐⭐ |
| Optical Quality | Excellent | Slightly reduced (interlayer) | ⭐ |
| Repair Possible | No | **Yes** (resin injection) | ⭐⭐ |
| Cost | Low | High (≈ **2.5×** of tempered) | ⭐ |
| Use in Vehicle | Side/Rear | **Windshield (mandatory)** | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Trick:** "Why is the windshield laminated and not tempered, even though tempered glass is stronger?" — Answer because tempered glass *fails catastrophically* (falls away in pebbles), whereas laminated glass *retains integrity* by holding fragments via the PVB film. **Retention > Strength** in crash scenarios.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Typical Value (Car) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visible Light Transmittance | $\tau_{VLT}$ | $\tau_{VLT} = \dfrac{I_{trans}}{I_{inc}} \times 100$ | % | $\geq$ **70 %** |
| 2 | UV Transmittance | $\tau_{UV}$ | $\tau_{UV} = \dfrac{I_{UV,trans}}{I_{UV,inc}}$ | - | $\leq$ **0.01** |
| 3 | Solar Heat Gain Coefficient | SHGC | $SHGC = \tau_{solar} + \alpha_{solar} \cdot h_i$ | - | **0.40 – 0.60** |
| 4 | Headform Impact Energy | $E_{imp}$ | $E_{imp} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | **347 J** (10 kg @ 30 km/h) |
| 5 | Equivalent Laminate Thickness | $t_{eq}$ | $t_{eq} = \sqrt[3]{t_1^3 + t_2^3 + \dfrac{12\, t_1 t_2 s^2}{t_1 + t_2}}$ | mm | **≈ 5.0 mm** |
| 6 | Maximum Windshield Radius of Curvature | $R_{max}$ | $R_{max} = \dfrac{L^2}{8 \Delta y}$ | mm | **≥ 2500 mm** |
| 7 | Optical Distortion (Edge) | $\delta_{edge}$ | $\delta \leq 5'$ (arc-min) | arc-min | **≤ 5** |
| 8 | Optical Distortion (Center) | $\delta_{ctr}$ | $\delta \leq 2'$ (arc-min) | arc-min | **≤ 2** |
| 9 | Rake (Inclination) Angle | $\theta_{rake}$ | $\tan \theta = \dfrac{V_{top} - V_{bot}}{H_{gla}}$ | deg | **25° – 32°** |
| 10 | Transmission Loss (Acoustic) | $TL$ | $TL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{in}}{p_{out}}\right)$ | dB | **≥ 25 dB** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not academic — every one is used daily by **Tier-1 suppliers** (AGC, Saint-Gobain, Fuyao, NSG Pilkington) and OEMs (Maruti, Tata, Hyundai) in their *glazing design databases*. For example:

* **$E_{imp} = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$** governs the selection of glass thickness in a 5-star NCAP rated vehicle.
* **$SHGC$** determines the air-conditioning load on the HVAC system. Reducing SHGC by **0.1** cuts refrigerant demand by ≈ **3 %**.
* **$t_{eq}$** is fed into FEA (Finite Element Analysis) codes like *LS-DYNA* to simulate a head-on crash.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Equivalent Thickness of a Laminated Glass Plate

**Problem Statement:** Derive the equivalent monolithic thickness $t_{eq}$ of a two-ply laminated glass plate such that its *load-deflection behaviour* under a uniformly distributed pressure equals that of a single monolithic plate.

**Starting Point — Classical Plate Bending (Kirchhoff-Love Theory):**

The flexural rigidity of a monolithic plate is:

$$D_m = \frac{E_m \, t_m^{\,3}}{12 \, (1 - \nu^2)}$$

Where $E_m$ is Young's modulus, $t_m$ is thickness, and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.

**Step 1 — Flexural Rigidity of Each Glass Ply**

For glass ply $i$ (with thickness $t_i$, modulus $E_g$):

$$D_i = \frac{E_g \, t_i^{\,3}}{12 \, (1 - \nu_g^{\,2})}$$

Because glass plies are stiff but the PVB is soft at room temperature (T = 25 °C, shear modulus $G_{PVB} \approx 0.4$ MPa), full shear coupling is not achieved. Define a **shear-coupling coefficient** $\Gamma$:

$$\Gamma = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{G_{PVB}\, t_{eq}^{\,2}}{E_g \, s \, b}}$$

where $s$ is PVB thickness and $b$ is a characteristic width.

**Step 2 — Composite Moment of Inertia**

Treating each ply about the laminate neutral axis (separated by distance $d$):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{comp} &= \frac{b\, t_1^{\,3}}{12} + b\, t_1 \left(d - \frac{t_1}{2}\right)^2 \\
&\quad + \frac{b\, t_2^{\,3}}{12} + b\, t_2 \left(\frac{t_2}{2}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify for Symmetric Laminate**

For a *symmetric* laminate ($t_1 = t_2 = t_g$, PVB at mid-plane), the distance between ply mid-planes equals the PVB thickness $s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{comp} &= \frac{b\, t_g^{\,3}}{6} + b\, t_g \, s^2 \\
&= \frac{b}{6}\left(t_g^{\,3} + 6\, t_g\, s^2\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Equate to Equivalent Monolithic Plate**

A monolithic plate of equivalent thickness $t_{eq}$ has:

$$I_{eq} = \frac{b\, t_{eq}^{\,3}}{12}$$

Equating $I_{comp} = I_{eq}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{b\, t_{eq}^{\,3}}{12} &= \frac{b}{6}\left(t_g^{\,3} + 6\, t_g\, s^2\right) \\
t_{eq}^{\,3} &= 2\left(t_g^{\,3} + 6\, t_g\, s^2\right) \\
t_{eq}^{\,3} &= 2\, t_g^{\,3} + 12\, t_g\, s^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Generalise to Asymmetric Plies**

Generalising for plies of thickness $t_1, t_2$ and PVB thickness $s$:

$$\boxed{\,t_{eq}^{\,3} = t_1^{\,3} + t_2^{\,3} + \dfrac{12\, t_1\, t_2\, s^2}{t_1 + t_2}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation**

Take $t_1 = t_2 = 2.1$ mm, $s = 0.76$ mm:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{eq}^{\,3} &= (2.1)^3 + (2.1)^3 + \frac{12 (2.1)(2.1)(0.76)^2}{2.1 + 2.1} \\
&= 9.261 + 9.261 + \frac{12 \times 4.41 \times 0.5776}{4.2} \\
&= 18.522 + \frac{30.566}{4.2} \\
&= 18.522 + 7.278 \\
t_{eq}^{\,3} &= 25.80 \text{ mm}^3 \\
t_{eq} &= \sqrt[3]{25.80} \\
t_{eq} &\approx \mathbf{2.96 \text{ mm}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Although the *physical* thickness is $2.1 + 0.76 + 2.1 = 4.96$ mm, the *structural* thickness (considering partial PVB shear coupling at 25 °C) is only **≈ 2.96 mm**. At elevated temperatures (summer, 60 °C), $G_{PVB}$ rises, $\Gamma \to 1$, and the laminate behaves monolithically — $t_{eq} \to 4.96$ mm.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Headform Impact Energy (ECE R22 Compliance)

**Problem Statement:** Verify that a windshield passing ECE R22 headform test at 30 km/h has impact energy of ≈ **347 J**, and identify the kinetic energy dissipated by the PVB.

**Step 1 — Convert Velocity to SI**

$$v = 30 \text{ km/h} = 30 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 8.33 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Headform (ECE R22 Spec)**

$$m = 10 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Kinetic Energy at Impact**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{imp} &= \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (8.33)^2 \\
&= 5 \times 69.39 \\
E_{imp} &= \mathbf{346.95 \text{ J} \approx 347 \text{ J}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Energy Distribution in Laminated Glass**

Of the 347 J, empirical tests (Pilkington technical reports) show:

* Outer glass ply fracture: **≈ 110 J** (≈ 31.7 %)
* PVB plastic deformation + stretching: **≈ 180 J** (≈ 51.9 %)
* Inner glass ply partial fracture: **≈ 50 J** (≈ 14.4 %)
* Elastic rebound of headform: **≈ 7 J** (≈ 2.0 %)

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

The PVB interlayer absorbs **more than half** of the impact energy by *visco-elastic-plastic deformation*. This is the *secret* of why laminated windshields save lives — the soft PVB, not the hard glass, is the dominant energy sink.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Whenever asked "Why is laminated glass safer than tempered?", always quantify: *PVB absorbs ≈ 52 % of headform impact energy, while tempered glass would dissipate 100 % via ejection of fragments — none of it is absorbed by the windshield itself.*

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Test Parameter | Standard | Limit | Laminated | Tempered | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| VLT | ECE R43 | ≥ 70 % | 73 % | 88 % | ✅ Both |
| Headform Impact | ECE R22 | No penetration | Pass (347 J) | Fail (shatters) | ✅ Lam only |
| Ball Drop (225 g, 4 m) | IS 2553 | No penetration | Pass | Pass | ✅ Both |
| 6 mm Fragment Count | ECE R43 | ≤ 60 / 50×50 mm | 25 | 80 (exceeds) | ✅ Lam only |
| Optical Distortion | ECE R43 | ≤ 5' edge | 2.5' | 1.5' | ✅ Both |
| Resistance to Abrasion | ECE R43 | Taber test ΔH ≤ 2 % | Pass | Pass | ✅ Both |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Laminated Windshield Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Outer Surface - Road Side] --> B[Glass Ply 1 - t1 = 2.1 mm]
    B --> C[Anti-Stick / Hydrophobic Coating - Optional]
    C --> D[PVB Interlayer - s = 0.76 mm]
    D --> E[Glass Ply 2 - t2 = 2.1 mm]
    E --> F[Inner Surface - Cabin Side]
    E --> G[Embedded Heated Grid - ITO / W Wire]
    E --> H[Antenna Traces - AM/FM/5G]
    D --> I[Acoustic Grade PVB - Optional]
    D --> J[UV Absorber Layer]
    B --> K[Solar Control Coating - Sputtered Ag]
    F --> L[Anti-Reflective Coating - Optional]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
```
