topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fluid Flywheel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 3 — Fluid Flywheel: Section 1

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

A **Fluid Flywheel** (also known as a **Hydraulic Coupling** or **Fluid Coupling**) is a hydrodynamic power transmission device that uses a liquid medium — typically automatic transmission fluid (ATF) or a specialized mineral oil — to transmit rotational torque from a prime mover (engine) to a driven shaft (gearbox input) **without any mechanical contact** between the driving and driven members.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **PESFT636 – Automobile Engineering and Safety**, a fluid flywheel is classified as a *frictional fluid power transmission system* where energy transfer occurs through the **momentum exchange of a working fluid** under centrifugal action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PESFT636 / Module 3):**
> The fluid flywheel is studied under the broad heading of *Power Transmission Systems in Automobiles*. It is the foundational concept upon which the **Torque Converter** (a stator-equipped variant) is built. Students must clearly distinguish between the two before entering torque converter analysis.

### Formal Constructional Definition

The device consists of two main rotors housed in a sealed casing filled with working fluid:
1. **Impeller (Driving Member / Pump)** — bolted to the engine flywheel/crankshaft.
2. **Runner (Driven Member / Turbine)** — connected to the gearbox input shaft.

There is **NO stator**, **NO mechanical link**, and **NO electrical connection** between the impeller and the runner. The only link is the kinetic energy carried by the fluid.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Two-Fan Principle"

Imagine holding **two identical table fans facing each other**, separated by about a foot:

- You switch ON Fan A (the *impeller*) — it begins to spin and **pushes air outward**.
- The stream of air strikes the blades of Fan B (the *runner*) and begins to **rotate Fan B** without any physical shaft, belt, or gear connecting them.
- If you look closely, you will notice that Fan B always spins **slightly slower** than Fan A. This speed gap is called **SLIP**.
- If you apply load (resistance) on Fan B's shaft, you can feel the air pushing harder from Fan A — more energy is being transferred.

> **Replace air with transmission oil, fans with curved-bladed rotors enclosed in a sealed donut-shaped housing, and Fan A connected to an engine — you now have a perfect working mental model of a Fluid Flywheel.**

The crucial insight is this: **a fluid flywheel cannot multiply torque** (unlike a torque converter). Whatever torque the engine delivers to the impeller, the runner receives **the same magnitude** minus small fluid friction losses. The fluid flywheel simply provides a *smooth, slip-controlled, and shock-absorbing* link.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Impeller Speed (Input) | $N_i$ | Rotational speed of the driving member (RPM) |
| Runner Speed (Output) | $N_o$ | Rotational speed of the driven member (RPM) |
| **Slip** | $s$ | Fractional difference between input and output speeds |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta$ | Ratio of output power to input power |
| Torque Ratio | $TR$ | Output torque / Input torque |
| Working Fluid | — | Mineral oil / ATF (viscosity ≈ 6–10 cSt at 100 °C) |
| Filling Ratio | — | % of housing volume occupied by fluid (typically 70–85%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Constants Used in Fluid Flywheel Analysis:**
> - Density of transmission oil: **ρ ≈ 0.85 – 0.90 g/cm³** at 25 °C
> - Kinematic viscosity of ATF: **ν ≈ 6 – 10 cSt at 100 °C**
> - Typical operating slip at full load: **2 % – 5 %**
> - Stall slip (output at 0 RPM): approaches **100 %**

---

## 1.4 Primary Functions of a Fluid Flywheel

1. **Replaces the friction clutch** — smooth engagement without driver effort (semi-automatic).
2. **Dampens torsional vibrations** from the engine crankshaft.
3. **Permits the engine to idle** while the vehicle is stationary (no stalling).
4. **Provides overload protection** — slip automatically increases under shock loads.
5. **Enables soft start** — torque builds gradually as fluid momentum transfers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# MODULE 3 — Fluid Flywheel: Section 2

## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis of Operating Principle

The fluid flywheel operates on the **Euler momentum-exchange principle** for turbomachinery, but in a *locked-circuit (torus)* configuration where the same fluid circulates continuously between the impeller and the runner.

### Step-by-Step Working Logic

**Step 1 — Engine Cranking:**
When the engine starts, the **impeller** (driving member) begins to rotate at engine speed $N_i$. The impeller contains curved blades forming a closed torus filled with transmission oil.

**Step 2 — Centrifugal Acceleration of Fluid:**
The rotating impeller imparts kinetic energy to the oil. By Newton's first law in the rotating frame, the oil experiences an **outward centrifugal force**:

$$
F_c \;=\; m \cdot \omega_i^2 \cdot r
$$

where $m$ is the mass of fluid, $\omega_i$ is the angular velocity of the impeller, and $r$ is the mean radius of the torus. The fluid velocity in the tangential direction reaches values of **15–30 m/s** in a typical automobile fluid flywheel.

**Step 3 — Tangential Discharge into Runner:**
The curved shape of the impeller blades redirects the fluid in a **tangential direction at high velocity**. The high-velocity oil jet strikes the curved blades of the **runner**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" Behind Curved Blades:** The blade curvature is designed so that the **absolute velocity** of the fluid entering the runner is tangential — this maximizes the **angular momentum transfer**, which is the heart of torque transmission.

**Step 4 — Momentum Transfer to Runner:**
By Newton's Second Law for rotating systems (angular impulse = change in angular momentum), the oil's tangential momentum is transferred to the runner blades, producing a **torque $T$ on the runner shaft**:

$$
T \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot (v_{t1} \cdot r_1 \;-\; v_{t2} \cdot r_2)
$$

**Step 5 — Runner Rotation and Energy Return:**
The runner rotates at speed $N_o$, slightly less than $N_i$. The oil, having lost some angular momentum, returns to the impeller's central region (the inner eye) at a lower tangential velocity, ready to be re-accelerated.

**Step 6 — Continuous Circulation (Closed Loop):**
A continuous **vortex-like circulation** of fluid is established between impeller and runner. The cycle repeats at high frequency (fluid may circulate 50–100 times per second).

**Step 7 — Slip Equilibrium:**
Under steady load, slip stabilizes at a value that balances the **input torque = resisting torque on output shaft**. If load increases momentarily, slip increases (more energy dissipated as fluid friction/heat), preventing shock transmission.

> [!WARNING]
> **Heat Generation Pitfall:** All the power lost to slip is converted to **heat in the oil**. This is why fluid flywheel housings must have **adequate cooling fins** or an external oil cooler. Prolonged slip (e.g., holding a vehicle on a hill with fluid coupling) can **boil the oil** and destroy seals.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Engineering Choice

| Design Choice | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| Curved (not straight) blades | Optimizes tangential momentum transfer |
| Donut/torus shape | Minimizes fluid friction, maximizes centrifugal effect |
| Partial fill (70–85% oil) | Allows fluid to "slip" back during overload without complete loss of transmission |
| Use of ATF, not engine oil | Designed for high shear stability and consistent viscosity |
| Sealed housing | Prevents contamination; contains vapor pressure |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s = \dfrac{N_i - N_o}{N_i}$ | Fractional slip | dimensionless | $0 \le s \le 1$ |
| 2 | $s_\% = \dfrac{N_i - N_o}{N_i} \times 100$ | Percentage slip | % | $0 \le s_\% \le 100$ |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{N_o}{N_i} = 1 - s$ | Efficiency (ratio of speeds) | dimensionless | $\eta \le 1$ (always) |
| 4 | $T_i \cdot \omega_i = T_o \cdot \omega_o + P_{loss}$ | Energy balance | W | $P_{loss} = T_i(\omega_i - \omega_o)$ |
| 5 | $T_i = T_o$ (ideal) | Torque equality | N·m | Valid at all speeds in fluid flywheel |
| 6 | $P_i = \dfrac{2\pi N_i T_i}{60}$ | Input power | W | $N_i$ in RPM |
| 7 | $P_o = \dfrac{2\pi N_o T_o}{60}$ | Output power | W | $N_o$ in RPM |
| 8 | $F_c = m \omega^2 r$ | Centrifugal force on fluid | N | Valid at mean torus radius |
| 9 | $T = \dot{m}(v_1 r_1 - v_2 r_2)$ | Euler's turbomachine equation | N·m | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate |
| 10 | $\dot{Q}_{heat} = T \cdot \omega_i \cdot s$ | Heat generated due to slip | W | Used for cooler sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight — Formula #5 ($T_i = T_o$):** This is the single most-tested statement on fluid flywheels. Unlike a torque converter (which has a stator and can multiply torque up to 2–3×), a fluid flywheel has **NO stator**, so **torque ratio = 1 (unity)**. Always state this clearly in the exam.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fluid flywheels are deployed where **smooth, clutchless engagement** is required without torque multiplication. Major applications include:

- **Heavy commercial buses and trucks** — historically used by FIAT, AEC, and Leyland in mid-20th-century buses.
- **Diesel locomotives** — as a transmission element between diesel engine and gearbox (e.g., in some British Rail units).
- **Industrial drives** — crushers, conveyors, and large pumps where motor starting under load is undesirable.
- **Marine propulsion** — coupling high-speed diesel engines to propellers.
- **Modern automobiles** — almost entirely replaced by torque converters in automatics, but still found in **Continuously Variable Transmissions (CVTs)** as the launching element.
- **Wind turbines** — limited use in pitch-control systems.

---

## 2.5 Comparison Snapshot — Fluid Flywheel vs. Torque Converter

| Feature | Fluid Flywheel | Torque Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Stator | Absent | Present (with one-way clutch) |
| Torque multiplication | No (TR = 1) | Yes (TR up to 2.5 – 3.0 at stall) |
| Efficiency at stall | 0 % | 0 % |
| Max efficiency | ~95–97 % | ~85–90 % |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Cost | Low | High |

# MODULE 3 — Fluid Flywheel: Section 3

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Slip and Efficiency Relationships

This section presents a complete algebraic derivation of every governing equation used in KTU board examinations for fluid flywheel analysis.

---

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Slip ($s$)

**Statement to derive:** Slip is defined as the ratio of the *difference* between input and output speeds to the input speed.

**Step 1 — Define input and output speeds.**
Let:
- $N_i$ = rotational speed of the impeller (driving member) in **RPM**
- $N_o$ = rotational speed of the runner (driven member) in **RPM**

**Step 2 — Express the speed difference.**
The relative loss in speed between input and output is:

$$
\Delta N \;=\; N_i \;-\; N_o
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize with respect to input speed.**
To express slip as a *dimensionless fraction*, divide the speed loss by the input speed:

$$
s \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta N}{N_i} \;=\; \dfrac{N_i - N_o}{N_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to percentage form.**
Multiplying both sides by 100:

$$
s_\% \;=\; \left(\dfrac{N_i - N_o}{N_i}\right) \times 100
$$

**Boundary Conditions Derived:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{At stall: } N_o = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s = \dfrac{N_i - 0}{N_i} = 1 \;\;(100\,\%) \\
&\text{At lock-up (ideal): } N_o = N_i \;\Rightarrow\; s = \dfrac{0}{N_i} = 0 \;\;(0\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves that slip is bounded as $0 \le s \le 1$.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Efficiency ($\eta$)

**Statement to derive:** The efficiency of a fluid flywheel equals the ratio of output power to input power, and is *numerically equal* to $(1 - s)$ when $T_i = T_o$.

**Step 1 — Write power equations.**
For rotating shafts:

$$
P_i \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N_i T_i}{60} \quad,\quad P_o \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N_o T_o}{60}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the efficiency definition.**

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{P_o}{P_i} \;=\; \dfrac{(2\pi N_o T_o/60)}{(2\pi N_i T_i/60)} \;=\; \dfrac{N_o \cdot T_o}{N_i \cdot T_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the fluid flywheel identity $T_i = T_o$.**
Because there is no stator and no torque multiplication in a fluid flywheel, the input torque and output torque are equal in magnitude (small friction losses ignored in idealized derivation):

$$
\eta \;=\; \dfrac{N_o \cdot T_i}{N_i \cdot T_i} \;=\; \dfrac{N_o}{N_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the slip expression.**
From the previous derivation, $s = (N_i - N_o)/N_i$. Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \dfrac{N_i}{N_i} - \dfrac{N_o}{N_i} \\
s &= 1 - \dfrac{N_o}{N_i} \\
\dfrac{N_o}{N_i} &= 1 - s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine results.**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta \;=\; \dfrac{N_o}{N_i} \;=\; 1 - s\;}
$$

This is a **uniquely clean identity** — and KTU examiners love to ask it.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Heat Generated Due to Slip

**Statement to derive:** All mechanical energy lost to slip is converted to heat in the fluid.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the coupling.**

$$
P_{input} \;=\; P_{output} \;+\; P_{loss}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute power formulas.**

$$
T_i \, \omega_i \;=\; T_o \, \omega_o \;+\; P_{loss}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply $T_i = T_o$ (fluid flywheel identity).**

$$
T_i \, \omega_i \;=\; T_i \, \omega_o \;+\; P_{loss}
$$

**Step 4 — Factor $T_i$.**

$$
P_{loss} \;=\; T_i (\omega_i - \omega_o)
$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of slip.**
Dividing both sides by $\omega_i$:

$$
\dfrac{P_{loss}}{\omega_i} \;=\; T_i \left(1 - \dfrac{\omega_o}{\omega_i}\right) \;=\; T_i \cdot s
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;P_{loss} \;=\; T_i \cdot \omega_i \cdot s \;=\; s \cdot P_{input}\;}
$$

This equation is used to **size the oil cooler** for a fluid flywheel. At a slip of 5 % and input torque of 400 N·m at 2000 RPM:

$$
P_{loss} \;=\; 0.05 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 2000 \times 400}{60} \;=\; 4189 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 4.2 \,\text{kW}
$$

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation of Euler Turbomachine Equation for Torque

**Statement to derive:** Torque transmitted equals the rate of change of angular momentum of the fluid.

**Step 1 — Angular momentum of a fluid element.**
For a fluid element of mass $m$ moving at tangential velocity $v_t$ at radius $r$:

$$
L_{ang} \;=\; m \cdot v_t \cdot r
$$

**Step 2 — Rate of change (mass flow rate $\dot{m}$):**

$$
\dfrac{dL_{ang}}{dt} \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \Delta(v_t \cdot r)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's turbomachine equation.**
For fluid entering the runner at radius $r_1$ with tangential velocity $v_{t1}$ and leaving at $r_2$ with $v_{t2}$:

$$
\boxed{\;T \;=\; \dot{m} \left( v_{t1} \, r_1 - v_{t2} \, r_2 \right)\;}
$$

This is **Euler's Pump/Turbine Equation** — and is the **mathematical heart of all hydrodynamic transmissions**.

---

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A fluid flywheel is supplied with **200 N·m** of input torque from a diesel engine running at **1800 RPM**. The output shaft rotates at **1728 RPM** under steady load. Find:
(a) Slip in percentage
(b) Efficiency
(c) Power lost as heat
(d) Output power

**Solution:**

**(a) Slip:**

$$
s_\% \;=\; \dfrac{N_i - N_o}{N_i} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{1800 - 1728}{1800} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{72}{1800} \times 100 \;=\; \boxed{4\,\%}
$$

**(b) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta \;=\; 1 - s \;=\; 1 - 0.04 \;=\; \boxed{0.96 \;\text{or}\; 96\,\%}
$$

**(c) Power lost as heat:**

$$
P_{loss} \;=\; s \times P_{input} \;=\; 0.04 \times \dfrac{2\pi \times 1800 \times 200}{60}
$$

$$
P_{loss} \;=\; 0.04 \times 37699 \,\text{W} \;=\; \boxed{1508 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 1.5 \,\text{kW}}
$$

**(d) Output power:**

$$
P_{out} \;=\; \eta \times P_{in} \;=\; 0.96 \times 37699 \;=\; \boxed{36191 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 36.2 \,\text{kW}}
$$

**Verification (energy balance):**

$$
P_{in} \;=\; P_{out} + P_{loss} \;\Rightarrow\; 37699 \;\stackrel{?}{=}\; 36191 + 1508 \;=\; 37699 \;\checkmark
$$

---

# MODULE 3 — Fluid Flywheel: Section 4

## 4.1 Structural Schematic of a Fluid Flywheel

The following Mermaid block represents the **internal functional architecture** of a fluid flywheel. It maps the energy and fluid flow paths between the engine, the impeller-runner pair, the working fluid, and the gearbox input shaft.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENG[Engine Crankshaft]
    HSG[Sealed Toroidal Housing]
    IMP[Impeller / Driving Member / Pump]
    RUN[Runner / Driven Member / Turbine]
    OIL[ATF Working Fluid]
    GBX[Gearbox Input Shaft]
    FIN[Cooling Fins on Housing]
    HX[External Oil Cooler optional]

    ENG -- bolted to --> IMP
    IMP -- rotates at Ni RPM --> HSG
    HSG -- encloses --> RUN
    RUN -- drives --> GBX
    IMP -- "centrifugal acceleration" --> OIL
    OIL -- "tangential momentum jet" --> RUN
    RUN -- "return flow at lower vt" --> OIL
    HSG -- "slip heat dissipated" --> FIN
    FIN -- "convection + radiation" --> AIR[Ambient Air]
    FIN -- "if equipped" --> HX
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **Engine Crankshaft** is rigidly bolted to the **Impeller** (Pump) — there is *no clutch plate* between them.
- The **Impeller and Runner** are separated by a small axial gap (typically 5–15 mm) and *never touch*.
- The **ATF (Working Fluid)** circulates in a closed torus, accelerated by the impeller and decelerated by the runner.
- **Slip heat** is dumped into the **Cooling Fins** on the housing outer surface.
- The **Runner** is splined to the **Gearbox Input Shaft**.

---

## 4.2 Fluid Circulation Path (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1 Acceleration]
        A1[Engine rotates impeller]
        A2[Oil gains kinetic energy KE = 1/2 m v squared]
    end

    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2 Transfer]
        B1[High vt oil jet strikes runner blades]
        B2[Angular momentum transferred]
        B3[Runner torque produced T = m dot vt r]
    end

    subgraph Stage3[Stage 3 Return]
        C1[De-energized oil returns to impeller eye]
        C2[Cycle repeats at 50 to 100 Hz]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1 --> C2 --> A1
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Summary

| Component | Role | Material (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Impeller | Pump — accelerates fluid | Cast aluminum alloy |
| Runner | Turbine — extracts energy | Cast aluminum alloy |
| Housing | Sealed casing with fins | Steel or aluminum |
| Working Fluid | Momentum carrier | ATF (Dexron III / Mercon V) |
| Seals | Prevent leakage at shaft | Nitrile rubber / Viton |
| Bearing (front) | Supports impeller on crankshaft | Roller / ball bearing |
| Bearing (rear) | Supports runner in housing | Tapered roller bearing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Schematic Point:** In KTU board exam answers, always draw the **impeller-runner cross-section** clearly showing the curved blades, the working fluid filling the torus, and label $N_i$ and $N_o$ on respective shafts. Marks are awarded specifically for *labelled diagrams*.
