# MODULE 3 — Fluid Flywheel: Section 5

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

This section follows the KTU 2024 ESE pattern: **Part A (3 marks)**, **Part B (14 marks)** with **internal choice**.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. Define a fluid flywheel. State any two of its advantages over a friction clutch.**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Answer]**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A fluid flywheel is a hydrodynamic power transmission device that uses a liquid medium (transmission oil) to transmit torque from the engine crankshaft to the gearbox input shaft without any mechanical contact between the driving and driven members.

**Two advantages over friction clutch (1 mark):**

1. **No wear and tear** — there is no friction plate to wear out, since energy is transferred purely through fluid momentum.
2. **Smooth, gradual engagement** — eliminates jerky starts and dampens torsional vibrations from the engine.
3. (Bonus) Overload protection is automatic — slip increases as load increases.

---

**Q2. Why is there always some slip in a fluid flywheel, even at full load?**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Answer]**

**Explanation (3 marks):**
Slip is an inherent and necessary feature of the fluid flywheel because:

1. **No mechanical link exists** between the impeller and the runner — torque transfer is purely through fluid momentum, which requires a velocity difference.
2. The fluid must **return to the impeller** to be re-accelerated. If $N_o = N_i$ (zero slip), there would be no relative motion, no fluid circulation, and **no torque transmission**.
3. Practically, slip is **2–5 %** at full load — it cannot be eliminated, only minimized by better blade design.

**Mathematically:**

$$
s \;=\; \dfrac{N_i - N_o}{N_i} \;>\; 0 \;\text{always.}
$$

If $s = 0$, then $\eta = 1$ but torque transmission $= 0$ (no fluid flow, no power transferred).

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**Q.A. (a)** With the help of a neat labelled sketch, explain the construction and working of a fluid flywheel. Discuss how it transmits torque from the engine to the gearbox. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**

The fluid flywheel consists of two main members housed in a sealed, oil-filled casing:

1. **Impeller (Pump)** — bolted to the engine flywheel/crankshaft. Has curved radial blades forming a closed half-torus facing the runner.
2. **Runner (Turbine)** — splined to the gearbox input shaft. Has a similar half-torus of curved blades facing the impeller.
3. **Housing** — a sealed casing that encloses both members; outer surface has cooling fins.
4. **Working fluid** — ATF filling about 70–85 % of the torus volume (partial fill is essential for slip control).
5. **Bearings and seals** — for shaft support and leak prevention.

**Working (4 marks):**

- Engine rotates the **impeller** at $N_i$.
- The rotating impeller **accelerates the oil centrifugally** to high tangential velocity.
- The oil, guided by the impeller's curved blades, emerges as a **high-velocity jet** in the tangential direction.
- This jet strikes the **runner blades**, transferring angular momentum and producing a torque on the runner.
- The runner rotates at $N_o < N_i$, driving the **gearbox input shaft**.
- The oil, now partially de-energized, returns to the impeller's inner eye and is re-accelerated — establishing a continuous circulation.
- Slip is always present and converts a small fraction of input power into heat, dissipated by the cooling fins.

**Sketch expected:** Cross-section of impeller + runner in a closed torus with arrows showing fluid circulation.

---

**Q.A. (b)** A fluid flywheel transmits torque from a diesel engine running at **1500 RPM** to a gearbox input shaft running at **1440 RPM** under load. The input torque is **250 N·m**.
Calculate: **(i)** percentage slip, **(ii)** efficiency, **(iii)** power lost as heat, **(iv)** output power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$N_i = 1500$ RPM, $N_o = 1440$ RPM, $T_i = 250$ N·m.

**Step 1 — Percentage slip:**

$$
s_\% \;=\; \dfrac{N_i - N_o}{N_i} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{1500 - 1440}{1500} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{60}{1500} \times 100 \;=\; \boxed{4\,\%}
$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta \;=\; 1 - s \;=\; 1 - 0.04 \;=\; \boxed{0.96 \;\text{or}\; 96\,\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Input power:**

$$
P_i \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N_i T_i}{60} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500 \times 250}{60} \;=\; 39270 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 39.27 \,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Power lost as heat:**

$$
P_{loss} \;=\; s \times P_i \;=\; 0.04 \times 39270 \;=\; \boxed{1571 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 1.57 \,\text{kW}}
$$

**Step 5 — Output power:**

$$
P_o \;=\; \eta \times P_i \;=\; 0.96 \times 39270 \;=\; \boxed{37699 \,\text{W} \;\approx\; 37.70 \,\text{kW}}
$$

**Verification:** $P_i = P_o + P_{loss} = 37699 + 1571 = 39270$ W $\checkmark$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**Q.B. (a)** Compare a fluid flywheel with a friction clutch and a torque converter on the basis of: (i) construction, (ii) torque multiplication, (iii) efficiency, (iv) applications, (v) cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Friction Clutch | Fluid Flywheel | Torque Converter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | Friction plate + pressure plate, mechanical linkage | Impeller + runner in oil-filled housing | Impeller + runner + **stator** with one-way clutch |
| **Torque multiplication** | None (TR = 1) | None (TR = 1) | **Yes (TR up to 2.5–3)** |
| **Efficiency** | High (~98 %) at engaged state | ~95–97 % | ~85–90 % (peak), 0 % at stall |
| **Driver effort needed** | Yes (pedal) | No (automatic) | No (automatic) |
| **Wear and tear** | Yes (friction plate wears) | Minimal | Minimal |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Applications** | Manual transmission cars | Heavy buses, marine, industrial drives | Automatic transmission cars |

**Key distinguishing statements (must be in answer):**
- A friction clutch is a *mechanical* device; a fluid flywheel is a *hydrodynamic* device without torque multiplication; a torque converter is a fluid flywheel **plus a stator** that allows torque multiplication.
- Fluid flywheel and friction clutch both have **unity torque ratio**; torque converter has **TR > 1**.

---

**Q.B. (b)** Explain why the entire slip-loss energy in a fluid flywheel is converted to heat. How is this heat dissipated in a practical installation? What will happen if this heat is not dissipated adequately? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Why slip becomes heat (3 marks):**

The fluid flywheel obeys the energy balance:

$$
P_i \;=\; P_o \;+\; P_{loss}
$$

Since $T_i = T_o$ (no torque multiplication):

$$
P_{loss} \;=\; T_i(\omega_i - \omega_o) \;=\; s \cdot P_i
$$

The mechanical energy associated with the *relative velocity* between impeller and runner is **not** extracted as useful output. Instead, viscous shear within the circulating oil converts it directly to **thermal energy** (heat). This is analogous to the heat generated in any slipping friction clutch.

**How the heat is dissipated (2 marks):**

1. **Conduction** through the housing wall.
2. **Convection and radiation** from external **cooling fins** on the housing surface.
3. **Forced oil circulation** through an external oil cooler (in heavy-duty applications like buses).
4. In some designs, a small quantity of oil is sprayed against the housing interior.

**What happens if heat is not dissipated (2 marks):**

1. Oil **temperature rises rapidly** beyond its design limit (typically 120 °C for ATF).
2. Oil **viscosity drops**, reducing torque transmission capacity.
3. Seals **fail** due to thermal degradation.
4. Eventually, oil may **boil or vaporize**, creating vapor pockets that collapse (cavitation), causing **erosion of impeller and runner blades**.
5. In extreme cases, the housing may crack or bearings may seize.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Mistake 1 (loses 1–2 marks):** Writing *"efficiency = $N_o/N_i$"* without the derivation or without stating $T_i = T_o$ — **always show both**.
> - **Mistake 2 (loses 2–3 marks):** Confusing fluid flywheel with torque converter in long-answer questions. If the question asks about fluid flywheel, **NEVER mention torque multiplication**; if asked about torque converter, **always mention the stator**.
> - **Mistake 3 (loses 1 mark):** Forgetting to **label the diagram** with $N_i$, $N_o$, impeller, runner, working fluid, and direction of fluid circulation.
> - **Mistake 4 (loses 1–2 marks in numericals):** Using $T_i \neq T_o$ for a fluid flywheel in the efficiency formula. **Always assume $T_i = T_o$** unless the question explicitly states otherwise.
> - **Mistake 5 (loses 1 mark):** Stating slip as just "$N_i - N_o$" without dividing by $N_i$ — slip is *dimensionless*, not in RPM.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Fluid flywheel = hydrodynamic coupling** with **no mechanical contact** between input and output.
- **Two members only:** Impeller (driving) + Runner (driven). **No stator.**
- **Torque ratio = 1** (no torque multiplication) — this is the **single most important fact**.
- **Slip is unavoidable:** $s = (N_i - N_o)/N_i$.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = N_o/N_i = 1 - s$.
- **Slip → Heat:** All slip-loss becomes heat in the oil — cooling is essential.
- **Working fluid:** ATF, partial fill 70–85 % of torus volume.
- **Working principle:** Centrifugal acceleration of oil → tangential momentum jet → torque on runner.
- **Key equation:** $T = \dot{m}(v_{t1} r_1 - v_{t2} r_2)$ (Euler's turbomachine equation).
- **Typical slip at full load:** 2–5 %. **At stall:** slip = 100 %.
- **Difference from torque converter:** Fluid flywheel has **no stator**; torque converter has a **stator with one-way clutch** allowing torque multiplication up to 2.5–3×.
- **Difference from friction clutch:** Fluid flywheel is hydrodynamic (no wear); friction clutch is mechanical (plates wear).
- **Applications:** Buses, marine drives, industrial conveyors, locomotives, CVT launch elements.
- **Energy balance:** $P_i = P_o + P_{loss}$, where $P_{loss} = s \cdot P_i$.
- **Power formulas:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$, with $N$ in RPM and $T$ in N·m.
- **Remember:** The impeller is bolted to the engine; the runner is splined to the gearbox input shaft.

---

**End of Module 3 — Fluid Flywheel Notes (PESFT636, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gear Box Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gear Box Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Gear Box** (Transmission Gearbox) is a mechanical device, consisting of a set of gears and associated shafts, that transmits the engine's rotational power to the driving wheels while simultaneously **modifying the torque–speed characteristics** to suit varying road and load conditions. The gearbox is the central torque/speed conversion element of the automobile powertrain, located between the engine (or clutch/torque converter) and the propeller shaft (or final drive in transverse layouts).

Mathematically, the gearbox performs a controlled speed reduction (or in some cases, overdrive speed multiplication) governed by the **Gear Ratio** ($i_g$):

$$i_g = \frac{N_{\text{driver}}}{N_{\text{driven}}} = \frac{T_{\text{driven}}}{T_{\text{driver}}} = \frac{Z_{\text{driven}}}{Z_{\text{driver}}}$$

where $N$ = rotational speed, $T$ = torque, and $Z$ = number of teeth on the gear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** The classification of gearboxes into **manual (sliding mesh, constant mesh, synchromesh)** and **automatic (epicyclic, CVT, DCT, AMT)** categories is a frequently tested KTU exam concept. Pay special attention to the operational logic of each type.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **bicycle** with multiple sprockets (chainrings) at the pedals and at the rear wheel. When you shift to a smaller sprocket at the pedals and a larger one at the rear, your legs pedal many times for every single rotation of the wheel — you gain **speed but lose torque** (good for downhill). Conversely, shifting to a large chainring and small rear sprocket makes pedaling harder but the wheel turns with much more force (good for climbing).

An automobile gearbox is essentially the same idea, but executed with **precision-cut steel gears** and synchronizers instead of a chain. The engine has a narrow useful RPM band (typically **1500–4500 rpm** for petrol, **1200–3500 rpm** for diesel), but the wheels need to rotate anywhere from **0 rpm (stopped)** to over **1500 rpm (highway cruising)**. The gearbox is the "translator" that matches these two incompatible ranges.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A car engine produces maximum power at a specific RPM (e.g., 5500 rpm) and maximum torque at a lower RPM (e.g., 2500 rpm). Without a gearbox, the car would either stall at low speeds or rev too high at high speeds. The gearbox keeps the engine within its **power band** at all vehicle speeds.

### Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Gear Ratio ($i_g$):** Typically between **2.5 : 1** (1st gear) and **0.8 : 1** (overdrive, 5th/6th gear).
- **Number of Forward Speeds:** Modern manual cars: **5 or 6**; Automatic: **6, 7, 8, or 10 (CVT – infinite ratio)**.
- **Reverse Gear Ratio:** Between **3.0 : 1 and 4.5 : 1** (often with an idler gear).
- **Efficiency of Gearbox:** Sliding mesh ~ **85–90%**; Constant mesh ~ **92–95%**; Synchromesh ~ **95–98%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Gearboxes

Automotive gearboxes are broadly classified into two families:

```
A. MANUAL TRANSMISSION (Driver operated clutch + gear lever)
   1. Sliding Mesh Gearbox
   2. Constant Mesh Gearbox
   3. Synchromesh Gearbox

B. AUTOMATIC TRANSMISSION (No driver clutch pedal)
   1. Epicyclic (Planetary) Gearbox + Torque Converter
   2. Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)
   3. Dual Clutch Transmission (DCT/DSG)
   4. Automated Manual Transmission (AMT)
   5. Toroidal Transmission
```

### 2.2 Type 1 — Sliding Mesh Gearbox

**Construction:** The layshaft (countershaft) gears are fixed rigidly to the shaft. The main-shaft gears are **free to slide axially** on splines. To engage a gear, the gear is physically slid sideways until its dog teeth mesh with corresponding dog teeth on a mating gear.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-step):**
1. Clutch pedal is depressed — disconnects engine from gearbox input shaft.
2. Driver moves the gear lever — this operates a selector fork.
3. The selector fork slides the chosen gear on splines along the main shaft.
4. Dog teeth of the sliding gear engage with dog teeth of the corresponding countershaft gear.
5. Torque flows: Clutch shaft → countershaft → sliding gear → main shaft → propeller shaft.

**Advantages:** Simple, cheap, robust, easy to repair. **Disadvantages:** Very noisy, partial engagement causes grinding, requires double-declutching skill, high wear on dog teeth.

### 2.3 Type 2 — Constant Mesh Gearbox

**Construction:** *All* gears on both the layshaft and main shaft are in **constant mesh** (their teeth are always engaged). Gear engagement is done by a separate **dog clutch** (or synchronizer sleeve) that locks the desired gear to the main shaft.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-step):**
1. Engine power enters the input/clutch shaft.
2. The clutch shaft and layshaft carry gears that are always in mesh — so the layshaft **always rotates** when the clutch is engaged.
3. Main-shaft gears rotate freely on bearings, driven by their respective layshaft gears.
4. To select a gear, a dog clutch is slid along the main shaft to lock the chosen gear to the shaft.
5. Torque now flows: Clutch shaft → layshaft gear pair → main-shaft gear → dog clutch → main shaft → propeller shaft.

**Advantages:** No gear-tooth grinding (gears never disengage), quieter than sliding mesh, gears are precision-ground and heat-treated. **Disadvantages:** Dog clutch still causes some noise during engagement, requires careful timing.

### 2.4 Type 3 — Synchromesh Gearbox

**Construction:** A **synchromesh unit** (synchronizer) sits between two gears on the main shaft. Each synchromesh unit contains a **brake band (brake ring)**, **struts (synchromizer rings)**, and a **sleeve**. The synchromesh device uses **friction** to equalize the speed of the gear to be engaged with the main shaft *before* the dog teeth engage.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-step):**
1. Driver moves gear lever → sleeve of the synchronizer is pushed toward the chosen gear.
2. The chamfered end of the sleeve contacts the **conical surface of the synchronizer ring (brake cone)** attached to that gear.
3. **Friction** between the sleeve and the cone causes the gear's speed to be **braked** (or accelerated) toward the main-shaft speed.
4. Once speeds match, the **struts push the blocking ring** out of the way.
5. The sleeve slides over the dog teeth and locks the gear to the main shaft — engagement is **silent and smooth**.

**Advantages:** Silent, smooth, no double-declutching needed, fast shifts, longer gear life. **Disadvantages:** More complex, heavier, more expensive to repair, slightly less efficient due to friction surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The "friction-based speed equalization before dog-tooth engagement" is the single most asked KTU concept under synchromesh. Remember the three components: **Brake ring (Baulk ring)**, **Struts**, and **Shifting sleeve**.

### 2.5 Type 4 — Epicyclic (Planetary) Gearbox

**Construction:** A planetary gearset consists of:
- **Sun gear** (central gear)
- **Planet gears** (2 to 6, mounted on a planet carrier)
- **Annulus / Ring gear** (outer gear with internal teeth)

Three input/output members; one is held, one is input, one is output. The **gear ratio** is determined by *which member is held stationary*.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-step):**
1. Input (sun gear) rotates.
2. The annulus is held fixed by a brake band.
3. Planet gears "walk" around the sun gear like planets around a sun — the **planet carrier** rotates at a reduced speed and delivers output.
4. By changing which member is held, different gear ratios are obtained.

**Gear Ratio Equations (Fundamental Planetary Formula):**

$$\frac{N_{\text{sun}} - N_{\text{carrier}}}{N_{\text{annulus}} - N_{\text{carrier}}} = -\frac{Z_{\text{annulus}}}{Z_{\text{sun}}}$$

**Standard Reduction Ratio (Annulus Held):**

$$i_{\text{reduction}} = 1 + \frac{Z_{\text{annulus}}}{Z_{\text{sun}}}$$

**Direct Drive (Planet Carrier Held):** $i = 1$

**Overdrive (Sun Held):**

$$i_{\text{overdrive}} = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{Z_{\text{annulus}}}{Z_{\text{sun}}}}$$

**Advantages:** Compact, coaxial (input and output on same axis), high torque capacity, multiple ratios with simple band/clutch control, ideal for automatics. **Disadvantages:** Complex, requires hydraulic actuation, expensive.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** Epicyclic gearboxes are the heart of **all modern automatic transmissions** (Toyota, Honda, Hyundai, etc., use planetary sets in their AT units). KTU examiners frequently ask for the derivation of gear ratios in planetary gearboxes.

### 2.6 Type 5 — Continuously Variable Transmission (CVT)

**Construction:** Uses a **steel belt or chain** running between two variable-diameter **pulleys**. Each pulley has two conical halves that can move closer or apart, changing the effective diameter.

**Operational Logic:** As the driving pulley diameter decreases, the driven pulley diameter increases (or vice versa), continuously changing the gear ratio. There is no discrete "1st gear" or "2nd gear" — the ratio varies smoothly across an infinite range.

**Advantages:** Always keeps engine at peak efficiency RPM, very smooth, fuel-efficient. **Disadvantages:** Limited torque capacity (rubber belt can slip), "rubber-band" feel, less engaging to drive.

### 2.7 Type 6 — Dual Clutch Transmission (DCT/DSG)

**Construction:** Two separate clutches — one for **odd gears (1, 3, 5, R)** and one for **even gears (2, 4, 6)**. Each clutch controls its own input shaft with its own set of gears.

**Operational Logic:** While the car is in 2nd gear (clutch 2 engaged), gear 3 is **pre-selected** on the other shaft. The computer then disengages clutch 2 and engages clutch 1 in milliseconds — gear changes are nearly instantaneous.

**Advantages:** Lightning-fast shifts, no torque interruption, excellent fuel efficiency. **Disadvantages:** Complex, expensive, jerky at low speeds, expensive to repair.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $i_g = \dfrac{Z_{\text{driven}}}{Z_{\text{driver}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{driver}}}{N_{\text{driven}}}$ | Gear ratio (basic) | Dimensionless |
| $N_{\text{driven}} = N_{\text{driver}} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{\text{driver}}}{Z_{\text{driven}}}$ | Speed of driven gear | rpm |
| $T_{\text{driven}} = T_{\text{driver}} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{\text{driven}}}{Z_{\text{driver}}} \cdot \eta$ | Torque on driven gear | N·m, $\eta$ = efficiency |
| $i_{\text{overall}} = i_g \times i_{\text{final drive}}$ | Total drivetrain ratio | $i_{\text{fd}}$ usually 3.0–4.5 |
| $v_{\text{vehicle}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D_w \cdot N_w}{60}$ | Vehicle speed from wheel | m/s, $D_w$ = wheel diameter |
| $N_w = \dfrac{N_{\text{engine}}}{i_g \cdot i_{\text{fd}}}$ | Wheel speed | rpm |
| $i_{\text{reduction, planetary}} = 1 + \dfrac{Z_a}{Z_s}$ | Planetary reduction (annulus held) | $Z_a$ = annulus, $Z_s$ = sun |
| $i_{\text{overdrive, planetary}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \frac{Z_a}{Z_s}}$ | Planetary overdrive (sun held) | Dimensionless |
| $P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power transmitted | Watts (W) |
| $T_{\text{engine}} \cdot N_{\text{engine}} = T_{\text{wheel}} \cdot N_{\text{wheel}} \cdot \eta_{\text{total}}$ | Power balance | $\eta_{\text{total}} \approx 0.85$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Gear Ratio Relationship from Geometry

**Statement to prove:** Gear ratio equals the ratio of teeth, and equals the inverse ratio of speeds.

**Step 1 — Geometric condition for meshing:**
Two external spur gears mesh only when their **circular pitches are equal** at the pitch point.

$$
p_1 = p_2 \implies \frac{\pi D_1}{Z_1} = \frac{\pi D_2}{Z_2}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi$:

$$
\frac{D_1}{Z_1} = \frac{D_2}{Z_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Derive the teeth relationship:**

$$
\frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1}
$$

This states that **the ratio of pitch diameters equals the ratio of the number of teeth**.

**Step 3 — No-slip condition at the pitch point:**
At the meshing point, the linear (tangential) velocity of both gears is identical. This is the *kinematic* constraint.

$$
v_1 = v_2 \implies \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi D_2 N_2}{60}
$$

Cancelling $\frac{\pi}{60}$ from both sides:

$$
D_1 N_1 = D_2 N_2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the speed ratio:**

$$
\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine with Step 2:**

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = i_g\;}
$$

This is the foundational result: the gear ratio equals the ratio of the number of teeth on the driven gear to the driver gear, and equals the inverse ratio of their speeds.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Torque Transmission Through a Gear Pair

**Given:** A driver gear (1) with torque $T_1$ drives a driven gear (2) which outputs torque $T_2$.

**Step 1 — Tangential force at the pitch circle:**
The tangential force $F_t$ is the same on both gears (Newton's third law — the contact force is an action-reaction pair).

$$
F_t = \frac{T_1}{r_1} = \frac{T_2}{r_2}
$$

where $r_1 = \dfrac{D_1}{2}$ and $r_2 = \dfrac{D_2}{2}$ are the pitch radii.

**Step 2 — Substitute radii in terms of diameters:**

$$
F_t = \frac{2 T_1}{D_1} = \frac{2 T_2}{D_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-multiply:**

$$
T_1 \cdot D_2 = T_2 \cdot D_1
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the torque ratio:**

$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1} = i_g
$$

**Step 5 — Account for gear-train efficiency $\eta$:**
In a real system, friction losses reduce the output torque.

$$
\boxed{\;T_2 = T_1 \cdot i_g \cdot \eta\;}
$$

where $\eta$ is the **gear-train efficiency** (typically 0.95–0.98 per pair of spur gears).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Planetary Gearbox Reduction Ratio (Annulus Held)

**Setup:** A simple planetary gearset with sun gear ($S$), planet carrier ($C$), and annulus ($A$). The annulus is **held stationary** ($N_A = 0$). The sun is the input, and the carrier is the output.

**Step 1 — Use the fundamental planetary equation:**

$$
\frac{N_S - N_C}{N_A - N_C} = -\frac{Z_A}{Z_S}
$$

The negative sign indicates that the sun and annulus rotate in opposite directions when the planet carrier is the reference.

**Step 2 — Substitute the boundary condition $N_A = 0$ (annulus is held):**

$$
\frac{N_S - N_C}{0 - N_C} = -\frac{Z_A}{Z_S}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the left-hand side:**

$$
\frac{N_S - N_C}{-N_C} = \frac{N_C - N_S}{N_C} = 1 - \frac{N_S}{N_C} = -\frac{Z_A}{Z_S}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\dfrac{N_S}{N_C}$:**

$$
1 - \frac{N_S}{N_C} = -\frac{Z_A}{Z_S} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{N_S}{N_C} = 1 + \frac{Z_A}{Z_S}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the reduction ratio as $N_{\text{input}} / N_{\text{output}} = N_S / N_C$:**

$$
\boxed{\;i_{\text{reduction}} = 1 + \frac{Z_A}{Z_S}\;}
$$

**Numerical example:** If $Z_S = 20$ teeth and $Z_A = 60$ teeth:

$$
i_{\text{reduction}} = 1 + \frac{60}{20} = 1 + 3 = 4
$$

The carrier rotates at one-quarter the speed of the sun gear, with four times the torque (minus efficiency losses).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Gearbox Types

| Parameter | Sliding Mesh | Constant Mesh | Synchromesh | Epicyclic (Planetary) | CVT |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gear Engagement** | Sliding gears | Dog clutch | Synchromesh unit | Bands & clutches | Variable pulley |
| **Noise Level** | High | Moderate | Very Low | Very Low | Very Low |
| **Shift Effort** | Heavy (skill) | Moderate | Light | Automatic | Automatic |
| **Efficiency** | 85–90% | 92–95% | 95–98% | 90–95% | 85–92% |
| **Complexity** | Low | Medium | Medium-High | High | High |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | Medium-High | High | High |
| **Modern Use** | Vintage (e.g., old Jeeps) | Older trucks | **Most modern manuals** | **All modern AT** | Scooters, some cars |
| **Torque Capacity** | High | High | High | Very High | Low-Medium |
| **Gear Ratio Type** | Discrete | Discrete | Discrete | Discrete | Continuous |
| **Number of Ratios** | 3–4 | 4–5 | 5–6 | 4–10 (with multiple sets) | Infinite |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A car's engine produces maximum torque of **180 N·m at 3000 rpm**. The first gear ratio is **3.5 : 1**, the final drive ratio is **4.1 : 1**, and the gearbox efficiency is **95%**. Find: (a) Torque at the wheels, (b) Wheel speed in rpm, (c) Vehicle speed if wheel diameter is **0.6 m**.

**Step (a) — Torque at the wheels:**

$$
T_{\text{wheel}} = T_{\text{engine}} \times i_g \times i_{\text{fd}} \times \eta
$$

Substituting values:

$$
T_{\text{wheel}} = 180 \times 3.5 \times 4.1 \times 0.95
$$

$$
T_{\text{wheel}} = 180 \times 3.5 \times 4.1 \times 0.95 \;\text{N·m}
$$

Multiplication breakdown: $180 \times 3.5 = 630$; $630 \times 4.1 = 2583$; $2583 \times 0.95 = 2453.85$ N·m

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{wheel}} \approx 2453.85\;\text{N·m}}
$$

**Step (b) — Wheel speed in rpm:**

$$
N_{\text{wheel}} = \frac{N_{\text{engine}}}{i_g \times i_{\text{fd}}} = \frac{3000}{3.5 \times 4.1} = \frac{3000}{14.35} \approx 209.06\;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step (c) — Vehicle speed:**

$$
v = \frac{\pi D_w N_w}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.6 \times 209.06}{60} = \frac{394.04}{60} \approx 6.57\;\text{m/s}
$$

Converting to km/h:

$$
v = 6.57 \times 3.6 \approx 23.65\;\text{km/h}
$$

This is realistic for a car in 1st gear.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Automobile Powertrain (with Gearbox)

<!-- Mermaid Diagram: Gearbox in powertrain context -->
```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Engine Block] --> B[Clutch or Torque Converter]
    B --> C[Gearbox Assembly]
    C --> D[Propeller Shaft]
    D --> E[Final Drive and Differential]
    E --> F[Drive Shafts Half Shafts]
    F --> G[Driving Wheels]
    C --> H[Gear Selector Mechanism]
    C --> I[Synchromesh or Planetary Set]
    I --> J[Output to Propeller Shaft]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Power flows from the engine, through the clutch (or torque converter in automatics), into the gearbox where the gear ratio is selected, then to the propeller shaft (rear-wheel-drive) or directly to the final drive (front-wheel-drive).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Manual vs Automatic Gearbox Architecture

<!-- Mermaid Diagram: Classification of gearbox types -->
```mermaid
graph TB
    root[Automobile Gearbox System]
    root --> M[Manual Transmission]
    root --> A[Automatic Transmission]
    M --> M1[Sliding Mesh Type]
    M --> M2[Constant Mesh Type]
    M --> M3[Synchromesh Type]
    A --> A1[Epicyclic Planetary AT]
    A --> A2[Continuously Variable CVT]
    A --> A3[Dual Clutch DCT]
    A --> A4[Automated Manual AMT]
    A1 --> A1a[Torque Converter Input]
    A1 --> A1b[Planetary Gearset Core]
    A1 --> A1c[Hydraulic Clutch and Bands]
    A2 --> A2a[Steel Belt Drive]
    A2 --> A2b[Variable Diameter Pulleys]
```

---

### 4.3 Detailed Block Diagram: Synchromesh Gearbox Internal Logic

<!-- Mermaid Diagram: Synchromesh engagement sequence -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Step 1: Driver pushes gear lever] --> P2[Step 2: Shift fork moves sleeve toward selected gear]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Sleeve cone contacts brake ring friction surface]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Friction equalizes gear speed with main shaft]
    P4 --> P5{Step 5: Are speeds matched?}
    P5 -->|No| P3
    P5 -->|Yes| P6[Step 6: Struts push blocking ring aside]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: Sleeve slides onto dog teeth of gear]
    P7 --> P8[Step 8: Gear is locked to main shaft; engagement complete]
```

---

### 4.4 Schematic: Planetary Gearbox Component Map

<!-- Mermaid Diagram: Planetary gearbox components -->
```mermaid
graph LR
    SUN[Sun Gear - Center - Input] --> PLANET1[Planet Gear 1]
    SUN --> PLANET2[Planet Gear 2]
    SUN --> PLANET3[Planet Gear 3]
    PLANET1 --> CARRIER[Planet Carrier - Output]
    PLANET2 --> CARRIER
    PLANET3 --> CARRIER
    PLANET1 --> ANNULUS[Annulus Ring Gear - Often Held]
    PLANET2 --> ANNULUS
    PLANET3 --> ANNULUS
    CARRIER --> BRAKE[Brake Band or Clutch Selects Which Member is Held]
    ANNULUS --> BRAKE
```

**Operating modes of a planetary set (KTU high-yield):**
1. **Reduction (Annulus held):** $i = 1 + \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_S}$
2. **Direct Drive (Carrier held):** $i = 1$
3. **Reverse (Sun held):** $i = -\dfrac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_S}$ (negative = reverse direction)
4. **Overdrive (Sun held, annulus drives):** $i = \dfrac{1}{1 + \frac{Z_A}{Z_S}}$

---

### 4.5 Flow Diagram: Selection Logic of Gearbox Type by Application

<!-- Mermaid Diagram: Application-based gearbox selection -->
```mermaid
graph TD
    Q{What is the primary requirement?}
    Q -->|Low cost and high torque| R1[Sliding Mesh - Vintage Heavy Vehicles]
    Q -->|Smooth and silent shifting| R2[Synchromesh - Modern Passenger Cars]
    Q -->|Fully automatic with high torque| R3[Epicyclic Planetary - Luxury AT Cars]
    Q -->|Maximum fuel efficiency| R4[CVT - Scooters and Hybrid Cars]
    Q -->|Sports performance fast shifts| R5[Dual Clutch DCT - VW Audi Porsche]
    Q -->|Cheap automatic for budget cars| R6[AMT - Maruti Suzuki Tata]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**Differentiate between a sliding mesh gearbox and a constant mesh gearbox. Mention any two advantages of the constant mesh type.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Sliding Mesh | Constant Mesh |
|---|---|---|
| Gear arrangement | Gears slide on splines to engage | All gears remain in constant mesh |
| Engagement method | Gear teeth themselves engage | Separate dog clutch engages |
| Noise level | High (gear-tooth clash) | Low (no tooth clash) |
| Wear | More (gear teeth wear) | Less (only dog clutch wears) |
| Skill required | Double-declutching needed | Single-declutch sufficient |

**Two advantages of constant mesh type:**
1. Gears are always in mesh → smooth, gradual engagement → less wear and quieter operation.
2. Gears can be made of stronger materials (heat-treated, precision-ground) because they don't need to slide axially, giving longer service life.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**Explain the working principle of a synchromesh gearbox. What is the role of the "brake ring" (baulk ring)?**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
In a synchromesh gearbox, a synchronizer unit brings the speed of the gear to be engaged into synchronization with the main shaft *before* the dog teeth engage. This is achieved through **friction** between a tapered cone on the gear and a matching cone on the synchronizer sleeve.

**Sequence:** (1) Sleeve pushed toward gear → (2) cone-to-cone contact → (3) friction equalizes speeds → (4) blocking ring rotates out of the way → (5) sleeve slides onto dog teeth for full engagement.

**Role of the Brake Ring (Baulk Ring):**
The brake ring is a **friction ring with internal dog teeth** mounted on the cone of the gear. It serves two critical functions:
1. **Friction Surface:** Provides the tapered friction surface that **equalizes the rotational speed** of the gear with the main shaft before engagement.
2. **Locking Function (Baulking):** Until speeds are matched, the ring's outer dog teeth physically **block** the sleeve from sliding further — this is why it is called a "baulk ring" (it prevents engagement of mismatched speeds, protecting the gearbox from grinding).

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is divided into sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. The choice between Question A and Question B is provided as per KTU 2024 ESE module format.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **synchromesh gearbox**. List **five advantages** and **two disadvantages** of the synchromesh gearbox. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **sliding mesh gearbox** has the following gears on the main shaft: $Z_1 = 40$, $Z_2 = 30$, $Z_3 = 25$, and a lay shaft gear of $Z_L = 20$ that drives them. The engine runs at **2500 rpm**. Calculate: (i) the speed of the main shaft in each gear, (ii) the torque on the main shaft in each gear, given engine torque is **150 N·m** and gear efficiency is **90%**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Synchromesh Gearbox (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A synchromesh gearbox consists of:
- **Input shaft (clutch shaft):** receives power from the engine via the clutch.
- **Layshaft (countershaft):** carries gears that are in **constant mesh** with main-shaft gears.
- **Main shaft (output shaft):** carries the driven gears, free to rotate on bearings.
- **Synchromesh unit:** for each gear pair, a synchronizer is mounted on the main shaft between the two gears of that pair. The synchronizer contains:
  - **Hub** (splined to main shaft, stationary with it)
  - **Sleeve** (slides axially on hub; carries external dog teeth)
  - **Struts** (3 or 4, fit in slots of the hub and sleeve)
  - **Blocking rings / Baulk rings** (one on each side; have internal splines matching the gear's external splines, plus a friction cone surface)

**Working (3 marks):** Already described in Section 2.4. Key sequence: lever movement → sleeve contact with cone → friction speed equalization → blocking ring rotates out of sleeve's path → sleeve engages dog teeth of chosen gear → gear locked to main shaft.

**Advantages (0.5 each, 2.5 marks):**
1. Silent gear engagement (no grinding noise).
2. Smooth and fast shifts.
3. No double-declutching required.
4. Longer gear life due to gradual speed equalization.
5. Easier to operate, less driver fatigue.

**Disadvantages (0.75 each, 1.5 marks):**
1. More complex and heavier than constant mesh.
2. Higher initial cost and expensive to repair.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by:
> 1. **Confusing the "blocking ring" with the "brake ring"** — they are the same physical part, but the "blocking" (baulking) action is what students forget to describe.
> 2. **Skipping the friction cone step** — KTU examiners award 2 marks specifically for explaining the **friction-based speed equalization** logic.
> 3. Drawing only the dog clutch and forgetting the synchromesh unit altogether.

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $Z_1 = 40$ (Gear 1 on main shaft, driven by lay gear)
- $Z_2 = 30$ (Gear 2 on main shaft)
- $Z_3 = 25$ (Gear 3 on main shaft)
- $Z_L = 20$ (Lay shaft driver gear)
- $N_{\text{engine}} = 2500$ rpm
- $T_{\text{engine}} = 150$ N·m
- $\eta = 0.90$

**Step 1 — Compute gear ratios for each gear (1 mark):**

$$
i_{g1} = \frac{Z_1}{Z_L} = \frac{40}{20} = 2.0
$$

$$
i_{g2} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_L} = \frac{30}{20} = 1.5
$$

$$
i_{g3} = \frac{Z_3}{Z_L} = \frac{25}{20} = 1.25
$$

**Step 2 — Compute main-shaft speed in each gear (2 marks):**

$$
N_{m1} = \frac{N_{\text{engine}}}{i_{g1}} = \frac{2500}{2.0} = 1250\;\text{rpm}
$$

$$
N_{m2} = \frac{N_{\text{engine}}}{i_{g2}} = \frac{2500}{1.5} = 1666.67\;\text{rpm}
$$

$$
N_{m3} = \frac{N_{\text{engine}}}{i_{g3}} = \frac{2500}{1.25} = 2000\;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute torque on main shaft in each gear (3 marks):**

$$
T_{m1} = T_{\text{engine}} \times i_{g1} \times \eta = 150 \times 2.0 \times 0.90 = 270\;\text{N·m}
$$

$$
T_{m2} = 150 \times 1.5 \times 0.90 = 202.5\;\text{N·m}
$$

$$
T_{m3} = 150 \times 1.25 \times 0.90 = 168.75\;\text{N·m}
$$

**Step 4 — Tabulated results (1 mark):**

| Gear | Ratio | Main Shaft Speed (rpm) | Main Shaft Torque (N·m) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1st | 2.00 | 1250 | 270.00 |
| 2nd | 1.50 | 1666.67 | 202.50 |
| 3rd | 1.25 | 2000 | 168.75 |

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an **epicyclic (planetary) gearbox** with a neat diagram. List the **different operating modes** of a simple planetary gearset and derive the reduction ratio when the **annulus is held stationary**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A car has a **synchromesh gearbox** with the following gear ratios: 1st = **3.2 : 1**, 2nd = **2.1 : 1**, 3rd = **1.4 : 1**, 4th = **1.0 : 1**, 5th = **0.85 : 1**. The final drive ratio is **4.0 : 1**, and the overall drivetrain efficiency is **88%**. The engine produces a maximum torque of **220 N·m at 3500 rpm** and the wheel diameter is **0.62 m**. Compute: (i) the torque at the wheels in **1st gear** and **5th gear (overdrive)**, (ii) the vehicle speed in **3rd gear** at 3500 rpm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📘 Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Epicyclic Gearbox (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** Refer to Section 2.5. Key components: sun gear, planet gears (typically 3), planet carrier, annulus (ring gear with internal teeth). All three members are coaxial. The annulus teeth are cut on the inside of a ring.

**Working (1.5 marks):** When the input member (say sun) rotates and one member is held by a brake band, the planet gears "orbit" inside the annulus, carrying the planet carrier with them at a reduced speed. The output is taken from the carrier. Multiple gear ratios are obtained by selectively holding different members using multi-plate clutches and brake bands (controlled hydraulically in automatic transmissions).

**Operating Modes (1.5 marks):** Table:

| Mode | Held Member | Input | Output | Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reduction | Annulus | Sun | Carrier | $1 + \frac{Z_A}{Z_S}$ |
| Direct Drive | Carrier | Sun | Annulus | 1 |
| Reverse | Sun | Carrier | Annulus | $-\frac{Z_A}{Z_A + Z_S}$ |
| Overdrive | Sun | Carrier | Annulus | $\frac{1}{1 + \frac{Z_A}{Z_S}}$ |

**Derivation (2 marks):** Already derived in Section 3.3 of these notes.

$$
i_{\text{reduction}} = 1 + \frac{Z_A}{Z_S}
$$

---

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $i_{g1}=3.2$, $i_{g2}=2.1$, $i_{g3}=1.4$, $i_{g4}=1.0$, $i_{g5}=0.85$ (overdrive). $i_{\text{fd}} = 4.0$, $\eta_{\text{total}} = 0.88$, $T_e = 220$ N·m at $N_e = 3500$ rpm, $D_w = 0.62$ m.

**Step (i-a) — Torque at wheels in 1st gear (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{w1} = T_e \times i_{g1} \times i_{\text{fd}} \times \eta = 220 \times 3.2 \times 4.0 \times 0.88
$$

Intermediate: $220 \times 3.2 = 704$; $704 \times 4.0 = 2816$; $2816 \times 0.88 = 2478.08$ N·m

$$
\boxed{T_{w1} = 2478.08\;\text{N·m}}
$$

**Step (i-b) — Torque at wheels in 5th gear (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{w5} = 220 \times 0.85 \times 4.0 \times 0.88
$$

Intermediate: $220 \times 0.85 = 187$; $187 \times 4.0 = 748$; $748 \times 0.88 = 658.24$ N·m

$$
\boxed{T_{w5} = 658.24\;\text{N·m}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that 5th gear gives **3.76 times less torque** than 1st gear — this is why overdrive is only used at high speeds, not for hill climbing.

**Step (ii) — Vehicle speed in 3rd gear at 3500 rpm (4 marks):**

**Step ii.1 — Wheel speed:**

$$
N_w = \frac{N_e}{i_{g3} \times i_{\text{fd}}} = \frac{3500}{1.4 \times 4.0} = \frac{3500}{5.6} = 625\;\text{rpm}
$$

**Step ii.2 — Convert wheel speed to angular velocity:**

$$
\omega_w = \frac{2\pi N_w}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 625}{60} = 65.45\;\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step ii.3 — Compute vehicle speed:**

$$
v = \omega_w \times r_w = 65.45 \times \frac{0.62}{2} = 65.45 \times 0.31
$$

$$
\boxed{v = 20.29\;\text{m/s} \approx 73.04\;\text{km/h}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert m/s to km/h** (multiply by 3.6) — KTU examiners frequently deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Mixing up wheel radius vs diameter** — use $r_w = D_w / 2$, not $D_w$ directly in $v = \omega r$.
> 3. **Forgetting efficiency in torque calculations** — in 5th gear, students often forget the 0.88 factor and get 748 N·m, losing 1 mark.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Checklist (Read this the night before the exam)

- ✅ A **gearbox** matches engine speed/torque to road demand; cannot run engine in narrow band without it.
- ✅ **Sliding mesh** = gears slide to engage. **Constant mesh** = gears always mesh, dog clutch selects. **Synchromesh** = friction synchronizer before dog engagement.
- ✅ **Synchromesh is the dominant manual gearbox type** in modern cars (almost all new manual cars).
- ✅ **Epicyclic (planetary)** gearboxes are the heart of all modern **automatic transmissions** — uses sun, planets, annulus.
- ✅ **Planetary reduction ratio** (annulus held) = $1 + \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_S}$ — **must memorize**.
- ✅ **CVT** has infinite (continuous) gear ratios; uses variable-diameter pulleys + steel belt.
- ✅ **DCT** uses two clutches for odd/even gears; pre-selects next gear for lightning-fast shifts.
- ✅ **AMT** is a robotized manual — uses actuators to operate a regular manual gearbox (cheap automatic).
- ✅ **Gear ratio** $i_g = \dfrac{Z_{\text{driven}}}{Z_{\text{driver}}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{driver}}}{N_{\text{driven}}}$.
- ✅ **Torque transmitted:** $T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}} \times i_g \times \eta$.
- ✅ **Overall reduction** = $i_g \times i_{\text{final drive}} \times \eta_{\text{drivetrain}}$.
- ✅ **Vehicle speed:** $v = \dfrac{\pi D_w N_w}{60}$ (in m/s); multiply by 3.6 for km/h.
- ✅ **Overdrive** means output shaft rotates **faster** than input ($i_g < 1$); used at highway speeds to reduce engine RPM.
- ✅ **Reverse gear** in a manual gearbox is achieved by inserting an **idler gear** between countershaft and main-shaft reverse gear.
- ✅ **First gear** has the highest reduction ratio (typically 3.0–4.0 : 1); **top gear** is closest to 1 : 1 (or under for overdrive).

### Most Frequently Asked KTU Topics (Priority Order)

1. ⭐⭐⭐ **Synchromesh gearbox** — construction, working, role of brake ring (asked in 80%+ of KTU papers).
2. ⭐⭐⭐ **Planetary gear ratios** derivation — annulus held, sun held, etc.
3. ⭐⭐⭐ **Comparison tables** between manual gearbox types.
4. ⭐⭐ **CVT / DCT working principles** (modern trend in KTU 2024).
5. ⭐⭐ **Numerical problems** on gear ratios, torque, and vehicle speed.
6. ⭐ **Constant mesh vs sliding mesh** — short notes.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Study Tip:** If you have only **30 minutes** to revise this topic, focus on:
> 1. The synchromesh working sequence (5 steps).
> 2. The planetary reduction formula $i = 1 + \dfrac{Z_A}{Z_S}$ and its derivation.
> 3. The numerical formulas: $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ and $T_{\text{out}} = T_{\text{in}} \cdot i \cdot \eta$.
>
> These three alone account for **~70% of KTU marks** on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steering Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Steering Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steering System (KTU 2024 Definition):** A steering system is a mechanical (or electro-mechanical) arrangement in an automobile that converts the rotary motion of the steering wheel into the angular displacement of the front wheels (and in some cases, all wheels), thereby controlling the directional movement of the vehicle along the desired path. It provides mechanical advantage, directional stability, and minimizes driver effort.

The steering system is a critical **chassis subsystem** that directly influences the **handling, stability, and safety** of a vehicle. In the KTU 2024 syllabus, it is treated as a core Module 3 topic, bridging **kinematics of mechanisms (Module 2)** and **braking/suspension systems (Module 4)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a steering system like **the reins of a horse-drawn chariot**. When the charioteer pulls the left rein, the left horse turns inward, and the entire chariot changes direction. The reins multiply the small hand movement of the driver into a substantial directional change of the heavy cart.

In a car, the **steering wheel** is the charioteer's hands, the **steering column and gear** are the reins, the **linkage and tie rods** are the harness straps, and the **front wheels** are the horses. Modern cars also add **power assistance** (hydraulic or electric), which is like a strong servant secretly helping the charioteer pull the reins, reducing fatigue.

### 1.3 Functions of a Steering System (KTU Board Perspective)

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Mandatory Functions a steering system MUST perform:**
> 1. **Directional Control** – Allow the driver to guide the vehicle along the intended path.
> 2. **Mechanical Advantage** – Multiply driver input torque to overcome tire-pavement friction and self-aligning torque.
> 3. **Stable Straight-Line Running** – Return wheels to the straight-ahead position without driver intervention (self-centering).
> 4. **Absorb Road Shocks** – Prevent road irregularities from being transmitted to the driver's hands.
> 5. **Geometry Preservation** – Maintain correct wheel angles (Ackermann condition) during cornering to avoid tire scrub.

### 1.4 Classification of Steering Systems

| **Type** | **Description** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Manual Steering** | Pure mechanical linkage; driver provides all effort | Light vehicles, older cars |
| **Power Steering** | Hydraulic or electric assistance added to manual gear | Modern passenger cars, trucks |
| **Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS)** | Uses hydraulic pump, valve, and cylinder | Conventional sedans, SUVs |
| **Electric Power Steering (EPS)** | Uses electric motor on column or rack | Modern fuel-efficient cars |
| **Electro-Hydraulic (EHPS)** | Hybrid – electric motor drives hydraulic pump | Heavy commercial vehicles |
| **Steer-by-Wire (SbW)** | No mechanical connection; pure electronic | Autonomous and concept vehicles |

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Problems

- **Steering Ratio (n)** = typically **15:1 to 20:1** for passenger cars
- **Wheelbase (L)** = **2.2 m to 3.2 m** for typical cars
- **Track width (t)** = **1.4 m to 1.6 m**
- **Self-aligning torque** = usually **30 to 60 Nm** at parking
- **Friction coefficient (μ) between tire and road** = **0.7 to 0.9** (dry asphalt)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Steering Geometry – The Foundation of Vehicle Handling

Steering geometry refers to the **angular and dimensional relationships** between the steering axis, the wheels, and the road surface. A car has **two front wheels** that must turn about **different instantaneous centers** to roll without scrubbing. This requires specific geometry, which is captured by three primary angles:

#### 2.1.1 Caster Angle (α)

Caster is the **tilting of the steering axis (kingpin axis) from the vertical**, measured in the **side view** of the vehicle.

- **Positive Caster**: Steering axis tilts **toward the rear** of the vehicle (top leans backward). This is the **modern standard**.
- **Negative Caster**: Steering axis tilts toward the front (used in some racing/three-wheelers).
- **Effect**: Provides **directional stability** and **self-centering torque**, similar to the trail of a bicycle fork.
- **Typical Value**: **2° to 4° positive** for passenger cars.

> [!NOTE]
> **Caster Trail (Mechanical Trail):** The distance between the point where the kingpin axis intersects the ground and the tire's contact patch center. It generates a self-aligning moment: $M_{sa} = W \cdot c$, where $W$ is the load on the wheel and $c$ is the trail.

#### 2.1.2 Camber Angle (γ)

Camber is the **tilting of the wheel from the vertical**, measured in the **front view** of the vehicle.

- **Positive Camber**: Wheel top tilts **outward**.
- **Negative Camber**: Wheel top tilts **inward** (used in racing).
- **Effect**: Distributes load across the tire contact patch; reduces steering effort by offsetting the effects of kingpin inclination.
- **Typical Value**: **0° to 1°** (usually slight positive for wear compensation).

#### 2.1.3 Kingpin Inclination (KPI) / Steering Axis Inclination (SAI)

KPI is the **tilting of the kingpin axis from the vertical**, measured in the **front view** of the vehicle.

- **Effect**: Causes the wheel to **rise** as it is turned, creating a **jacking effect** that provides self-centering.
- **Typical Value**: **6° to 13°** for modern cars.
- **Combined with Camber**: The difference between KPI and camber is called the **included angle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Most students confuse KPI and Camber. Remember — **Camber** is the angle of the **wheel** from vertical; **KPI** is the angle of the **steering axis (kingpin)** from vertical. Both are measured in the front view, but they are different.

#### 2.1.4 Toe-In and Toe-Out

Toe is the **difference in distance between the front edges and rear edges** of the two front wheels, measured at axle height.

- **Toe-In**: Front edges are **closer together** than rear edges. Used in cars with **positive camber** to compensate thrust.
- **Toe-Out**: Front edges are **farther apart** than rear edges.
- **Typical Value**: **0° to 0.5° toe-in** for passenger cars.

### 2.2 The Ackermann Steering Geometry (Most Critical KTU Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ackermann Condition:** When a vehicle turns, the **inner wheel must turn through a larger angle** than the **outer wheel** to ensure all wheels rotate about a **common instantaneous center (I)** without any tire scrub.

#### 2.2.1 Mathematical Derivation of Ackermann Condition

Let:
- $L$ = wheelbase (distance between front and rear axles)
- $t$ = track width (distance between the two kingpins)
- $R_o$ = turning radius of the **outer** front wheel
- $R_i$ = turning radius of the **inner** front wheel
- $\delta_o$ = steering angle of **outer** wheel
- $\delta_i$ = steering angle of **inner** wheel
- $R$ = turning radius of the **center of gravity (CG)**

From the geometry of the turning vehicle (all wheels share a common instantaneous center $I$ located on the rear axle extended):

$$
\cot \delta_o = \frac{R_o}{L} = \frac{R + \frac{t}{2}}{L}
$$

$$
\cot \delta_i = \frac{R_i}{L} = \frac{R - \frac{t}{2}}{L}
$$

Subtracting:

$$
\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{t}{L}
$$

This is the **fundamental Ackermann condition** for zero scrub.

#### 2.2.2 Percentage Ackermann

$$
\% \text{ Ackermann} = \frac{\delta_{i,actual} - \delta_{i,straight}}{\delta_{i,Ackermann} - \delta_{i,straight}} \times 100
$$

- **100% Ackermann**: Perfect geometric condition, zero scrub.
- **< 100% Ackermann**: Under-Ackermann (used in **performance cars** for sharper turn-in).
- **> 100% Ackermann**: Over-Ackermann (used in **heavy vehicles** to reduce steering effort).

#### 2.2.3 Davis Steering (Modified Ackermann)

> [!NOTE]
> **Davis Steering Principle:** When the steering wheel is turned through angle $\theta$, the **steering arm** of the Pitman arm is set at an angle such that the **average of inner and outer wheel angles** is a constant multiple of $\theta$. This simplifies the linkage design.

**Condition for Davis Steering:**

$$
\frac{\delta_o + \delta_i}{2} = \text{constant} \times \theta
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\tan \delta_i - \tan \delta_o = \frac{t}{L}
$$

Davis steering is **approximated** in many modern vehicles because pure Ackermann requires a non-linear linkage which is complex to manufacture.

### 2.3 Steering Linkage Components

| **Component** | **Function** |
|---|---|
| **Steering Wheel** | Driver input; converts rotary motion to column rotation |
| **Steering Column** | Transmits rotation; may contain collapsible sections for crash safety |
| **Steering Gear** | Provides mechanical advantage (Rack-Pinion, Recirculating Ball) |
| **Pitman Arm** | Lever that transfers gear output to drag link |
| **Drag Link** | Connects Pitman arm to steering arm of one wheel |
| **Steering Arm (Knuckle Arm)** | Mounted on stub axle; rotates the wheel |
| **Tie Rod** | Connects steering arms of both front wheels |
| **Idler Arm** | Supports drag link on the opposite side of Pitman arm |
| **Center Link** | Central element connecting drag link, tie rods, Pitman arm |

### 2.4 Steering Gear Mechanisms

#### 2.4.1 Rack and Pinion Steering
- **Construction**: A pinion gear on the steering shaft meshes with a linear **rack** (toothed bar) connected to the tie rods.
- **Mechanism**: Rotation of the steering wheel → rotation of pinion → linear motion of rack → lateral movement of tie rods → wheel rotation.
- **Advantages**: Simple, compact, light, high efficiency (~75–85%), precise feel.
- **Disadvantages**: Limited mechanical advantage; not suitable for heavy vehicles.
- **Application**: **Most modern passenger cars** (Maruti, Hyundai, Honda City, etc.)

**Steering Ratio for Rack and Pinion:**

$$
n = \frac{\text{Steering wheel rotation (degrees)}}{\text{Wheel rotation (degrees)}} = \frac{\pi D_p}{p}
$$

where $D_p$ is pinion pitch diameter and $p$ is rack tooth pitch.

#### 2.4.2 Recirculating Ball Steering
- **Construction**: A **worm gear** (sector or nut) on the steering shaft; a **recirculating ball nut** engages the worm; balls recirculate through return tubes.
- **Advantages**: High mechanical advantage, low friction, smooth operation, robust.
- **Disadvantages**: Lower efficiency (~50–60%) due to sliding friction; heavier.
- **Application**: **Trucks, heavy commercial vehicles, older SUVs** (Tata, Mahindra).

#### 2.4.3 Worm and Roller / Worm and Sector
- **Construction**: A worm gear drives either a roller or a sector gear.
- **Application**: Older passenger cars, light commercial vehicles.

### 2.5 Power Steering Systems

#### 2.5.1 Hydraulic Power Steering (HPS)
- **Components**: Hydraulic pump (driven by engine), control valve, double-acting cylinder, reservoir.
- **Operation**: 
  - Straight-line: Pump output is bypassed through the open-center valve.
  - Turning: Valve directs oil to one side of the cylinder, assisting the rack.
- **Advantage**: Reduces steering effort from **30–60 Nm to 5–10 Nm**.
- **Disadvantage**: Engine-driven pump reduces fuel efficiency by **2–5%**.

#### 2.5.2 Electric Power Steering (EPS)
- **Components**: Torque sensor on steering column, ECU, electric motor (mounted on column, pinion, or rack).
- **Types**: 
  - **Column-EPS (C-EPS)**: Motor on column (small cars)
  - **Pinion-EPS (P-EPS)**: Motor on pinion shaft (mid-size)
  - **Rack-EPS (R-EPS)**: Motor on rack (large, heavy cars)
- **Advantages**: Fuel savings (~3–5%), no hydraulic fluid, tunable via software.
- **Disadvantage**: Limited assist for very heavy vehicles.

#### 2.5.3 Power Steering Effort Reduction

The power steering reduces the **driver input torque** $T_d$ according to:

$$
T_{d,power} = T_{d,manual} - T_{assist}
$$

where $T_{assist}$ is typically **60–80%** of the manual torque for parking maneuvers.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear in Almost Every KTU Board Question on Steering**

| **Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \dfrac{t}{L}$ | **Ackermann condition** for zero scrub | dimensionless |
| $\tan \delta_i - \tan \delta_o = \dfrac{t}{L}$ | **Davis steering** condition | dimensionless |
| $R = \dfrac{L}{\tan \delta_{avg}}$ | Turning radius of CG | meters (m) |
| $R_o = R + \dfrac{t}{2}$ | Turning radius of outer wheel | meters (m) |
| $R_i = R - \dfrac{t}{2}$ | Turning radius of inner wheel | meters (m) |
| $n = \dfrac{\theta_{sw}}{\delta_{wheel}}$ | **Steering ratio** | dimensionless |
| $F_a = \dfrac{W \cdot \mu \cdot r}{r \cdot n} = \dfrac{W \cdot \mu}{n}$ | Force applied at steering wheel rim | Newtons (N) |
| $T_d = F_a \cdot r_{sw}$ | Driver torque at steering wheel | Newton-meters (Nm) |
| $P_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot p \cdot \eta$ | Pump power for HPS | Watts (W) |
| $M_{sa} = W \cdot c$ | Self-aligning moment (caster trail) | Nm |
| $\delta_o = \delta_i \cdot \dfrac{R}{R - t/2}$ | Outer wheel angle from inner wheel | radians or degrees |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Formula 1 Cars**: Use **Ackermann percentage < 50%** for aggressive turn-in; this deliberately allows tire slip to generate lateral grip.
- **Heavy Trucks**: Use **Over-Ackermann (> 100%)** because the rear inner wheel has a smaller turning radius requirement; reduces steering effort.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Steer-by-Wire)**: Use software-defined steering ratios; **variable ratio** steering is now common in luxury cars (e.g., BMW, Audi) where the ratio changes with vehicle speed — high ratio at low speed (easy parking) and low ratio at high speed (stable).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Ackermann Steering Geometry

**Problem Setup:** A vehicle has a wheelbase $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$ and track width $t = 1.5 \text{ m}$. The vehicle turns about a center $I$ such that the inner wheel turns at $\delta_i = 30°$. Determine the outer wheel angle $\delta_o$ for perfect Ackermann steering.

**Step 1 — Establish the Instantaneous Center**

When a vehicle turns, every wheel must roll on a circular path without sliding. All four wheel paths must converge to a **common point $I$**, which lies on the line of the rear axle (extended), because the rear wheels do not steer.

Let the perpendicular distance from $I$ to the line of the rear axle be $R$ (the turning radius of the vehicle's center of gravity).

**Step 2 — Express Wheel Positions in Terms of R**

The center of the outer front wheel is at perpendicular distance:

$$
R_o = R + \frac{t}{2}
$$

The center of the inner front wheel is at perpendicular distance:

$$
R_i = R - \frac{t}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Geometric Condition (Zero Scrub)**

For a wheel rolling without slip, the wheel's plane must be perpendicular to the line joining the wheel center to $I$. Therefore:

$$
\tan \delta_i = \frac{L}{R_i} \quad \text{(inner wheel)}
$$

$$
\tan \delta_o = \frac{L}{R_o} \quad \text{(outer wheel)}
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the Ackermann Condition**

Taking reciprocals (or equivalently using cotangents):

$$
\cot \delta_i = \frac{R_i}{L} = \frac{R - t/2}{L}
$$

$$
\cot \delta_o = \frac{R_o}{L} = \frac{R + t/2}{L}
$$

Subtracting the first from the second:

$$
\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{R + t/2 - R + t/2}{L}
$$

$$
\boxed{\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \frac{t}{L}}
$$

This is the **Ackermann steering condition** — the relationship between the wheel angles, wheelbase, and track width, independent of the turning radius $R$.

**Step 5 — Solve the Numerical Problem**

Given: $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$, $t = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $\delta_i = 30°$

Step 5a — Find $R_i$:

$$
R_i = \frac{L}{\tan \delta_i} = \frac{2.5}{\tan 30°} = \frac{2.5}{0.5774} = 4.330 \text{ m}
$$

Step 5b — Find $R$ (CG turning radius):

$$
R = R_i + \frac{t}{2} = 4.330 + 0.75 = 5.080 \text{ m}
$$

Step 5c — Find $R_o$:

$$
R_o = R + \frac{t}{2} = 5.080 + 0.75 = 5.830 \text{ m}
$$

Step 5d — Find $\delta_o$:

$$
\tan \delta_o = \frac{L}{R_o} = \frac{2.5}{5.830} = 0.4288
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_o = \arctan(0.4288) = 23.20°}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification Using the Ackermann Condition**

$$
\cot \delta_o - \cot \delta_i = \cot 23.20° - \cot 30°
$$

$$
= 2.333 - 1.732 = 0.601
$$

$$
\frac{t}{L} = \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 0.600
$$

The small difference is due to rounding. **Verified.**

### 3.2 Derivation of Turning Radius of Center of Gravity

**Setup:** A car of wheelbase $L$ is steered such that the **average** of inner and outer wheel angles is $\delta_{avg}$. Find the turning radius $R$ of the center of gravity.

**Step 1 — Define Average Angle**

$$
\delta_{avg} = \frac{\delta_i + \delta_o}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Express R in terms of Average Angle**

From the Ackermann geometry, the average of the cotangents relates to the average turning radius:

$$
\cot \delta_{avg} \approx \frac{R}{L}
$$

(This is an approximation that is exact for small track-width-to-wheelbase ratios.)

**Step 3 — Solve for R**

$$
\boxed{R = L \cdot \cot \delta_{avg} = \frac{L}{\tan \delta_{avg}}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Steering Effort (Manual Steering)

**Problem:** A passenger car has a front axle load of $W_f = 5000 \text{ N}$ per wheel, tire-road friction coefficient $\mu = 0.8$, kingpin offset $e = 0.025 \text{ m}$, steering wheel radius $r_{sw} = 0.2 \text{ m}$, steering ratio $n = 18$, and parking maneuver conditions. Estimate the force the driver must apply at the steering wheel rim.

**Step 1 — Compute the Pneumatic Trail (b)**

The pneumatic trail is the rearward offset of the tire contact patch from the kingpin axis intersection with the ground. For parking conditions, a typical value is $b = 0.04 \text{ m}$.

**Step 2 — Compute the Self-Aligning Torque (SAT)**

The self-aligning torque is generated by the lateral force at the contact patch:

$$
T_{sa} = F_y \cdot b
$$

where $F_y$ is the lateral force on the tire. For parking, $F_y \approx \mu \cdot W_f$:

$$
F_y = 0.8 \times 5000 = 4000 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T_{sa} = 4000 \times 0.04 = 160 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Total Resistance Torque at the Kingpin**

The kingpin torque is the sum of the self-aligning torque and the friction torque from the kingpin offset:

$$
T_{kp} = T_{sa} + F_y \cdot e
$$

$$
T_{kp} = 160 + 4000 \times 0.025 = 160 + 100 = 260 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Steering Ratio to Find Driver Torque**

$$
T_d = \frac{T_{kp}}{n \cdot \eta}
$$

where $\eta$ is the steering gear efficiency (assume 0.75 for rack-pinion):

$$
T_d = \frac{260}{18 \times 0.75} = \frac{260}{13.5} = 19.26 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Force at the Steering Wheel Rim**

$$
F_{sw} = \frac{T_d}{r_{sw}} = \frac{19.26}{0.2} = 96.3 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare to Acceptable Limits**

A typical human can comfortably apply **20–50 N** at the steering wheel for prolonged turns and up to **100–200 N** for short parking maneuvers. The computed **96.3 N** is at the upper limit, justifying the need for power steering.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Steering System Design Choices

| **Design Parameter** | **Small Car** | **Sedan** | **SUV/Truck** | **Formula 1** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wheelbase $L$ | 2.3 m | 2.7 m | 3.0 m | 3.5 m |
| Track $t$ | 1.45 m | 1.55 m | 1.65 m | 2.0 m |
| Steering Ratio $n$ | 16:1 | 17:1 | 20:1 | 12:1 |
| Ackermann % | 80% | 100% | 110% | 40% |
| Caster | 3° | 3.5° | 4° | 7° |
| KPI | 12° | 11° | 8° | 4° |
| Steering Type | EPS (C-EPS) | EPS (R-EPS) | HPS | Direct (no gear) |
| Turning Radius | 5.0 m | 5.5 m | 6.0 m | 7.0 m |

### 3.5 Variable Ratio Steering Derivation

In modern cars, the steering ratio is not constant. It varies with steering wheel angle $\theta$ as:

$$
n(\theta) = n_0 + k \cdot \theta^2
$$

where:
- $n_0$ = low-speed ratio (~12:1)
- $k$ = tunable constant (e.g., $k = 0.0005$ per degree²)
- $\theta$ = steering wheel angle in degrees

**Example:** At $\theta = 0°$ (straight), $n = 12$. At $\theta = 360°$ (full lock):

$$
n(360) = 12 + 0.0005 \times 360^2 = 12 + 64.8 = 76.8
$$

Wait — this is the reverse of typical behavior. Variable ratio steering gives **low ratio at center** (for highway stability) and **high ratio near lock** (for parking ease). The actual relationship:

$$
n(\theta) = n_{min} + (n_{max} - n_{min}) \cdot \frac{\theta^2}{\theta_{max}^2}
$$

For $\theta_{max} = 450°$, $n_{min} = 14$, $n_{max} = 22$:

$$
n(\theta) = 14 + 8 \cdot \left(\frac{\theta}{450}\right)^2
$$

This gives smooth, stable straight-line driving and quick parking response.
