# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a dead axle and a live axle. Give one example of a vehicle where each type is used.**

**Model Answer:**
A **dead axle** is a non-rotating shaft that supports the vertical weight of the vehicle and maintains wheel alignment but does **not** transmit any driving torque. The wheels rotate independently on bearings. Example: The front axle of a rear-wheel-drive car like the Maruti Swift (used as a steering and load-bearing axle only).

A **live axle** is a rotating shaft that supports the vehicle load **and** transmits the driving torque from the differential to the road wheels. The wheels are rigidly fixed to the shaft. Example: The rear axle of the same Maruti Swift (drives the rear wheels).

| Parameter | Dead Axle | Live Axle |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rotation | Stationary | Rotates with the wheels |
| Torque | Zero | Transmits engine torque |
| Loading | Bending only | Bending + Torsion |
| Example Location | Front axle of RWD car | Rear axle of any driven vehicle |

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is a full-floating axle preferred over a semi-floating axle in heavy commercial vehicles?**

**Model Answer:**
A full-floating axle is preferred in heavy commercial vehicles (trucks, buses, tractors) for three critical reasons:

1. **Safety:** In a full-floating axle, the axle shaft transmits *only* torque. The vertical load is taken entirely by the axle housing. Hence, even if the shaft breaks in operation, the wheel remains firmly supported by the housing — the vehicle can be safely driven to a workshop.

2. **Load Capacity:** Because the housing carries the entire weight, the axle shaft is relieved of bending stress and is designed purely for torsion. This allows the shaft to be much smaller, lighter, and yet transmit high torque.

3. **Maintenance:** Wheel bearings, brake assemblies, and the shaft can be inspected and replaced independently without disturbing the rest of the axle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write only "safer" and lose 1 mark. You must explain **mechanically why** it is safer — the housing carries the load even after shaft failure. Always quantify if possible.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, every Part B question carries an **internal choice**. You are given **Question A (14 marks)** and **Question B (14 marks)** — answer any one.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **full-floating rear axle**. List its main components. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A semi-floating rear axle of a light truck has a wheel load of $W = 18 \text{ kN}$ and transmits a torque of $T = 1200 \text{ N·m}$ at each wheel. The distance between the wheel centre and the differential centre is $L = 0.5 \text{ m}$. Determine the required diameter of the axle shaft if the permissible bending stress is **60 MPa**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a) Full-Floating Rear Axle

**Construction:**
A full-floating rear axle consists of the following main components:

1. **Axle Housing:** A large-diameter tubular or banjo-type cast-steel housing that supports the entire vehicle weight.
2. **Axle Shaft:** A solid (or tubular) steel shaft that runs *inside* the housing. It transmits engine torque from the differential to the wheel hub.
3. **Differential Unit:** Mounted at the centre of the axle housing; splits torque between the two wheels.
4. **Wheel Hub:** Mounted on the *outside* of the axle housing on two large tapered roller bearings.
5. **Wheel Bearings:** Two large tapered roller bearings per side that allow the hub to rotate while the shaft transmits torque.
6. **Drive Flange:** A bolted flange at the outer end of the axle shaft that is bolted to the wheel hub, transferring torque to the hub.
7. **Brake Drum / Disc:** Mounted on the wheel hub.
8. **Stub Axle / Spindle:** The stationary stub on which the hub bearings are mounted; part of the housing itself.

**Working:**
Engine torque flows: Engine → Gearbox → Propeller Shaft → Differential Crown Wheel & Pinion → **Axle Shaft** → **Drive Flange** → **Wheel Hub** → **Road Wheel**.

The vertical load from the vehicle flows: Vehicle Body → Leaf Springs → Axle Housing → Wheel Bearings → Wheel Hub → Tyre → Road. **The axle shaft carries NO vertical load.**

#### Model Solution — (b) Semi-Floating Axle Design

**Given:**
$W = 18 \text{ kN} = 18000 \text{ N}$
$T = 1200 \text{ N·m}$
$L = 0.5 \text{ m}$
$\sigma_{allow} = 60 \text{ MPa} = 60 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1: Bending Moment on the Shaft**
The shaft acts as a cantilever fixed at the differential end:

$$
M = W \cdot L = 18000 \cdot 0.5 = 9000 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2: Equivalent Bending Moment**
Combined loading (bending + torsion):

$$
M_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \sqrt{9000^{2} + 1200^{2}}
$$

$$
M_{e} = \sqrt{8.1 \times 10^{7} + 1.44 \times 10^{6}} = \sqrt{8.244 \times 10^{7}}
$$

$$
M_{e} = 9079.6 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 3: Required Shaft Diameter**
Using the bending stress formula with the equivalent moment:

$$
\sigma_{allow} = \frac{32 \cdot M_{e}}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}
$$

Solving for $d$:

$$
d^{3} = \frac{32 \cdot M_{e}}{\pi \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{32 \cdot 9079.6}{\pi \cdot 60 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
d^{3} = \frac{290547.2}{1.885 \times 10^{8}} = 1.5413 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

$$
d = (1.5413 \times 10^{-3})^{1/3} = 0.1154 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d \approx 115.4 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4: Verification (Torsional Shear Stress Check)**
For a complete design check, the actual maximum shear stress at the surface should be computed:

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16}{\pi \cdot d^{3}} \cdot \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}} = \frac{16 \cdot M_{e}}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \cdot 9079.6}{\pi \cdot (0.1154)^{3}} = \frac{145273.6}{4.829 \times 10^{-3}} = 30.08 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\tau_{max} = 30.08 \text{ MPa} \quad (\text{less than } 0.5 \cdot \sigma_{allow} = 30 \text{ MPa, within safe limits})
$$

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify the different types of **live axles** used in automobiles. Compare the load-carrying responsibilities of the axle shaft and axle housing in each type with neat sketches. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A solid steel dead front axle (I-beam cross-section) of a truck has a total front-axle load of $W_{total} = 40 \text{ kN}$ and a wheel-centre distance of $L = 1.8 \text{ m}$. The I-beam has overall height $H = 100 \text{ mm}$, flange width $B = 80 \text{ mm}$, web thickness $t = 12 \text{ mm}$, and flange thickness $f = 15 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the maximum bending stress in the axle. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a) Classification of Live Axles

(See Section 2.2 of these notes for the full classification table — students should reproduce it neatly in the exam with a comparison table covering **Semi-Floating**, **Three-Quarter Floating**, and **Full-Floating** types. Each type must include: (i) load distribution sketch, (ii) load-sharing between shaft and housing, (iii) typical vehicle application, (iv) safety feature.)

#### Model Solution — (b) Dead Front Axle I-Beam Stress

**Given:**
$W_{total} = 40 \text{ kN}$, so $W = 20 \text{ kN} = 20000 \text{ N}$ per wheel
$L = 1.8 \text{ m}$
$H = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
$B = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
$t = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$
$f = 15 \text{ mm} = 0.015 \text{ m}$

**Step 1: Maximum Bending Moment**

$$
M = \frac{W \cdot L}{4} = \frac{20000 \cdot 1.8}{4} = 9000 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 2: Section Modulus of the I-Beam**
First, find the cut-out dimensions:
$b = \dfrac{B - t}{2} = \dfrac{0.08 - 0.012}{2} = 0.034 \text{ m}$
$h = \dfrac{H - 2f}{2} = \dfrac{0.1 - 0.03}{2} = 0.035 \text{ m}$

Section modulus of outer rectangle:

$$
Z_{outer} = \frac{B \cdot H^{2}}{6} = \frac{0.08 \cdot (0.1)^{2}}{6} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{6} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Section modulus of the two cut-outs (combined):

$$
Z_{cutout} = 2 \cdot \frac{b \cdot h^{2}}{6} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.034 \cdot (0.035)^{2}}{6} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.034 \cdot 1.225 \times 10^{-3}}{6}
$$

$$
Z_{cutout} = \frac{8.33 \times 10^{-5}}{6} = 1.388 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Net section modulus:

$$
Z = Z_{outer} - Z_{cutout} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} - 1.388 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
Z = 1.194 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum Bending Stress**

$$
\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{9000}{1.194 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{b,max} = 75.4 \text{ MPa}}
$$

This is well within the allowable bending stress of forged steel axles (300–450 MPa), confirming a safe design.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistake:** Forgetting to convert mm to m. If you leave $H$ in mm, the section modulus comes out in $10^{-9}$ instead of $10^{-4}$ — a $10^{5}$ error. Always check units first.
> 2. **Wrong Beam Model:** Treating the dead front axle as a *cantilever* (fixed at the centre, free at the wheel) instead of a *simply supported* beam with a central load. The correct model is the latter. KTU valuators deduct 2 full marks for this.
> 3. **Forgetting the Factor of 2:** Forgetting to divide $W_{total}$ by 2. The load is on the axle (which spans the two wheels), and each wheel contributes half.
> 4. **Elliot vs. Reverse Elliot Confusion:** In sketches, students often mix up the direction of the king-pin taper. Memorise: **Elliot** = king-pin tapers *towards the beam centre*; **Reverse Elliot** = king-pin tapers *away* from the beam centre.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section for last-minute revision before the exam.**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Dead Axle** = stationary, supports weight only, **bending stress only**, no torque.
- [ ] **Live Axle** = rotates, supports weight **and** transmits torque, **bending + torsional stress**.

### Dead Axle Front Axle Types (Memorise All Four)
- [ ] **Elliot** — Yoke at end of beam, king-pin held by wedge.
- [ ] **Reverse Elliot** — Yoke inverted; king-pin below beam.
- [ ] **Lamoine** — Inverted spindle; gives camber-in-steering.
- [ ] **Reverse Lamoine** — Lowers centre of gravity.

### Live Axle Types (Increasing Safety & Load Capacity)
- [ ] **Semi-Floating** — Hub on shaft, shaft carries full load + torque.
- [ ] **Three-Quarter Floating** — Hub on bearings, small share of load on housing.
- [ ] **Full-Floating** — Hub on housing, shaft carries torque only. **Standard for heavy vehicles.**

### Critical Formulas (Memorise These Five)
- [ ] $M = \dfrac{W \cdot L}{4}$ (central load, simply supported beam)
- [ ] $Z = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{3}}{32}$ (solid circular shaft)
- [ ] $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ (bending stress)
- [ ] $\tau = \dfrac{16 \cdot T}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}$ (torsional shear stress)
- [ ] $M_{e} = \sqrt{M^{2} + T^{2}}$ (equivalent bending moment for combined loading)

### High-Yield Traps in KTU Exams
- [ ] For a **full-floating** axle, the shaft has **NO bending moment** — design by torsion alone.
- [ ] For a **dead axle**, there is **NO torque** — design by bending alone.
- [ ] A **semi-floating** shaft needs the **equivalent moment** $M_e$.
- [ ] Always convert mm → m in section modulus calculations.
- [ ] Always divide $W_{total}$ by 2 to get the load per wheel.

### Standard Allowable Stresses (For Quick Reference)
- [ ] Forged carbon steel: $\sigma_{allow} \approx 60$–$80 \text{ MPa}$ in bending.
- [ ] Alloy steel axles (heat-treated): $\sigma_{allow} \approx 150$–$200 \text{ MPa}$.

### Materials Used
- [ ] **Dead Axle:** Medium carbon steel (AISI 1040 / 1050), drop-forged and heat-treated.
- [ ] **Live Axle Shaft:** AISI 4140 / 4340 alloy steel, induction-hardened at bearing seats.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — Module 3, Dead and Live Axles. Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE examination!** 🌟
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rims and Tyres
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rims and Tyres

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of a Wheel Rim

A **Wheel Rim** is the outer circular metallic (or composite) flange of a vehicle wheel that provides the mounting surface onto which the pneumatic tyre is seated and inflated. The rim is the structural backbone of the wheel assembly, transferring the entire vertical load of the vehicle from the axle hub to the tyre, while also transmitting braking torque, driving torque, and lateral cornering forces to the road surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A rim is defined by IS / SAE standards as the "part of the wheel on which the tyre is mounted and supported." It is the load-bearing interface between the tyre bead and the axle hub.

### 1.2 Definition of a Tyre

A **Pneumatic Tyre** is a toroidal-shaped, flexible, and elastic rubber composite structure mounted on the rim that, when inflated with compressed air, supports the vehicle load, provides traction, absorbs road shocks, and transmits driving, braking, and steering forces to the road surface.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The "Shoe and Foot" Model:**
> Think of the **rim as the skeletal foot of a human** and the **tyre as the cushioned running shoe** wrapped around it. The foot (rim) gives rigid structural shape and bears the body's weight, while the shoe (tyre) provides grip on the ground, cushions every step (shock absorption), and lets the runner change direction (steering). Just as wearing the wrong shoe ruins a marathon runner, choosing the wrong rim-tyre combination ruins vehicle handling, fuel economy, and safety.

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics

- **Tyre Air Pressure Range:** Typically **30 psi (2.1 bar)** for passenger cars; **100–120 psi** for commercial trucks.
- **Standard Rim Diameter Codes:** 13", 14", 15", 16", 17", 18", 19", 20" inches.
- **Material Standard:** **IS 2062 (Steel)** for pressed steel rims; **A356-T6 Cast Aluminium Alloy** for alloy rims.
- **Ply Rating (PR):** 4 PR, 6 PR, 8 PR, 10 PR, 12 PR (light to heavy commercial).

### 1.5 Functions of the Rim-Tyre System

1. **Load Support** — Sustain static and dynamic vehicle weight.
2. **Traction Generation** — Convert engine torque into forward motion via road friction.
3. **Shock Absorption** — Dampen road irregularities using air-spring effect.
4. **Steering & Cornering** — Transmit lateral forces during turns.
5. **Braking Force Transfer** — Deliver friction torque to the road.
6. **Heat Dissipation** — Radiate heat generated during rolling and braking.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Wheel Rim

The rim is not a single piece of metal. It is a precision-engineered component with the following geometric features:

- **Rim Well (Drop Centre):** The central depression that allows easy mounting/demounting of the tyre bead.
- **Rim Flange:** The outer raised edge (J-shape) that retains the tyre bead axially.
- **Rim Seat:** The conical surface where the tyre bead seats and seals.
- **Bead Seat Angle:** Typically **5°** (for passenger cars) or **15°** (for trucks).
- **Hub Mounting Surface:** The central pad with lug holes and centre bore.

### 2.2 Types of Rims (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| Sl. No. | Type of Rim | Description | Application |
|:-------:|:------------|:------------|:------------|
| 1 | **Pressed Steel Disc Wheel** | Single-piece deep-drawn steel with integral disc | Passenger cars, LCVs |
| 2 | **Spoke Wheel** | Hub + detachable rim connected by wire/cast spokes | Vintage cars, racing cars |
| 3 | **Split Rim (Two-Piece)** | Removable side ring bolted to base rim | Commercial vehicles, off-road |
| 4 | **Alloy Rim (Cast/Forged)** | Aluminium/Magnesium alloy, lightweight | Premium cars, performance vehicles |
| 5 | **Tubeless Safety Rim (Hump Type)** | Special ribs (H, FH, AH) on bead seat to retain bead on deflation | All tubeless-tyre vehicles |
| 6 | **Drop Centre Rim** | Symmetrical well at the centre to fit the lower bead first | Most modern cars |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite:** The **Hump (H)** in a safety rim is a small rib projecting from the bead seat. On sudden deflation, the bead cannot slip into the drop centre — preventing the tyre from coming off the rim. **H = Hump on one side; DH = Double Hump; FH = Flat Hump; SH = Symmetric Hump.**

### 2.3 Anatomy of a Tyre (Cross-Section Walk-through)

Going from outside to inside:

1. **Tread** — Outer rubber layer with grooves/sipes; contacts the road.
2. **Tread Cap (Under-Tread)** — Thinner layer between tread and belt.
3. **Belt Package (Radial Tyres Only)** — Steel belts + nylon caps for stability.
4. **Carcass (Ply)** — Layer of rubber-coated fabric cords (rayon/nylon/polyester/steel).
5. **Inner Liner** — Halobutyl rubber layer; air-retention membrane (tubeless).
6. **Bead** — Ring of high-tensile steel wire that anchors the tyre to the rim.
7. **Bead Filler (Apex)** — Rubber triangle above the bead for stiffness.
8. **Sidewall** — Protects the carcass; carries size/brand markings.
9. **Chafer** — Protects carcass from rim friction.
10. **Cap Ply (JLB)** — Nylon overlay belt for high-speed stability.

### 2.4 Tyre Construction Types (Critical for KTU)

#### A. Diagonal (Bias-Ply) Tyre

Cords run diagonally from bead to bead at **30°–45°** angles, criss-crossing each other.

$$\theta_{\text{bias}} = 30^{\circ} \text{ to } 45^{\circ}$$

**Characteristics:** Stiffer sidewall, more heat build-up, lower speed rating, longer life, cheaper, better puncture resistance.

#### B. Radial-Ply Tyre

Carcass cords run **radially (90°)** from bead to bead; stabilised by **circumferential steel belts** at the crown.

$$\theta_{\text{radial}} = 90^{\circ}$$

**Characteristics:** Softer sidewall, larger contact patch, lower rolling resistance, better fuel economy, better heat dissipation, higher speed rating, shorter life in some applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "B-RAD":** **B**ias = cords **B**etween beads at angle; **RAD**ial = cords **RAD**ial (90°), with belts.

#### C. Tubeless Tyre

Has an airtight **inner liner** of halobutyl rubber; no separate inner tube. The tyre-rim interface acts as the seal.

#### D. Tubed Tyre

Uses a separate **butyl rubber inner tube** inflated inside the tyre casing. Common in old vehicles, two-wheelers, and commercial trucks.

### 2.5 Tyre Designation (Reading a Tyre Sidewall)

**Example: 205/55 R 16 91 V**

| Code | Meaning |
|:----:|:--------|
| **205** | Section width in **mm** |
| **55** | Aspect ratio (sidewall height as % of width) |
| **R** | Radial construction |
| **16** | Rim diameter in **inches** |
| **91** | Load index (max load = 615 kg per tyre) |
| **V** | Speed symbol (max speed = 240 km/h) |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|:-------:|:---------|:--------|:------|:------|
| 1 | Aspect Ratio (AR) | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{H}{W} \times 100$ | % | H = sidewall height, W = section width |
| 2 | Sidewall Height (H) | $H = \dfrac{\text{AR} \times W}{100}$ | mm | |
| 3 | Overall Tyre Diameter (D) | $D = 2H + d_{\text{rim}}$ | mm | $d_{\text{rim}}$ in mm (× 25.4) |
| 4 | Rolling Radius ($R_r$) | $R_r = 0.5 \times D \times \text{Deflection Factor}$ | mm | Typically 0.96–0.98 of static radius |
| 5 | Tyre Revolutions per km | $N = \dfrac{1000}{\pi D}$ | rev/km | $D$ in metres |
| 6 | Vehicle Speed (v) | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | N in rpm |
| 7 | Load Index Reference | Table lookup | kg | Standard ETRTO/IS tables |
| 8 | Ply Rating (PR) Equivalent | PR × 50 kPa ≈ load capability | kPa | Approximate rule-of-thumb |
| 9 | Rolling Resistance (F_r) | $F_r = C_r \cdot W$ | N | $C_r$ = coefficient (0.008–0.015) |
| 10 | Air Spring Rate | $k = (n+1) \cdot P_a \cdot A$ | N/m | $n$ = polytropic index (1.4), $P_a$ = gauge+atm, A = contact area |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, note the use of `\vert` is avoided where possible; vertical bar separators are kept strictly outside the math expression to prevent markdown table breakage.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Alloy rims** reduce **unsprung mass** by 30–40 %, improving ride, handling, and braking response.
- **Tubeless tyres** lose pressure slowly after puncture (safer) and run cooler (longer life).
- **Low-AR tyres (e.g., 205/55 R16)** improve cornering grip; **high-AR (e.g., 185/70 R14)** improves ride comfort.
- **Run-Flat Tyres (RFT)** use reinforced sidewalls to allow 80 km at 80 km/h even after deflation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Tyre Overall Diameter from Designation

**Problem Statement:** A passenger car tyre is marked **205/65 R 15**. Compute (i) Section Width, (ii) Sidewall Height, (iii) Overall Tyre Diameter, and (iv) Rolling Radius (assume deflection factor = 0.965).

#### Given Data

- Section Width, $W = 205 \text{ mm}$
- Aspect Ratio, $\text{AR} = 65$
- Rim Diameter, $d_{\text{rim}} = 15 \text{ inches} = 15 \times 25.4 \text{ mm} = 381 \text{ mm}$
- Deflection Factor, $f_d = 0.965$

#### Step 1: Section Width

$$W = 205 \text{ mm}$$

(Read directly from designation.)

#### Step 2: Sidewall Height (H)

Using the definition of aspect ratio:

$$\text{AR} = \frac{H}{W} \times 100$$

Rearranging for H:

$$H = \frac{\text{AR} \times W}{100}$$

Substituting the values:

$$H = \frac{65 \times 205}{100}$$

$$H = \frac{13325}{100}$$

$$H = 133.25 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 3: Overall Tyre Diameter (D)

The overall diameter spans the **two sidewalls** plus the **rim diameter**:

$$D = 2H + d_{\text{rim}}$$

Substituting the values:

$$D = 2 \times 133.25 + 381$$

$$D = 266.5 + 381$$

$$D = 647.5 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 4: Rolling Radius ($R_r$)

The rolling radius is smaller than the static radius because the tyre deflects under load. We apply the deflection factor:

$$R_r = f_d \times \frac{D}{2}$$

$$R_r = 0.965 \times \frac{647.5}{2}$$

$$R_r = 0.965 \times 323.75$$

$$R_r = 312.42 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 5: Tyre Revolutions per km

Using the standard kinematic relationship (1 km = 1000 m; circumference = $\pi D$):

$$N = \frac{1000}{\pi \times D}$$

Converting D to metres: $D = 0.6475 \text{ m}$

$$N = \frac{1000}{\pi \times 0.6475}$$

$$N = \frac{1000}{2.0341}$$

$$N = 491.62 \text{ revolutions per km}$$

#### Step 6: Vehicle Speed at N rpm

If the wheel rotates at 800 rpm, the vehicle speed is:

$$v = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{60 \times 1000} \text{ (km/s multiply by 60) — here in m/s:}$$

$$v = \frac{\pi \times 0.6475 \times 800}{60}$$

$$v = \frac{1627.05}{60}$$

$$v = 27.12 \text{ m/s} = 97.64 \text{ km/h}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Air-Spring (Pneumatic) Stiffness of a Tyre

The tyre is essentially a flexible air chamber behaving as a pneumatic spring. The effective spring rate can be derived from the polytropic compression of the trapped air.

#### Starting Equation (Isothermal/Polytropic Process)

For a gas undergoing polytropic compression:

$$P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n}$$

where $n$ is the polytropic index ($n = 1$ for isothermal, $n = 1.4$ for adiabatic). For typical slow rolling, $n \approx 1.35$.

#### Change in Pressure for Small Deflection

Differentiating both sides:

$$n P V^{n-1} dV + V^{n} dP = 0$$

$$dP = -\frac{nP}{V} dV$$

#### Relating Volume Change to Vertical Deflection

If the contact patch area is $A$ and the vertical deflection is $x$:

$$dV = A \cdot dx$$

Therefore:

$$dP = -\frac{nPA}{V} dx$$

#### Effective Spring Force

Force on the contact patch due to pressure rise:

$$F = dP \times A = \frac{n P A^{2}}{V} x$$

Comparing with $F = kx$:

$$k = \frac{n P A^{2}}{V}$$

Since $V = A \cdot H_{eff}$ (effective air column height):

$$k = \frac{n P A}{H_{eff}}$$

For a typical passenger tyre: $P \approx 200 \text{ kPa}, A \approx 0.02 \text{ m}^2, H_{eff} \approx 0.15 \text{ m}, n = 1.4$:

$$k = \frac{1.4 \times 200{,}000 \times 0.02}{0.15}$$

$$k = \frac{5600}{0.15}$$

$$k \approx 37{,}333 \text{ N/m} \approx 37.3 \text{ kN/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This low spring rate (compared to a steel spring of kN/m order of hundreds) is precisely why tyres provide the "soft cushion" ride in vehicles.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bias vs Radial Comparison

| Parameter | Bias-Ply Tyre | Radial-Ply Tyre |
|:----------|:--------------|:----------------|
| Cord angle to bead centre line | 30°–45° | 90° (radial) |
| Number of plies (for same load) | More | Fewer |
| Sidewall flex | Stiffer | Softer (larger deflection) |
| Contact patch | Smaller, more rounded | Larger, flatter |
| Rolling resistance | Higher (12 % more) | Lower (5–8 % less) |
| Heat dissipation | Poor | Excellent |
| Speed capability | Low–Medium | High |
| Tyre life (km) | ~40,000 | ~50,000–80,000 |
| Puncture resistance | High | Lower (but tubeless safer) |
| Cost | Lower | Slightly higher |
| Steering response | Slower | Sharper |
| Application | Trucks (load), vintage, off-road | Passenger cars, modern trucks |

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate clearly between a "Diagonal (Bias-Ply) Tyre" and a "Radial-Ply Tyre" in terms of cord orientation, sidewall flexibility, and rolling resistance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Bias-Ply Tyre | Radial-Ply Tyre |
|:--------|:--------------|:----------------|
| Cord angle | 30°–45° to bead centre line | 90° (radial) |
| Sidewall flex | Stiff, less deflection | Soft, larger deflection |
| Rolling resistance | Higher (≈ 12 % more) | Lower (better fuel economy) |

Radial tyres also have steel-belted stabilisers at the crown, which bias tyres lack. Hence radials give a larger, flatter contact patch, better grip, and lower fuel consumption.

*(Distribution: 1 mark for cord angle, 1 mark for sidewall comparison, 1 mark for rolling resistance + supporting comment.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a "Hump" in a tubeless safety rim? Why is it provided?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **hump** is a small raised rib (typically 1.5–2 mm high) projected on the bead seat of a rim. Its purpose is to **prevent the tyre bead from slipping into the drop-centre well** during sudden deflation, hard cornering, or blow-out conditions. By physically blocking the bead, the hump ensures the tyre remains locked on the rim, preventing catastrophic dismounting. Common types are **H (single)**, **DH (double)**, **FH (flat)**, and **SH (symmetric)**.

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark function, 1 mark for types.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - When asked to "draw and explain" a tubeless tyre, students forget the **inner liner** — losing 2 marks.
> - In numericals, **do not forget to convert inches to mm** (× 25.4). Many students compute aspect ratio using inches — a guaranteed 2-mark cut.
> - Always state the **deflection factor assumption** when calculating rolling radius. Examiners allocate a separate 1 mark for this.
> - When asked "differentiate," use a **tabulated comparison** — half-page answers lose marks due to lack of structure.
> - For radial-tyre explanations, **mention the steel belt package** explicitly — it is the most-missed technical detail.

---

**PART B — Q1 (14 Marks). OPTION A**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, explain the **constructional features of a modern tubeless radial-ply tyre**. Label at least **eight** parts clearly. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A passenger car tyre is marked **195/65 R 15 91 H**. Calculate: (i) section width, (ii) sidewall height, (iii) overall diameter, (iv) rolling radius (assume deflection factor = 0.96), and (v) the vehicle speed in km/h if the wheel rotates at 900 rpm. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction of a Tubeless Radial Tyre (7 Marks):**

A tubeless radial-ply tyre consists of the following parts (sketch a cross-section showing 8 labelled parts):

1. **Tread** — The outer rubber layer with patterned grooves/sipes; contacts the road and provides traction, water evacuation, and wear resistance.
2. **Tread Cap (Under-Tread)** — A thinner rubber layer between the tread and the belt; cushions impacts and bonds the tread to the belt.
3. **Belt Package** — Two or more crossed steel-cord belts wrapped circumferentially; stabilise the tread, reduce wear, and improve handling.
4. **Carcass (Ply)** — A single (or two) layer of polyester/rayon cords running radially (90°) from bead to bead; carries the inflation pressure and load.
5. **Inner Liner** — A layer of halobutyl rubber lining the inside of the tyre; replaces the inner tube by providing air retention.
6. **Sidewall** — Protects the carcass from side impacts and carries size/brand markings; flexes during cornering.
7. **Bead** — A ring of high-tensile steel wire bundles coated with rubber; anchors the tyre firmly onto the rim flange.
8. **Bead Filler (Apex)** — A triangular rubber section above each bead; provides stiffness and smooth load transfer from bead to sidewall.
9. **Chafer** — Abrasive-resistant strip protecting the bead area from rim-friction damage.
10. **Cap Ply (Jointless Band - JLB)** — Nylon overlay belt for high-speed uniformity and stability.

The **radial orientation** of the carcass cords, combined with the **circumferential steel belts**, is the defining feature of a modern radial tyre.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Section Width, $W = 195 \text{ mm}$
- Aspect Ratio, $\text{AR} = 65$
- Rim Diameter, $d_{\text{rim}} = 15 \text{ in} = 15 \times 25.4 = 381 \text{ mm}$
- Deflection Factor, $f_d = 0.96$
- Wheel Speed, $N = 900 \text{ rpm}$

**Step (i): Section Width**
$$W = 195 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1 Mark]}$$

**Step (ii): Sidewall Height**
$$H = \frac{\text{AR} \times W}{100} = \frac{65 \times 195}{100} = 126.75 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1 Mark]}$$

**Step (iii): Overall Diameter**
$$D = 2H + d_{\text{rim}} = 2(126.75) + 381 = 253.5 + 381 = 634.5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1.5 Marks]}$$

**Step (iv): Rolling Radius**
$$R_r = f_d \times \frac{D}{2} = 0.96 \times \frac{634.5}{2} = 0.96 \times 317.25 = 304.56 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1.5 Marks]}$$

**Step (v): Vehicle Speed at 900 rpm**

Converting D to metres: $D = 0.6345 \text{ m}$

$$v = \frac{\pi \times D \times N}{60} \times 3.6 \text{ (to get km/h)}$$

$$v = \frac{\pi \times 0.6345 \times 900}{60} \times 3.6$$

$$v = \frac{1793.36}{60} \times 3.6$$

$$v = 29.889 \times 3.6$$

$$v = 107.6 \text{ km/h} \quad \text{[2 Marks]}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| Section Width (W) | 195 mm |
| Sidewall Height (H) | 126.75 mm |
| Overall Diameter (D) | 634.5 mm |
| Rolling Radius ($R_r$) | 304.56 mm |
| Vehicle Speed (v) | ≈ 107.6 km/h |

---

**PART B — Q1 (14 Marks). OPTION B**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Classify and explain the **different types of wheel rims** used in automobiles, with neat sketches. Highlight the role of the **hump** in tubeless safety rims. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A truck is fitted with **11.00 R 20 16 PR** tyres inflated to 700 kPa. The wheel rotates at 500 rpm on a level road. Calculate: (i) overall diameter, (ii) rolling radius (assume $f_d$ = 0.97), (iii) vehicle speed in km/h, and (iv) number of tyre revolutions per km. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Wheel Rims (7 Marks):**

1. **Pressed Steel Disc Wheel (2 Marks):**
   - One-piece deep-drawn steel disc; common in passenger cars and LCVs.
   - Combines rim and disc in a single welded or seamless unit.
   - Cheapest, most reliable; heavier than alloy.

2. **Spoke Wheel (1 Mark):**
   - Detachable rim laced to a hub by wire/cast spokes.
   - Used in vintage cars, racing cars (wire-spoked for unsprung-mass reduction).
   - Allows easy tyre changing on roadside.

3. **Split Rim (Two-Piece) (1 Mark):**
   - Base rim + bolted side ring or lock ring.
   - Common in commercial vehicles, off-road trucks (military).
   - Easy demounting; side ring prevents bead kick-off.

4. **Alloy Rim (1 Mark):**
   - Cast or forged aluminium (A356-T6) or magnesium alloy.
   - Lightweight, aesthetic, better heat dissipation.
   - Used in premium / sports cars.

5. **Tubeless Safety Rim (Hump Type) (2 Marks):**
   - Features small ribs (H, DH, FH, SH, AH) on the bead seat.
   - **Role of the Hump:** On sudden deflation, the bead cannot slip into the drop centre. The hump physically locks the bead, preventing the tyre from dismounting and causing loss of vehicle control. **DH (Double Hump)** is the most common in modern tubeless car rims.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given (inferred from designation "11.00 R 20"):**
- Section Width, $W = 11.00 \text{ in} = 11.00 \times 25.4 = 279.4 \text{ mm}$
- Aspect Ratio (truck convention — high AR): $\text{AR} \approx 85$ (truck standard interpretation)
- Rim Diameter, $d_{\text{rim}} = 20 \text{ in} = 20 \times 25.4 = 508 \text{ mm}$
- $f_d = 0.97$
- $N = 500 \text{ rpm}$

> **Note:** In **truck designation "11.00 R 20"**, the convention differs: the first number is **section width in inches**, and the **aspect ratio is implicit (~85 %)** for radial truck tyres. The rim diameter is in inches.

**Step (i): Sidewall Height**
$$H = \frac{85 \times 279.4}{100} = 237.49 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1 Mark]}$$

**Step (ii): Overall Diameter**
$$D = 2H + d_{\text{rim}} = 2(237.49) + 508 = 474.98 + 508 = 982.98 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1.5 Marks]}$$

**Step (iii): Rolling Radius**
$$R_r = 0.97 \times \frac{982.98}{2} = 0.97 \times 491.49 = 476.74 \text{ mm} \quad \text{[1.5 Marks]}$$

**Step (iv): Vehicle Speed**
$D = 0.98298 \text{ m}$

$$v = \frac{\pi \times 0.98298 \times 500}{60} \times 3.6$$

$$v = \frac{1543.81}{60} \times 3.6$$

$$v = 25.73 \times 3.6 = 92.6 \text{ km/h} \quad \text{[1.5 Marks]}$$

**Step (v): Revolutions per km**
$$N_{\text{per km}} = \frac{1000}{\pi \times 0.98298} = \frac{1000}{3.0878} = 323.86 \text{ rev/km} \quad \text{[1.5 Marks]}$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:------|
| Sidewall Height (H) | 237.49 mm |
| Overall Diameter (D) | 982.98 mm |
| Rolling Radius ($R_r$) | 476.74 mm |
| Vehicle Speed (v) | 92.6 km/h |
| Revolutions per km | 323.86 rev/km |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Must Memorise for KTU Board:**

- **Rim** = structural backbone of the wheel; **Tyre** = flexible air-filled cushion mounted on rim.
- Rim types: **Pressed Steel Disc, Spoke, Split, Alloy, Tubeless Safety (Hump)**.
- **Hump (H, DH, FH, SH, AH)** = safety rib on tubeless rim bead seat; prevents tyre dismount on deflation.
- **Bead seat angle:** 5° (cars) or 15° (trucks).
- **Drop centre (rim well)** = central depression for easy tyre mounting.
- Tyre parts: **Tread, Tread Cap, Belt Package, Carcass, Inner Liner, Sidewall, Bead, Apex, Chafer, Cap Ply (JLB)**.
- **Bias tyre:** cords at **30°–45°**; **Radial tyre:** cords at **90°** + steel belts at crown.
- Radial = softer sidewall, larger contact patch, **lower rolling resistance, better fuel economy, higher speed**.
- Bias = stiffer, more heat, longer life, cheaper, better puncture resistance.
- **Tubeless** uses **halobutyl inner liner**; **Tubed** uses separate inner tube.
- **Tyre designation** e.g. **205/55 R 16 91 V**:
   - 205 = width in mm; 55 = aspect ratio; R = radial; 16 = rim in inches; 91 = load index; V = speed symbol (240 km/h).
- **Aspect Ratio** $\text{AR} = H/W \times 100$.
- **Overall diameter** $D = 2H + d_{\text{rim}}$.
- **Rolling radius** $R_r = f_d \times D/2$ (use $f_d = 0.96$–$0.98$).
- **Revs per km** $N = 1000/(\pi D)$ (D in metres).
- **Speed** $v = (\pi D N / 60) \times 3.6$ km/h.
- **Air spring stiffness** $k = nPA/H_{eff}$; ~30–40 kN/m for car tyres.
- **Alloy rims** reduce unsprung mass by 30–40 %.
- **Run-Flat Tyres (RFT)** use reinforced sidewalls → 80 km @ 80 km/h after deflation.
- **Always convert rim inches → mm** by × 25.4 in KTU numericals.
- **Ply Rating (PR):** rating of load capacity; 4 PR = light, 14 PR = heavy truck.
- **Standard materials:** IS 2062 steel; A356-T6 aluminium alloy.
- **Speed symbols** to remember: S=180, T=190, H=210, V=240, W=270, Y=300 km/h.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brakes - Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Brakes - Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **brake** in an automobile is an energy-conversion device whose primary function is to convert the **kinetic energy** of a moving vehicle into **heat energy** through controlled frictional resistance, thereby reducing the vehicle's speed or bringing it to a complete halt. The braking system must perform this energy dissipation reliably under all operating conditions — from light deceleration in city traffic to emergency stops at highway speeds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *An automobile brake is a mechanism that applies a controlled frictional force to the rotating wheels or a rotating member connected to the wheel, to produce a retarding torque opposing the motion of the vehicle, ensuring safety, stability, and stopping performance.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of brakes as the **"controlled friction paddles"** of a vehicle. Imagine you are pushing a heavy shopping cart on a flat supermarket floor — to stop it, you press your foot against the wheel. The harder you press, the faster the cart stops. In a car, this is exactly what happens, but instead of your foot, we use engineered friction surfaces (pads/shoes) pressed against a rotating disc or drum by a **hydraulic, mechanical, pneumatic, or electric** actuator.

A more relatable analogy is the **"squeezing a spinning plate"**:
- The **drum brake** is like a hollow cylinder with brake shoes pushing outward against the inner surface.
- The **disc brake** is like a flat plate with brake pads pinching from both sides — like fingers grabbing a spinning disc.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of an Automobile Brake System

| Function | Engineering Objective |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Decelerate the vehicle** | Controlled reduction in speed |
| **Stop the vehicle** | Bring to rest within shortest safe distance |
| **Hold the vehicle stationary** | Parking function on inclines |
| **Maintain directional stability** | No skidding during braking |
| **Heat dissipation** | Prevent thermal fade |

> [!NOTE]
> A well-designed brake must satisfy: **Effectiveness, Fade resistance, Friction stability, Resistance to water/fade, and Compatibility with ABS (Anti-lock Braking System)**.

## 1.4 Classification of Brakes (Comprehensive Overview)

Automobile brakes are classified based on the **mode of operation**, the **type of braking element**, and the **application domain**.

### A. Classification by Actuation Mechanism (Energy Source)
1. **Mechanical Brakes** — Lever/cable/rod linkage (e.g., parking brake)
2. **Hydraulic Brakes** — Fluid pressure actuation (most common in passenger cars)
3. **Pneumatic (Air) Brakes** — Compressed air actuation (trucks, buses)
4. **Vacuum-Assisted Hydraulic Brakes** — Vacuum-boosted hydraulics
5. **Electric Brakes** — Electromagnetic force (EV regenerative + friction)
6. **Hydraulic-Pneumatic (Air-over-Hydraulic) Brakes** — Hybrid systems

### B. Classification by Friction Element Geometry
1. **Drum Brakes** — Internal expanding shoes / External contracting band
2. **Disc Brakes** — Pads clamping a disc rotor
3. **Band Brakes** — Flexible band around a drum
4. **Cone Brakes** — Conical friction surfaces (rare in modern cars)

### C. Classification by Application
1. **Service Brakes** — Primary, foot-operated
2. **Parking Brakes** — Hand-operated, mechanical
3. **Emergency Brakes** — Fail-safe backup
4. **Auxiliary/Retarder Brakes** — Engine exhaust brake, electric retarder

## 1.5 Boundary Conditions and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Brake Performance Metrics (Bold for emphasis)**
> - **Stopping distance** — typically **< 50 m from 100 km/h** (legal limit in many countries)
> - **Brake efficiency (η)** — ideally **> 75 %** for passenger cars
> - **Coefficient of friction (μ)** — pad-to-rotor: **0.30 to 0.50**
> - **Pedal effort** — typically **< 70 N** for light vehicles
> - **Pedal travel** — typically **< 100 mm** for full application
> - **Fade temperature** — must withstand **> 400 °C** without failure
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How" of Braking

### Step 1: Driver Input
The driver depresses the brake pedal, generating an **input force (F_p)** over a **pedal travel (s_p)**. This mechanical work input is the trigger.

### Step 2: Force Amplification
Through a **mechanical advantage** (lever ratio, hydraulic multiplication via Master Cylinder, or pneumatic pressure), the small pedal force is amplified into a large clamping force at the wheel.

$$\boxed{F_{\text{clamp}} = F_p \times A_{\text{ratio}} \times \eta_{\text{system}}}$$

Where $A_{\text{ratio}}$ is the amplification factor (mechanical × hydraulic) and $\eta_{\text{system}}$ is the system's mechanical efficiency.

### Step 3: Friction Engagement
The amplified force pushes friction material (pad/shoe) against the rotating member (disc/drum). The resulting **frictional force (F_f = μN)** opposes wheel rotation.

### Step 4: Energy Conversion
The vehicle's **kinetic energy** is progressively converted to **heat** at the friction interface:

$$E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

This energy must be absorbed and dissipated by the friction surface. Failure to dissipate leads to **brake fade**.

### Step 5: Wheel Lockup Control
The braking force must not exceed the **frictional limit between tire and road**, otherwise wheels lock and the vehicle skids. This is exactly the condition monitored by **ABS (Anti-lock Braking System)**:

$$F_{\text{brake,max}} = \mu_{\text{tire-road}} \times W_{\text{wheel}}$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $E = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | Kinetic energy of vehicle | Joules (J) | Energy to be dissipated |
| 2 | $F_f = \mu N$ | Friction force at brake | N | Frictional force generation |
| 3 | $T_b = F_f \times r$ | Braking torque | N·m | Torque at wheel |
| 4 | $F_b = \frac{T_b}{r_w}$ | Equivalent brake force at road | N | Force at tire contact |
| 5 | $\eta_b = \frac{F_b}{W} \times 100$ | Brake efficiency | % | Braking performance |
| 6 | $s = \frac{v^2}{2\mu g}$ | Theoretical stopping distance | m | Performance estimate |
| 7 | $F_f = \frac{W \cdot \mu \cdot L_r}{L}$ | Front-wheel brake force | N | Ideal brake distribution (front) |
| 8 | $F_r = \frac{W \cdot \mu \cdot L_f}{L}$ | Rear-wheel brake force | N | Ideal brake distribution (rear) |
| 9 | $P_b = \frac{mv^2}{2 \cdot t_s}$ | Power to be absorbed | W (J/s) | Brake thermal sizing |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \frac{Q}{m \cdot c_p}$ | Brake temperature rise | °C / K | Fade analysis |
| 11 | $P_h = p \cdot A_m$ | Hydraulic pressure generation | Pa × m² = N | Master cylinder analysis |
| 12 | $\text{SF} = \frac{(\mu \cdot b - a)}{(\mu \cdot b + a)}$ | Self-energizing factor | dimensionless | Drum brake analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Conventions**
> $m$ = vehicle mass, $v$ = velocity, $\mu$ = friction coefficient, $N$ = normal force, $r$ = effective brake radius, $W$ = weight on wheel, $L$ = wheelbase, $L_f, L_r$ = CG distance from front/rear axle, $t_s$ = stopping time, $c_p$ = specific heat of brake material, $b, a$ = brake geometry parameters, $A_m$ = master cylinder area, $p$ = hydraulic pressure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Passenger Cars** — Hydraulic disc brakes (front) + drum/disc (rear) with **ABS** and **EBD (Electronic Brake-force Distribution)**.
- **Heavy Commercial Vehicles (HCVs)** — Pneumatic (air) brakes with dual-circuit fail-safe design.
- **Racing Cars** — Carbon-ceramic discs, multi-piston calipers, brake cooling ducts.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** — **Regenerative braking** (motor acts as generator) + hydraulic friction brakes.
- **Motorcycles** — Disc brakes with CBS (Combined Braking System) and ABS.

## 2.4 Why Brakes Fail — Engineering Concerns

| Failure Mode | Cause | Remedy |
|--------------|-------|--------|
| **Brake Fade** | Excessive heat reduces μ | Ventilated discs, high-temp pads |
| **Water Fade** | Wet friction surface | Cross-drilled rotors, squeegee action |
| **Judder/Vibration** | Disc thickness variation (DTV) | Resurfacing/replacement |
| **Pull to one side** | Uneven friction, caliper seizure | Caliper overhaul, pad replacement |
| **Spongy pedal** | Air in hydraulic system | Bleeding the brake lines |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ideal Brake-Force Distribution Between Front and Rear Wheels

### Step 1 — Define the Problem
When a vehicle of weight **W** (acting at the center of gravity, CG) decelerates at rate **a**, an inertial force **ma** acts forward at the CG. This causes **dynamic weight transfer** from the rear to the front axle.

### Step 2 — Free-Body Diagram Analysis
Let the vehicle have:
- Total weight: $W = m \cdot g$
- Wheelbase: $L$
- Horizontal distance of CG from front axle: $L_f$
- Horizontal distance of CG from rear axle: $L_r = L - L_f$
- Deceleration: $a$
- Weight on front axle (static + dynamic): $W_f$
- Weight on rear axle: $W_r$

### Step 3 — Equilibrium of Vertical Forces
Vertical force balance:

$$
W_f + W_r = W
$$

### Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium About Rear Contact Point
Taking moments about the rear tire contact point (eliminating $W_r$ and the inertial force contribution at that point):

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_f \cdot L - W \cdot L_f &= m \cdot a \cdot h_{\text{cg}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $h_{\text{cg}}$ is the height of the center of gravity.

### Step 5 — Solve for $W_f$ and $W_r$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_f &= \frac{W \cdot L_f}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_{\text{cg}}}{L} \\
W_r &= \frac{W \cdot L_r}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_{\text{cg}}}{L}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** During braking (positive $a$), front axle load increases and rear axle load decreases.

### Step 6 — Optimum Brake Force Distribution
For the front and rear wheels to lock simultaneously (ideal anti-lock behavior), the brake forces must be proportional to axle loads:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{bf} &= \mu \cdot W_f = \mu \left( \frac{W \cdot L_f}{L} + \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_{\text{cg}}}{L} \right) \\
F_{br} &= \mu \cdot W_r = \mu \left( \frac{W \cdot L_r}{L} - \frac{m \cdot a \cdot h_{\text{cg}}}{L} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Deceleration in Terms of Brake Force
Total braking force = ma:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{bf} + F_{br} &= m \cdot a \\
\mu \cdot W &= m \cdot a \\
a &= \mu \cdot g
\end{aligned}
$$

The **maximum achievable deceleration** is $a_{\max} = \mu \cdot g$ (theoretically).

### Step 8 — Substitute and Simplify

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{F_{bf}}{F_{br}} &= \frac{L_f + \mu \cdot h_{\text{cg}}}{L_r - \mu \cdot h_{\text{cg}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **ideal brake-force distribution ratio**. Modern vehicles use **EBD (Electronic Brake-force Distribution)** to approximate this curve dynamically.

## 3.2 Derivation: Self-Energizing Action in Drum Brakes (Leading Shoe)

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Expanding Shoe
A drum brake has two shoes, each pivoted at one end and expanded outward by a wheel cylinder. The friction between shoe and drum creates a tangential force that either **assists** or **opposes** the wheel cylinder.

### Step 2 — Free-Body of the Shoe
Consider a single leading shoe with:
- Wheel-cylinder actuation force: $F$ (horizontal, expanding shoe outward)
- Normal reaction from drum on shoe: $N$
- Friction force: $F_f = \mu N$ (acts tangentially)
- Pivoted at point **A** (one end of shoe)

### Step 3 — Moment Equilibrium About the Pivot
Summing moments about pivot **A**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot c + F_f \cdot d &= N \cdot b
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $c$ = moment arm of wheel-cylinder force
- $d$ = moment arm of friction force
- $b$ = moment arm of normal reaction

### Step 4 — Solve for $N$ and $F_f$

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{F \cdot c + F_f \cdot d}{b} \\
F_f &= \mu N = \frac{\mu \cdot F \cdot c}{b - \mu \cdot d}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Define Self-Energizing Factor
The **self-energizing factor (SF)** is the ratio of the friction force to the applied actuation force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SF} &= \frac{F_f}{F} = \frac{\mu \cdot c}{b - \mu \cdot d}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Physical Interpretation
- If $\text{SF} > 1$: Shoe is **self-energizing** (leading shoe). Less pedal effort is needed.
- If $\text{SF} < 1$: Shoe is **non-self-energizing** (trailing shoe). More pedal effort is needed.
- If $b = \mu \cdot d$: **System becomes self-locking** (unstable).

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy and Power Dissipation During Braking

### Step 1 — Initial Kinetic Energy
A vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $v$ has kinetic energy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{kinetic}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Power Dissipated
If the vehicle is brought to rest in time $t_s$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b = \frac{E_{\text{kinetic}}}{t_s} = \frac{m v^2}{2 t_s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Heat Generation Per Brake
Assuming four brakes share the dissipation equally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{per brake}} = \frac{m v^2}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Temperature Rise of the Disc
The temperature rise $\Delta T$ of a disc/drum of mass $m_d$ and specific heat $c_p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T = \frac{Q_{\text{per brake}}}{m_d \cdot c_p} = \frac{m v^2}{8 \cdot m_d \cdot c_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A 1500 kg car braking from 100 km/h (= 27.78 m/s):
- $E_k = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 27.78^2 = 5.79 \times 10^5$ J
- Per brake: $\approx 1.45 \times 10^5$ J
- For a 5 kg cast-iron disc ($c_p = 460$ J/kg·K): $\Delta T \approx 63$ °C
- A continuous mountain descent: $\Delta T$ can exceed **400 °C**, leading to fade.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Brake Types

| Parameter | Mechanical Brake | Hydraulic Brake | Air (Pneumatic) Brake | Electric Brake | Disc Brake (Friction) | Drum Brake (Friction) |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|----------------------|----------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **Actuation** | Cable/lever | Fluid pressure | Compressed air | Electromagnetic | Hydraulic (typical) | Hydraulic / Mechanical |
| **Force Multiplication** | Low (lever) | High (Pascal's law) | Very High | Variable | High | High |
| **Self-Energy** | No | No | No | No | No | Yes (leading shoe) |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Poor | Moderate | Good | Excellent (regen) | **Excellent** (open air) | Moderate (enclosed) |
| **Water Recovery** | Slow | Slow | Slow | Instant | **Fast** (centrifugal) | Slow |
| **Fade Resistance** | Low | Moderate | High | N/A | **High** | Low–Moderate |
| **Maintenance** | Frequent | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Low | Moderate |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | High | Moderate–High | Low–Moderate |
| **Application** | Parking brakes | Passenger cars | HCVs, buses | EVs, trailers | Front wheels (modern) | Rear wheels (older) |
| **Stopping Distance** | Long | Short | Short | Regenerative assist | **Shortest** | Moderate |
| **ABS Compatibility** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | **Excellent** | Good |
