## 4.2 Accident Investigation & Reporting Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    sA[ACCIDENT SITE ARRIVAL]
    sB[SECURE THE SITE AND PRESERVE EVIDENCE]
    sC[MEASURE SKID MARKS CRUSH DEPTH]
    sD[PHOTOGRAPH VEHICLES AND ROAD GEOMETRY]
    sE[INTERVIEW WITNESSES AND DRIVERS]
    sF[RECONSTRUCT SPEED USING ENERGY EQUATIONS]
    sG[IDENTIFY CAUSAL FACTORS HADDON MATRIX]
    sH[PREPARE FINAL ACCIDENT REPORT]
    sI[DATA ENTRY INTO NATIONAL DATABASE]

    sA --> sB --> sC --> sD --> sE --> sF --> sG --> sH --> sI
```

---

## 4.3 Vehicle Collision Geometry — Two-Car Frontal Impact

```mermaid
graph TD
    vA[VEHICLE A MASS M1 VELOCITY V1]
    vB[VEHICLE B MASS M2 VELOCITY V2]
    vC[POINT OF IMPACT]
    vD[DEFORMED CRUSH ZONE A]
    vE[DEFORMED CRUSH ZONE B]
    vF[POST COLLISION COMMON VELOCITY VF]
    vG[ENERGY DISSIPATION EK LOSS]
    vH[MOMENTUM CONSERVATION EQUATION]

    vA --> vC
    vB --> vC
    vC --> vD
    vC --> vE
    vC --> vF
    vC --> vG
    vC --> vH
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Diagram Rationale:** Because automobile accident scenes involve complex physical geometry (overlapping crush zones, three-dimensional deformation, dynamic yaw), the Mermaid renderer is better utilised for *functional architecture flow* rather than literal engineering drawings. The above block diagrams faithfully map the engineering analytical workflow that KTU examiners expect in 7-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below strictly follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**: Part A (3 marks × 2 questions), Part B (14 marks with internal choice between Question A and Question B, each with sub-parts of 7 marks). Bloom's taxonomy cognitive levels are tagged for each question.

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [Part A — 3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Cognitive Level:** Remember / Understand
**Q:** Define the term **"Road Traffic Accident"** as per the Indian Motor Vehicles Act, 1988, and list the **three categories** of automobile accidents based on severity.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
A **Road Traffic Accident (RTA)** is any accident occurring on a public or private road involving one or more motor vehicles, resulting in death, bodily injury, or property damage exceeding the legally prescribed threshold.

The **three categories** are:
1. **Fatal Accident** — Causes death of one or more persons within 30 days.
2. **Injury Accident** — Causes non-fatal injuries (minor, serious, grievous).
3. **Property Damage Only (PDO) Accident** — No injury, only material loss ≥ ₹ 25,000.

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for definition + 1.5 marks for categories + 0.5 mark for threshold value.

---

### Question 2 [Part A — 3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Cognitive Level:** Understand / Apply
**Q:** What is the **Haddon Matrix**? Name its three temporal phases.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)
The **Haddon Matrix** is a public-health and engineering framework developed by Dr. William Haddon Jr. (1968) that analyses accident causation across three temporal phases and four causal factors (Human, Vehicle, Road, Environment).

The **three temporal phases** are:
1. **Pre-Crash Phase** — Accident prevention.
2. **Crash Phase** — Injury mitigation during impact.
3. **Post-Crash Phase** — Rehabilitation, rescue, and recovery.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for explanation + 1 mark for the three phases.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Braking Distance* with *Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)* — SSD includes a 2.5 s perception-reaction term.
> - **Do NOT** drop units in mid-derivation — examiners deduct **0.5 mark per step** where SI units are omitted.
> - **Do NOT** use absolute-value bars `|x|` directly in markdown formulas — KTU expects clean LaTeX rendering.
> - **Always** convert km/h to m/s by multiplying by **5/18**, and m/s to km/h by multiplying by **18/5**.
> - In Haddon Matrix questions, **always** mention the engineering countermeasure — purely legal answers fetch zero credit.

---

### Question A — Choice 1 [Part B — 14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyse
**Q:** *A two-lane highway segment recorded **180 accidents** in one year. The total vehicle-kilometres travelled on this segment is **9.0 × 10⁸ veh-km**. The road has a posted speed limit of **65 km/h**, a longitudinal gradient of **+3%**, and a measured pavement friction coefficient of **0.65**. The road authority wants to evaluate the following:*

*(a) Compute the **accident rate** in accidents per million vehicle-kilometres, and comment on whether this rate is **above or below** the Indian national benchmark of **0.10 accidents per million veh-km**.*

*(b) Calculate the **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** for the design speed of 65 km/h assuming a perception-reaction time of 2.5 seconds, and recommend whether the existing road geometry satisfies the IRC:66 standard minimum SSD of **80 m**.*

---

#### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{acc}} = 180$
- $VKT = 9.0 \times 10^{8}$ veh-km

**Step 1 — Apply the Accident Rate Formula**

$$ R_{\text{acc}} = \frac{N_{\text{acc}} \times 10^{6}}{VKT} $$

$$ R_{\text{acc}} = \frac{180 \times 10^{6}}{9.0 \times 10^{8}} $$

$$ R_{\text{acc}} = \frac{1.8 \times 10^{8}}{9.0 \times 10^{8}} $$

$$ R_{\text{acc}} = 0.20 \,\text{accidents per million veh-km} $$

**Step 2 — Comparison with National Benchmark**

Since $R_{\text{acc}} = 0.20 > 0.10$ (national benchmark), the rate is **2× the national average**, indicating a **HIGH-RISK segment** requiring engineering intervention (signage, median, anti-skid surface treatment).

> **Mark Split:** 3 marks for formula + 2 marks for substitution + 1 mark for comparison + 1 mark for recommendation.

---

#### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $v = 65$ km/h $= 65 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 18.06$ m/s
- $t_{r} = 2.5$ s
- $f = \mu = 0.65$
- $n = +3\% = 0.03$ (uphill, hence **minus** sign in formula)
- Standard benchmark SSD $= 80$ m

**Step 1 — Recall the Standard IRC SSD Formula**

$$ SSD = v \cdot t_{r} + \frac{v^{2}}{2g(f \pm n)} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the Lag Distance (First Term)**

$$ d_{\text{lag}} = v \cdot t_{r} = 18.06 \times 2.5 = 45.15 \,\text{m} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the Braking Distance (Second Term)**

$$ d_{\text{brake}} = \frac{v^{2}}{2g(f - n)} = \frac{(18.06)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times (0.65 - 0.03)} $$

$$ d_{\text{brake}} = \frac{326.16}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.62} $$

$$ d_{\text{brake}} = \frac{326.16}{12.1644} $$

$$ d_{\text{brake}} \approx 26.81 \,\text{m} $$

**Step 4 — Total SSD**

$$ SSD = d_{\text{lag}} + d_{\text{brake}} = 45.15 + 26.81 $$

$$ SSD = 71.96 \,\text{m} \approx 72.0 \,\text{m} $$

**Step 5 — Comparison & Recommendation**

Since calculated SSD (≈ 72 m) < benchmark SSD (80 m), the road **satisfies** the IRC:66 minimum requirement **with a margin of ~8 m**. However, considering wet-pavement friction drop (~30%) and driver reaction variability, **engineering upgradation** (rumble strips, anti-skid surfacing, advance warning signs) is recommended as a safety margin.

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for formula + 2 marks for lag distance + 2 marks for braking distance + 1 mark for total + 1 mark for recommendation.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [Part B — 14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Cognitive Level:** Apply / Analyse / Evaluate
**Q:** *(a) Explain the **Haddon Matrix** in detail. For the **Pre-Crash phase**, propose three engineering interventions in the **Vehicle factor** that can prevent or reduce accident probability in India.*

*(b) A **1,200 kg car** moving at **54 km/h** applies brakes on a road with friction coefficient **0.6**. After the brakes are applied, the car skids **20 m** before hitting a fixed object. Compute: (i) the **kinetic energy** of the car just before braking, (ii) the **speed** of the car at the moment of impact, and (iii) the **crush distance** required to limit the occupant deceleration to **15 g**.*

---

#### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define Haddon Matrix**

The Haddon Matrix is a 3×4 analytical grid that cross-tabulates accident causation across **three temporal phases** and **four causal factors**. It is the globally accepted analytical tool for road safety engineering, originally developed by Dr. William Haddon Jr. in 1968 and adopted by WHO.

**Step 2 — Describe the Framework**

| Phase | Human | Vehicle | Road | Environment |
|-------|-------|---------|------|-------------|
| Pre-Crash | Driver training, fatigue monitoring | Braking systems, tyre tread depth, lighting | Signage, lane markings, sight distance | Weather forecasts, road lighting |
| Crash | Seatbelt, helmet use | Crumple zones, airbags, ABS | Median barriers, crash cushions | Speed-limit enforcement |
| Post-Crash | First aid, Golden Hour care | Fuel cutoff, door-release | Ambulance corridors | Emergency comms |

**Step 3 — Three Engineering Interventions in the *Vehicle Factor (Pre-Crash Phase)***

1. **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** — Prevents wheel lock-up during emergency braking, maintaining steering control and reducing skid-related accidents by ~**18%** (NHTSA data).

2. **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** — Uses yaw-rate sensors and individual wheel braking to prevent skidding and rollover. Reduces single-vehicle accidents by ~**38%** and rollover by ~**80%**.

3. **Tyre Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS) + Adaptive Headlights** — TPMS alerts the driver to under-inflation (a major cause of blowouts); adaptive headlights improve night-time cornering visibility, reducing night accidents by ~**10%**.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for framework explanation + 3 marks for three interventions (1 mark each) + 2 marks for stating quantitative benefits.

---

#### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $m = 1{,}200$ kg
- $v_{\text{initial}} = 54$ km/h $= 54 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 15$ m/s
- $\mu = 0.6$
- $s_{\text{skid}} = 20$ m
- $a_{\text{allowable}} = 15g = 15 \times 9.81 = 147.15$ m/s²

**Sub-part (i): Kinetic Energy Just Before Braking**

$$ KE_{\text{initial}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_{\text{initial}}^{2} $$

$$ KE_{\text{initial}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1200 \times (15)^{2} $$

$$ KE_{\text{initial}} = 600 \times 225 = 135{,}000 \,\text{J} = 135 \,\text{kJ} $$

**Sub-part (ii): Speed at the Moment of Impact**

Energy dissipated during skidding:

$$ E_{\text{dissipated}} = \mu \cdot m \cdot g \cdot s_{\text{skid}} $$

$$ E_{\text{dissipated}} = 0.6 \times 1200 \times 9.81 \times 20 $$

$$ E_{\text{dissipated}} = 141{,}264 \,\text{J} \approx 141.26 \,\text{kJ} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since the dissipated energy (141 kJ) is **greater** than the initial KE (135 kJ), mathematically the car would have stopped *before* the impact. This is a *physical inconsistency* in the problem statement, indicating that either $s_{\text{skid}}$ is over-reported or $v$ is higher. For *pedagogical purposes* in KTU valuation, the standard approach is to use energy conservation:

**Remaining KE at impact:**

$$ KE_{\text{impact}} = 135{,}000 - 141{,}264 = -6{,}264 \,\text{J} $$

Since negative energy is non-physical, we conclude $v_{\text{impact}} \approx 0$ m/s (car stopped before impact).

**Sub-part (iii): Crush Distance for Deceleration = 15 g**

Using $v^{2} = u^{2} - 2 a s$, with $u = 15$ m/s, $v = 0$:

$$ s_{\text{crush}} = \frac{u^{2}}{2 a_{\text{allow}}} = \frac{(15)^{2}}{2 \times 147.15} $$

$$ s_{\text{crush}} = \frac{225}{294.30} \approx 0.765 \,\text{m} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This 0.765 m crush distance is the engineering specification for the front crumple zone of the car — a **directly measurable** design parameter that KTU expects students to derive.

> **Mark Split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks (with energy-balance + physical commentary), (iii) 2 marks for formula + substitution.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Automobile Accidents**

- ✅ **Definition:** RTA = motor vehicle + road + fatality/injury/PDO; reportable threshold in India = ₹ 25,000.
- ✅ **Three Severity Categories:** Fatal / Injury / Property Damage Only (PDO).
- ✅ **Seven Accident Types:** Single-vehicle, multi-vehicle, pedestrian, rollover, fire, submersion, mechanical failure.
- ✅ **Haddon Matrix:** 3 phases × 4 factors — must be memorised with one engineering countermeasure per cell.
- ✅ **Key Formulas:**
  - $R_{\text{acc}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{acc}} \times 10^{6}}{VKT}$
  - $R_{\text{fat}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{death}} \times 10^{4}}{N_{\text{veh}}}$
  - $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$
  - $v_{i} = \sqrt{2\mu g s}$ (forensic reconstruction)
  - $SSD = v t_{r} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g(f \pm n)}$
  - $a_{\text{peak}} = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2s}$ (crumple zone design)
- ✅ **Unit Conversions:** km/h → m/s (×5/18); m/s → km/h (×18/5).
- ✅ **Golden Hour:** First 60 minutes post-trauma; mortality drops by 25–40% with timely medical care.
- ✅ **India-specific Data (MoRTH 2022):** 1,55,622 deaths; fatality rate ≈ 11.4/10,000 vehicles; over-speeding is the #1 cause (66.5%).
- ✅ **Engineering mantra:** *"Longer crush distance + shorter deceleration time = lower occupant injury."*
- ✅ **Static Stability Factor (SSF):** $SSF = T/(2h) \geq 1.20$ for safe rollover resistance.
- ✅ **Common Valuation Pitfall:** Forgetting the *friction coefficient drop* (~30%) for wet roads in SSD design.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Engineers cannot prevent accidents — they can only engineer the systems (human, vehicle, road) so that the probability of an accident is minimised and the severity of an accident, when it occurs, is reduced.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMV Rules Regarding Safety Devices for Drivers and Passengers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT636 - AUTOMOBILE ENGINEERING AND SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CMV Rules Regarding Safety Devices for Drivers and Passengers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

The **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989** is the principal subordinate legislation under the **Motor Vehicles Act, 1988** of India that prescribes the technical specifications, safety standards, and statutory requirements applicable to every motor vehicle operating on Indian roads. Under the CMVR framework, specific chapters and rule numbers govern **in-vehicle safety devices** designed to protect drivers and passengers during normal operation, collision events, and post-crash scenarios.

For the scope of Module 4, the relevant CMV Rules cover mandatory fitment of:

| CMV Rule | Safety Device / Aspect |
|----------|------------------------|
| Rule 17 | Steering control (energy absorbing) |
| Rule 100 | General constructional & safety requirements |
| Rule 101 | Brakes, parking brakes, endurance tests |
| Rule 102 | Tyres (load rating, condition, tread depth) |
| Rule 103 | Safety belts, anchorages, and child restraints |
| Rule 104 | Safety glasses (windshields & windows) |
| Rule 105 | Rear-view mirrors (interior + exterior) |
| Rule 106 | Horns & audible warning devices |
| Rule 107 | Windshield wipers and demisters |
| Rule 108 | Lighting devices & reflectors |
| Rule 109 | Speedometer and tell-tales |
| Rule 110 | Pillion rider footrests (2-wheelers) |
| Rule 138 | Airbags (mandatory under amended rules) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Salience:** The CMVR does not operate in isolation. It is reinforced by the **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards)** drafted by the **Automotive Research Association of India (ARAI)** and notified by the **Ministry of Road Transport and Highways (MoRTH)**. AIS-001, AIS-005, AIS-008, AIS-010, AIS-016, AIS-018, AIS-019, AIS-022, AIS-052, AIS-053, AIS-096 are the most frequently cited safety standards in KTU examinations.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Cockpit Shield" Analogy

Imagine a soldier going into combat. He is not given just one piece of protection — he is given a **layered armor system**: a helmet (head), a vest (torso), boots (feet), and goggles (eyes). Each piece addresses a different failure mode.

A modern motor vehicle is engineered on exactly the same **defense-in-depth philosophy**. The CMV Rules codify this layered safety architecture:

- **Layer 1 — Active Safety:** Devices that *prevent* a crash (brakes, lamps, mirrors, horn, wipers, speedometer).
- **Layer 2 — Passive Safety:** Devices that *mitigate injury* during a crash (seat belts, airbags, padded steering, laminated glass, crumple zones).
- **Layer 3 — Post-Crash Safety:** Devices that *prevent secondary harm* after a crash (fuel cut-off, door unlock, hazard lamps, fire-resistant materials).

> [!NOTE]
> **Student Takeaway:** Whenever a CMV Rule is asked in exams, immediately classify it into **Active / Passive / Post-Crash** layer. Examiners reward this layered thinking in 14-mark questions.

---

## 1.3 Governing Statutory Bodies (Mandatory for 3-Mark Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The five statutory bodies every KTU student must remember:
>
> 1. **MoRTH** (Ministry of Road Transport & Highways) — apex policy maker.
> 2. **ARAI** (Automotive Research Association of India) — technical arm that drafts AIS.
> 3. **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) — material & component standards (e.g., IS 2553 for safety glass).
> 4. **iCAT** (International Centre for Automotive Technology) — testing & homologation.
> 5. **CIRT** (Central Institute of Road Transport) — public transport specific testing.

---

## 1.4 Scope of "Driver + Passenger" in CMVR

The phrase *Drivers and Passengers* in the CMV Rules is legally distinct:

- **Driver** — the person in actual control of the vehicle (occupant of the front-left seat in LHD/Indian-spec RHD cars). Driver-specific rules cover steering wheel energy absorption (Rule 17), driving controls ergonomics (AIS-023), and seat belt anchorage on the driver side.
- **Passenger** — every other occupant including front passenger, rear seat occupants, pillion rider on 2-wheelers, and standees in buses. Passenger-specific rules cover seat belt anchorages, airbag deployment zones, child restraint anchorages (ISOFIX per AIS-068), and seat strength.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rule 17 — Steering Mechanism Safety

The steering wheel must be of the **energy-absorbing type** such that under a frontal impact at **≥48 km/h** against a rigid barrier, the steering column collapses progressively to prevent penetration of the steering shaft into the driver's chest cavity.

**Key specifications:**
- Steering wheel diameter: **min 380 mm** for M1 category vehicles.
- Steering column collapse stroke: **min 100 mm** axial collapse before rearward intrusion exceeds **127 mm**.
- Tested per **AIS-010 (Part 1)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**48 – 127 – 100**" — Test speed 48 km/h, max rearward intrusion 127 mm, minimum collapse 100 mm.

---

## 2.2 Rule 103 — Safety Belts & Anchorages

Three-point retractable safety belts are mandatory for:
- Front outboard seats of all passenger cars manufactured after **01.10.2002**.
- All seating positions of vehicles manufactured under **BS-VI Phase 2** norms (post April 2023).

**Test Load on Anchorages:**

$$F_{anchor} = 13.5 \times m_{occupant} \times g$$

Where:
- $m_{occupant}$ = **75 kg** (standard occupant mass per AIS-005).
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** (gravitational acceleration).

Therefore, the standard test load on each lower anchorage is approximately **13.5 × 75 × 9.81 ≈ 9,930 N ≈ 10 kN**.

> For upper anchorage, the load is applied at **45° angle** to the horizontal, while the lower anchorage is loaded at **0° ± 5°**.

---

## 2.3 Rule 104 — Safety Glazing Materials

Windshields and windows must use:
- **Laminated safety glass** for the windshield (two sheets of float glass bonded by a **0.76 mm PVB (Poly-Vinyl-Butyral)** interlayer).
- **Tempered (toughened) safety glass** permitted for side and rear windows.

**Fragmentation test (per IS 2553 Part 3):**
- A **226 g ± 2 g** steel ball is dropped from a height of **4 m** onto the glass sample.
- The sample must not allow the ball to penetrate completely.

**Light transmission:**

$$T_{windshield} \geq 70\%$$

$$T_{side\,window} \geq 50\%$$

---

## 2.4 Rule 138 — Airbags (Mandatory Fitment Notification)

Following the **MoRTH GSR 148(E) dated 15.02.2022**, dual front airbags became mandatory for all **M1 category** vehicles manufactured on or after **01.04.2021**.

**Deployment Threshold (typical per AIS-096):**

$$\Delta V_{deploy} \geq 16 \text{ to } 25 \text{ km/h}$$

Where $\Delta V$ is the **velocity change** during the crash pulse.

**Key metrics of an airbag:**
- Inflation time: **30 to 55 milliseconds**.
- Deflation time: **100 to 200 milliseconds**.
- Operating gas: **Sodium azide (NaN₃) propellant** in older systems; modern inflators use **compressed argon/nitrogen gas or hybrid inflators**.

$$2\,NaN_3 \rightarrow 2\,Na + 3\,N_2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the M1 category with N1. **M1 = passenger vehicles ≤ 9 seats including driver; N1 = goods vehicles ≤ 3.5 t.** Dual front airbag mandate applies ONLY to M1.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Topic | Formula / Standard | Variables & Units | Reference Rule / AIS |
|---|-------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Lower anchorage test load | $F = 13.5 \cdot m \cdot g$ | $m$ in kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s² | Rule 103 / AIS-005 |
| 2 | Head injury criterion (HIC) | $HIC = \left(t_2 - t_1\right)\left[\dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\displaystyle\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a\,dt\right]^{2.5}$ | $a$ in g, $t$ in s | AIS-008 |
| 3 | Windshield light transmission | $T \geq 0.70$ | Dimensionless | Rule 104 / IS 2553 |
| 4 | Side window light transmission | $T \geq 0.50$ | Dimensionless | Rule 104 / IS 2553 |
| 5 | Stopping distance | $d = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g} + v \cdot t_r$ | $v$ in m/s, $\mu$ dimensionless, $t_r$ in s | Rule 96 |
| 6 | Braking deceleration | $a \geq 5.4$ m/s² (passenger) | – | Rule 96 / IS 11852 |
| 7 | Tire tread depth | $t \geq 1.6$ mm (PC) / $\geq 2.0$ mm (CV) | mm | Rule 102 |
| 8 | Airbag inflation energy | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} m_{gas} v_{gas}^2$ | J | AIS-096 |
| 9 | Crash pulse delta-V | $\Delta V = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{T} a(t)\,dt$ | m/s | AIS-096 |
| 10 | Steering column intrusion | $\delta_{max} \leq 127$ mm | mm | Rule 17 / AIS-010 |

---

## 2.6 Rule 102 — Tyres & Tread Depth Standards

Tyres must conform to:
- **Load index** & **speed symbol** markings per ECE R30 / R54.
- **Tread depth** $\geq$ **1.6 mm** for LMV and **2.0 mm** for HMV.
- No re-treaded tyres permitted on front axle of passenger vehicles.
- Tubeless radials mandatory for all M1 vehicles manufactured after **01.04.2021** (per AIS-044).

---

## 2.7 Rule 105 — Rear-View Mirrors

| Mirror Class | Position | Field of View Required |
|--------------|----------|------------------------|
| Class I (Interior) | Inside, top of windshield | **20° horizontal × 18° vertical** |
| Class II (Main exterior — driver side) | Outside left | **17°30′ horizontal × 9° vertical** |
| Class III (Main exterior — passenger side, large vehicle) | Outside right | **15° horizontal × 9° vertical** |
| Class IV (Wide-angle — passenger side, M1) | Outside right bottom corner | **15° horizontal × 9° vertical** |
| Class V (Close-proximity "kerb") | Outside front fender | **12° horizontal × 9° vertical** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> From **01.04.2021**, AIS-002 mandates that **every M1 vehicle** must have an interior Class I mirror AND an exterior driver-side mirror (Class II). Additionally, if the rearward field of view is obscured, **Class IV wide-angle exterior passenger-side mirror** is compulsory.

---

## 2.8 Rule 107 — Windshield Wipers & Demisters

- Windshield wipers must operate at a minimum of **two speeds** (low & high).
- Demister/defogger must clear a windshield of **frost** within **15 minutes** at ambient temperature **−8 °C**.
- Wiper blade pressure must be between **0.12 N/cm to 0.25 N/cm**.

---

## 2.9 Engineering Utility in Real-World Industry

The CMV Rules form the **homologation checklist** for every vehicle OEM in India. Failure to comply results in:
1. **Rejection of type approval** by the testing agency (ARAI/iCAT).
2. **Non-issuance of Form 22** (road worthiness certificate).
3. **Non-registration** by the RTO.
4. **Penalty under Section 190 of MV Act 1988** — up to ₹10,000 fine / imprisonment up to 6 months for first offence.

For OEM R&D teams, the CMVR is consulted at the **concept freeze stage** of every new vehicle program.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Anchor Test Load on a 3-Point Seat Belt

We must verify the anchor test load value mandated by AIS-005.

**Step 1:** Identify the standard test load factor recommended by AIS-005 and ECE R14.

The test load factor for a **three-point belt system** is taken as:

$$k = 13.5 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 2:** Write the expression for the test load on the lower anchorage.

$$F_{lower} = k \cdot m_{occupant} \cdot g$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the standard occupant mass and gravity.

$$F_{lower} = 13.5 \times 75 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the product of 75 and 9.81.

$$75 \times 9.81 = 735.75 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by 13.5.

$$F_{lower} = 13.5 \times 735.75 = 9{,}932.625 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6:** Express in engineering rounded form.

$$\boxed{F_{lower} \approx 10 \text{ kN per anchorage}}$$

This is the **load that the anchorage points must sustain for at least 0.2 seconds without separation** during a sled test.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stopping Distance Formula (Rule 96)

A vehicle traveling at velocity $v$ applies brakes. The reaction time is $t_r$ and the coefficient of friction is $\mu$.

**Step 1:** Distance travelled during driver's perception-reaction time.

$$d_1 = v \cdot t_r$$

**Step 2:** Distance travelled during the braking phase (kinematics with constant deceleration).

Using $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a d$ and $a = \mu g$:

$$d_2 = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$$

**Step 3:** Total stopping distance.

$$d_{total} = d_1 + d_2 = v \cdot t_r + \dfrac{v^2}{2 \mu g}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute typical Indian dry-road values: $v = 20$ m/s (72 km/h), $t_r = 1.5$ s, $\mu = 0.7$.

$$d_2 = \dfrac{(20)^2}{2 \times 0.7 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{400}{13.734} = 29.12 \text{ m}$$

$$d_1 = 20 \times 1.5 = 30 \text{ m}$$

$$d_{total} = 30 + 29.12 = 59.12 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** KTU board exam convention — convert $v$ in **km/h to m/s** by dividing by 3.6.

If $V$ is in km/h:

$$\boxed{d_{total} = 0.278 \, V \cdot t_r + \dfrac{V^2}{254 \cdot \mu} \text{ (metres)}}$$

This is the form students must reproduce in the answer script.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Head Injury Criterion (HIC)

The **HIC** is used in CMV Rule / AIS-008 to assess head injury severity.

**Step 1:** Define the head acceleration time-history $a(t)$ expressed in **g** (multiples of gravitational acceleration).

**Step 2:** Choose the most critical time interval $[t_1, t_2]$ within the first 36 ms of the crash, such that:

$$t_2 - t_1 \leq 36 \text{ ms} = 0.036 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the average acceleration over $[t_1, t_2]$.

$$\bar{a} = \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4:** Plug into the HIC expression.

$$\boxed{HIC = (t_2 - t_1)\left[\dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\displaystyle\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5}}$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the limits (per AIS-008 and FMVSS 208):

| HIC Value | Injury Severity |
|-----------|------------------|
| HIC $\leq$ 240 | No significant head injury |
| 240 $\lt$ HIC $\leq$ 1000 | Moderate to severe |
| HIC $\gt$ 1000 | Severe / fatal |

**Step 6:** Design implication — a windshield with airbag interaction must keep HIC $\leq$ 1000 for unbelted dummy occupants.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Layered Safety Device Mapping

| Device | CMVR Rule | AIS Standard | Test Speed / Load | Layer |
|--------|-----------|--------------|-------------------|-------|
| Seat belt | 103 | AIS-005 / AIS-068 | 10 kN anchorage | Passive |
| Airbag (driver) | 138 | AIS-096 | $\Delta V \geq 25$ km/h | Passive |
| Laminated windshield | 104 | IS 2553 Pt 3 | 226 g ball, 4 m drop | Passive |
| Side window tempered | 104 | IS 2553 Pt 2 | Fragmentation test | Passive |
| Crumple zone (passive frame) | 100 | AIS-097 | 56 km/h frontal | Passive |
| Service brakes | 96 | IS 11852 | $\mu = 0.7$, 56 km/h | Active |
| Rear-view mirror | 105 | AIS-002 | Geometric field | Active |
| Headlight beam pattern | 108 | AIS-008 (lighting) | Photometric | Active |
| Horn (audible) | 106 | AIS-014 | 93–112 dB at 7 m | Active |
| Wipers | 107 | AIS-045 | 2-speed operation | Active |
| Steering column | 17 | AIS-010 | 48 km/h barrier | Passive |
| Tyres | 102 | AIS-044 | 1.6 mm tread depth | Active |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Crash Pulse $\Delta V$ from Acceleration Time-History

**Step 1:** Define acceleration profile $a(t)$ from accelerometer trace during a crash.

**Step 2:** Integrate over the crash duration $T$ to get velocity change.

$$\Delta V = \int_0^T a(t)\,dt$$

**Step 3:** Approximate using trapezoidal rule for sampled data (sample rate $f_s$ in Hz, $N$ samples).

$$\Delta V \approx \dfrac{1}{2 f_s} \sum_{k=1}^{N-1} \left[a(k) + a(k+1)\right]$$

**Step 4:** Sanity check — for a 56 km/h frontal barrier test ($V_0 = 15.56$ m/s) into a non-deformable barrier, $\Delta V \approx 15.56$ m/s $\approx$ **56 km/h** (full velocity change).

For a deformable barrier, $\Delta V \approx 60\text{–}70\%$ of $V_0$ depending on energy absorption.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Layered Safety Architecture of a CMV-Compliant Vehicle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[CMV Safety Architecture for Driver and Passenger]
    
    A0 --> A1[Layer 1 - Active Safety - Crash Prevention]
    A0 --> A2[Layer 2 - Passive Safety - Crash Survival]
    A0 --> A3[Layer 3 - Post-Crash - Secondary Harm Prevention]

    A1 --> A11[Rule 96 Brakes Service and Parking]
    A1 --> A12[Rule 102 Tyres Tread and Load Index]
    A1 --> A13[Rule 105 Mirrors Class I II III IV V]
    A1 --> A14[Rule 106 Horn 93 to 112 dB]
    A1 --> A15[Rule 107 Wipers and Demisters]
    A1 --> A16[Rule 108 Lighting Headlamps Indicators]
    A1 --> A17[Rule 109 Speedometer and Tell Tales]

    A2 --> A21[Rule 17 Steering Energy Absorbing Column]
    A2 --> A22[Rule 100 General Body Strength]
    A2 --> A23[Rule 103 Seat Belts Three Point ISOFIX]
    A2 --> A24[Rule 104 Laminated Safety Glass]
    A2 --> A25[Rule 138 Airbags Front Side Curtain]
    A2 --> A26[AIS 097 Crumple Zone Frame Design]

    A3 --> A31[Fuel Cut off Inertia Switch]
    A3 --> A32[Auto Door Unlock on Impact]
    A3 --> A33[Hazard Lamps Auto Activation]
    A3 --> A34[Fire Retardant Interior Materials]
```

**Figure 4.1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of CMV-Compliant Safety Layers**

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Seat Belt Anchorage Load Direction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A1[Lower Anchorage Test - 0 degrees]
        L1[Load 13.5 x 75 x g = 10 kN]
        L2[Direction Forward along seat base]
        L1 --> L2
    end
    
    subgraph A2[Upper Anchorage Test - 45 degrees]
        U1[Load 13.5 x 75 x g = 10 kN]
        U2[Direction 45 degrees from horizontal downward rearward]
        U1 --> U2
    end
    
    subgraph A3[Anchor System]
        O[Occupant Dummy 75 kg]
        B[Three Point Belt]
        O --- B
        B --- L1
        B --- U1
    end
```

**Figure 4.2: Three-Point Belt Anchorage Load Test Configuration (per AIS-005)**

---

## 4.3 Schematic — Airbag Deployment Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Crash Sensor Signal a in g]
    D0 --> D1{Delta V Calculated}
    D1 --> D2{Delta V between 16 and 25 km per hour}
    D1 --> D3{Delta V greater than 25 km per hour}
    D1 --> D4{Delta V less than 16 km per hour}
    
    D2 --> D5[Stage 1 Inflation - 30 percent gas]
    D5 --> D6[Evaluate Severity - Stage 2 Decision]
    D6 --> D7{Still Severe}
    D6 --> D8[Vent and Deflate]
    
    D3 --> D9[Full Deployment - 100 percent in 30 ms]
    
    D4 --> D10[No Deployment - Seat Belt Only]
    
    D7 --> D9
```

**Figure 4.3: Airbag Staging Algorithm per AIS-096**

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Steering Energy Absorption Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C0[Frontal Impact at 48 km per hour]
    C0 --> C1[Rigid Barrier Contact]
    C1 --> C2[Axial Compression of Column]
    C2 --> C3{Intrusion Less than 127 mm}
    C3 --> C4[Pass - Energy Absorbed]
    C3 --> C5[Fail - Steering Wheel Rearward Intrusion]
    
    C2 --> C6[Telescopic Joint Collapse]
    C2 --> C7[Ball Detent Slip]
    C2 --> C8[Mesh Strip Deformation]
```

**Figure 4.4: Steering Column Collapse Mechanism — AIS-010 Test Pathway**

---

## 4.5 Schematic — CMV Rule Reference Map for Safety Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[CMV Rules 1989 and AIS]
    
    R --> R1[Rule 17 Steering]
    R --> R2[Rule 100 General Safety]
    R --> R3[Rule 101 Brakes]
    R --> R4[Rule 102 Tyres]
    R --> R5[Rule 103 Seat Belts]
    R --> R6[Rule 104 Safety Glass]
    R --> R7[Rule 105 Mirrors]
    R --> R8[Rule 106 Horn]
    R --> R9[Rule 107 Wipers]
    R --> R10[Rule 108 Lighting]
    R --> R11[Rule 109 Speedometer]
    R --> R12[Rule 138 Airbags]
    
    R1 --> A1[AIS 010]
    R5 --> A2[AIS 005 and AIS 068]
    R6 --> A3[IS 2553]
    R7 --> A4[AIS 002]
    R8 --> A5[AIS 014]
    R12 --> A6[AIS 096]
    
    A1 --> T1[ARAI Testing Pune]
    A2 --> T1
    A3 --> T2[BIS Certification]
    A4 --> T1
    A5 --> T1
    A6 --> T1
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List any six mandatory safety devices prescribed by the Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR) for the protection of the driver of a passenger car (M1 category). State the corresponding rule number for each.**

**Model Answer (6 points × 0.5 mark each = 3 marks):**

1. **Steering wheel of energy-absorbing type** — Rule 17.
2. **Service and parking brakes** — Rule 96 / Rule 101.
3. **Seat belt with three-point anchorage** — Rule 103.
4. **Laminated safety glass windshield** — Rule 104.
5. **Rear-view mirrors (interior Class I + exterior Class II)** — Rule 105.
6. **Windscreen wipers and demister** — Rule 107.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is AIS-096? Explain its scope with respect to driver and passenger protection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **AIS-096** is the **Automotive Industry Standard** notified by MoRTH governing the **type approval of airbag systems** for M1 and N1 category vehicles in India.
- It prescribes the **test procedures** for frontal airbag modules including the **deployment threshold**, **inflator performance**, **dummy interaction**, and **out-of-position (OOP) occupant assessment**.
- It is referenced under **Rule 138 of CMVR** which mandates dual front airbags in all new M1 vehicles manufactured on or after **01.04.2021**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently lose marks by (a) writing in paragraphs without sub-headings, (b) omitting the relevant CMV Rule number, (c) confusing AIS numbers, and (d) failing to provide numerical examples. Every 14-mark answer should have **at least three numerical values** and **at least four AIS/Rule references**.

---

### Question B (A) — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

**(a)** Explain in detail the CMVR specifications for **safety belts and their anchorages** in passenger cars. Include the test load calculation, anchor directions, and child restraint provisions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **CMV Rules for safety glazing materials** used in motor vehicles. Discuss the differences between laminated and tempered glass, the fragmentation test, and light transmission requirements. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Detailed Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Seat Belts & Anchorages (7 marks)

**1. Statutory Basis (1 mark)**

The fitment of safety belts in M1 category vehicles in India is governed by **Rule 103 of CMVR 1989**, reinforced by **AIS-005 (anchorages)** and **AIS-068 (child restraints / ISOFIX)**. The mandate became effective in three phases:
- **01.10.2002** — front outboard seats of all passenger cars.
- **01.10.2017** — rear outboard seats (BS-VI Phase 1).
- **01.04.2021** — all seating positions with **ISOFIX child anchorages** (mandatory for cars with child seats).

**2. Types of Safety Belts Permitted (1 mark)**

CMVR Rule 103 permits:
- **Three-point retractable belt** (lap + diagonal sash) — mandatory for front outboard.
- **Lap belt only** — permitted only for centre rear seat in older vehicles.
- **Four-point belt** — for special purpose vehicles.
- **Five-point belt** — mandated for child restraints (per AIS-068).

**3. Anchor Test Load Derivation (2 marks)**

The lower anchorage is tested with a load:

$$F_{lower} = k \cdot m \cdot g$$

Where $k = 13.5$ and $m = 75$ kg (75th percentile male occupant).

$$F_{lower} = 13.5 \times 75 \times 9.81 \approx 9{,}932.6 \text{ N} \approx 10 \text{ kN}$$

For the upper anchorage, the test load of **4.5 kN** is applied at **45° ± 10° to the horizontal**, directed forward and downward.

**4. Anchor Directions (1 mark)**

| Anchorage | Load | Direction |
|-----------|------|-----------|
| Lower (2 points) | 10 kN each | Forward, horizontal, $\pm$ 5° |
| Upper (1 point) | 4.5 kN | 45° forward-downward |
| Effective load duration | $\geq$ 0.2 s | – |

**5. Child Restraint — ISOFIX (2 marks)**

AIS-068 mandates two rigid **ISOFIX lower anchorages** (each rated for **8 kN**) and one **top tether anchorage** (rated for **5 kN**) for child seats in the rear. The anchor spacing is **270 mm ± 20 mm** centre-to-centre.

---

#### Part (b) — Safety Glazing (7 marks)

**1. Statutory Basis (1 mark)**

**Rule 104 of CMVR 1989** read with **IS 2553 (Parts 1 to 3)** prescribes the specifications for safety glazing in motor vehicles.

**2. Laminated vs Tempered Glass (2 marks)**

| Property | Laminated | Tempered |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| Structure | Two glass sheets bonded by 0.76 mm PVB | Single sheet, surface-compressed |
| Breakage pattern | Cracks but does not shatter (PVB holds shards) | Shatters into small blunt pieces |
| Mandated location | Windshield (mandatory) | Side and rear windows |
| Optical clarity | Slightly less (PVB tint) | High |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

**3. Fragmentation Test (2 marks)**

Per IS 2553 Part 3:
- A steel ball of mass **226 g ± 2 g** is dropped from a height of **4 m**.
- The ball must not completely penetrate the glass.
- For tempered glass, the number of fragments within a **5 cm × 5 cm** square must be between **40 and 400** (avalanche of small pieces prevents large lacerations).

**4. Light Transmission (1 mark)**

$$T_{windshield} \geq 70\%, \quad T_{side\,window} \geq 50\%$$

**5. Marking Requirements (1 mark)**

Each piece of safety glass must be permanently marked with:
- Manufacturer's trade mark.
- Type designation (e.g., "L" for laminated, "T" for tempered).
- Standard number (IS 2553).
- Country of origin.

---

### Question B (B) — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, describe the **CMV Rules for rear-view mirrors** in passenger cars. Classify the mirror types and state the field-of-view requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **CMV Rule 138 provisions for airbags** in India. Explain the deployment algorithm, the dual front airbag mandate timeline, and the difference between driver and passenger airbag inflators. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Detailed Model Solution**

#### Part (a) — Rear-View Mirrors (7 marks)

**1. Statutory Basis (1 mark)**

**Rule 105 of CMVR 1989** read with **AIS-002 (Rev. 2)** governs rear-view mirrors.

**2. Classification of Mirrors (3 marks)**

| Class | Type | Position | Field Required |
|-------|------|----------|----------------|
| I | Interior mirror | Top-centre of windshield | 20° H × 18° V |
| II | Main exterior driver-side | Left fender / door | 17°30′ H × 9° V |
| III | Main exterior large-vehicle passenger-side | Right side (truck / bus) | 15° H × 9° V |
| IV | Wide-angle exterior passenger-side (M1) | Right fender | 15° H × 9° V |
| V | Close-proximity "kerb" mirror | Front bumper / fender | 12° H × 9° V |

**3. Mandate Summary for M1 Cars (1 mark)**

- Class I interior mirror — mandatory.
- Class II exterior driver-side mirror — mandatory.
- Class IV wide-angle passenger-side — mandatory IF rearward visibility through interior mirror is obstructed.

**4. Material & Mechanical (1 mark)**

Mirror surface reflectance:
- **Interior mirror:** ≥ 40% reflectance.
- **Exterior mirror:** ≥ 35% reflectance.
- Mirrors must withstand a **20 N static load** perpendicular to the surface without permanent deformation.

**5. Test Procedure (1 mark)**

The vehicle is placed on a turntable with a 1 m radius. Target markers are placed at 10 m, 12 m, 15 m, 20 m, 30 m, 40 m, 50 m, and 60 m behind the vehicle along the longitudinal axis. The mirror adjustment is iterated to capture the required field angles.

---

#### Part (b) — Airbags under Rule 138 (7 marks)

**1. Statutory Timeline (2 marks)**

| Date | Mandate | Reference |
|------|---------|-----------|
| 01.04.2021 | Dual front airbags (driver + front passenger) for all new M1 models | GSR 148(E), 15.02.2022 |
| 01.01.2022 | Side and curtain airbags for new models above 4 m length | Draft AIS-096 |
| Future | Pedestrian airbag (draft under AIS-100) | Under development |

**2. Deployment Algorithm (2 marks)**

The airbag control unit (ACU) processes the **crash sensor signal** every **5 ms** and computes:

$$\Delta V = \int_0^T a(t)\,dt$$

If $\Delta V \geq 16$ km/h, first-stage ignition is triggered. If $\Delta V$ continues to rise and exceeds 25 km/h within 25 ms, the second stage (if dual-stage inflator) fires.

**3. Driver vs Passenger Inflator (2 marks)**

| Parameter | Driver Airbag | Passenger Airbag |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Volume | 35 to 65 litres | 60 to 160 litres |
| Shape | Round / oval | Rectangular / T-shaped |
| Inflator energy | ~ 80 to 120 kJ | ~ 150 to 250 kJ |
| Distance from occupant | 300 to 350 mm | 500 to 700 mm |
| Stage deployment | 1 or 2 stages | 1 or 2 stages |

**4. OOP (Out-of-Position) Protection (1 mark)**

Modern systems use **smart airbag algorithms** that suppress deployment if a child seat is detected in the front passenger seat (via seat occupancy sensor) or if the occupant is within **100 mm** of the dashboard.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — Things You MUST Memorize Before Exam**

- [ ] **Rule 17** — Steering energy absorbing; **48 km/h test, 127 mm intrusion limit**.
- [ ] **Rule 96** — Braking deceleration ≥ **5.4 m/s²** for passenger vehicles.
- [ ] **Rule 102** — Tyre tread depth **1.6 mm LMV**, **2.0 mm HMV**; tubeless radials mandatory **01.04.2021**.
- [ ] **Rule 103** — Anchor test load = **13.5 × 75 × 9.81 = 10 kN**; ISOFIX spacing **270 ± 20 mm**.
- [ ] **Rule 104** — Laminated glass **0.76 mm PVB**; **226 g ball, 4 m drop**; $T_{windshield} \geq 70\%$.
- [ ] **Rule 105** — Mirror classes **I, II, III, IV, V**; AIS-002 governs.
- [ ] **Rule 106** — Horn **93 to 112 dB at 7 m**; AIS-014 governs.
- [ ] **Rule 107** — Wiper ≥ **2 speeds**; demister clears windshield in **15 minutes at −8 °C**.
- [ ] **Rule 108** — Headlamp pattern: dip beam asymmetric; DRL mandatory from **01.04.2017**.
- [ ] **Rule 138** — Dual front airbags mandatory from **01.04.2021** for M1.
- [ ] **AIS-096** — Airbag type approval standard.
- [ ] **AIS-097** — Frontal crash test at **56 km/h** into deformable barrier.
- [ ] **HIC limit** — ≤ 1000 for severe injury threshold; ≤ 240 for no significant injury.
- [ ] **Three statutory bodies** — MoRTH, ARAI, BIS (and iCAT, CIRT for homologation).
- [ ] **Standard occupant mass** — **75 kg** for all safety tests.
- [ ] **PVB thickness** — **0.76 mm** for laminated windshield.
- [ ] **$\Delta V$ airbag deployment threshold** — **16 to 25 km/h**.
- [ ] **Steering column collapse** — minimum **100 mm** axial stroke.
